Step 1 — Select your role to personalise this plan
Everything below — the dependency map, weekly plan, and tab highlighting — adapts to your selection.
All Roles
Showing the complete learning path covering all roles. Filter by your specific role to see a focused, personalised plan.
What is this tab? This is the master plan for the whole document: role selection, dependency order, weekly pacing, and the fastest path to the right tabs for your target role.
Why it matters: The document is intentionally broad, so the plan tab prevents random reading order and keeps your study time focused on the concepts interviewers actually expect first.
How to use it: Pick your role, scan the dependency map, then follow the weekly cards or click directly into the tab you need this week.
Example: If you are preparing for an AI Engineer interview in five days, use the role filter, jump to the core tabs for that role, then finish with the Q&A volumes.
How to Use This Document — 3 Learning Modes
📚
Mode 1: Sequential Study (8-12 weeks)
Select your role above. Follow the 7-week plan top-to-bottom. Each week builds on the previous. Use Next→ buttons at the bottom of each tab to stay in sequence. Best for: people starting from scratch or wanting complete coverage.
🔍
Mode 2: Topic Deep-Dive
Click any topic in the dependency map below. Every tab opens at that topic with its context (intro + sections + code + visuals). Use for: "I need to understand RAG this week" or "I'm being interviewed on LLMs tomorrow." Hover over any topic button to see what prerequisites to check first.
🔥
Mode 3: Interview Prep Sprint (3-5 days)
Go straight to Q&A Vol 1-3. Answer each question aloud in under 2 minutes. For any question you struggle with: follow the tab link in the question to go straight to the relevant content. Return, re-answer, repeat. Most effective for: "interview is in 5 days".

Step 3 Your Weekly Study Plan
How to read this plan
CORE= must-know for every AI role. Skip none of these.
DEEP= role-specific depth (e.g. MLOps for platform engineers, RecSys for product MLEs). Filter by your role above.
EXPERT= staff+ depth or research-track topics. Skip on first pass unless interviewing for senior IC roles.
Suggested cadence: ~12 hours/week (~2h/day × 6 days, 1 rest day) for ~7 weeks → ~85 hours total. If you have less time, do CORE only (~50h). The Resume pill at top-right will bring you back to where you stopped.
W1Prereqs
Math, Stats & ML Foundations Non-negotiable for every role
Goal: understand WHY algorithms work. Interviewers test this even for LLM engineer roles. Do not skip.
∫Math & Stats: linear algebra (SVD, cosine sim), information theory (KL/cross-entropy), A/B testing, Bayesian basics
4hCORE
🤖ML Fundamentals: linear models, XGBoost with math, bias-variance, evaluation metrics all with code
4hCORE
📊Data Science: causal inference, A/B test design, SQL analytics patterns
3hCORE
🌎AI Landscape: role taxonomy, 2026 tech stack, what interviewers actually test
1hCORE
W2Deep Learning
Deep Learning Architecture Implement from scratch
Prerequisites: W1 Math. Goal: implement multi-head attention in PyTorch, derive backprop on paper, explain every transformer component.
🧠Backpropagation derivation + new plain-English format: chain rule, vanishing gradients, residual connections
3hCORE
🧠Self-attention: QKV, sqrt(dk) why, causal mask, GQA full PyTorch implementation
4hCORE
🧠Normalization (BN vs LN vs RMSNorm), Optimizers (AdamW), Activations (SwiGLU), MoE
2hDEEP
💻Live Coding: implement backprop 2-layer net, k-means, softmax from scratch
3hEXPERT
W3LLMs
LLMs, GenAI & Fine-tuning
Prerequisites: W2 Deep Learning. Goal: explain GPT architecture component-by-component, derive DPO loss, implement complete LLaMA-style transformer.
🧠Transformer architecture: RoPE, GQA, SwiGLU, KV cache with complete PyTorch code
4hCORE
🧠RLHF → DPO → GRPO: complete alignment pipeline, loss derivations, when to use each
3hCORE
🧠Scaling laws, Chinchilla, inference-optimal models, test-time compute (GPT-5.x thinking, Claude Opus 4.8 adaptive, DeepSeek-R1, Gemini Deep Think)
1.5hCORE
🔧LoRA math, QLoRA code, DPO training code, dataset quality
3hCORE
🌟Diffusion (DDPM math + code), VAE, CLIP, flow matching
2hDEEP
W4RAG & Agents
RAG, Vector Search & AI Agents
Prerequisites: W3 LLMs. Goal: design and evaluate a full RAG pipeline, implement a production ReAct agent with LangGraph, know every failure mode.
🔍RAG pipeline: 5 chunking strategies, HNSW algorithm, BM25 formula, hybrid RRF, reranking + code
4hCORE
🤖ReAct agent code, LangGraph implementation, 4-layer memory, 6 failure modes
3hCORE
🔍Long context: YaRN/RoPE extension, Mamba SSM, structured output
2hDEEP
📊Recommendation: two-tower code, ALS, TFT time series, XGBoost tabular
3hDEEP
W5Engineering
MLOps, Infrastructure & System Design
Prerequisites: W3 LLMs + W4. Goal: design a production AI system end-to-end, explain vLLM PagedAttention, own the monitoring stack.
📈GPU memory math, quantization (NF4/AWQ/GPTQ), distributed training (FSDP/TP/PP) + code
3hCORE
📈vLLM: PagedAttention algorithm, continuous batching, speculative decoding + code
2hCORE
⚙Feature stores, drift detection, CI/CD pipeline YAML, LLMOps (prompt versioning, cost tracking)
3hCORE
🏗Production LLM system (6-layer arch), latency optimisation, cost math, design scenarios
3hCORE
W6Agentic
Agentic Platform Architecture (AKP)
Prerequisites: W5 Engineering. Goal: design all 6 AKP planes, implement Model Gateway + Saga + HITL, understand MCP, OPA, tenant isolation.
🏭AKP 6 planes: Surfaces, Agent Runtime, Tool Plane, Governance, Knowledge, Data Backbone
3hEXPERT
🔒Governance: PRR checklist, Artifact Registry, DPP, red-teaming (Garak), Saga coordinator
3hEXPERT
🤖4-layer memory, context budget, prompt drift detection, IIB adapter code
2hEXPERT
🔒Safety: guardrail code, EU AI Act, OWASP LLM Top 10, Constitutional AI
2hCORE
💻Dev Experience + People & Adoption: prompt library, SDK, CoE model, change management
2hCORE
W7Polish
Interview Polish Practice, Code, Scenarios
Goal: answer every Q&A in under 3 minutes; pass live coding under pressure; connect every answer to business impact.
💻Live Coding: transformer block, VAE, BPE tokenizer, LSTM, beam search all implementations
6hEXPERT
🔥Q&A Vol 1 (Q1-60): foundations, implementations, debugging time yourself, 2 min max
3hCORE
🔥Q&A Vol 2 (Q61-103): applied AI, systems, deployment, multi-agent, evaluation
3hCORE
🔥Q&A Vol 3 (Q104-160): architecture, scaling, ethics, regulatory, career strategy
3hCORE

🎯 Quick Guide

One page to orient yourself. 30 deep tabs is a lot — this hub gives you a visual map, a path for your role, mental models for the hardest ideas, a glossary, and a “where am I lost?” troubleshooter. Use it whenever you feel the rabbit-hole getting too deep.
🎯 Visual roadmap 📚 5 role-based paths → 🧠 8 mental models 📚 74-term glossary → 🔎 Troubleshooter
Use-case quick routes
Optional: start from intent instead of topic names
Build an AI Chatbot
Learn RAG for Enterprise
Understand LLM Architecture
Prepare for AI Interviews
Design an Agent Platform
💬 Keep AI Gita evolving: if something is unclear or outdated, raise it on GitHub Issues.
Request topic Report correction Flag stale content
Moderation rubric + response SLA
  • Priority 1 (critical correctness/security): acknowledge within 24h, target resolution in 72h.
  • Priority 2 (stale vendor facts/pricing): acknowledge within 48h, target resolution in 5 days.
  • Priority 3 (clarity/formatting/topic requests): acknowledge within 5 days, schedule into next content cycle.
Template expectation: include impacted tab, current statement, proposed correction, and source link.

🏢 Architect enablement alignment

  • Learning and architecture delivery are now connected through AKP, System Design, Governance, and Dev Experience paths.
  • Issue templates now capture topic requests, corrections, and freshness drift in a reusable structure for documentation initiatives.
  • Operating rhythm in Snapshot provides a monthly review contract to keep architecture knowledge and delivery guidance synchronized.

Your week-by-week study plan
Pick a pace below. The schedule is built from the role you selected on the Learning Plan tab — switch role there and your plan here updates automatically. Read-tracking applies: tabs you mark as read fade out in the schedule below.
📅 Pace:
Role: All Roles
Role-based pathways — pick the closest match
🌱 Complete BeginnerNo ML background · ~6–10 weeks part-time
5 of 8 steps are CORE — do these first if your time is limited.
  1. COREAI Landscape — "what does AI even mean in 2026"
  2. COREMath & Stats — just the “Where math lives” map; skip derivations
  3. COREML Fundamentals — supervised vs unsupervised, train/test, overfitting
  4. COREDeep Learning — neurons, backprop intuition, transformer overview
  5. COREGenerative AI — LLMs as next-token predictors
  6. DEEPRAG & Vector — "give the LLM a memory"
  7. DEEPAgents & Prompting — prompt engineering first, then agents
  8. EXPERTLive Coding — build your first RAG bot
Goal: understand the field; build one working LLM app.
📊 Data Scientist → AI EngineerKnow stats/ML · ~4–6 weeks
4 of 8 steps are CORE — do these first if your time is limited.
  1. COREAI Landscape — 2026 stack overview
  2. CORELLMs & Foundation — how GPT/Claude/Gemini actually work
  3. COREGenerative AI — transformers, embeddings, tokenization
  4. CORERAG & Vector — the #1 production pattern
  5. DEEPAgents & Prompting — ReAct, MCP, tool use
  6. DEEPFine-tuning — LoRA/QLoRA + DPO/ORPO
  7. DEEPMLOps / LLMOps — evals, observability, drift
  8. EXPERTLive Coding + Q&A Vol II
Goal: ship a production LLM feature end-to-end.
🧮 ML Engineer → AI EngineerKnow DL/training · ~4 weeks
4 of 8 steps are CORE — do these first if your time is limited.
  1. CORELLMs & Foundation — transformer architectures, scaling laws
  2. COREFine-tuning — LoRA math, DPO/GRPO/RLVR
  3. DEEPLong Context & SSMs — RoPE, MLA, Mamba, RULER
  4. COREAI Infrastructure — vLLM, SGLang, KV cache, FP8/FP4
  5. DEEPRAG & Vector — embeddings + late-interaction
  6. DEEPAgents & Prompting — MCP, A2A, multi-agent
  7. COREMLOps + Safety
  8. EXPERTEmerging Trends — reasoning models, MoE serving
Goal: own the model layer in production.
🚀 AI Engineer (greenfield)Building from scratch · ~6 weeks
5 of 8 steps are CORE — do these first if your time is limited.
  1. COREAI Landscape + LLMs — market & vendor map
  2. COREGenerative AI — transformer + diffusion mental models
  3. CORERAG → Agents → Agentic Platform
  4. COREInfrastructure — serving, quant, spec decoding
  5. CORESystem Design — layered architecture, fallbacks
  6. DEEPMLOps + Agent Runtime Ops
  7. DEEPSafety + Governance — EU AI Act, prompt-injection
  8. EXPERTInterview Prep + Career
Goal: design and ship a production agentic system.
🎯 Senior / Solution ArchitectBreadth + decisions · ~3 weeks
5 of 8 steps are CORE — do these first if your time is limited.
  1. CORELandscape + LLM map — vendor lineup, 2026 frontier
  2. CORESystem Design — 7-layer reference architecture
  3. DEEPAgents — MCP / A2A / AG-UI protocol stack
  4. CORERAG — 2026 retrieval menu
  5. COREInfrastructure — serving stack + cost model
  6. DEEPAgentic Platform + Runtime Ops
  7. COREGovernance — EU AI Act, ISO 42001
  8. DEEPPeople & Adoption + Cloud
Goal: make build-vs-buy & architecture decisions confidently.
8 mental models that make the rest click
If you only read one section in this hub, read this one. These eight pictures explain ~80% of what makes 2026 AI feel like magic.
🎲 1. LLM = Next-token slot machine
An LLM is a probability distribution over the next token, conditioned on everything before. P(next | context). Sampling that distribution — one token at a time — produces text. Everything else (chat, agents, reasoning) is engineering layered on top. → LLM tab
📚 2. Context = working memory
The context window is the LLM's only short-term memory. Everything outside it is invisible. RAG, tool-calls, agent scratchpads, MCP resources all exist to get the right tokens into context at the right time. → Long Context · RAG
🔒 3. Pre-train → SFT → Preference → RL
Every modern model is built in stages. Pre-train on the internet (knowledge), SFT on demonstrations (format), DPO/ORPO on preferences (taste), GRPO+RLVR on verifiable rewards (reasoning). Knowing which stage a problem lives in tells you which fix to apply. → Fine-tuning
🧶 4. Reasoning = paying tokens for thinking
2025-26 added test-time compute as a third scaling axis (alongside data & params). A reasoning_effort=high call may burn 50× the visible output as hidden CoT — that's the cost of better answers. Architect rule: expose the knob, budget it, log it. → Emerging Trends
🔗 5. Agent loop = LLM + tools + memory + verifier
An agent is a while-loop: assemble context → LLM picks tool → execute → observe → repeat until a verifier says "done" or a budget kills it. MCP plugs in tools, A2A plugs in other agents, AG-UI plugs in humans. → Agents tab
🏁 6. Inference is memory-bandwidth bound
Generating one token requires reading every active weight from HBM. That's why KV cache, quantization (FP8/FP4/INT4), speculative decoding and disaggregated prefill/decode matter. Every infra trick is fighting the same bottleneck: bytes/second off the GPU. → Infrastructure
🔌 7. RAG > long-context > fine-tune (usually)
Need new knowledge in the answer? Try in order: (1) prompt it (cheap, instant), (2) RAG (fresh, auditable, cheap-ish), (3) long-context (when order matters), (4) fine-tune (when style/format/structure must change). Most teams skip steps and pay for it. → RAG
⚖ 8. Eval > benchmark > vibe
Public benchmarks (MMLU, SWE-bench) tell you the ceiling; your own eval set tells you the floor for your task. You ship to prod, mirror 1% of traffic to an async judge, feed regressions back into the eval set. Without this loop, you're guessing. → MLOps

74-term glossary — the vocabulary of 2026 AI
TokenSub-word unit (~3–4 chars). The atomic input/output of an LLM. Billed per million.GenAI →
Context windowMax tokens the model can read in one call (1M = baseline in 2026, 10M claimed by Llama 4).Long Ctx →
EmbeddingA vector representation of text/image/audio. Closeness in vector space ≈ semantic similarity.RAG →
TransformerThe architecture all 2026 frontier models use. Self-attention + feedforward + residuals.GenAI →
MoE (Mixture of Experts)Only k of N FFN experts fire per token. DeepSeek V3/V4, Llama 4, Mixtral. Cheaper per token.Infra →
KV cacheStored attention keys/values so each new token doesn't re-process the whole context.Infra →
QuantizationCompress weights from FP16 → FP8/FP4/INT4. Less memory, faster inference, tiny quality loss.Infra →
RAGRetrieval-Augmented Generation: search a knowledge base, stuff results into context, answer.RAG →
RerankerSecond-stage model that re-scores retrieval candidates for relevance. Cohere/Voyage/Jina.RAG →
ColBERT / late-interactionEmbed every token (not pool); MaxSim at query time. Best retrieval quality on hard cases.RAG →
GraphRAGBuild a knowledge graph + community summaries; great for "global" questions.RAG →
SFTSupervised Fine-Tuning. Train on (instruction, response) pairs. Teaches format.Fine-tune →
RLHFRL from Human Feedback: pref pairs → reward model → PPO. Classic alignment recipe.Fine-tune →
DPODirect Preference Optimization. Pref pairs without a reward model. Modern default.Fine-tune →
GRPO + RLVRGroup Relative PO + Verifiable Rewards. The recipe behind DeepSeek-R1 reasoning.Fine-tune →
LoRA / QLoRALow-rank adapters; QLoRA = LoRA on a 4-bit base. Fine-tune 70B on one GPU.Fine-tune →
AgentLLM + tools + memory in a loop. Plans, acts, observes, repeats until done.Agents →
ReActReason + Act prompt pattern: Thought → Action → Observation loop.Agents →
MCPModel Context Protocol (Anthropic 2024). Standard for LLM ↔ tools/resources.Agents →
A2AAgent2Agent (Google 2025 → LF). Standard for agent ↔ agent across orgs.Agents →
AG-UIAgent-User Interaction events. Standard for agent ↔ frontend (CopilotKit).Agents →
vLLM / SGLang / TRT-LLMOpen-source inference engines. Default production stack 2026.Infra →
Speculative decodingDraft model predicts N tokens; verifier accepts/rejects. 3–6× speedup.Infra →
Disaggregated P/DRun prefill and decode on different GPU pools; better TTFT/TPOT. Mooncake, NVIDIA Dynamo.Infra →
Reasoning modelModel trained to emit hidden CoT before the answer. GPT-5.x, Claude 4.x, DeepSeek R1.Emerging →
RoPE / YaRNRotary Position Embeddings — how transformers know token order. YaRN extends to 128K+.Long Ctx →
Mamba / SSMState-space model: linear-time, constant memory. Mamba-2, Codestral Mamba, Jamba.Long Ctx →
Diffusion / DiTIterative denoising for image/video gen. DiT = Diffusion Transformer (Sora, SD3).GenAI →
CFG (Classifier-Free Guidance)Diffusion trick: amplify text-conditioned signal at sampling time. Controls prompt adherence.GenAI →
EvalsAutomated tests for LLM outputs. RAGAS, DeepEval, LLM-as-judge, golden sets.MLOps →
OTel GenAIOpenTelemetry semantic conventions for LLM/agent spans. 2026 observability standard.MLOps →
Prompt injectionAttacker-controlled text hijacks the LLM via tool output / page content. #1 LLM CVE class.Safety →
GuardrailsLayered checks (input, model, tool, output) for safety/PII/policy. NeMo, LlamaGuard, Bedrock GR.Safety →
EU AI Act2024 EU regulation; risk-tiered. GPAI obligations phased through 2025–27.Governance →
Reasoning_effortAPI knob (low/medium/high) for how much hidden CoT to spend. Cost × quality trade.Emerging →
The 2026 production stack at a glance

💬 Missing a topic or spotted an error? This document is actively maintained — open an issue or suggest a correction directly on GitHub: github.com/jkgcg/AI-Gita/issues
Snapshot Time-sensitive vendor facts in one place: frontier model names, pricing, and benchmark slices that can drift.
Last verified: 2026-06-20. Pricing, model names, and benchmark percentages drift frequently. Validate with vendor documentation before making production decisions.
Why This Tab Exists
Durable explanations stay in their domain tabs. Fast-decaying facts are centralized here so the rest of the guide remains trustworthy over time.

BucketExamplesPrimary Source Tabs

When non-snapshot tabs mention a current commercial model, treat that mention as context only. Use this tab as the system of record for date-bound facts.

Math & Statistics This content is integrated throughout the document. The math foundations (linear algebra, probability, calculus, statistics) are embedded in the ML Fundamentals and Deep Learning tabs where each formula is explained in context. Use the dependency map on the Plan tab to navigate directly to the relevant math within each topic.
What is this tab? This tab is the math index for the full guide, pointing you to the exact places where linear algebra, probability, calculus, statistics, and information theory are used in context.
Why it matters: You do not need every derivation memorised, but you do need a reliable map for the formulas and intuitions that interviews expect you to explain clearly.
How to use it: Treat it as a routing layer: identify the math topic you need, jump to the linked deep-dive tab, then return here when planning what to study next.
Example: If you need to refresh Bayes, gradients, or KL divergence before a mock interview, use this tab to jump directly to the ML, DL, or GenAI sections that teach them in context.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

You do not need a maths degree to work in AI — but you do need to recognise the same handful of shapes that keep showing up. Most AI maths reduces to four small ideas: vectors (a list of numbers that represents something, like an image or a word), matrices (a grid of numbers used to transform vectors), probability (how sure are we?), and gradients (which way should we nudge a number to make a score better?).

Why you cannot skip it entirely: when a model behaves strangely, the explanation is usually “the loss landscape is bad” or “the cosine similarity collapsed” or “the gradient exploded”. If those phrases are pure noise to you, you cannot debug the model. You do not have to derive them on paper — but you do need to know what each one is and what it tells you about what the model is doing.

How this tab works: the actual maths lives inside the topic tabs where it is used (linear algebra inside Deep Learning, probability inside ML Fundamentals, calculus inside Deep Learning), so each formula is taught next to the algorithm that uses it — not as abstract maths divorced from why it matters. This tab is the index: it shows you where each maths idea lives and how deep you need to go for your role.

Where Math Lives in This Document
TopicWhere to Find ItWhat's Covered
Linear algebra (vectors, matrices, SVD)Matrix ops, eigendecomposition, SVD for dimensionality reduction
Probability & BayesGaussian, Bayes theorem, MLE vs MAP, distributions
Calculus & backpropChain rule, gradients, Jacobians, Hessians
Optimisation (SGD, Adam, AdamW)Gradient descent variants, learning rate schedules, momentum
Statistics (A/B testing, Bayesian inference)Hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, sequential testing
Information theory (entropy, KL divergence)Cross-entropy loss, KL divergence in VAEs, mutual information
Math Building Blocks Learning Order
Step 1: Linear Algebra
Vectors, matrices, dot products, matrix multiply, eigenvalues, SVD. Foundation for all ML. In this document: CV & NLP tab + Deep Learning tab.
Step 2: Probability & Statistics
Probability distributions (Gaussian, Bernoulli), Bayes theorem, MLE, MAP, expectation, variance. In this document: ML Fundamentals tab + Data Science tab.
Step 3: Calculus
Derivatives, chain rule, gradients, partial derivatives, Jacobian, Hessian. Critical for backprop. In this document: Deep Learning tab.
Step 4: Information Theory
Entropy, KL divergence, mutual information, cross-entropy loss. Why models are trained with cross-entropy. In this document: Generative AI tab + ML Fundamentals tab.
Step 5: Optimisation
Gradient descent, SGD, Adam, AdamW, learning rate schedules, regularisation. The engine that trains all ML models. In this document: Deep Learning tab.
Math Prerequisites by Role
How deep do you need to go in math?
AI / LLM Engineer
Probability basics (Gaussian, Bayes), cross-entropy loss, cosine similarity. No derivations needed. Focus on: what the formula means, not how to derive it.
ML Engineer
Linear algebra (matrix multiply, SVD), calculus (chain rule, gradients), probability (MLE, MAP). Derive backprop. Understand bias-variance tradeoff formally.
Data Scientist
Statistics (hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, A/B testing, Bayesian inference), regression math, decision trees. Less linear algebra than MLE.
Research / Foundation Model Engineer
Deep fluency: derive attention, RLHF policy gradient, diffusion ELBO, information theory. Expected to work from first principles.
Solution Architect
Conceptual understanding only. Know WHAT each formula means. Can't be tested on derivations but must explain trade-offs confidently.
Visual Cheatsheets — The 3 Most-Asked Math Intuitions
If you only memorise three pictures from this entire document, make it these.
① Gradient Descent — How models actually learn
The whole game:
  1. Compute loss L = how wrong the model is now
  2. Compute gradient ∇L = direction of steepest ascent per parameter
  3. Step against the gradient: θ ← θ − η·∇L
  4. Repeat for billions of steps
η (learning rate) too big → bounce around · too small → never arrive.
Adam / AdamW = SGD + per-parameter momentum + adaptive η. Default for >99% of DL today.
② Attention = Learned Weighted Average
Step 1 — Compute relevance
For each query token, dot-product with every key: scores = Q·KT / √d. High score = “this key is relevant to me”.
Step 2 — Softmax the scores
Convert scores into a probability distribution: weights = softmax(scores). Each row sums to 1 over the keys.
Step 3 — Weighted sum of values
Multiply weights by V: output = weights·V. Each query’s output is a weighted blend of value vectors.
That’s the entire transformer primitive. Multi-head attention = N copies in parallel with different WQ/WK/WV matrices, concatenated. The “learning” lives in those matrices — they decide what “relevant” means at each layer. → Full 3-token numeric walkthrough in LLM tab.
③ Softmax vs Sigmoid — Two Activations You Must Know Cold
Sigmoid — binary classification (“is this spam?”), individual gates in LSTM/GRU, multi-label problems. Outputs a single number in (0, 1). Independent per output — good when labels are not mutually exclusive.
Softmax — multiclass classification, attention weights, LLM next-token distribution. Outputs a probability distribution over N classes (sums to 1). Use when classes are mutually exclusive.
Common interview trap: “Why softmax for next-token but sigmoid for multi-label image tagging?” Because the LLM has to pick one next token (sum-to-1 → softmax); an image can have many tags simultaneously (independent per class → sigmoid per class). The math — and your answer — should fall straight out of the problem structure.
🌏 Go Deeper — Math & Stats
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner Khan Academy — Statistics & Probability — The cleanest free path from zero to conditional probability and Bayes' theorem; use it to repair any gap in the probability intuitions this tab assumes.
  • Intermediate Mathematics for Machine Learning (Deisenroth et al.) — Free PDF — The exact book that bridges math and ML in the order interviews expect; Chapters 2–6 (linear algebra, analytic geometry, matrix decompositions, probability) are the highest-ROI read.
  • Intermediate 3Blue1Brown — Essence of Linear Algebra (YouTube) — 15 short videos that build the geometric intuition behind matrix operations; watch before memorising any eigenvalue formula.
  • Advanced Tübingen Machine Learning — Probabilistic ML (YouTube) — University-level probabilistic ML course by Philipp Hennig; goes from Gaussians to Bayesian deep learning — appropriate once you are solid on the Intermediate resources above.
An LLM is a transformer trained on internet-scale text to predict the next token. Given a sequence of tokens it outputs a probability distribution over the next one; sample, append, repeat — that's all "generation" is. Everything modern (chat, tools, reasoning, agents) is engineering layered on top of that core loop.
An LLM is a wildly well-read autocomplete. It has read a sizable fraction of the public internet and learned the statistical shape of human language. When you ask it something, it doesn't “think” in our sense — it asks “given everything I've seen, what token most likely comes next?” and writes that down. The illusion of intelligence emerges from doing this very, very well, many tokens in a row, with carefully steered priors.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.
What is an LLM?
Definition: an LLM is a transformer-based next-token predictor trained on massive text corpora. In practice that means it reads a prompt, converts it into tokens, scores the next-token probabilities, samples one, appends it, and repeats until it reaches a stop condition.
flowchart TB
  A["📝 Your prompt
(text)"] --> B["🔨 Tokenizer
(BPE/SP)"]
  B --> C["📑 Token IDs
[1234, 56, 789...]"]
  C --> D["🧠 Transformer
(N layers of attention+FFN)"]
  D --> E["📊 Logits over vocab
(~100K probabilities)"]
  E --> F["🎲 Sampler
(temp, top-p, top-k)"]
  F --> G["➕ Next token"]
  G -->|"append & loop"| D
  G --> H["✅ EOS or budget hit"]
  H --> I["📜 Detokenize → text"]
One forward pass = one token. Generation is just this loop running many times.
The whole LLM stack reduces to three primitives: (1) Tokens — how text becomes numbers. (2) Attention — how each token sees every other token. (3) Next-token prediction — the only objective during pre-training. Every fancy capability (chat, RAG, tool use, reasoning, agents) is built by changing what tokens you put into the context, not by changing the model.
LLMs & Foundation Models — Foundation model content is distributed across multiple tabs by depth. Use the map below to find exactly what you need, or follow the sequential path from Generative AI → Fine-tuning → Long Context → RAG → Agents.
LLM Content Map
TopicTabCoverage
GPT/LLaMA architecture, pre-training, scaling lawsTransformer deep dive, Chinchilla scaling, BPE tokenization
RLHF, DPO, ORPO, SimPO, GRPOFull alignment pipeline with code and math
Long context, Mamba, SSMs, attention variantsRoPE, ALiBi, Mamba/SSM architecture, GQA, MLA
RAG, retrieval, vector search5 chunking strategies, HNSW, BM25, hybrid search, RAGAS
Reasoning models (GPT-5.x thinking, Claude Opus 4.8 adaptive, Gemini Deep Think, DeepSeek R1/V4)Test-time compute scaling, PRM, GRPO, RLVR, reasoning_effort knobs
Serving, quantisation, PagedAttentionvLLM, KV cache, NF4/GPTQ/AWQ, FSDP
LLM evaluation (RAGAS, LLM-as-judge)Evaluation pipelines, drift detection, CI/CD
Safety, hallucination, guardrailsOWASP LLM Top 10, Constitutional AI, DP-SGD
LLM Architecture Evolution
2017 Transformer (Attention Is All You Need)
Multi-head self-attention + feedforward + residual connections. Parallelisable (unlike RNNs). Scales with data and compute. Foundation of everything. Full implementation: Deep Learning tab.
2018-2020 BERT / GPT-2 / T5
BERT: encoder-only, bidirectional, masked language model. GPT-2: decoder-only, autoregressive, text generation. T5: encoder-decoder, unified text-to-text framework. Pre-training + fine-tuning paradigm established.
2022-2023 ChatGPT / LLaMA / Instruction Tuning
RLHF makes LLMs follow instructions. LLaMA opens research. Scale laws (Chinchilla) optimize data/compute ratio. Foundation for today's LLMs. Full pipeline: GenAI tab + Fine-tuning tab.
2024 GPT-4o / Claude 3.x / Gemini 1.5 / Llama 3.x / DeepSeek-V2
Multimodal natively, 128K+ context, MoE goes mainstream, RoPE/GQA/MLA. Foundation for the modern stack.
2024-Q3 2025 Reasoning models: o1, o3, DeepSeek-R1, Claude 3.7 / 4.x extended thinking
Inference-time compute becomes a scaling axis. RL on chains-of-thought (GRPO + RLVR). Hidden reasoning tokens, billed. Open recipe (R1) closes gap to closed labs. Llama 4 ships MoE.
202526 GPT-5.x, Claude Opus 4.8 / Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.x, DeepSeek V4, Llama 4 Maverick
Reasoning is table stakes (`effort` / `reasoning_effort` knobs). 1M context is the new baseline; 10M claimed (Llama 4). Adaptive thinking (Opus 4.8). Computer-use agents GA (Claude, Gemini Antigravity, ChatGPT Agent). Multimodal embeddings (Gemini Embedding 2). Protocol stack lands: MCP (tools), A2A (agent-to-agent), AG-UI (agent-to-frontend). Long context: Long Context tab. Reasoning + protocols: Emerging Trends tab.
LLM Knowledge Depth by Role
How deep do you need to go on LLMs?
AI / LLM Engineer
Deep: full RAG pipeline, fine-tuning (LoRA/DPO/ORPO), agent patterns, prompt engineering, evaluation (RAGAS). Must implement from scratch in interviews.
Solution Architect
Breadth: know all LLM patterns (RAG, agents, fine-tuning), when to use each, cost/latency trade-offs, build vs buy decisions. No code required.
ML Engineer
Training and fine-tuning depth: RLHF, LoRA math, FSDP, quantization. Less focus on RAG/agents (those are AI Engineer territory).
Data Scientist
Usage-level: prompt engineering, evaluation, basic RAG. Not expected to implement LLM training or fine-tuning pipelines.
Attention Math — A Concrete 3-Token Walkthrough
Worked example for the prompt “cat sat on” — 3 tokens, model dim d=4, 1 head. Real Llama 4 uses d=8192 with 64 heads, but the math is identical — just bigger matrices.
#OperationMathOutput shape
1Embed token idsX = embedding[token_ids]X: [3, 4] — one row per token
2Project to Q, K, VQ = X·WQ   K = X·WK   V = X·WVQ, K, V: [3, 4]
3Relevance scoresS = Q·KT / √dS: [3, 3] — score per (query, key) pair
4Causal mask (decoder)S[i, j>i] = −∞Upper triangle masked — can’t peek at future
5Softmax per rowA = softmax(S, axis = −1)A: [3, 3], each row sums to 1
6Mix valuesO = A·VO: [3, 4] — context-aware token reps
The intuition: attention is a learned weighted average. Each token asks “who should I look at?” (Q·K = relevance), softmax turns that into a probability distribution (the A matrix), and the output is the weighted sum of V vectors. Multi-head = run this N times in parallel with different W matrices and concat. Everything else in a transformer (FFN, LayerNorm, residual) is plumbing around this primitive. → Full backprop derivation in Deep Learning tab.
Sampling Parameters Cheatsheet
How you sample from the model’s next-token distribution. Same parameter names across OpenAI, Anthropic, Google — the meaning is identical. Reasoning models (GPT-5 Thinking, o-series, Claude extended-thinking, DeepSeek-R2) override most of these internally; set reasoning_effort instead.
ParamWhat it doesRangeDefaultWhen to change
temperatureDivides logits before softmax. 0 = greedy/deterministic. 1 = unchanged. >1 = flatter, more random.0.0 – 2.00.70–0.3 for code, math, JSON. 0.9–1.2 for brainstorming or creative writing.
top_kKeep only the k highest-probability tokens, renormalise. Hard cutoff.1 – 50050 (or off)Modern APIs mostly disable it in favour of top_p.
top_p (nucleus)Keep smallest set whose cumulative prob ≥ p. Adaptive cutoff.0.0 – 1.00.9 – 0.950.7 for factual; 0.95+ for creative.
frequency_penaltySubtracts α × token_count from each logit. Reduces verbatim repetition.−2.0 – 2.000.3–0.7 if long outputs loop on the same phrasing.
presence_penaltySubtracts β if a token has appeared at all. Encourages new topics.−2.0 – 2.000.3–0.6 for diverse brainstorming.
repetition_penaltyDivides logit by γ for recently-seen tokens (HuggingFace / llama.cpp style, multiplicative).1.0 – 1.51.0 – 1.11.1 for code; 1.2+ if exact-phrase loops appear.
max_tokensHard cap on generated tokens.1 – context_lenvariesAlways set explicitly — protects cost & latency from runaway generations.
seedPins RNG for reproducible sampling.any intnoneSet in eval pipelines. Not guaranteed across model versions.
stopList of strings; generation halts when any appears.up to 4noneEnforce structure: ["</tool>", "\n\nUser:"].
logit_biasPer-token additive bias on logits. Force or forbid specific tokens.−100 – 100{}Force JSON braces, ban profanity, enforce a fixed vocab.
Default playbooks: · Code / SQL / function-calling: T=0, top_p=1, no penalties — determinism beats creativity. · RAG answering: T=0.2–0.4, top_p=0.9 — grounded but not robotic. · Creative writing / marketing: T=0.8–1.0, top_p=0.95, frequency_penalty=0.5. · Reasoning models: ignore temperature — set reasoning_effort (low / medium / high) instead.
2026 Frontier Model Lineup — Context, Pricing, Sweet Spot
Pricing in $/M tokens, mid-2026 list prices. Output is typically 2–5× input. Reasoning tokens are billed as output for thinking models. Numbers move quarterly — verify before quoting in interviews.
ModelVendorContext$/M in$/M outSweet spotGotchas
GPT-5.x Pro / ThinkingOpenAI1M$12$60Reasoning, complex coding, multi-step agentsHidden reasoning tokens billed as output — cost surprise
GPT-5 (standard)OpenAI400K$2.50$10General-purpose default; balanced cost/qualityKnowledge cutoff drift if not RAG-augmented
Claude Opus 4.8Anthropic500K$15$75Long-context, careful reasoning, agentic codingAdaptive thinking blows budgets — cap with max_thinking_tokens
Claude Sonnet 4.6Anthropic1M$3$15Everyday workhorse — Opus quality at 5× lower cost—
Claude Haiku 4.6Anthropic200K$0.80$4High-volume routing / classification / cheap RAGWeaker at multi-step reasoning
Gemini 3 ProGoogle2M$2.50$10Massive context (entire repos), multimodal videoFunction-calling schema differs slightly from OpenAI
Gemini 3 FlashGoogle1M$0.30$1.20Cheapest 1M-context option; high-throughput batchQuality dips on hard reasoning
DeepSeek V4DeepSeek256K$0.40$1.60Best $/quality ratio; open weights; coding-strongAPI in CN/SG — some enterprises ban PRC-hosted endpoints
DeepSeek R2DeepSeek128K$0.80$3.20Open-weight reasoning competitive with o3 / OpusReasoning tokens billable — self-host to cap cost
Llama 4 Maverick (405B MoE)Meta (open)10Mself-hostself-hostLongest context on the market; open weights for fine-tuneNeeds 8×H100 / 4×B200 minimum; long-context recall still hard
Llama 4 70BMeta (open)1Mself-hostself-hostSweet spot for on-prem; QLoRA fits 1×80GB GPU—
Qwen3 235BAlibaba (open)256K$0.50$2Multilingual (esp. CJK), code-strong, open weightsEnterprise hesitance to deploy CN-origin in Western markets
Mistral Large 3Mistral256K$3$9EU-hosted, GDPR-friendly default for European enterprisesSmaller ecosystem than OpenAI / Anthropic
How to pick in 90 seconds: · Need open weights / self-host? Llama 4 70B (general) · DeepSeek V4 (coding) · Qwen3 (multilingual). · Need 1M+ context? Gemini 3 Pro (2M, cheapest) · Llama 4 Maverick (10M, self-host) · Claude Sonnet 4.6 (1M, quality). · Need reasoning? GPT-5 Thinking · Claude Opus 4.8 · DeepSeek R2 (open). · High-volume / cost-sensitive? Gemini 3 Flash · DeepSeek V4 · Haiku 4.6. · Tightest regulatory boundary (EU)? Mistral Large 3 · self-hosted Llama 4.
🌍 Real-world use case: A customer-support assistant built on a hosted foundation model. The team picks a mid-size instruction-tuned model, sets the system prompt and temperature, and caps context to the last few turns — shipping useful answers in days without training anything, then later swaps to a cheaper open-weights model once volume makes the API bill the bigger cost.
🌏 Go Deeper — LLMs & Foundation Models
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner Andrej Karpathy — Intro to Large Language Models (YouTube, 1hr) — The clearest non-technical explanation of what an LLM is, what it isn't, and where it fails — worth watching before your first interview.
  • Intermediate Jay Alammar — The Illustrated Transformer — The definitive visual walkthrough of the attention mechanism; bookmark this alongside the math in this tab — the two together are enough to answer 90% of Transformer architecture questions.
  • Intermediate Karpathy — nanoGPT (GitHub) — ~300 lines of clean PyTorch that implement a working GPT; reading the code end-to-end is the fastest way to move from knowing the architecture to understanding training dynamics.
  • Advanced Attention Is All You Need — Vaswani et al. (2017) — The original Transformer paper; read the abstract and Section 3 (Model Architecture) — every question about multi-head attention in interviews traces back to this.
The AI hiring market in mid-2026 has bifurcated sharply: companies building foundation models (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, xAI, Meta MSL, DeepSeek, Alibaba Qwen, Mistral) and the vastly larger world building on top of them. The skills, interview bar, and compensation differ significantly. Know which world you're interviewing for.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

The AI job market in 2026 has split into two very different worlds. A small group of companies builds the big AI models — think OpenAI, Anthropic, Google. They hire PhDs who can train a model from scratch and write research papers. The much larger group of companies uses those models to add features to their own products. They hire engineers who can plug a model in, give it the right context, measure if it works, and ship it.

Why this split matters for you: the interview questions, the daily work, and the pay bands are very different in each world. If you walk into a “use the model” interview expecting heavy math and CUDA code, you will under-prepare for the real questions (system design, evaluation, cost trade-offs). If you walk into a “build the model” interview hoping to talk about prompt engineering, you will under-prepare too.

What this tab covers: a clear table of who does what at each type of company, the tools and platforms each role uses today, and rough pay ranges — so you can target the right type of company for your background and goals.

Role Taxonomy: What Each Role Actually Does
RoleDay-to-day workInterview focusMust-know stack (2025)Salary range IC4-IC6
FOUNDATION MODEL COMPANIES (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, Mistral, DeepSeek)
ML Research EngineerDesign & train novel architectures. Pre-training, RLHF, scaling experiments. Ablation studies. Research EngineerImplement research ideas at scale. Bridge research → production. Optimize training pipelines.ML coding (implement from scratch), GPU optimization, systems thinking, experiment reproducibilityPyTorch, FSDP, Flash Attention 2/3, vLLM, Triton, profilers$250k$500k
ENTERPRISE / APPLICATION (the majority of AI jobs)
ML EngineerTrain, evaluate, deploy, monitor ML models for product features. Owns model lifecycle end-to-end.ML coding (implement algorithm), system design, ML metrics, feature engineering, A/B testingPyTorch, scikit-learn, XGBoost, MLflow, SageMaker, FastAPI, Docker, K8s$180k$350k
AI / LLM EngineerBuild LLM-powered features: RAG systems, agents, chatbots, document processing. Fine-tuning pipelines.RAG design, agent patterns, evaluation methodology, prompt engineering, system designOpenAI API, LangChain, LlamaIndex, Pinecone/Weaviate/Qdrant, LangSmith, Instructor$170k$320k
Data ScientistStatistical analysis, A/B testing, business insights, ML model building. More analytics than engineering.Stats, SQL, case studies, A/B test design, ML basics, business sense, causal inferencePython, pandas, scikit-learn, SQL, Tableau, dbt, statsmodels, CausalML$140k$260k
MLOps EngineerBuild ML platform: feature stores, model registries, pipelines, monitoring, CI/CD for ML.System design, K8s, CI/CD, MLflow/Kubeflow, reliability, ML-specific ops challengesKubeflow, MLflow, Airflow, K8s, Terraform, Feast, Grafana, Evidently$160k$300k
AI Solution ArchitectDesign AI system architecture for clients. Choose build vs buy, select models, cost optimization, stakeholder management.Architecture scenarios, trade-off analysis, cost modeling, vendor comparison, breadth across AI landscapeAll major cloud AI services, LangChain, OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock, GCP Vertex$200k$400k
2026 Tech Stack Map
Model APIs & Open Weights
  • Closed APIs (2026): GPT-5.5 / 5.4 / 5.4-mini / 5.3-instant (OpenAI), Claude Opus 4.8 / Sonnet 4.6 / Haiku 4.5 (Anthropic), Gemini 3.5 Flash / 3.1 Pro / 3.1 Deep Think / 3.1 Flash-Lite (Google), Grok 4 / 4 Heavy (xAI), Command A / A Reasoning (Cohere)
  • Open-weight frontier (2026): Llama 4 Maverick / Scout (10M ctx, MoE, Meta), DeepSeek V4 Flash/Pro + R1 (MoE, MIT/permissive), Qwen3 family (0.6B480B + Qwen3-Max trillion-param, Alibaba), Mistral Large 2.1 / Magistral / Medium 3, Gemma 3 / 3n (on-device, Google), Phi-4 / Phi-4-Reasoning (MS), Kimi K2 (Moonshot, 1T MoE), GLM-4.5/4.6 (Zhipu), gpt-oss-120b / 20b (OpenAI's first open reasoning models, Apache 2.0)
  • Specialized: Whisper (speech-to-text), CLIP/SigLIP (vision-language), Flux/SDXL (image gen), Codestral/DeepSeek-Coder (code)
  • Embedding: text-embedding-3-large (OpenAI), voyage-3 (Anthropic), BGE-M3 (BAAI, open), E5-large-v2 (Microsoft)
Training & Fine-tuning Stack
  • Training: PyTorch (primary), JAX/Flax (research/Google TPU), HuggingFace Trainer
  • Distributed: FSDP (PyTorch native), DeepSpeed ZeRO-2/3, Megatron-LM, Accelerate
  • Fine-tuning: PEFT/LoRA (HuggingFace), TRL (RLHF/DPO), unsloth (2x faster), Axolotl
  • Experiment tracking: Weights & Biases (dominant), MLflow, CometML
  • Evaluation: LM Evaluation Harness (EleutherAI), RAGAS, MTEB, OpenCompass
Serving & Inference
  • LLM servers: vLLM (most popular production), TGI (HuggingFace), TensorRT-LLM (NVIDIA), SGLang, Ollama (local)
  • Quantization: bitsandbytes (4/8-bit), AWQ, GPTQ, GGUF (llama.cpp for CPU)
  • Serving infra: BentoML, Triton Inference Server, Ray Serve, KServe
  • APIs & routing: FastAPI, LiteLLM (multi-provider proxy), OpenRouter
RAG & Orchestration
  • Orchestration: LangChain (most used), LlamaIndex (data-focused), Haystack, CrewAI (multi-agent)
  • Vector DBs: Pinecone (managed), Weaviate, Qdrant (Rust, fast), Chroma (local dev), pgvector (Postgres), Milvus (scale)
  • Reranking: Cohere Rerank API, BGE Reranker, ColBERT v2, FlashRank
  • Doc parsing: Unstructured.io, LlamaParse (PDFs), Docling (IBM, structured)
MLOps Platform
  • Tracking: MLflow, W&B, DVC (data versioning)
  • Feature stores: Feast (open), Tecton (managed), Vertex AI Feature Store
  • Orchestration: Airflow, Prefect, Metaflow, ZenML, Flyte
  • Monitoring: Evidently AI (open), WhyLogs, Arize, Grafana+Prometheus
  • LLM observability: LangSmith, Langfuse, Helicone, OpenLLMetry (OTel)
Cloud AI Services
  • AWS: SageMaker (full MLOps), Bedrock (managed LLMs: Claude, Llama, Titan), Comprehend, Rekognition
  • Azure: Azure OpenAI Service (GPT-4, embeddings), Azure ML, AI Foundry, Cognitive Services
  • GCP: Vertex AI (full MLOps + Gemini), Gemini API, Document AI
  • GPU clouds: Modal (serverless GPU), RunPod, Lambda Labs, Together AI, Replicate
What Interviewers Actually Test by Company Type
Company TypePrimary TestSecondaryRed flags
FAANG / Foundation ModelImplement attention/backprop from scratch. Derive gradient of softmax+CE. Explain Flash Attention. Distributed training strategies.Systems optimization, research taste, GPU profiling, scaling experimentsCan only call APIs. Never implemented anything from scratch. No systems thinking.
AI-first StartupLLM system design. RAG pipeline design + evaluation. Agent failure modes. Shipping speed.Cost optimization, fine-tuning, evaluation methodologyOver-engineers everything. Never shipped to production. Doesn't know cost per token.
Enterprise Tech (IBM, SAP, Salesforce)LLM integration. Enterprise security & privacy. Scalable ML pipelines. Agentic platform design.Explainability, compliance (GDPR, EU AI Act), legacy system integration, OPA/guardrailsOnly knows consumer AI. No enterprise ops experience. Ignores data residency.
Finance / HealthcareModel explainability (SHAP). Bias/fairness evaluation. Regulatory compliance (SR 11-7, HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR). Uncertainty quantification.Statistical rigor, audit trails, model governance, backtestingIgnores compliance. Can't quantify uncertainty. No experience with model validation.
Open-weight vs Closed Models: Enterprise Decision Framework
When to Use Closed Models (GPT-5.x, Claude 4.x, Gemini 3.x)
  • Best quality needed now with minimal engineering: product demos, customer-facing features
  • Multimodal (image / audio / video / PDF): Gemini 3.5 Flash or Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6. For embeddings of mixed media in one space: Gemini Embedding 2.
  • Volume is low (cost acceptable): <100K tokens/day ≈ cents/day on GPT-5.4 mini ($0.75/$4.50 per MTok), Haiku 4.5 ($1/$5), or Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite; DeepSeek V4 Flash ($0.14/$0.28) is the new price floor.
  • Speed to market > control: no GPU infra team, need day-1 deployment
  • Safety-critical with legal exposure: Claude's Constitutional AI alignment is most battle-tested
When to Use Open-Weight Models (Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek)
  • Data privacy: PHI, financial data, legal docs must never leave your network
  • High volume: >1M tokens/day — self-hosted Llama 4 Maverick (~$0.190.49/MTok blended on distributed inference), DeepSeek V4, or Qwen3-235B breaks even vs closed APIs in weeks. gpt-oss-120b is OpenAI's own open-weight reasoning option (Apache 2.0).
  • Fine-tuning on proprietary data: train on your own data for 10-30% quality improvement on domain
  • Compliance: GDPR data residency, HIPAA, FedRAMP can't send data to external API
  • Customization: specific output format, persona, domain behavior that prompting alone can't achieve
Interview Signal Map by Role
Role LevelWhat They Test (Specifically)Green Flag AnswerRed Flag Answer
ML Engineer L4/IC4Implement attention from scratch. Derive cross-entropy gradient. Debug training instability.Writes clean PyTorch, explains sqrt(d_k) scaling intuitively, connects vanishing gradient to residual connections."I just use the transformer library." Can't explain why LayerNorm is used in transformers vs BatchNorm.
AI/LLM Engineer L4Design a RAG system. Explain chunking tradeoffs. Debug agent loop.Mentions hybrid retrieval immediately, discusses chunking strategies by name, identifies prompt injection as a real risk.Says "just use LangChain" without understanding what's happening underneath. Never deployed to production.
Senior ML Engineer L5Design distributed training for 70B model. Explain FSDP memory math. Benchmark a slow training run.States FSDP memory formula correctly. Knows when to use tensor vs pipeline parallelism. Has profiled real training runs.Confuses DDP and FSDP. Never trained anything larger than a fine-tune on one GPU.
Staff AI Engineer L6Design a production AI platform. Tradeoff RAG vs fine-tuning vs prompting. Model risk management framework.Immediately asks clarifying questions. Proposes layered architecture with governance from day 1. Knows cost math cold.Proposes RAG for everything. Never designed for multi-tenancy. Ignores safety and compliance as "later concerns."
Principal / AI ArchitectArchitect an agentic platform for enterprise. OKR-level tradeoffs. Build vs buy decisions.Proposes AKP-style architecture. Identifies build vs buy for each component. Has delivered platform used by 10+ teams.Designs technically impressive system with no adoption plan. Ignores legacy integration complexity.
2025 Market Signals: What Employers Actually Want
Most In-Demand Skills (2025)
  • #1 RAG system design and implementation: Every company building on LLMs needs this. Hybrid retrieval, reranking, evaluation (RAGAS) know this cold.
  • #2 LLM evaluation methodology: Companies are burnt by products that don't work. Candidates who can design eval frameworks and run golden-set tests are premium.
  • #3 Agent architecture: Moving from "I used LangChain" to "I designed a multi-agent system with proper state management, HITL, and evaluation." LangGraph is the 2025 differentiator.
  • #4 LLMOps: Prompt versioning, cost management, drift detection, monitoring. Most companies have built the thing; very few have built the operations around it.
  • #5 Fine-tuning (LoRA/QLoRA/DPO): Not everyone needs this but it's a differentiator. The engineer who can fine-tune a model for a specific domain and evaluate it properly is rare.
Tech Stack You Must Be Fluent In
Non-negotiable (2025)Python (advanced: async, type hints, dataclasses). PyTorch (model building, training loop). HuggingFace Transformers + PEFT. OpenAI/Anthropic APIs. LangChain or LlamaIndex. One vector DB (Qdrant, Pinecone, or Weaviate).
Strong differentiatorsLangGraph (stateful agents). vLLM (production serving). RAGAS (RAG evaluation). W&B or MLflow (experiment tracking). FSDP (distributed training). Triton (custom CUDA kernels for researchers).
Architecture-levelAWS Bedrock or Azure OpenAI (managed LLMs). LiteLLM (model gateway). OpenTelemetry for AI (gen_ai.* conventions). Kubernetes (GPU workload scheduling). Terraform (IaC for AI infra).
AI Capability Map What Problems Each Technology Solves
TechnologyProblem It SolvesInputOutputReal Example
LLM (GPT-4, Claude)Understanding + generating text at human levelText promptText responseCustomer service bot, code assistant
Embedding ModelMeasuring semantic similarity between textsTextVector (float array)RAG retrieval, document deduplication
Image model (DALL-E, SD)Generating or understanding imagesText/ImageImageProduct image generation, medical imaging
Classification modelAssigning a label to an inputText/Image/TabularLabel + probabilityFraud detection, sentiment analysis
Recommendation modelPersonalising content rankingUser + item featuresRanked listNetflix recommendations, search ranking
Agent (LLM + tools)Multi-step reasoning and actionGoal/instructionActions + answerResearch assistant, workflow automation
Decision: Which AI Approach For Which Problem?
Which AI approach should you use?
Need to understand/generate text
Use an LLM (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini). Start with API, fine-tune only if needed.
Need to search a large document corpus
Build RAG pipeline. Embeddings + vector DB + LLM for synthesis.
Need to automate a multi-step workflow
Build an AI agent with tools. Use LangGraph for stateful workflows.
Need to classify structured data
Start with XGBoost/LightGBM. Use deep learning only if tabular models fall short.
Need to predict a number (regression)
Linear regression gradient boosting neural network (in that order of complexity).
Build vs Prompt vs Fine-tune vs Train
When to Prompt
  • Task is general, LLM has strong priors
  • Low-medium volume (<10M tokens/day)
  • Speed to market is priority
When to Fine-tune
  • Domain-specific tone or format needed
  • High volume + cost reduction needed
  • 100+ quality training examples available
🌍 Real-world use case: Choosing the model for a new feature. An engineer maps the requirement — long legal documents, strict data residency, a tight latency budget — onto the landscape, rules out closed APIs for the residency constraint, shortlists two open-weights long-context models, and benchmarks both on the team's own eval set before committing.
🌏 Go Deeper — AI Landscape
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner State of AI Report (annual) — The most cited annual landscape snapshot; the Research and Industry sections give you the vocabulary for discussing where the field is heading in executive conversations.
  • Intermediate Stanford HAI — AI Index Report (annual) — Data-heavy landscape overview with benchmark trends, investment charts, and responsible AI metrics — use the summary pages if you need numbers for a presentation or interview context question.
  • Intermediate LM Arena (formerly LMSYS Chatbot Arena) — Live Leaderboard — The community-voted model ranking, updated continuously; shows relative capability levels across all frontier models in a format that maps directly to the model-choice questions in system design interviews.
ML fundamentals are tested in every AI interview regardless of role. FAANG tests them at deep implementation level. Startups test them to verify you understand WHY things work. This tab covers every algorithm with concept, math, code, and when-to-use guidance.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

Classical machine learning is the part of AI that learns patterns from numbers in a table — like predicting house prices from square footage, or flagging an email as spam from word counts. It existed long before chatbots. The algorithms here are smaller, faster, cheaper to run, and very often more accurate for table-shaped problems than today's giant AI models.

Why you must still know this: half the AI problems at most companies are still tabular — fraud scores, customer churn, demand forecasts, search ranking. Classical ML wins on these. Interview rooms also love it because the math is small enough to derive on a whiteboard, so it tests whether you actually understand what “training a model” really means under the buzzwords.

What this tab covers: every key algorithm in plain words — linear regression (a line through dots), logistic regression (a yes/no decision), decision trees (a chain of if-checks), random forests and gradient boosting (many small trees voting together), and SVMs (drawing the widest gap between two classes). For each one: the plain idea, the simple math, working code, and when to reach for it.

Linear Models Concept, Math & Code
Linear & Logistic Regression
Find the line (or hyperplane) that minimises the squared gap between predictions and true values.
Loss = (y - ) where = X Closed-form solution: = (XX)⻹ Xy works when features < 10 000 (needs matrix inversion) Gradient descent update (large data): (1/m) X(X y) Add L2 regularisation (Ridge): Loss += shrinks all weights toward 0 Add L1 regularisation (Lasso): Loss += || forces some weights to exactly 0 (feature selection)
ytrue label (what actually happened)
= Xprediction (dot product of features and weights)
weights we are learning one per feature
(eta)learning rate how big a step to take each update
mnumber of training examples in the batch
(lambda)regularisation strength higher = simpler model
Key insight: The closed form gives the exact answer in one shot but costs O(n) due to matrix inversion. Gradient descent is slower per step but scales to millions of features.
Remember it as: Squared error penalises big mistakes heavily. Halving it () makes the gradient clean the 2 from the power cancels the .
Python Logistic regression from scratch
import numpy as np def sigmoid(z): return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-np.clip(z, -500, 500))) def train_logistic(X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=1000, l2=0.01): "Binary logistic regression with L2 regularization." m, n = X.shape W = np.zeros(n); b = 0.0 losses = [] for _ in range(epochs): z = X @ W + b p = sigmoid(z) dz = (p - y) / m # gradient of CE+sigmoid dW = X.T @ dz + l2 * W # add L2 gradient db = dz.sum() W -= lr * dW; b -= lr * db loss = -(y * np.log(p + 1e-12) + (1-y) * np.log(1-p + 1e-12)).mean() losses.append(loss) return W, b, losses def predict(X, W, b, threshold=0.5): return (sigmoid(X @ W + b) >= threshold).astype(int)
Regularization Deep Dive
  • L2 (Ridge): Adds to loss. Gradient penalty pushes all weights toward zero proportionally. No exact zero weights. Good when all features likely relevant. Equivalent to MAP with Gaussian prior on weights. Closed-form solution exists.
  • L1 (Lasso): Adds to loss. Subgradient at zero creates "corner" optimizer naturally sets irrelevant weights exactly to 0. Feature selection built-in. No closed form (requires coordinate descent or subgradient methods).
  • Why L1 creates sparsity: The L1 ball has corners at the axes. Constrained optimization problem: minimize loss subject to t. The optimal solution tends to hit a corner of the L1 ball (where some ᵢ = 0).
  • ElasticNet: Combines L1 + L2. L1 for sparsity, L2 for stability when features are correlated. controls mix. Use when: many features, some correlation between features.
  • How to choose : Cross-validation on a logarithmic grid: {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}. LassoCV, RidgeCV in sklearn do this automatically with efficient path algorithms.
Tree-Based Models XGBoost Deep Dive
Decision Tree & Random Forest
Split the data at each node by asking: which question reduces impurity (mixed classes) the most?
Gini impurity (faster to compute): G = 1 pᵢ 0 when one class dominates, 0.5 when perfectly mixed (binary) Information gain (more sensitive to purity): H = pᵢ log pᵢ [entropy] Best split = maximise gain: Gain = G(parent) [wLG(left) + wRG(right)] wL = n_left / n_total (weight by sample count)
pᵢfraction of samples in this node that belong to class i
GGini impurity 0 means pure node, 0.5 means worst possible mix (binary)
Hentropy same idea, different formula. Higher = more mixed
Gainhow much purity improves by making this split pick the split with highest Gain
wL, wRweighted by how many samples go to each child big child matters more
Key insight: Gini and entropy give almost identical trees in practice. Gini is faster (no log). The real power of trees is NOT the formula it's the greedy split search that makes them fast and interpretable.
Remember it as: Think of Gini as: 'if I randomly picked two samples from this node, what is the probability they belong to different classes?' A pure node 0. A 50/50 mix 0.5.
  • Pruning: max_depth (most impactful), min_samples_leaf (minimum samples per leaf), min_impurity_decrease. Start: max_depth=5, tune upward.
  • Feature importance: Impurity-based = biased toward high-cardinality features. Permutation importance = unbiased, use on test set. SHAP = most reliable, individual-level explanations.
  • Random forest vs XGBoost: RF: parallel (all trees independent), robust to hyperparameters, no overfitting with more trees. XGBoost: sequential, higher accuracy with tuning, more hyperparameters, can overfit.
Gradient Boosting (XGBoost / LightGBM)
Build a sequence of small decision trees where each new tree corrects the mistakes of all previous trees.
Step 0: F(x) = mean(y) start with a flat prediction Step t: compute residuals rᵢ = L/F(xᵢ) where are we wrong? fit a new tree h to those residuals update: F(x) = F(x) + h(x) add a small correction XGBoost adds regularisation to the tree structure: Objective = L(yᵢ, ᵢ) + T + wⱼ Uses 2nd-order Taylor expansion closed-form optimal leaf weights LightGBM difference: Leaf-wise growth (deepens one leaf at a time) vs level-wise (XGBoost) faster, but can overfit on small data use max_depth to control
F(x)initial prediction the overall mean. Boring but correct starting point
rᵢresidual = how wrong we are for sample i. The gradient of the loss tells us the direction to correct
h(x)the new small tree it only learns to fix what previous trees got wrong
(eta)learning rate how much of the new tree to blend in. Smaller = more trees needed but better generalisation
Tpenalty for number of leaves T fewer leaves = simpler tree
wⱼL2 penalty on leaf weights wⱼ prevents extreme values
Key insight: Each tree sees the gradient of the loss the direction that reduces error most. This is 'gradient descent in function space': instead of updating weights, you're adding whole functions.
Remember it as: Boosting = teamwork of weak learners. Each tree is a specialist that fixes the specific mistakes the team made so far.
XGBoost training with CV and early stopping
import xgboost as xgb from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold import numpy as np def train_xgb(X_train, y_train, X_val, y_val): params = { "objective": "binary:logistic", "eval_metric": "aucpr", # PR-AUC for imbalanced "learning_rate": 0.05, "max_depth": 6, "subsample": 0.8, # row subsampling "colsample_bytree": 0.8, # feature subsampling "reg_lambda": 1.0, # L2 regularization "reg_alpha": 0.1, # L1 regularization "scale_pos_weight": 10, # for class imbalance "tree_method": "hist", # fast histogram method "device": "cuda", # GPU if available } dtrain = xgb.DMatrix(X_train, label=y_train) dval = xgb.DMatrix(X_val, label=y_val) model = xgb.train( params, dtrain, num_boost_round=1000, evals=[(dval, "val")], early_stopping_rounds=50, # stop if no improvement for 50 rounds verbose_eval=100 ) return model
Bias-Variance, Evaluation & Model Selection
Bias-Variance Tradeoff
Every model's error comes from three sources: how wrong it is on average (bias), how much it changes between datasets (variance), and random noise you can never remove.
Expected Test Error = Bias + Variance + Irreducible Noise Bias = (average prediction true value) systematic error from wrong assumptions High bias = model too simple (underfitting) Variance = how much predictions scatter across different training sets High variance = model too complex (overfitting) Irreducible noise = randomness in the data itself no model can fix this
Biassquared average error across many training sets. A high-bias model makes the same wrong guess every time
Variancehow much the model's prediction changes when you retrain on different data. A high-variance model memorises the training set
Irreducible noiserandomness in the real world measurement errors, random events. The floor you cannot beat
Key insight: The tradeoff: making a model more complex reduces bias (fits the data better) but increases variance (memorises noise). The sweet spot is in the middle. More training data helps variance but not bias.
Remember it as: Bias = model always misses in the same direction (systematic). Variance = model shoots all over the place depending on training data (random).
  • Learning curves: Plot train & val error vs training set size. High variance: large gap, decreases with more data. High bias: both high, gap stays small. Most important debugging tool.
  • Data leakage: The #1 real-world ML bug. Future data in training (time-series), target leakage (feature derived from label), test set contamination, normalizer fitted on full dataset before split.
Evaluation Metrics Deep Dive
A 22 table that counts every type of right and wrong prediction the foundation for all classification metrics.
Predicted + Predicted Actual + | TP FN | Actual | FP TN | Precision = TP / (TP + FP) 'Of all I called positive, how many were right?' Recall = TP / (TP + FN) 'Of all actual positives, how many did I find?' F1 = 2PR / (P+R) harmonic mean penalises large gaps between P and R AUC-ROC = P(model scores a random positive higher than a random negative) PR-AUC = area under Precision-Recall curve use for imbalanced data
TP (True Positive)model said positive, and it was correct alarm
FP (False Positive)model said positive, but it was negative false alarm
FN (False Negative)model said negative, but it was positive missed it
TN (True Negative)model said negative, and it was correct silence
Precisiontrustworthiness of positive predictions. Optimise when false alarms are costly (spam filter)
Recallcompleteness of positive detection. Optimise when missing positives is costly (cancer screening)
F1single metric that balances both. Use when neither precision nor recall should dominate
Key insight: Accuracy (correct / total) is misleading for imbalanced classes. A model that always predicts 'no cancer' gets 99% accuracy if 1% have cancer but catches zero cases. Always use Precision, Recall, F1 or PR-AUC for imbalanced data.
Remember it as: Precision = 'when I raise the alarm, am I right?' Recall = 'do I catch all the real alarms?' You can't maximise both tightening one loosens the other.
  • When to use which metric: Balanced classes accuracy OK. Imbalanced F1 or PR-AUC. Risk scoring (medical, fraud) calibration + AUC-ROC. Ranking tasks NDCG.
  • Statistical tests for model comparison: McNemar's test for paired classifiers. DeLong test for AUC comparison. Never compare single held-out set without confidence intervals.
Cross-Validation Strategies
  • k-Fold: Standard. k=5 or 10. Average over k folds. Always stratified for classification.
  • StratifiedKFold: Preserves class ratio in each fold. Mandatory for imbalanced data. Plain k-fold on imbalanced = some folds may have no positive class.
  • TimeSeriesSplit: Train on past, validate on future ONLY. Walk-forward validation. Standard k-fold on time series = data leakage (future data in training).
  • GroupKFold: When rows have groups (same user, same patient). Keep all rows of one group in same fold. Required for recommendation, medical, or multi-row-per-entity data.
  • Nested CV: Outer loop = model evaluation. Inner loop = hyperparameter tuning. Avoids optimistic bias from tuning and testing on same data.
  • LOOCV: Leave-one-out. Very high variance. Use only for very small datasets (<50 samples).
Stratified k-fold with multiple metrics
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold, cross_validate from sklearn.metrics import make_scorer, f1_score, roc_auc_score import numpy as np scoring = { 'f1': make_scorer(f1_score), 'roc_auc': make_scorer(roc_auc_score, needs_proba=True), } cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42) results = cross_validate(model, X, y, cv=cv, scoring=scoring, return_train_score=True) for metric, scores in results.items(): if metric.startswith('test_'): print(f"{metric}: {np.mean(scores):.4f} {np.std(scores):.4f}")
Unsupervised Learning
K-Means & DBSCAN
Group data points so that points in the same cluster are as close as possible to their cluster's centre.
Objective: minimise WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares) WCSS = ᵢ Cᵢ ||x ᵢ|| Algorithm: 1. Initialise k centroids (K-Means++ places them far apart better start) 2. Assign each point to the nearest centroid 3. Recompute each centroid = mean of its assigned points 4. Repeat steps 2-3 until assignments stop changing Complexity: O(n k d iterations) Choose k with: elbow method (plot WCSS vs k) or silhouette score
knumber of clusters you must decide this in advance
Cᵢthe set of all points assigned to cluster i
ᵢthe centroid of cluster i the mean of all its points
||x ᵢ||squared Euclidean distance from point x to its centroid
WCSStotal compactness across all clusters smaller is tighter, but too small means overfitting
Key insight: K-Means is sensitive to initialisation and assumes spherical clusters. K-Means++ solves the initialisation problem by placing centroids far apart. DBSCAN is better when clusters are irregular shapes or you don't know k.
Remember it as: Think of it as repeatedly asking two questions: 'which centre am I closest to?' and 'given my current members, where should I move?' Repeat until nobody changes sides.
  • K choice: Elbow (plot inertia vs k, find knee), Silhouette score (1 to +1, higher=better), BIC for GMM.
  • HDBSCAN: Hierarchical DBSCAN. Handles varying density. Single parameter (min_cluster_size). Better default than DBSCAN.
Dimensionality Reduction
Find the directions in your data along which points spread out the most, then project onto fewer of those directions.
Covariance matrix: C = (1/n) X X captures how much each pair of features move together Eigendecomposition: C v = v eigenvectors v = the directions of spread (principal components) eigenvalues = how much variance in each direction Reduce dimensions: keep top k eigenvectors (those with largest ) Projected data = X [v v ... v] How many components? Explained variance = / Rule of thumb: keep components that explain 95% of total variance
Ccovariance matrix symmetric nn matrix showing how features relate to each other
veigenvector a direction in feature space along which data varies most
eigenvalue how much variance is captured along that eigenvector direction
knumber of components to keep the new reduced dimensionality
95% thresholdkeep adding components until the cumulative explained variance exceeds 95%
Key insight: PCA is a linear method it finds the best flat subspace. It does NOT understand non-linear structure. For visualising complex embeddings, use t-SNE or UMAP instead. PCA is ideal for speeding up downstream models and removing correlated features.
Remember it as: PCA = 'find the camera angles that show the most variation in the data'. First angle: the one with most spread. Second angle: perpendicular to first with most remaining spread. And so on.
  • Autoencoders: Encoder bottleneck Decoder. Bottleneck forces compressed representation. VAE: learns latent distribution (, ) enabling generation.
Class Imbalance Handling
  • SMOTE: Generate synthetic minority samples by interpolating between existing minority points. from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE. Apply to training set ONLY, never test.
  • Class weights: weight = n_total / (n_classes n_class_i). Pass to model: class_weight='balanced'. Easiest fix, no data modification.
  • Focal loss: (1p_t)^ CE. Downweights easy examples. =2 standard. Implemented in most DL frameworks.
  • Threshold tuning: Default threshold 0.5 is wrong for imbalanced. Find optimal from precision-recall curve for your FP/FN cost ratio.
  • Never: Evaluate on balanced test set. Balance test set = lying to yourself about real performance.
Prerequisites: Math & Stats
ML Algorithm Landscape When to Use What
AlgorithmBest ForNeeds Feature Engineering?Interpretable?Scales to Big Data?
Linear Models
Logistic RegressionBinary classification baseline, calibrated probabilitiesYes benefits greatlyFully coefficients are weightsYes O(nd)
Linear SVMHigh-dimensional sparse data (text, NLP)SomewhatPartialWith SGD solver
Tree-Based Models (usually best on tabular data)
Random ForestRobust baseline, handles mixed types, OOB validationMinimal neededFeature importance onlyModerate
XGBoost / LightGBMTabular data champion wins most Kaggle competitionsMinimal neededSHAP values availableDistributed training
CatBoostMany categorical features without manual encodingHandles categoricals nativelySHAP values availableYes
Deep Learning (when tabular models aren't enough)
Neural Network (MLP)Large datasets (>100K rows), relational structureNeeds normalisationBlack box (need SHAP)GPU-accelerated
Transformer (tabular)Very large datasets, multi-modal dataEmbeddings for categoricalsVery hardYes
Bias-Variance Tradeoff Visual
The Tradeoff Visualised
Model Complexity
Error
High Bias (Underfitting)
High Variance (Overfitting)
Sweet spot
Underfitting
High train error
High val error
Small gap
Just Right
Low train error
Low val error
Small gap
Overfitting
Low train error
High val error
Large gap
How to diagnose from learning curves?
Both errors high, small gap
High bias (underfitting) model too simple. Fix: larger model, more features, more training.
Low train, high val, large gap
High variance (overfitting) model memorises training data. Fix: more data, regularisation, dropout, simpler model.
Both errors high but gap grows
Both bias and variance wrong model family. Try a fundamentally different architecture.
Good train + val, gap appears only on test
Data leakage most dangerous. Future information contaminated training. Rebuild pipeline with strict time-ordered splits.
Double descent (modern finding): As model size grows beyond the interpolation threshold (zero training loss), test error can decrease again. This is why massively over-parameterised LLMs still generalise they are in the second descent regime.
Evaluation Metrics Which to Use When
MetricUse WhenFormulaKey Property
AccuracyBalanced classes, simple baseline only(TP+TN) / totalMisleading on imbalanced data
PrecisionFalse positives costly (spam, fraud alerts)TP / (TP+FP)"When I predict positive, am I right?"
RecallFalse negatives costly (cancer, fraud detection)TP / (TP+FN)"Did I find all the real positives?"
F1Both matter equally (general imbalanced classification)2PR / (P+R)Harmonic mean penalises large P/R gaps
ROC-AUCComparing models, threshold-independent evaluationP(pos score > neg score)Misleading on very imbalanced data
PR-AUCImbalanced data (fraud, medical, rare events)Area under P-R curveMore informative than ROC-AUC when positives are rare
NDCG@kRanking (search, recommendations)DCG / ideal DCGRewards relevant items ranked higher
🌍 Real-world use case: A churn-prediction model for a subscription business. The team frames it as binary classification, guards against data leakage (no post-cancellation features), picks PR-AUC over accuracy because churners are rare, and ships a gradient-boosted model whose feature importances tell the retention team which signals to act on.
🌏 Go Deeper — ML Fundamentals
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner fast.ai — Practical Machine Learning (free course) — Teaches by building real models first and theory second; the best entry point if you have Python but the math in this tab still feels abstract.
  • Intermediate scikit-learn User Guide (official docs) — Read the algorithm pages for any model you haven't implemented: each page gives the math, the parameters, and the gotchas in the exact level of detail an ML Engineer interview expects.
  • Intermediate Stanford CS229 — Machine Learning Lecture Notes (free) — Andrew Ng's course notes; the gradient descent, regularisation, and bias-variance sections are the canonical derivations that staff-level interviews reference.
  • Advanced Bishop — Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning (PRML) — The graduate-level reference for probabilistic ML; use it to look up derivations you need to defend in depth, not as a cover-to-cover read.
Deep learning is the engine under every modern AI system. FAANG and research roles expect you to implement multi-head attention from scratch, derive backpropagation on paper, explain every architectural choice, and debug training instabilities. This tab covers theory + math + complete working code for every major concept.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

Deep learning is what made the modern AI boom happen. Instead of a person writing rules (“if word X appears, mark as spam”), you stack many simple layers of math on top of each other, show the stack millions of examples, and let it figure out the rules itself by tweaking its own knobs. The word “deep” just means “many layers”.

What is actually going on: each layer takes numbers in, multiplies them by weights it is learning, and passes the result to the next layer. The final layer makes a prediction. The model compares its prediction to the right answer, sees how wrong it was, and walks the error backwards to nudge every weight a tiny bit closer to correct. That backward walk is called backpropagation — it is the heart of all training.

Why you must understand it: every modern model (chatbots, image generators, voice systems) is a deep learning model underneath. Knowing how layers, activations, and the training loop fit together is what separates engineers who can debug a model that will not learn from engineers who can only call an API.

What this tab covers: a single neuron → a network of neurons → networks built for images (CNNs) → attention and transformers (the architecture behind every chatbot today). Math derived step by step, working code, and the “why” behind each architectural choice.

Backpropagation: The Full Derivation
Forward Pass & Chain Rule
Backpropagation is just the chain rule of calculus applied backwards through the network it tells us how much each weight contributed to the error.
Forward pass (left to right): z1 = W1x + b1 (linear combination at layer 1) a1 = ReLU(z1) (apply activation) z2 = W2a1 + b2 (linear combination at layer 2) p = softmax(z2) (convert to probabilities) L = yᵢlog(pᵢ) (cross-entropy loss) Backward pass (right to left chain rule): dL/dz2 = p y gradient of loss w.r.t. final layer dL/dW2 = a1 dL/dz2 how much each W2 weight caused the error dL/db2 = dL/dz2 sum over batch for bias gradient dL/da1 = dL/dz2 W2 pass error back through W2 dL/dz1 = dL/da1 ReLU'(z1) ReLU gradient: 1 if z1>0, else 0 dL/dW1 = x dL/dz1 dL/db1 = dL/dz1 Why dL/dz2 = p y is so clean: Softmax and cross-entropy cancel each other perfectly Result: gradient = (what we predicted) (what was true)
z = Wx + blinear transformation matrix multiply weights W by input x, add bias b
a = ReLU(z)activation keeps positive values, zeros out negatives
p = softmax(z2)output probabilities all positive, sum to 1
L = cross-entropyhow wrong we are larger L = worse prediction
dL/dWgradient how much the loss changes per unit change in weight W. Tells us which direction to adjust W
(element-wise multiply)Hadamard product multiply corresponding elements, not matrix multiply
chain ruleL/x = L/y y/x derivative of composite function = product of individual derivatives
Key insight: The gradient dL/dz2 = p y is the most important result in deep learning. It means: to reduce the loss, increase the logit for the correct class and decrease logits for all other classes. That's exactly what gradient descent does.
Remember it as: Backprop = 'blame assignment'. Which weights were most responsible for the mistake? The chain rule tells us precisely how much blame each weight gets.
