Python 2-layer net backprop from scratch
import numpy as np class TwoLayerNet: def __init__(self, d_in, d_h, d_out, lr=0.01): # He init for ReLU: Var(W) = 2/fan_in self.W1 = np.random.randn(d_in, d_h) * np.sqrt(2/d_in) self.b1 = np.zeros(d_h) self.W2 = np.random.randn(d_h, d_out) * np.sqrt(2/d_h) self.b2 = np.zeros(d_out) self.lr = lr def softmax(self, z): z = z - z.max(axis=1, keepdims=True) # numerical stability e = np.exp(z) return e / e.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True) def forward(self, X): self.X = X self.z1 = X @ self.W1 + self.b1 self.a1 = np.maximum(0, self.z1) # ReLU self.z2 = self.a1 @ self.W2 + self.b2 self.p = self.softmax(self.z2) return self.p def backward(self, y_onehot): N = y_onehot.shape[0] # Gradient of softmax + cross-entropy dz2 = (self.p - y_onehot) / N dW2 = self.a1.T @ dz2 db2 = dz2.sum(axis=0) # Backprop through ReLU da1 = dz2 @ self.W2.T dz1 = da1 * (self.z1 > 0) # ReLU gradient dW1 = self.X.T @ dz1 db1 = dz1.sum(axis=0) # SGD update self.W2 -= self.lr * dW2; self.b2 -= self.lr * db2 self.W1 -= self.lr * dW1; self.b1 -= self.lr * db1 return -(y_onehot * np.log(self.p + 1e-12)).sum(axis=1).mean()
Vanishing Gradients & Fixes
In deep networks, gradients must travel backwards through many layers and they shrink at each layer, eventually becoming so small that early layers learn nothing.
Gradient at layer 1 in a 10-layer network: dL/dW1 = dL/dz10 (dz10/dz9) ... (dz2/dz1) (dz1/dW1) = product of 10 terms Problem: each term is typically < 1 (sigmoid/tanh saturate) Sigmoid max gradient = 0.25 After 10 layers: 0.25 0.0000001 gradient vanishes! Residual connection fix (ResNets, Transformers): y = F(x) + x add input directly to output gradient: dL/dx = dL/dy (dF/dx + 1) The '+1' is a gradient highway even if dF/dx 0, gradient = dL/dy Other fixes: ReLU: gradient = 1 when active (no saturation when z > 0) Pre-norm: LayerNorm before each sublayer stabilises scale He initialisation: Var(W) = 2/fan_in (keeps gradients unit scale at start)
Vanishing gradientgradients shrink to near-zero as they travel back through many layers early layers stop learning
Sigmoid max gradient (0.25)sigmoid activation saturates even at its steepest point, gradient is only 0.25. Multiplied 10 times = negligible
Residual connection y=F(x)+xadds a skip connection gradient has a 'highway' path that bypasses potentially small F(x) gradients
The '+1' in gradienteven if the sublayer F learns nothing (dF/dx = 0), gradient still equals dL/dy it never vanishes through the skip path
He initialisationsets initial weight variance proportional to 1/fan_in ensures gradients neither vanish nor explode at layer 1
Key insight: Residual connections are the single most important architectural innovation for training deep networks. Without them, networks beyond ~20 layers were untrainable. With them, we can train thousands of layers. The '+1' in the gradient equation is doing all the work.
Remember it as: Vanishing gradient = 'Chinese whispers'. By the time the error message reaches the first layer, it's too quiet to hear. Residual connections = direct phone line that bypasses all the layers.
  • Gradient clipping: if g > threshold: g = g threshold/g. Preserves direction. Standard for RNNs (max_grad_norm=1.0) and LLM training.
  • Exploding gradient symptoms: loss = NaN suddenly, weight norms growing unboundedly. Gradient clipping is the fix.
  • Modern LLMs use: Pre-norm (LayerNorm BEFORE sublayer) + residual connections + AdamW. This combination makes 100B+ layer networks trainable.
Normalization Techniques
BatchNorm vs LayerNorm vs RMSNorm
Normalisation layers rescale activations to a stable range preventing them from exploding or vanishing as they travel through deep networks.
BatchNorm normalise across the BATCH dimension: For each feature j: ⱼ = (1/N)ᵢ xᵢⱼ (batch mean) ϲⱼ = (1/N)ᵢ(xᵢⱼⱼ) (batch variance) Normalise: xᵢⱼ = (xᵢⱼ ⱼ) / (ϲⱼ + ) Scale + shift: yᵢⱼ = ⱼxᵢⱼ + ⱼ (, are learned) Problem: statistics depend on batch size unstable for small batches Problem: does NOT work for sequences of variable length LayerNorm normalise across the FEATURE dimension (used in Transformers): For each sample i: ᵢ = (1/H)ⱼ xᵢⱼ (feature mean for this sample) Normalise and scale: same formula as above, but ᵢ is per-sample Works for any batch size, any sequence length standard in all LLMs RMSNorm LayerNorm without mean subtraction (LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen): RMS(x) = ((1/H)ⱼ xᵢⱼ) (root mean square, no mean) xᵢⱼ = xᵢⱼ / RMS(xᵢ) ⱼ (~3% faster, equivalent quality)
BatchNormnormalises per-feature across the batch. Batch statistics drift problematic at small batch sizes or variable-length sequences
LayerNormnormalises per-sample across all features. Each token/sample normalised independently no batch dependency. Standard in all transformers
RMSNormsimplified LayerNorm skips mean subtraction. Mathematically: just divide by the root-mean-square. Used in LLaMA, Mistral, Gemma, Qwen
, (gamma, beta)learned scale and shift parameters let the network decide how much to un-normalise after normalisation
(epsilon)small constant (1e-8) added to prevent division by zero when variance is very small
Pre-norm vs post-normModern LLMs apply norm BEFORE each sublayer (pre-norm). Original transformer applied it AFTER (post-norm). Pre-norm is more stable for very deep networks
Key insight: The reason LayerNorm replaced BatchNorm in transformers is simple: a sentence with 5 words and a sentence with 100 words are in the same batch. BatchNorm statistics across those are meaningless. LayerNorm normalises each token independently length doesn't matter.
Remember it as: BatchNorm: 'make all examples in this batch look similar on each feature'. LayerNorm: 'make all features of this one example look similar'. Completely different normalisation axes.
  • Why not BatchNorm in transformers: Variable sequence length noisy batch statistics. Small NLP batches unreliable batch-level normalization. Inference uses running stats which may mismatch test distribution.
  • Pre-norm vs Post-norm: Original transformer: Post-norm (LN after residual). Modern LLMs: Pre-norm (LN before sublayer). Pre-norm = more stable for very deep networks, gradients better behaved.
Activation Functions
Activation functions introduce non-linearity without them, stacking linear layers is mathematically identical to having just one linear layer.
ReLU: f(x) = max(0, x) gradient = 1 if x>0, else 0 Fast. Sparse (many zeros). Risk: 'dying ReLU' neurons stuck at 0 forever GELU: f(x) = x (x) = standard normal CDF Smooth version of ReLU. Used in BERT, GPT-2/3. ~2% better than ReLU in transformers SiLU/Swish: f(x) = x (x) = sigmoid Self-gated. Non-monotonic. Used in LLaMA, Mistral SwiGLU (LLaMA 2+, most modern LLMs): FFN(x) = W3 (SiLU(W1x) W2x) Three weight matrices, not two. Width = 8/3 d (not 4) to keep param count same Softmax (output layer): p_k = exp(z_k) / exp(z_j) Temperature T: softmax(z/T) T < 1 sharper (more confident) T > 1 flatter (more uncertain)
ReLUmost common. Simple, fast. 'Dying ReLU' problem: if a neuron's input is always negative, its gradient is always 0 and it never updates
GELUGaussian Error Linear Unit. Smooth curve that gates activation by probability. Default in most transformers
SiLU/Swishx times sigmoid(x). Self-gated the activation gates itself. Used in LLaMA, Mistral
SwiGLUSwish-Gated Linear Unit. Three projections: gate, content, and output projection. Empirically best FFN for LLMs
Softmax temperature Tdividing logits by T before softmax. T<1 makes the model more decisive (exaggerates differences). T>1 makes it more uncertain (smooths differences)
(Hadamard product)element-wise multiplication between two vectors of the same size
Key insight: The reason SwiGLU outperforms GELU FFN is the gating mechanism: W2x acts as a learned filter that controls which information from W1x passes through. This is similar to LSTM gates the network learns to selectively open and close information pathways.
Remember it as: All activations answer the same question: 'should this neuron fire, and how much?' ReLU = binary (on/off). GELU/SiLU = soft decision (probability of firing). SwiGLU = two signals multiply both must agree.
Output Head
Final RMSNorm linear projection (d_model vocab_size). Weight tying: output projection = transpose of input embedding (saves 500M+ params for large vocab). Logits temperature scaling sampling (greedy/top-p/top-k).
KV Cache (Inference)
Cache K,V from all previous tokens. At step T: only compute Q for new token. Full K,V available from cache. Complexity: O(T) per step vs O(T) without cache. Memory: 2 n_layers seq_len n_kv_heads d_head 2 bytes. GQA: reduces this by n_heads/n_groups.
Attention Variants Deep Dive
Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel, each learning to look for different types of relationships.
MHA (Multi-Head Attention) original: Split d_model into h heads, each with d_k = d_model/h h independent Q,K,V projections h attention outputs concatenate project Memory cost: h seq_len d_k per layer KV cache MQA (Multi-Query Attention) used in Falcon, PaLM: Single K,V pair shared by ALL heads h Q projections only KV cache: 1 seq_len d_k (h smaller than MHA!) Slight quality drop. Big memory saving for long sequences. GQA (Grouped-Query Attention) current standard (LLaMA 2/3, Mistral, Qwen): G groups of heads (G=8 typical). Each group shares one K,V pair KV cache: G seq_len d_k (between MHA and MQA) Quality MHA. Speed MQA. Best tradeoff. Flash Attention not an attention variant but an implementation: Standard: materialises the full nn attention matrix in slow HBM memory Flash: tiles into fast SRAM, computes online softmax never stores nn matrix Same mathematical output. 2-4 faster. O(n) memory vs O(n). Required for context windows > 32K tokens on any hardware.
h (number of heads)how many parallel attention computations. Each head attends to different aspects (syntax, semantics, coreference). Typical: 8, 16, 32
d_k = d_model / hdimension per head. Smaller heads each head has a narrower, more focused 'question'
MQAextreme case: all queries share the same keys and values. Drastic KV cache reduction but slight quality loss
GQAG groups share K,V. With G=8 heads and 2 KV groups: 4 smaller KV cache vs MHA. Used in LLaMA 2/3
KV cacheduring generation, K and V from all past tokens are cached. Without it: must recompute entire past on every new token. Memory grows with sequence length
Flash Attention SRAM tilingGPU has fast SRAM (~50MB) and slow HBM (~80GB). Standard attention writes/reads the nn matrix from slow HBM. Flash Attention keeps everything in fast SRAM by computing in tiles
Key insight: The shift from MHA GQA in modern LLMs is almost entirely driven by inference cost. A KV cache for LLaMA 3 70B with MHA at 128K context would require ~70GB impossible on consumer hardware. GQA reduces this to ~18GB. Same model, much more deployable.
Remember it as: MHA = full committee (everyone has their own notes). MQA = one shared notepad for everyone. GQA = shared notepads within subgroups. Flash Attention = better photocopier that doesn't waste paper.
Why Each Architectural Choice
RMSNorm over LayerNormNo mean subtraction: ~3% faster. Same quality empirically (mean subtraction was found unnecessary for stability). Used in LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, Gemma.
Pre-norm over post-normLN before sublayer (not after). Gradients flow more cleanly through residual path. More stable training for very deep networks (100+ layers).
GQA (n_kv_heads < n_heads)K,V heads shared within groups. KV cache size n_kv_heads. GQA n_kv=2, n_heads=8: 4 smaller KV cache vs MHA. Near-identical quality. Standard since LLaMA 2.
SwiGLU over GELU FFNGate (W2x) controls information flow. Gated architecture empirically 2-5% better perplexity. Three weight matrices so FFN width = 8/3d (not 4) to keep param count similar.
Weight tying (emb = lm_head.T)Input embedding and output projection share weights. Saves vocab_sized params (saves 500M+ params for 32K vocab, 4096d). Works because both map between token space and model space.
F.scaled_dot_product_attentionPyTorch 2.0+ built-in Flash Attention. Automatically uses Flash Attention kernel when available on GPU. O(T) memory vs O(T). Use this in production, not manual attention loop.
Model Family Quick Comparison
ModelAttentionContextOpen?Standout
GPT-5.5 / 5.4MoE (est.), multimodal, unified "thinking" router~400K in / 128K outAPI onlyTop intelligence (5.5); 5.4 = coding workhorse. `reasoning_effort` knob.
Claude Opus 4.8 / Sonnet 4.6Constitutional AI; adaptive thinking1M / 128K out (Opus); 1M/64K (Sonnet)API onlyAdaptive thinking auto-selects effort; top agentic-coding scores. 300K output beta on Batch API.
Gemini 3.5 Flash / 3.1 Pro / 3.1 Deep ThinkMultimodal native; parallel reasoning paths (Deep Think)1M (2M experimental)API onlyBest intelligence-per-dollar (3.5 Flash); video understanding; ARC-AGI-2 84.6%.
Llama 4 Maverick / ScoutMoE (128 / 16 experts, 17B active), native multimodal (early fusion)10M (claimed)Open weightsSingle-H100 efficient (Scout); MMLU-Pro 80.5 (Maverick). ~$0.190.49/MTok distributed.
DeepSeek V4 (Flash/Pro) + R1MoE, MLA, DSA (DeepSeek Sparse Attention); thinking + non-thinking modes in one model1M / 384K outOpen weights$0.14/$0.28 (V4 Flash) redefines the price floor; R1 = pure-RL reasoning (GRPO+RLVR), MIT license.
Qwen3 + Qwen3-MaxDense + MoE; dual-mode (thinking / non-thinking)up to 1M (Max: 256K)Open weights (Apache 2.0)119 languages; trillion-param Max; coder & VL refreshes.
Qwen 2.5/3GQA, multilingual128KOpen weightsBest multilingual + math + code open
Transformer Architecture Visual Layer Stack
Tokens Embedding table + RoPE positional encoding
△ Layer N: Pre-RMSNorm
Normalise activations BEFORE the sublayer. More stable than original post-norm for 100+ layer networks.
🧠 Multi-Head Causal Self-Attention
Q: what am I looking for? K: what do I advertise? V: what do I contribute? Causal mask blocks future tokens. GQA: shared K,V within groups smaller KV cache.
x = x + attention_output  ←  Residual connection (gradient highway)
△ Pre-RMSNorm again
⚡ SwiGLU Feed-Forward Network
FFN(x) = W3(SiLU(W1x) W2x). Three weight matrices. Width = 8/3d_model. Stores factual knowledge. Only per-position (no cross-token mixing).
x = x + ffn_output  ←  Residual connection
Repeat N times (e.g., 32 layers for LLaMA 3.1 8B)
📄 Final RMSNorm LM Head
Linear projection to vocab_size. Logits temperature scaling top-p sampling next token. Weight-tied with input embedding.
Attention: Q, K, V Explained
Q
Query = "What am I looking for?"
Each token projects to a query vector representing what information it needs from the rest of the sequence.
K
Key = "What do I advertise?"
Each token projects to a key vector: how it presents itself to other tokens' queries.
V
Value = "What do I contribute?"
Each token's actual content what flows to the output when this token is attended to.
Score = QK/d: High score = strong match between query and key attend more to that token's value. Softmax converts scores to probabilities. Output = weighted sum of Values.
Residual Connection Why It Enables Deep Networks
Input: x
↓
Sublayer F(x)
↓
+
x
(skip)
Output = F(x) + x
Gradient math:
dL/dx = dL/dy (dF/dx + 1)

The +1 is a gradient highway even if F learns nothing (dF/dx 0), the gradient still flows through unchanged.

Without residuals: training fails beyond ~20 layers.
With residuals: LLaMA 3 has 32 layers and trains stably.
🌍 Real-world use case: An image-defect detector on a factory line. A convolutional network trained on a few thousand labelled photos flags scratches in real time; when accuracy stalls, the team adds augmentation and batch-norm rather than more layers, and uses the learned feature maps to show operators where the model is looking.
🌏 Go Deeper — Deep Learning
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner 3Blue1Brown — Neural Networks (YouTube) — 4 videos that build the gradient descent and backpropagation intuition from scratch; watch before reading any derivation in this tab.
  • Intermediate Dive into Deep Learning (d2l.ai) — Free interactive book — Code-first DL textbook with runnable PyTorch notebooks for every concept; use the CNN, RNN, and Attention chapters to fill gaps left after reading this tab.
  • Intermediate Stanford CS231n — CNNs for Visual Recognition (lecture notes) — The backprop and optimisation notes are the best written derivations available; relevant even if you're not doing computer vision.
  • Advanced Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville — Deep Learning (free HTML) — The graduate reference; Part II (Modern Deep Learning) is the highest-yield read for practitioner-level interview depth.
Prereq: Deep Learning · LLMs · Next: Fine-tuning · RAG

Generative AI is the family of models that make new things — text, pictures, music, video, code — instead of just classifying or scoring something that already exists. Chatbots make new text. Image models (DALL·E, Stable Diffusion, Flux) make new pictures. Music models make new songs. They all share one trick: they learned the “shape” of real data from millions of examples, then they sample from that shape to invent something new.

Why one tab covers all of them: the three big families (text, image, multi-modal) use very different machinery underneath — transformers for text, diffusion for images, hybrid backbones for chat models that can also see and hear. But the high-level idea is the same: learn the pattern of real things, then roll a many-sided dice shaped by that pattern. Knowing the shared idea lets you reason about all of them with one mental model.

What this tab covers: how transformers, diffusion, and multi-modal models actually generate output; the maths of embeddings (turning text into numbers that capture meaning); how sampling controls let you trade creativity for accuracy; and where each family wins or loses in real products.

Generative AI = models that produce new content (text, image, audio, video, code). Three families dominate 2026: autoregressive transformers (text/code), diffusion / DiT (image/video, e.g. Sora, SD3, Flux), and multimodal hybrids (GPT-4o/5, Gemini 3, Claude 4) that share one backbone across modalities. Two ideas explain almost everything: (1) learn the distribution of real data, (2) sample from it.
Imagine teaching a parrot. Autoregressive models learn the order of words in millions of sentences and parrot the most likely next word, forever. Diffusion models learn to un-blur noisy images — show them static, and they recover the photo hidden “underneath”. Both are just learning the shape of the data so well that sampling from their learned distribution produces things that look brand new. 
flowchart TB
  G["🎨 Generative AI"] --> AR["Autoregressive
(transformers)"]
  G --> DF["Diffusion
(score / DiT)"]
  G --> OT["Older: GAN, VAE,
Flow, EBM"]
  AR --> T1["Text & Code
GPT-5.x, Claude 4.x,
Gemini 3, DeepSeek V4"]
  AR --> T2["Audio tokens
Suno, MusicGen"]
  DF --> I1["Image
SD3, Flux, Imagen 3"]
  DF --> I2["Video
Sora, Veo, Runway"]
  DF --> I3["3D / motion
NeRF, GS, MotionDiff"]
  AR -.shared backbone.-> MM["🧠 Multimodal
(one model, many modalities)"]
  DF -.-> MM
The 2026 generative landscape: pick a family by output modality and latency budget.
Every generative model solves the same problem: approximate P(x), the distribution of real data, then sample from that approximation. Differences are just in how you factor and parameterize P(x) — autoregressive uses the chain rule (one dimension at a time), diffusion uses a noising/denoising trajectory, GANs play a two-player game, VAEs use a variational lower bound. Same target, different math.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.
Diffusion Models: Complete Math & Architecture
DDPM: Forward & Reverse Process Derivation
Forward process (gradually add noise): q(x_t|x_{t-1}) = N(x_t; (1-_t)x_{t-1}, _tI) Closed form (skip to any t directly): x_t = ᾱ_tx_0 + (1-ᾱ_t), ~N(0,I) ᾱ_t = _{s=1}^t(1-_s) [cumulative product] At t=T (T=1000): ᾱ_T 0 x_T N(0,I) Noise schedules: Linear: _t = _1 + (_T-_1)t/T [simple, poor quality at T0] Cosine: ᾱ_t = cos(t/2Tf/(1+f)) [better, standard now] Training objective (denoising score matching): _*(x_t,t) = argmin E|| - _(ᾱ_tx_0+(1-ᾱ_t), t)|| Simple MSE! No adversarial training. Very stable. Reverse sampling (DDPM, slow): x_{t-1} = (1/⚱_t)(x_t - _t/(1-ᾱ_t)_(x_t,t)) + _tz _t = ⚲_t, z~N(0,I). Requires T=1000 steps. DDIM (faster, 10-50 steps): x_{t-} = ᾱ_{t-}((x_t-(1-ᾱ_t)_)/ᾱ_t) + (1-ᾱ_{t-}-_t)_ + _t =0: fully deterministic. Same quality at 20 fewer steps. Classifier-free guidance: _guided = _uncond + w(_cond - _uncond) w=1: no guidance. w=7.5: standard. w=15+: over-saturated. Train: randomly drop condition (p=0.1) model learns both
Latent Diffusion & DiT Architecture
Diffusion models generate images by REVERSING a process that gradually adds Gaussian noise to data. Training teaches a neural network to predict and remove the noise at each step. Generation starts from pure noise and repeatedly denoises until a clean image emerges.
Forward process (add noise, closed form): x_t = √ā_t · x_0 + √(1−ā_t) · ε, ε ~ N(0,I) ā_t = Π(1−β_s) for s=1..t [cumulative noise schedule] At t=T: ā_T ≈ 0 → x_T ≈ pure Gaussian noise Training objective (predict the noise): L = E || ε − ε_θ(x_t, t) ||² Simple MSE! No adversarial loss. Very stable to train. Reverse (denoise, one step): x_{t-1} = (1/√α_t)(x_t − β_t/√(1−ā_t) · ε_θ(x_t,t)) + σ_t·z DDIM (faster, 20× fewer steps): Deterministic reverse: η=0 → no added noise Same quality as DDPM in 20–50 steps vs 1000 steps Classifier-free guidance (CFG): ε_guided = ε_uncond + w·(ε_cond − ε_uncond) w=1: no guidance | w=7.5: standard | w>15: over-saturated
x_0original clean image — what we want to generate
x_tnoisy image at timestep t — mixture of signal and noise
ε (epsilon)Gaussian noise added — N(0,I), what the neural network learns to predict
ā_tcumulative signal fraction — how much of x_0 remains at step t. Starts at ~1, decays to ~0
ε_θ(x_t, t)the U-Net denoiser — takes noisy image + timestep, predicts the noise that was added
w (CFG scale)guidance strength — how hard to push toward the text condition. 7.5 is standard for high-quality images
Key insight: The genius of diffusion models is the training objective: just predict the noise. Simple MSE loss, no adversarial training (unlike GANs). The denoising U-Net implicitly learns the full data distribution by learning to remove noise at every noise level. Stable, scalable, and empirically powerful.
Remember it as: DDPM = teach a network to clean up blurry photos. Start with white noise, ask "what noise was added?", subtract it, repeat 1000 times. DDIM = do the same thing in 20 steps by being deterministic. CFG = mix the conditioned and unconditioned predictions to steer toward your text prompt.
Latent Diffusion (Stable Diffusion): VAE encoder: E(x) z R^{H/8 W/8 4} 8 spatial compression: 512512 6464 latent 64 fewer pixels to diffuse over! Diffusion in latent space: x_t = noisy version of z Denoising network: U-Net or DiT in latent space VAE decoder: D(z) x (reconstruct image) U-Net for diffusion: Encoder: downsample + ResBlocks + attention Bottleneck: full self-attention Decoder: upsample + ResBlocks + cross-attention (text) Skip connections: preserve spatial detail DiT (Diffusion Transformer, used in SD3, Sora): Patchify latent: z R^{HWC} N patches, N=HW/p AdaLN-Zero: timestep + class embedding scale,shift,gate x = gate_attnAttn(AdaLN(x)) + x Standard ViT blocks otherwise Scales better than U-Net (predictable with more compute)
Python DDPM noise and loss from scratch
import torch def make_schedule(T=1000, beta_start=1e-4, beta_end=0.02): betas = torch.linspace(beta_start, beta_end, T) alphas = 1 - betas alpha_bar = torch.cumprod(alphas, dim=0) # _t return betas, alphas, alpha_bar def q_sample(x0, t, alpha_bar, noise=None): "Forward process: add noise to x0 at timestep t." if noise is None: noise = torch.randn_like(x0) sqrt_ab = alpha_bar[t].sqrt()[:, None, None, None] sqrt_1ab = (1 - alpha_bar[t]).sqrt()[:, None, None, None] return sqrt_ab * x0 + sqrt_1ab * noise, noise def p_losses(model, x0, t, alpha_bar): "Denoising loss: predict noise at timestep t." x_noisy, noise = q_sample(x0, t, alpha_bar) predicted_noise = model(x_noisy, t) # U-Net or DiT return torch.nn.functional.mse_loss(noise, predicted_noise) # Training loop: betas, alphas, alpha_bar = make_schedule() for batch in dataloader: x0 = batch["image"].cuda() t = torch.randint(0, 1000, (x0.shape[0],), device=x0.device) loss = p_losses(denoising_model, x0, t, alpha_bar.cuda()) optimizer.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); optimizer.step()
GANs, VAEs & Multimodal
VAE: Reparameterization Trick
Latent Diffusion Models (Stable Diffusion) run the diffusion process in a compressed LATENT space (6464) instead of pixel space (512512), making it 8-16 faster. A separate VAE encodes images to latent space; a U-Net denoises there; the VAE decoder returns to pixel space.
VAE Encoder compresses image to latent: z = Encoder(x), z R^{H/8 W/8 4} 8 spatial compression: 512512 image 6464 latent Diffusion runs in latent space on z (not pixel x): z_t = ᾱ_t z_0 + (1ᾱ_t) U-Net _(z_t, t, c) denoises latent z, conditioned on text c Text conditioning via cross-attention: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK/d) V Q = latent features, K=V = CLIP text embeddings text guides image generation at every U-Net layer VAE Decoder reconstructs pixel image: x = Decoder(z_0) Trained with reconstruction + perceptual + adversarial loss Speed advantage: Diffusion in 6464 latent is 64 fewer operations than diffusion directly in 512512 pixel space
zlatent code compressed representation of the image, 8 smaller than pixel space
_(z_t, t, c)U-Net denoiser predicts noise added to latent z at timestep t, conditioned on text c
cconditioning signal CLIP text embedding guiding the generation via cross-attention
ᾱ_tcumulative noise schedule how much signal (x_0) vs noise () at timestep t
H/8 W/8latent spatial dimensions 512512 image becomes 6464 latent (4-channel)
Key insight: Transformers replaced LSTMs not because LSTMs are bad they still work well but because LSTMs are fundamentally sequential: process token 1, then 2, then 3. Transformers process all tokens in parallel. This makes transformer training 10-100x faster on GPUs, enabling far larger models.
Remember it as: Latent Diffusion = DDPM on a compressed photo. The VAE squeezes a 512512 image into a 6464 latent. DDPM runs in that tiny space (64 fewer pixels = 64 faster). The VAE decoder blows it back up. Stable Diffusion, SDXL, and Flux all work this way.
VAE ELBO objective: L = E_{q(z|x)}[log p(x|z)] - KL(q(z|x)||p(z)) Reconstruction loss - KL regularization Encoder: q(z|x) = N((x), ϲ(x)) [outputs , log ] Decoder: p(x|z) reconstruct x from z Prior: p(z) = N(0,I) KL term (closed form for Gaussian): KL(N(,ϲ)||N(0,1)) = ( + ϲ - log ϲ - 1) Reparameterization trick (KEY): Naive: z ~ q(z|x) = N(,ϲ) non-differentiable! Reparameterize: z = + Ϸ, ~N(0,I) Now: dL/d and dL/d computed via backprop through z is just external noise (not a parameter) This makes the entire VAE end-to-end differentiable
VAE: Complete PyTorch Implementation
VAE from Scratch with Reparameterization Trick
PyTorch VAE: encode, reparameterize, decode, ELBO
import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F class VAE(nn.Module): def __init__(self, input_dim=784, hidden=400, latent=20): super().__init__() self.enc_h = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden) self.enc_mu = nn.Linear(hidden, latent) self.enc_lv = nn.Linear(hidden, latent) self.dec_h = nn.Linear(latent, hidden) self.dec_x = nn.Linear(hidden, input_dim) def encode(self, x): h = F.relu(self.enc_h(x.view(-1, 784))) return self.enc_mu(h), self.enc_lv(h) def reparameterize(self, mu, logvar): "KEY TRICK: z = mu + sigma*eps, eps~N(0,I). Makes sampling differentiable." if self.training: std = (0.5 * logvar).exp() eps = torch.randn_like(std) return mu + std * eps # gradient flows through mu and std return mu # deterministic at eval time def decode(self, z): return torch.sigmoid(self.dec_x(F.relu(self.dec_h(z)))) def forward(self, x): mu, logvar = self.encode(x) z = self.reparameterize(mu, logvar) return self.decode(z), mu, logvar def vae_loss(x_hat, x, mu, logvar, beta=1.0): "ELBO = E[log p(x|z)] - beta*KL(q(z|x)||p(z))" recon = F.binary_cross_entropy(x_hat, x.view(-1,784), reduction="sum") # KL = -0.5 * sum(1 + logvar - mu^2 - exp(logvar)) kl = -0.5 * torch.sum(1 + logvar - mu.pow(2) - logvar.exp()) return (recon + beta * kl) / x.shape[0] # Training vae = VAE().cuda() optim = torch.optim.Adam(vae.parameters(), lr=1e-3) for x_batch, _ in loader: x_hat, mu, logvar = vae(x_batch.cuda()) loss = vae_loss(x_hat, x_batch.cuda(), mu, logvar) optim.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); optim.step() # Generate: sample from prior z~N(0,I), decode z = torch.randn(16, 20).cuda() imgs = vae.decode(z) # (16, 784) pixel probs # Interpolate between two images in latent space z1, _ = vae.encode(img1.cuda()) z2, _ = vae.encode(img2.cuda()) for a in torch.linspace(0, 1, 10): interp = vae.decode((1-a)*z1 + a*z2)
Why Reparameterization Trick is Critical
Problem without trick: z ~ q(z|x) = N(mu, sigma^2) -- sampling is non-differentiable! Cannot backpropagate through a random node. dL/d_mu and dL/d_sigma = undefined via autograd. Reparameterization fix: z = mu + sigma * eps, eps ~ N(0,I) eps is external randomness (not a parameter) Now: dL/d_mu = dL/dz * dz/d_mu = dL/dz * 1 dL/d_sigma = dL/dz * dz/d_sigma = dL/dz * eps Both are well-defined gradients! The randomness is separated from the parameters. KL divergence (closed form for Gaussian): KL(N(mu,sigma^2) || N(0,1)) = 0.5*(mu^2 + sigma^2 - log(sigma^2) - 1) Summed over all latent dimensions. Forces latent distribution toward N(0,I) prior.
GANs: Training Dynamics
GANs pit two networks against each other: a Generator tries to fool the Discriminator by creating realistic fakes; the Discriminator tries to tell real from fake. The Generator improves by learning from the Discriminator's feedback until the Discriminator can no longer tell them apart.
GAN Minimax Objective (original): min_G max_D E[log D(x)] + E[log(1 D(G(z)))] D maximises: distinguish real (1) from fake (0) G minimises: make D(G(z)) 1 (fool discriminator) Non-saturating G loss (used in practice): L_G = E[log D(G(z))] Avoids vanishing gradient when D is strong early in training WGAN (Wasserstein GAN) fixes training instability: W(P_r, P_g) = max_{||f||_L1} E[f(x)] E[f(G(z))] Critic f replaces Discriminator D (no sigmoid, no probability) Gradient penalty: ||f(x)|| 1 (enforces 1-Lipschitz) Result: training curves correlate with visual quality Mode collapse: G learns one mode, ignores z diversity Fix: minibatch discrimination, feature matching, spectral norm Fix: progressive growing (StyleGAN), adaptive augmentation (StyleGAN2) Training tip: balance D and G updates (D steps: 15 per G step)
G(z)Generator output maps noise z to a fake sample. Goal: make D(G(z)) 1
D(x)Discriminator output probability that input x is real (not generated). Range [0,1]
z ~ N(0,I)latent noise the Generator's random input. Different z values produce different generated samples
min_G max_Dminimax game G minimises the objective (wants to fool D), D maximises it (wants to detect fakes)
Wasserstein distanceWGAN's more stable loss measures the 'effort' to transform the fake distribution into the real one. Correlates with visual quality.
Key insight: The reason cross-entropy is the universal classification loss: minimising H(P,Q) is mathematically identical to minimising D_KL(P||Q) because H(P) is constant (we can't change the data). So we're always finding the Q that is most similar to the true distribution P.
Remember it as: Entropy = how surprised you are on average. Cross-entropy = how surprised your MODEL is by the true data. KL = how different two probability distributions are.
GAN minimax objective: min_G max_D [E_x[log D(x)] + E_z[log(1-D(G(z)))]] D: maximize (real1, fake0) G: minimize (fakeD says 1) Practical G loss (non-saturating): max E_z[log D(G(z))] instead of min E_z[log(1-D(G(z)))] Better gradient signal when D is strong Training instabilities: Mode collapse: G maps all z same output Fix: minibatch discrimination, unrolled GANs Vanishing gradient: D perfect log(1-D(G(z))) 0 Fix: WGAN Wasserstein distance instead of JSD W(P_r,P_g) = inf_ E[(x,y)~][||x-y||] (Earth Mover) Gradient penalty: ||D(x)|| = 1 (1-Lipschitz) Why diffusion replaced GANs: No adversarial training instability Better distribution coverage (no mode collapse) More controllable (CFG, ControlNet) Scales predictably with compute
CLIP & Multimodal Models
CLIP contrastive training: Batch: N (image, text) pairs I_i = ImageEncoder(img_i) / ||...|| [normalized] T_i = TextEncoder(text_i) / ||...|| [normalized] Similarity matrix: S_ij = I_i T_j InfoNCE loss (symmetric): L_img = -ᵢ log(exp(S_ii/)/ⱼexp(S_ij/)) L_txt = -ᵢ log(exp(S_ii/)/ⱼexp(S_ji/)) L = (L_img + L_txt) / 2 Zero-shot image classification: Embed N class descriptions: "a photo of a {class}" Embed test image Predict class = argmax cosine_sim(image, class_embs) Works on classes never seen during training! LLaVA architecture: CLIP ViT linear projection LLM input space Visual tokens concatenated with text tokens Fine-tune projection (phase 1) then LLM too (phase 2) Simple but effective: image = 256 visual "words"
Flow Matching & Modern Image Generation Stack
Flow Matching (Flux, SD3)
Flow matching trains a neural network to learn a vector field that transforms a simple noise distribution into a complex data distribution via a straight-line (or curved) interpolation path. It is simpler to train than diffusion (no noise schedule) and the key innovation in Stable Diffusion 3 and Flux.
Continuous Normalizing Flow (CNF) ODE: dx/dt = v_(x, t), t [0, 1] x_0 ~ N(0,I) [noise] x_1 ~ p_data [image] v_: neural network predicting velocity field at each point Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) objective: L_CFM = E_{t,x_0,x_1} || v_(x_t, t) (x_1 x_0) || x_t = (1t)x_0 + tx_1 [straight-line interpolation path] Target velocity = x_1 x_0 [constant along straight path] Simple MSE loss! No score function needed. Why straight paths beat DDPM curved paths: DDPM: curved stochastic path needs T=1000 steps to follow Flow Matching: straight path 8-20 NFE (neural function evaluations) Same quality, 50-100 faster inference Stable Diffusion 3 / FLUX use flow matching: SD3: multimodal DiT with text+image cross-attention FLUX: 12B param model, CFG-distilled for real-time generation Both outperform SD2/SDXL in prompt adherence and detail
v_(x, t)velocity field the neural network that predicts which direction to move at position x and time t
x_0starting noise sample drawn from N(0,I), the source distribution
x_1target data sample a real image from the training set, the target distribution
x_t = (1t)x_0 + tx_1straight-line interpolation the training path. At t=0: pure noise. At t=1: clean data.
NFENeural Function Evaluations how many times the network runs during generation. FM: 8-20. DDPM: 1000.
Key insight: The common mistake in A/B testing: checking p-value repeatedly until it crosses 0.05, then stopping. This inflates false positive rate from 5% to ~30%. Either pre-commit to a sample size, or use sequential testing methods (SPRT, alpha-spending) that are designed for continuous monitoring.
Remember it as: Flow matching = teach a neural network to push noise toward data along the SHORTEST path. DDPM takes 1000 steps along a curved, noisy path. Flow matching takes 8-20 steps along a straight line. Same destination, dramatically fewer steps. SD3 and FLUX use this.
Flow matching vs DDPM: DDPM: learn to denoise at each step t Flow matching: learn a VECTOR FIELD v_(x_t, t) that transports samples from noise N(0,I) to data distribution Conditional flow matching objective: L_CFM = E[||v_(x_t, t) - u_t(x_t|x_0)||] u_t = (x_0 - x_t)/(1-t) [optimal transport path] x_t = (1-t)x_0 + t [linear interpolation] ~ N(0,I) Why flow matching is better than DDPM: Straighter transport paths fewer NFE (function evaluations) DDPM: 50-1000 steps for quality Flow matching: 10-20 steps for same quality 3-5 faster inference at same quality Classifier-free guidance: v_guided = v_uncond + w(v_cond - v_uncond) Same formula as DDPM CFG, just applied to vector field Flux default: w=3.5 (much lower than SD's 7.5)
2026 Image & Video Generation Stack
Flux.1 (Black Forest)Flow matching + DiT. Best text rendering (historical SD weakness). 12B params. Two variants: dev (open, non-commercial), schnell (faster, commercial). API via fal.ai, Replicate. Production standard for image gen 2024-2025.
SDXLTwo U-Nets (base + refiner). Two text encoders. 6.9B params. High photorealism. Many community fine-tunes available (clothing, faces, styles). Still widely deployed despite Flux being newer.
GPT-image-1 / DALL-E lineageDALL-E 3 was the 2023–24 default; OpenAI's 2025 successor is gpt-image-1 (image-out of GPT-5.x). Trained on re-captioned data — excellent prompt following, native in ChatGPT.
Sora 2 / Sora 2 Pro (Sept 2025)DiT-based, spacetime patches; Sora 2 GA Sept 2025, then iOS app + API. Synchronised audio, much improved physics. Veo 3.1 (Google) is the strongest competitor; Runway Gen-4, Kling 2.x, Hailuo 02 round out the pack.
Imagen 4 / Nano Banana Pro / Veo 3.1 / Lyria 3Google's 2025–26 multimodal stack: Imagen 4 (image), Nano Banana Pro / 2 (Gemini-powered editor), Veo 3.1 (video), Lyria 3 (music). All accessible via Gemini API and Vertex.
Open stackFLUX.1 / FLUX.1.1 Pro (Black Forest Labs, dominant open), SD 3.5, Stable Diffusion XL Turbo, HunyuanVideo, Wan 2.1 (Alibaba), OpenSora.
Distilled inferenceLCM, SDXL-Turbo, Flux Schnell, Hyper-SD, DMD2 — 1–4 step generation. Production standard for real-time UX.
ControlNetCondition diffusion on structural inputs: depth map, edge map, pose, segmentation mask. Trainable copy of encoder + zero-initialized conv. Preserves structure while changing style. Works with any Stable Diffusion model.
Production Image Generation & VAE Implementation
VAE Implementation from Scratch
PyTorch VAE with reparameterization trick
import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F class VAE(nn.Module): def __init__(self, input_dim=784, latent_dim=20, hidden=400): super().__init__() self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden) self.fc_mu = nn.Linear(hidden, latent_dim) self.fc_lv = nn.Linear(hidden, latent_dim) # log variance self.fc3 = nn.Linear(latent_dim, hidden) self.fc4 = nn.Linear(hidden, input_dim) def encode(self, x): h = F.relu(self.fc1(x)) return self.fc_mu(h), self.fc_lv(h) # , log ϲ def reparameterize(self, mu, logvar): "z = + Ϸ, ~N(0,I) enables backprop through sampling" std = torch.exp(0.5 * logvar) eps = torch.randn_like(std) # external noise return mu + eps * std # differentiable path through , def decode(self, z): return torch.sigmoid(self.fc4(F.relu(self.fc3(z)))) def forward(self, x): mu, logvar = self.encode(x.view(-1, 784)) z = self.reparameterize(mu, logvar) return self.decode(z), mu, logvar def vae_loss(recon_x, x, mu, logvar): # Reconstruction: binary cross-entropy per pixel recon = F.binary_cross_entropy(recon_x, x.view(-1,784), reduction="sum") # KL divergence: D_KL(N(,ϲ)||N(0,1)) = (+ϲ-log ϲ-1) kl = -0.5 * torch.sum(1 + logvar - mu.pow(2) - logvar.exp()) return recon + kl
When to Use Which Generative Model
TaskBest ModelWhy
Text-to-image (high quality)Flux.1, SDXLBest quality. Flux: better text rendering. SDXL: more community fine-tunes.
Text-to-image (API, easy)GPT-image-1 / Imagen 4 / FLUX.1.1 ProGPT-image-1 = best prompt following in ChatGPT. FLUX = best open. Imagen 4 = Google stack.
Controlled generation (pose/depth)ControlNet + SDStructural conditioning. Maintains content while controlling structure.
Image-to-image (style transfer)SD img2imgDenoise from noisy version of input. Strength parameter controls how much to change.
Video generationSora 2 / Sora 2 Pro · Veo 3.1 · Runway Gen-4 · Kling 2.xSora 2 GA Sept 2025 (iOS app + API). Veo 3.1 is the closest peer. Runway / Kling remain accessible alternatives.
Anomaly detectionVAEHigh reconstruction error = anomaly. Simple, interpretable, no labels needed.
Molecule generation (drug discovery)VAE + graph neural netContinuous latent space enables gradient-based molecular optimization.
Face synthesis / deepfakeStyleGAN3GAN still best for high-quality, fast face generation (inference <1ms).
VAE Complete Implementation
VAE from Scratch: Reparameterization Trick
PyTorch Complete VAE with ELBO loss
import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F class VAE(nn.Module): def __init__(self, input_dim=784, hidden=400, latent=20): super().__init__() # Encoder: x (, log ϲ) self.enc_h = nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden) self.enc_mu = nn.Linear(hidden, latent) self.enc_lv = nn.Linear(hidden, latent) # log-variance # Decoder: z x self.dec_h = nn.Linear(latent, hidden) self.dec_x = nn.Linear(hidden, input_dim) def encode(self, x): h = F.relu(self.enc_h(x.view(-1, 784))) return self.enc_mu(h), self.enc_lv(h) # , log ϲ def reparameterize(self, mu, logvar): "z = mu + sigma*eps, eps~N(0,I). Enables backprop through sampling." if self.training: # stochastic during train std = (logvar * 0.5).exp() # = exp(log ϲ / 2) eps = torch.randn_like(std) # ~ N(0,I) return mu + std * eps # differentiable path return mu # deterministic at eval def decode(self, z): h = F.relu(self.dec_h(z)) return torch.sigmoid(self.dec_x(h)) # pixel probs [0,1] def forward(self, x): mu, logvar = self.encode(x) z = self.reparameterize(mu, logvar) x_hat = self.decode(z) return x_hat, mu, logvar def elbo_loss(x_hat, x, mu, logvar, beta=1.0): "ELBO = E[log p(x|z)] - beta*KL. beta=1: VAE, beta>1: beta-VAE." recon = F.binary_cross_entropy(x_hat, x.view(-1,784), reduction="sum") # KL divergence (closed form): -(1 + logϲ - - ϲ) kl = -0.5 * torch.sum(1 + logvar - mu.pow(2) - logvar.exp()) return (recon + beta * kl) / x.shape[0] # per-sample average # Training loop vae = VAE() optim = torch.optim.Adam(vae.parameters(), lr=1e-3) for x_batch, _ in mnist_loader: x_hat, mu, logvar = vae(x_batch) loss = elbo_loss(x_hat, x_batch, mu, logvar) optim.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); optim.step() # Generate new images: sample z ~ N(0,I), decode z_sample = torch.randn(16, 20) # 16 samples from prior generated = vae.decode(z_sample) # (16, 784) pixel probs # Interpolate in latent space (smooth morphing) z1, z2 = vae.encode(img1)[0], vae.encode(img2)[0] for alpha in torch.linspace(0, 1, 10): z_interp = (1-alpha)*z1 + alpha*z2 # linear interp img_interp = vae.decode(z_interp)
VAE vs GAN vs Diffusion: When to Use Which
VAE: use whenYou need a structured latent space (anomaly detection, drug design, interpolation). Reconstruction fidelity secondary to latent structure. Training stability matters. Fast inference needed (no iterative sampling).
GAN: use whenHighest visual quality for a narrow distribution (faces, specific domain). Fast inference required (single forward pass). Willing to accept training instability. StyleGAN3 for photorealistic faces.
Diffusion: use whenBest overall quality + diversity. Text-to-image generation. Controllability (ControlNet, CFG). Willing to accept slower inference (10-50 steps). Production standard 2023-2025.
VQ-VAE: use whenYou want discrete latent space (tokens for transformers). Tokenizing images for LLM-style generation (DALL-E v1, image GPT). More structured than continuous VAE latent space.
GenAI Architecture Patterns Visual Overview
Pattern 1: Direct API Call
App LLM API response. Simplest. Stateless. Good for: single-turn Q&A, summarisation, classification. Latency: 0.53s. Cost: per token.
Pattern 2: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented)
Query embed vector search retrieve chunks LLM with context answer. Good for: document Q&A, knowledge base. Latency: 15s. Reduces hallucination by 60-80%.
Pattern 3: Agentic (Tool Use)
LLM decide tool execute tool observe decide next ... final answer. Good for: multi-step tasks, code execution, web search. Latency: 560s. Non-deterministic.
Pattern 4: Fine-tuned Model
Custom training smaller/cheaper model deploy. Good for: domain-specific tasks at high volume, specific output format. One-time cost: $500$5K. Per-token cost: 60-80% lower.
Prompt Engineering Techniques When to Use Each
Zero-shot (No Examples)
  • Just the instruction. "Classify this review as positive/negative."
  • Use when: task is simple, LLM has strong priors for it
  • Fastest, cheapest, least reliable for edge cases
Few-shot (Examples Provided)
  • "Here are 3 examples: [ex1][ex2][ex3]. Now classify: [input]"
  • Use when: output format matters, task has nuance
  • 3-5 examples typically optimal; more can hurt
🌍 Real-world use case: A marketing team auto-drafts product descriptions. A generative model turns a few bullet-point specs into on-brand copy; the team controls tone with the system prompt, constrains length and format with structured output, and routes anything mentioning price or claims through a human review step before publishing.
🌏 Go Deeper — Generative AI
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner Hugging Face — Diffusion Models from Scratch (free course) — Hands-on 4-unit course that walks you from Gaussian noise to a working diffusion pipeline; the best entry point if the DDPM math in this tab felt overwhelming.
  • Intermediate Jay Alammar — The Illustrated Stable Diffusion — Visual walkthrough of the full latent diffusion pipeline; read this alongside the architecture section to build a mental model you can draw on a whiteboard.
  • Advanced Rombach et al. — High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models (2021) — The Stable Diffusion paper; read Sections 3 (Method) and 4 (Experiments) to understand the design choices that made latent diffusion production-viable.
Prereq: LLMs · GenAI · Next: Long Context · RAG

Fine-tuning means taking an already-trained AI model (like GPT or Llama) and training it a little bit more on your data so it behaves the way you want. Think of it as “teaching a smart new graduate the style and habits of your team” — you are not teaching them maths from scratch, you are shaping their tone and process to fit your job.

What it is good at (and not): fine-tuning is great at changing behaviour — how the model speaks, what format it outputs (JSON, a specific report layout), what tone it uses, which task pattern it follows. Fine-tuning is bad at teaching new facts. If you want the model to know your company's holiday policy, do NOT fine-tune — use RAG (look up the policy at answer time). Most teams reach for fine-tuning too early; try a better prompt first, then RAG, then fine-tune only if those fail.

What this tab covers: the modern 2026 recipe (SFT → DPO → GRPO — three training steps explained inside), why we use adapters (small add-on weights called LoRA / QLoRA instead of changing the whole model, which saves 100× on cost), how to prepare training data, and how to measure if fine-tuning actually made things better.

Fine-tuning = continuing to train a pre-trained model on your data so it adopts your format, style, structure, or skill. In 2026 the recipe is SFT → DPO/ORPO → GRPO+RLVR, almost always with LoRA/QLoRA adapters (not full-parameter updates). It does NOT reliably add new facts — that's what RAG is for. Try RAG/prompting first; fine-tune only when you need a behavior the model just won't do.
Pre-training is like medical school — broad, expensive, takes years. Fine-tuning is the residency — you teach a fully-educated doctor your hospital's protocols, your charting style, your bedside manner. You wouldn't re-teach them anatomy (RAG handles “look up the latest”), but you absolutely do drill them on the way you want things done. LoRA adapters are like swappable lab coats — same doctor, different specialty per shift.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.
Post-training Stack: SFT → DPO → GRPO
flowchart LR
  PT["🎓 Pre-trained base
(GPT/Llama/Mistral)"] --> SFT["✏️ SFT
(instruction → response
pairs)"]
  SFT --> PREF["👍 Preference tuning
DPO / ORPO / SimPO
(chosen vs rejected)"]
  PREF --> RL["🎯 Verifiable RL
GRPO + RLVR
(math/code rewards)"]
  RL --> OUT["🤗 Aligned model"]
  LO["🧮 LoRA / QLoRA
adapters"] -.attached at every stage.-> SFT
  LO -.-> PREF
  LO -.-> RL
  EVAL["⚖ Eval suite
(your golden set)"] -. gates every stage .-> OUT
The 2026 post-training stack. Each stage is optional, but skipping the eval gate is not.
Fine-tuning shifts the model's output distribution toward your data. Three regimes: (1) SFT shifts the mode (what gets emitted), (2) Preference (DPO/ORPO) shifts the preference ordering between alternatives, (3) RL with verifiable rewards shifts reasoning trajectories the verifier scores high. None of these reliably add facts — they all steer existing capability.
When to Fine-tune vs RAG vs Prompting
GoalBest ApproachWhy
Use fresh / proprietary knowledgeRAGFine-tuning does NOT reliably update factual knowledge. LLMs may hallucinate trained facts. RAG provides fresh, citeable context.
Specific output formatFine-tuning (SFT)When format is complex/irregular and prompting is unreliable. Format is stored in weights.
Domain-specific style/toneFine-tuning (SFT)Style is in model weights. RAG can't teach writing style. Prompting helps but fine-tuning is more robust.
Reduce hallucinationsRAG first, then fine-tuningRAG gives factual grounding. Fine-tuning on verified data helps but doesn't eliminate hallucination.
Align model behavior / safetyDPO / RLHFSafety requires changing model behavior, not providing context. Can't prompt away harmful outputs reliably.
Reduce latency & costFine-tune smaller modelFine-tune 7B to match 70B on specific task 10 cheaper inference. Ongoing savings compound.
New capability (new language, new task type)Continued pre-training + SFTSFT alone can't add new knowledge. Need sufficient training data to update model internals.
LoRA Visual Explanation
Full Fine-tuning (expensive)
W (d k)
ALL dk parameters
updated each step
16.7M params for d=k=4096
LoRA (parameter-efficient)
B (dr)
init zeros
A (rk)
init random
r=16: only 131K params (0.78%!)
h = Wx + (BAx)(/r)   W frozen, only B&A train
Why B starts at zeros: At initialisation, W = BA = 0A = 0. The model starts exactly at its pretrained weights and gradually learns the adaptation. If B were random, training would start from a corrupted model.
Fine-tuning vs RAG vs Prompting Decision Guide
What approach should I use for my use case?
Need fresh / proprietary knowledge
RAG fine-tuning cannot reliably inject facts. Model may hallucinate trained facts confidently. RAG provides citeable, updatable context.
Consistent output format / style
SFT Fine-tuning complex formats or domain-specific tone are learned in weights. Prompting alone is unreliable for complex format requirements.
Make model safer / more aligned
DPO / RLHF alignment requires changing model behaviour, not providing context. Can't prompt away harmful outputs reliably.
Reduce inference cost (high volume)
Fine-tune smaller model 7B model fine-tuned on your task can match 70B with general prompting. 10 cheaper inference, forever.
Quick prototype / exploring the task
Prompt engineering first always start here. If a well-crafted prompt solves it: zero cost. Fine-tuning only when prompting plateaus.
New capability the base model lacks
Continued pre-training + SFT if the model has never seen this domain/language, SFT alone won't add it. Need enough pre-training data first.
LoRA: The Math & Implementation
LoRA Derivation
Full fine-tuning: update W (dk parameters) W_new = W + W (modify all dk = millions/billions of params) LoRA: learn low-rank decomposition of W W = B A where B R^(dr), A R^(rk), r << min(d,k) Parameters: rd + rk = r(d+k) vs original dk Example: d=k=4096, r=16 LoRA params: 164096 + 164096 = 131,072 Original: 40964096 = 16,777,216 Reduction: 0.78% of original! Forward pass: h = Wx + (BAx)/scaling [base weights frozen + adapter] scaling = /r ( is hyperparameter, typically =r scaling=1) Initialization: A: random Gaussian N(0, ϲ) [produces non-zero gradient] B: zeros [so W=BA=0 at start preserves pretrained behavior] Critical: B=zeros ensures we start exactly at pretrained model Merging (zero inference overhead): W_merged = W + (/r)BA Delete A,B. Model runs at full speed, no extra computation.
Python QLoRA fine-tuning with HuggingFace PEFT
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training from trl import SFTTrainer, SFTConfig import torch MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B" # Step 1: Load in 4-bit quantization (QLoRA) bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig( load_in_4bit=True, bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4", # NF4 optimal for normal-dist weights bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16, bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True, # nested quantization, saves ~0.4 bits ) model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained( MODEL_ID, quantization_config=bnb_config, device_map="auto" ) model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model) # enable gradient checkpointing # Step 2: LoRA configuration lora_cfg = LoraConfig( r=16, # rank (higher = more capacity but more params) lora_alpha=32, # scaling = alpha/r = 2 target_modules=["q_proj","k_proj","v_proj","o_proj", "gate_proj","up_proj","down_proj"], lora_dropout=0.05, bias="none", # standard: no bias in adapters task_type="CAUSAL_LM" ) model = get_peft_model(model, lora_cfg) model.print_trainable_parameters() # Typical output: trainable params: 41M (0.49% of 8.03B) # Step 3: Training configuration train_cfg = SFTConfig( output_dir="./llama3-finetuned", num_train_epochs=3, per_device_train_batch_size=4, gradient_accumulation_steps=4, # effective batch = 16 learning_rate=2e-4, lr_scheduler_type="cosine", warmup_ratio=0.05, fp16=False, bf16=True, optim="paged_adamw_32bit", # memory-efficient optimizer logging_steps=25, save_strategy="epoch", max_seq_length=2048, ) trainer = SFTTrainer(model=model, args=train_cfg, train_dataset=dataset, dataset_text_field="text") trainer.train()
DPO Training Pipeline
DPO trains the model to prefer chosen responses over rejected ones using a single loss function no reward model, no RL loop needed.
DPO Loss: L = -E[ log sigmoid( beta * (log pi_theta(y_w|x)/pi_ref(y_w|x) - log pi_theta(y_l|x)/pi_ref(y_l|x)) ) ] y_w = chosen (preferred) response y_l = rejected response pi_ref = frozen SFT model (reference) beta = 0.1 typical (controls how much to deviate from reference) What this loss does in plain terms: For each (prompt, chosen, rejected) triple: -> Increase probability of the chosen response (relative to reference) -> Decrease probability of the rejected response (relative to reference) -> The beta term prevents the model drifting too far from the SFT baseline Connection to reward model: DPO implicitly defines a reward: r(x,y) = beta * log pi_theta(y|x)/pi_ref(y|x) The optimal DPO policy IS the solution to the RLHF PPO problem So DPO and RLHF converge to the same answer DPO just gets there more simply
y_w (winner)the preferred response chosen by humans or an AI judge
y_l (loser)the rejected response worse, harmful, or less helpful
pi_ref (reference)frozen SFT model. The KL 'anchor' model must improve while staying similar to it
log pi_theta / pi_reflog probability ratio: how much more likely is the current policy vs reference? Positive = current policy prefers this response more
sigmoidsquashes the difference to (0,1). Acts like a soft comparison: 'which response does the current policy prefer over the reference?'
betatemperature of the KL constraint. Low beta = model can deviate more from reference. High beta = stays close to SFT
Key insight: DPO is simpler than RLHF not because it is a weaker approximation it mathematically converges to the same optimal policy. The reward model in RLHF is just an unnecessary intermediate step. DPO skips it and goes directly from preference data to policy update.
Remember it as: DPO = 'given a pair of responses, move the policy to make the good one more likely and the bad one less likely, but don't change too much from your starting point'.
Python DPO training with TRL
from trl import DPOTrainer, DPOConfig # Dataset format required for DPO: # {"prompt": "...", "chosen": "...", "rejected": "..."} dpo_config = DPOConfig( beta=0.1, # KL penalty coefficient max_length=1024, max_prompt_length=512, per_device_train_batch_size=2, gradient_accumulation_steps=8, learning_rate=5e-7, # much lower than SFT! num_train_epochs=1, # DPO converges fast, 1-3 epochs bf16=True, loss_type="sigmoid", # standard DPO loss ) dpo_trainer = DPOTrainer( model=model, # SFT model to align ref_model=ref_model, # frozen reference (or None for implicit ref) args=dpo_config, train_dataset=preference_dataset, tokenizer=tokenizer, ) dpo_trainer.train()
  • DPO dataset construction: Human annotators rate pairs (expensive). GPT-4 as judge (cheaper). Contrastive pairs from SFT model at temperature 0 vs temperature 1. Constitutional AI self-critique. Aim: 1K+ pairs for meaningful alignment, 10K+ for strong alignment.
  • Common DPO mistakes: Learning rate too high (5e-5+) model diverges. Too many epochs chosen/rejected probabilities converge to zero (numerical instability). Always monitor chosen/rejected rewards during training.
Dataset Preparation & Quality
SFT Dataset Design
  • Chat template: Every model has a specific format. LLaMA 3: <|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>system...assistant. Wrong template = dramatically worse performance. Use tokenizer.apply_chat_template().
  • Quality over quantity: LIMA paper (2023): 1000 carefully curated examples outperform 50K mediocre ones. Filter ruthlessly. Remove: inconsistent Q&A, incomplete responses, formatting errors, contradictions.
  • Diversity: Cover all task types you want to improve. Single-task fine-tuning catastrophic forgetting on other tasks. Mix in 5-10% general data.
  • Synthetic data generation: Use GPT-4 to generate dataset. "Generate 100 examples of {task} in format {format}." LLM judge filters quality. Distillation: smaller model learns from larger model's outputs. Risk: model may amplify teacher's errors.
Evaluation During & After Training
  • Training metrics: Training loss, validation loss (perplexity). Loss on held-out domain examples. LLM judge score on 50-100 representative examples during training (expensive but informative).
  • Catastrophic forgetting check: Run MMLU/HellaSwag/GSM8K on fine-tuned model. Compare to base model. Acceptable: <5% degradation on general benchmarks. If degraded: increase LoRA rank, add general data to mix, lower learning rate.
  • Win rate evaluation: Head-to-head comparison: fine-tuned vs base model on your task. LLM judge (GPT-4) selects winner. Win rate >60% on target task = successful fine-tuning.
  • Production evaluation: Shadow deployment: run new model alongside old, log outputs. Human review on sample. A/B test with real users. Offline metrics rarely capture production quality fully.
Catastrophic Forgetting Prevention
  • LoRA (primary fix): Adapters don't modify base weights. Only BA matrices updated. Base model's general knowledge preserved. Most effective prevention at zero extra cost.
  • Replay: Mix 5-10% of original pre-training data with fine-tuning data. Anchors model to original capabilities. Used in LLaMA instruction tuning recipes.
  • Lower learning rate: 2e-4 (SFT) vs 2e-5 (QLoRA fine-tuning). Smaller updates = less overwriting of pre-trained knowledge.
  • Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC): Adds penalty for changing weights important for old tasks (measured by Fisher information matrix). Rarely used in practice (expensive to compute Fisher); LoRA is simpler and more effective.
Fine-tuning Hyperparameter Reference & Dataset Quality
Hyperparameter Guide
Learning rate (LoRA SFT)1e-4 to 2e-4. Too high (>5e-4): loss diverges or catastrophic forgetting. Too low (<1e-5): convergence too slow. Use cosine decay with 5% warmup.
LoRA rank rr=8: minimal capacity (fast, few params). r=16: standard for instruction tuning. r=64: complex domain adaptation. Higher r = more params, more risk of overfitting on small datasets.
LoRA alphaSet alpha=2r (scaling=2). Or alpha=r (scaling=1). Higher scaling = stronger adaptation signal. If model quality degrades: reduce alpha. alpha=r is most conservative starting point.
Batch sizeEffective batch = per_device grad_accum n_gpus. Target: 32-128 total. Smaller batch: more gradient noise (sometimes helpful for generalization). Larger: more stable, need higher LR.
Epochs1-3 for SFT on large datasets (>10K examples). 3-5 for small datasets (<2K). Monitor val loss: stop when it plateaus or increases (early stopping).
Max seq lengthMatch your actual data distribution. Padding to 4096 when avg length is 512 = 8 wasted compute. Pack multiple short examples into one sequence (packing). Reduces training time 2-4.
Dataset Quality Checklist
  • LIMA principle (Zhou et al. 2023): 1,000 carefully curated examples outperform 50,000 mediocre ones. The quality of each (instruction, response) pair matters enormously. Filters to apply: Does the response actually follow the instruction? Is the response factually correct? Is the format consistent? Remove contradictions and ambiguous cases.
  • Diversity coverage: Cover all task types you want to improve. If you train only on extraction examples: model forgets how to generate. Include 10-15% general-purpose data from the original instruction tuning mix to prevent catastrophic forgetting.
  • Format consistency: Use the exact same chat template as the base model. LLaMA 3: <|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>user... Mistral: [INST] ... [/INST]. Wrong template = model confuses instruction boundary = poor quality regardless of data quality.
  • LLM-as-judge for dataset filtering: Use GPT-4 to score each training example quality (1-5). Remove examples scoring below 3. Automated pipeline: generate examples score filter train. Can generate and filter 10K examples in hours for ~$50.
Evaluation & Production Deployment
  • Merge LoRA before deployment: W_merged = W + (alpha/r) B A. No inference overhead. One model file. Saves serving memory vs keeping adapter + base separate.
  • Multi-adapter serving (vLLM): vLLM supports loading multiple LoRA adapters on one base model. Route requests to correct adapter based on tenant or task. Memory: base model loaded once + small adapters. Cost-effective for multi-tenant fine-tuning.
  • Regression testing: After every fine-tuning run: evaluate on general benchmarks (MMLU, HellaSwag) + your domain golden set. Acceptable degradation on general: <5%. If more: lower LR, add general data to mix, reduce epochs.
Fine-tuning Decision Tree
Does prompting achieve >85% accuracy?
If YES: ship it. Prompting is faster, cheaper, and easier to update. Fine-tuning for 15% improvement is rarely worth it unless you're at 10M+ requests/day.
Do you have 100+ quality examples?
If NO: collect data first. Fine-tuning on fewer examples than this often overfits. Invest in data collection before training.
Is task format/style the issue?
If YES: SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning) with format examples. The model knows the answer it just needs to know the output format you want.
Is task quality/safety the issue?
If YES: Preference training (DPO/ORPO). You need the model to prefer good outputs over bad ones. Requires pairs: (chosen, rejected).
GPU constraint?
Limited GPU (<40GB): use QLoRA (4-bit quantised LoRA). 40-80GB: use LoRA. Multiple GPUs: full fine-tune with FSDP. QLoRA on a single A100 can fine-tune a 70B model.
LoRA vs Full Fine-tune Side by Side
Full Fine-tune
  • Updates ALL model weights (7B params = 28GB in fp32)
  • Requires multi-GPU for anything over 3B
  • Risk of catastrophic forgetting
  • Result: one model per task
  • Use when: budget available + large proprietary dataset
LoRA Fine-tune
  • Trains only low-rank adapter matrices (0.11% of params)
  • 7B model fine-tuning on single A100 (40GB)
  • Base model unchanged, adapters are swappable
  • Merge at inference for zero overhead
  • Use when: limited GPU + multiple tasks to fine-tune
LoRA Math Complete Derivation
LoRA avoids updating all W by learning a low-rank correction delta-W = B times A. Because rank r is tiny (8 to 64) compared to the full dimension (4096+), trainable parameters drop by 99%+. The base model is frozen throughout. Only B and A receive gradients. After training, B times A can be merged back into W with zero inference overhead.
Full fine-tune: update W directly, cost = d x k parameters LoRA: learn delta-W = B . A where B in R^(d x r), A in R^(r x k), r much less than min(d,k) Forward pass with LoRA: h = W.x + (B . A . x) * (alpha / r) W is frozen | B and A are trained | alpha/r scales the correction Parameter count comparison (d = k = 4096, r = 16): Full fine-tune: 4096 x 4096 = 16,777,216 parameters LoRA adapters: 4096 x 16 + 16 x 4096 = 131,072 parameters (0.78%) Initialisation: B initialised to ZEROS -> delta-W = B.A = 0 at start Model begins at pretrained weights; adapters gradually learn the correction A initialised to random Gaussian (non-zero) so gradients flow through A from first step Alpha / r scaling: alpha is a fixed hyperparameter (set to 2 x r as a safe default) Dividing by r keeps effective update magnitude stable when you change r Changing r without adjusting alpha scales all outputs -- use (alpha/r) as a unit Merging after training (zero inference cost): W_final = W + B.A * (alpha/r) Merge once, discard adapters, deploy W_final -- identical forward pass to baseline QLoRA extension: Base model quantised to 4-bit NF4 (frozen, dequantised on-the-fly for each forward pass) LoRA adapters trained in bfloat16 Memory for 70B model: ~35 GB instead of ~140 GB at fp16 Enables fine-tuning on single A100 80GB GPU
W (d x k)frozen pretrained weight matrix never updated during LoRA training, preserves general capability
B (d x r)down-projection adapter initialised to zeros so the model starts at pretrained weights
A (r x k)up-projection adapter initialised random Gaussian, receives gradient, provides non-zero signal from step 1
r (rank)bottleneck dimension. r=8 minimal, r=64 richer adaptation. Sweet spot: r=16 to r=32 for most tasks. Higher r = more params, diminishing returns beyond task complexity
alphascaling hyperparameter. Effective update scale = (alpha/r) x learning_rate. Default: set alpha = 2 x r. Decouples rank choice from learning rate tuning
delta-W = B.Athe learned weight correction. Merged into W after training: W_final = W + B.A*(alpha/r). Zero inference overhead after merging
NF4 (QLoRA)Normal Float 4-bit quantisation grid matched to the normal distribution of pretrained weights. Less error than uniform INT4 for the same bit-width
Key insight: LoRA works because the weight updates needed for fine-tuning live in a low-dimensional subspace. The pretrained W captures all general knowledge in thousands of dimensions. Task adaptation only needs to steer a handful of those directions. Rank-16 B.A captures those directions using 0.78% of the parameters of a full update. This is why LoRA matches full fine-tune quality on most downstream tasks while using 99% fewer trainable parameters.
Remember it as: LoRA = learn a thin sketch on top of the original painting. The original canvas (W) is untouched. You add a very thin overlay (B.A) that corrects only what needs correcting. Merge the overlay when done original plus overlay equals improved model, zero extra cost at inference time.
Preference Optimisation Zoo — pick the right loss in 2026
DPO opened the door; 2024–26 brought a family of variants. The interview-grade question is no longer “what is DPO?” but “given my data and goal, which member of the family and why?”
MethodDataReference model?Use it when
RLHF (PPO)Pref pairs → reward model → PPOYes (KL anchor)You need a reusable reward model and can afford 4-model RL pipeline.
DPOPref pairs (chosen, rejected)Yes (frozen ref)Default baseline. Simpler than PPO, similar quality, no reward model. Watch for likelihood-displacement.
IPOPref pairsYesDPO overfits noisy/non-deterministic preferences. IPO regularises the implicit reward; safer with crowd labels.
KTOSingle good/bad labels (unpaired)YesYou can’t collect pairs — only thumbs up/down. Uses Kahneman-Tversky utility; works with imbalanced data.
ORPOPref pairsNoCombine SFT + preference in one loss, one pass, no reference model. Best when compute is tight and SFT data is paired.
SimPOPref pairsNoLength-normalised, reference-free. Often beats DPO on chat benchmarks; very lightweight.
cDPO / RDPO / APO-zero/APO-downPref pairsYesVariants robust to label noise or designed for “anchored” preference (when ref model is much better/worse than data).
GRPOGroups of K samples per prompt + scalar rewardsYes (KL)Reasoning RL on a verifier (math/code unit-tests). No value/critic network; group-relative advantage. Powered DeepSeek-R1.
RLOO / Reinforce++ / Dr.GRPOSame as GRPOYesCritic-free alternatives that fix various GRPO biases; rising in 2026 RL pipelines.
GSPO (Group Sequence PO)Sequence-level GRPOYesStable training for MoE / very long reasoning traces (Qwen3 RL recipe).
RLAIF / Constitutional AILLM-generated preferences from a written constitutionYesScale safety alignment without humans in the loop on every comparison.
GRPO + RLVR — the reasoning-model recipe
Setup. Sample K completions per prompt (typically 8–64). Each completion gets a verifiable reward ri — e.g. 1 if math answer matches, 1 if all unit tests pass, 0 otherwise. Compute group-relative advantage
  Ai = (ri − mean(r)) / std(r)
and optimise the clipped surrogate (PPO-style) plus a KL penalty to the SFT reference model. No value/critic network — the group itself provides the baseline. This is RLVR: RL with Verifiable Rewards. DeepSeek-R1-Zero proved you can do this with zero SFT and still get strong CoT to emerge; production recipes use SFT-on-good-rollouts (rejection sampling) plus GRPO loops.
  • When to reach for RLVR: the task has a cheap, programmable correctness check — math, code (tests), JSON-schema, SQL execution, theorem proving, formal logic, tool-use traces. Reward hacking is the #1 failure; keep a held-out verifier and human spot-checks.
  • When to skip: open-ended quality (style, helpfulness, harmlessness) — stay with DPO/ORPO/RLHF here.
  • Reasoning distillation: generate long-CoT traces with a teacher (R1, GPT-5.x, Gemini Deep Think), SFT a smaller student on them. Cheaper than RL and gives most of the gain. Open recipes: Open-Reasoner-Zero, OpenThoughts, S1, Sky-T1.
PEFT in 2026 — LoRA is just the start
MethodIdeaPick when
LoRALow-rank ΔW = BADefault; everywhere supported
QLoRA4-bit NF4 base + LoRA in bf16Single-GPU fine-tune of 70B
DoRADecompose ΔW into direction + magnitude; LoRA only directionSqueeze a few more points out at small extra cost
LoRA+ / rsLoRADifferent LR for A vs B / scale-stable LoRALarger ranks (≥64) without diverging
PiSSA / OLoRASVD-init the adapters from W itselfFaster convergence, better small-data quality
GaLore / BAdamTrain full weights with low-rank gradient projectionCloser to full fine-tune accuracy on a single GPU
(IA)³ / SpectrumEven fewer params; or train only high-SNR layersEdge fine-tuning, very small adapters
X-LoRA / MoLEMixture of LoRA experts at inferenceOne base, many domain adapters routed dynamically
Tooling stack (2026): Unsloth (2–5× faster QLoRA), Liger Kernels (drop-in Triton kernels), Axolotl / LLaMA-Factory / OpenRLHF / TRL / verl for trainers; vLLM for fast rollouts inside RL loops. Model merging (mergekit: TIES, DARE, SLERP, Sakana evolutionary) is the cheap alternative to multitask fine-tuning when you have several adapters or several full fine-tunes.
🌍 Real-world use case: A support bot that must answer in a company's exact voice and follow its escalation rules. Rather than retrain from scratch, the team LoRA-fine-tunes an open model on a few thousand curated past tickets — cheaply teaching style and policy the base model never had, while keeping the base weights frozen so they can roll back instantly.
🌏 Go Deeper — Fine-tuning
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner Hugging Face — Fine-tuning a Pretrained Model (official docs) — The canonical walkthrough for SFT with Trainer API; read the full-fine-tune version first before touching LoRA, to understand what you're approximating.
  • Intermediate Hugging Face PEFT blog — Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning — Practical overview of LoRA, QLoRA, and prefix tuning with code; this is the go-to reference for "how do I actually run this on a single GPU?"
  • Intermediate Hu et al. — LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models — The LoRA paper; read the abstract and Section 4 (the rank decomposition math) — 20 minutes that make every LoRA hyperparameter choice self-explanatory.
  • Advanced Rafailov et al. — Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) — The paper that replaced PPO-RLHF for most production fine-tuning; read Sections 3–4 to understand why it's simpler and when it still fails.
Prereq: LLMs · Attention basics · Next: RAG · Infrastructure
What is this tab? This tab is the long-context decision guide for transformers, RoPE extensions, state-space models, and the retrieval tradeoffs around very large context windows.
Why it matters: Long-context claims are easy to repeat but expensive to ship, so you need a framework for deciding when bigger context beats better retrieval and when it does not.
How to use it: Start with the decision tree, then read the RoPE and SSM sections before comparing them against the RAG alternatives later in the document.
Example: If a stakeholder asks for a 1M-token contract assistant, use this tab to estimate the latency, recall, and evaluation work before saying yes.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

“Long context” means asking an AI model to read and remember a huge amount of text in one go — a whole book, a year of meeting notes, an entire codebase — instead of just the last few messages. The hard part: the attention mechanism inside transformers gets much slower and much more expensive as the input grows. Doubling the text usually quadruples the cost. Naively giving a model 1 million tokens would cost a fortune and take ages.

How 2026 models fit 1M+ tokens anyway: with a stack of tricks — smarter ways to mark position in the text (so the model still knows what came first), faster attention math (FlashAttention reorders the work to fit in fast GPU memory), entirely different architectures that scale linearly with text length (Mamba, Jamba), and clever caching that reuses earlier work. You do not need to memorise the names — just know these tricks exist and each one has a different trade-off.

What this tab covers: when long context is actually the right answer (vs. just using RAG to fetch only what you need), which trick fits which use case, the “needle in a haystack” test (does the model actually find the buried fact?), and the latency / cost math you need before promising stakeholders “feed it a million tokens”.

Vanilla attention is O(n²) in sequence length, so naive long context is unaffordable. In 2026 we get 1M+ tokens via four tricks: (1) better position encodings (RoPE + YaRN/LongRoPE), (2) attention rewrites (FlashAttention, MLA, Ring), (3) state-space models (Mamba-2, Jamba, Codestral Mamba) that run in linear time, and (4) KV-cache offload & sparse retrieval. Pick by latency budget and what "needle in haystack" recall you need.
Vanilla transformer attention is like a committee meeting where every attendee must shake every other attendee's hand — with 1M attendees, you'll never finish lunch. Long-context tricks are different rules for that meeting: RoPE/YaRN = better name-tags so people remember who's who; FlashAttention = a faster handshake protocol; Mamba/SSM = a single town-crier who summarizes as they walk down the line; retrieval/sparse = a host who only invites the relevant people in the first place. 
flowchart TB
  P["❓ Need long context?"] --> Q1{"How long?"}
  Q1 -->|"< 32K"| A1["Vanilla attention
+ FlashAttention-3"]
  Q1 -->|"32K - 200K"| A2["RoPE extension
(YaRN / NTK)
+ KV cache offload"]
  Q1 -->|"200K - 1M"| A3["MLA / Ring attention
+ chunked prefill
+ sparse / sink tokens"]
  Q1 -->|"1M+ or streaming"| A4["🚀 SSM hybrid
(Mamba-2, Jamba,
Codestral Mamba)"]
  Q1 -->|"Sparse needles"| A5["🔎 Just use RAG
(don't pay for context
you don't read)"]
  A1 --> E["⚖ Test on RULER /
InfiniteBench / NIAH"]
  A2 --> E
  A3 --> E
  A4 --> E
  A5 --> E
Decision tree: don't pick a long-context technique without measuring on RULER first.
Two physical limits drive every long-context design: (1) Compute — attention is O(n²) FLOPs, so anything sub-quadratic (SSMs at O(n), sparse/local at O(n·k)) wins eventually. (2) Memory bandwidth — the KV cache grows linearly with context, and reading it dominates inference time. Every trick (PagedAttention, MLA, KV quantization, prefix caching) is fighting one of these two limits.
RoPE Context Extension: YaRN & LongRoPE
RoPE Mechanics & Extension Math
RoPE encodes the position of each token by rotating its query and key vectors so the attention score between two tokens automatically depends on their relative distance.
For token at position pos, dimension pair (2i, 2i+1): Rotation angle: theta_i = 10000^(-2i / d_model) Apply rotation to Q and K vectors: Q_pos = Rotate(Q, theta_i * pos) K_pos = Rotate(K, theta_i * pos) Key property - dot product depends on relative position: Q_pos . K_pos' = f(Q, K, pos - pos') -> attention score encodes distance between tokens, not absolute positions Frequencies per dimension: Low i (small index): theta is large -> fast oscillation -> encodes nearby positions High i (large index): theta is small -> slow oscillation -> encodes long-range positions Why RoPE extrapolates better than learned PE: Learned PE: position 8193 has no embedding (was never trained on) RoPE: position 8193 just means a larger angle -> still meaningful YaRN extends this: scale frequencies so long positions map to seen angle ranges
theta_irotation frequency for dimension i. Based on 10000^(-2i/d). High i = slow rotation = encodes long-range position
Rotate(v, angle)rotate vector v by 'angle' in the complex plane. Equivalent to multiplying pairs of dimensions by [cos, -sin; sin, cos]
Relative position propertythe crucial mathematical property: Q_pos . K_pos' only depends on pos-pos'. The model sees relative distance, not absolute position
YaRN scalingto extend context: scale theta_i by a factor s so that long positions fall in the range the model was trained on. Requires short fine-tuning on long-context data
Key insight: RoPE's key advantage over other positional encodings: the attention dot product Q.K naturally encodes relative position. The model doesn't need to subtract position embeddings it's built into the geometry of the rotation. This is why RoPE is used in every major open-source LLM.
Remember it as: RoPE = spin each token's vectors by an angle proportional to its position. Two tokens that are far apart have very different rotation angles. Two close tokens have similar angles. The dot product (cos of the angle between them) captures this distance automatically.
Mamba SSM: Implementation
