What this tab covers: the architectures interview rooms still test (YOLO and DETR for object detection, ViT for image classification, CLIP for image↔text linking), how tokenisation actually works (BPE, WordPiece — how text gets chopped into pieces the model predicts), sentence embeddings (turning a whole sentence into one searchable vector), and which pre-trained model to reach for in each common task.

Computer Vision: Architectures & Algorithms
Object Detection: YOLO vs DETR vs Faster R-CNN
YOLO detects all objects in an image with a single neural network pass dividing the image into a grid and predicting boxes and classes simultaneously.
YOLO grid approach: Divide image into S x S grid (e.g., 7x7 = 49 cells) Each cell predicts B bounding boxes + confidence + C class probabilities One forward pass -> all predictions simultaneously Bounding box format: [x, y, w, h, confidence] x, y: centre of box relative to cell (0-1) w, h: box width/height relative to image (0-1) confidence: P(object) * IoU(predicted, ground_truth) IoU (Intersection over Union): IoU = area(intersection) / area(union) IoU = 0: boxes don't overlap at all IoU = 1: boxes are identical Threshold: keep box if IoU with ground truth > 0.5 Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) - remove duplicates: Sort boxes by confidence score (highest first) Keep highest-confidence box Remove any remaining box with IoU > threshold with the kept box Repeat until no boxes left to consider Modern YOLOv8/v10 improvements: Anchor-free: predict box centre directly (no preset anchor shapes) CSP backbone: better feature extraction with fewer parameters Multi-scale heads: detect objects at different scales simultaneously
S x S griddivides the image into cells. Each cell is responsible for detecting objects whose centre falls within that cell
B (boxes per cell)each cell predicts B candidate bounding boxes. Only the one with highest confidence for each detected object is kept after NMS
C (class probabilities)probability of each of C object classes, conditioned on an object being present. Multiplied with confidence for final class-specific confidence
IoUhow much two boxes overlap. Area of overlap divided by total area covered by both. Threshold 0.5: 'close enough'. Used for both training (matching predictions to ground truth) and NMS
NMSremoves duplicate detections of the same object. Without it: YOLO would output 10 overlapping boxes for one car. NMS keeps the most confident one
Key insight: YOLO's key insight is treating object detection as a single regression problem rather than a two-stage pipeline (propose regions, then classify). This makes it 30-100x faster than two-stage detectors like Faster R-CNN, enabling real-time video detection on consumer hardware.
Remember it as: YOLO = 'you only look once'. Grid the image, have each cell answer: 'is there something here? What shape box? What class?' All at once. Compare to two-stage detectors: 'first find interesting regions, then classify each one separately' slower but historically more accurate.
Python IoU and NMS from scratch
import numpy as np def iou(box_a, box_b): "Intersection over Union for two boxes [x1,y1,x2,y2]." xa = max(box_a[0], box_b[0]); ya = max(box_a[1], box_b[1]) xb = min(box_a[2], box_b[2]); yb = min(box_a[3], box_b[3]) inter = max(0, xb-xa) * max(0, yb-ya) area_a = (box_a[2]-box_a[0]) * (box_a[3]-box_a[1]) area_b = (box_b[2]-box_b[0]) * (box_b[3]-box_b[1]) return inter / (area_a + area_b - inter + 1e-8) def nms(boxes, scores, iou_threshold=0.5): "Non-maximum suppression." order = scores.argsort()[::-1] keep = [] while len(order): i = order[0]; keep.append(i) ious = np.array([iou(boxes[i], boxes[j]) for j in order[1:]]) order = order[1:][ious < iou_threshold] return keep
Vision Transformers (ViT) & Segmentation
Vision Transformers treat an image as a sequence of patches just like words in a sentence and apply the standard transformer architecture to process them.
ViT steps: 1. Patchify: divide image into P x P patches N = (H/P) * (W/P) patches -> e.g., 224x224 image, P=16: N=196 patches 2. Embed patches: linear projection of flattened patch pixels Each patch: P*P*C pixels -> d_model dimensional embedding 3. Add position embeddings (learned) to each patch Tells model where each patch is in the image 4. Prepend [CLS] token (learnable vector) Will hold the global image representation after attention 5. Apply standard transformer encoder (12 layers for ViT-Base) Full self-attention: every patch can attend to every other patch 6. [CLS] token's output -> classification head Why ViT needs more data than CNNs: CNN: translation invariance built in (same filter applied everywhere) ViT: no such inductive bias -> must learn spatial structure from data With enough data (ImageNet-21K, JFT-300M): ViT beats CNNs With small data (100K images): CNNs still win
Patcha small square region of the image (16x16 pixels for ViT-B/16). Treated as a single 'token' in the transformer sequence
[CLS] tokenspecial learnable vector prepended to the sequence. After transformer processing, its output represents the entire image. Passed to the classification head
Position embeddingslearned vectors added to each patch embedding. Tells the model that patch at row 3, column 5 is different from patch at row 0, column 0
Inductive biasassumptions baked into the architecture. CNN: nearby pixels are related (locality). ViT: no such assumption -> must learn from data. More data needed but can learn more flexible representations
d_modelthe embedding dimension. Same as in text transformers. Typical: ViT-B=768, ViT-L=1024, ViT-H=1280
Key insight: ViT showed that the transformer architecture is not specific to text it can process any data that can be represented as a sequence of vectors. This insight led directly to multimodal models (CLIP, LLaVA) where images and text share the same architecture and can be processed together.
Remember it as: ViT = 'cut the image into a jigsaw puzzle, turn each piece into a word embedding, then run BERT on the sequence of pieces'. The transformer figures out how the pieces relate spatially through learned attention.
  • Swin Transformer: Hierarchical ViT. Shifted window attention: local windows (O(T) not O(T)). Multi-scale features like CNN. Best for dense prediction (segmentation, detection).
  • ConvNext: CNN redesigned with transformer insights. Patch stem, depthwise conv, GELU, LayerNorm. CNN simplicity + transformer performance. Strong baseline.
NLP: Tokenization, Embeddings & Tasks
Tokenization Deep Dive
BPE tokenisation builds a vocabulary of sub-word pieces by starting with individual characters and repeatedly merging the most common adjacent pairs.
BPE training algorithm: 1. Start: vocabulary = all individual characters (a, b, ..., z, space, etc.) 2. Count: frequency of all adjacent token pairs in the corpus 3. Merge: combine the most frequent pair into a new single token 4. Repeat steps 2-3 for N merges (N = target vocabulary size - initial chars) Example progression: corpus: 'low lower lowest' (simplified) Initial tokens: l, o, w, e, r, s, t (individual chars) Iter 1: 'lo' most frequent -> merge -> new token 'lo' Iter 2: 'low' most frequent -> merge -> 'low' Final: 'low' is one token, 'lower' = 'low' + 'er', etc. Byte-level BPE (tiktoken, GPT-4): Start with 256 bytes (not characters) as base vocabulary ANY input encodable - no <UNK> token ever '🎯' (emoji) = 4 bytes -> 1-4 tokens depending on training frequency Why tokenisation quality matters: 'cat' -> 1 token (frequent): model treats as single concept 'supercalifragilistic' -> many tokens: model sees fragments Numbers like '1234' -> ['12', '34']: model never sees full number -> poor arithmetic
Vocabulary sizetotal number of distinct tokens. GPT-2: 50,257. GPT-4 tiktoken: 100,277. LLaMA 3: 128,256. Larger = fewer tokens per text = shorter sequences = cheaper inference
Merge rulea pair of tokens to be combined. After training, these rules are applied in order to tokenise new text
Byte-level baseusing all 256 possible byte values as the starting vocabulary. Guarantees any UTF-8 text (any language, any emoji, any code) can be tokenised without unknown tokens
Fertilityaverage tokens per word in a language. English: ~1.3. Arabic: ~2.0. High fertility = language uses more tokens per concept = longer sequences = higher cost = less effective context window
Subword granularityBPE finds the sweet spot: full words (too many OOV) vs characters (too many tokens per sentence). Common words = single tokens. Rare words = broken into familiar pieces
Key insight: Tokenisation is the most overlooked factor affecting LLM quality. A model with a small vocabulary (32K) spends more tokens on each piece of text, giving it a shorter effective context window and worse arithmetic (numbers split across tokens). LLaMA 3's jump from 32K to 128K vocabulary is not just efficiency it means fewer tokens per sentence, longer effective context, and better mathematical reasoning.
Remember it as: BPE = building a language by starting with letters and gluing together the most common letter combinations. 'the' gets glued first (most frequent), then 'ing', then 'tion', etc. End result: common words are single tokens, rare words are split into familiar pieces.
Python BPE from scratch (simplified)
from collections import Counter def get_pairs(vocab): pairs = Counter() for word, freq in vocab.items(): symbols = word.split() for i in range(len(symbols)-1): pairs[(symbols[i], symbols[i+1])] += freq return pairs def merge_pair(vocab, pair): new_vocab = {} merged = " ".join(pair); new_token = "".join(pair) for word, freq in vocab.items(): new_vocab[word.replace(merged, new_token)] = freq return new_vocab def train_bpe(corpus, num_merges=10): vocab = Counter(" ".join(list(w)) + " </w>" for w in corpus.split()) merges = [] for _ in range(num_merges): pairs = get_pairs(vocab) if not pairs: break best = max(pairs, key=pairs.get) vocab = merge_pair(vocab, best) merges.append(best) return vocab, merges
Sentence Embeddings & Bi/Cross Encoder
Sentence embeddings map variable-length text to fixed-size vectors so that similar texts end up near each other in vector space enabling fast semantic similarity search.
SBERT contrastive training: Positive pair (p, q): semantically similar sentences Negative pair (p, n): semantically different sentences Multiple Negatives Ranking loss: Batch: N positive pairs (p1,q1), (p2,q2), ..., (pN,qN) For pair (pi, qi): all other qj in batch = in-batch negatives L = -log( exp(sim(pi,qi)/tau) / sum_j exp(sim(pi,qj)/tau) ) tau = temperature (0.05-0.1 typical) Pooling strategy (how to get one vector from token embeddings): Mean pooling: average all token embeddings -> best for semantic similarity [CLS] token: use classification token -> better for supervised classification Max pooling: take maximum per dimension -> occasionally used Bi-encoder vs Cross-encoder: Bi-encoder: encode query and document separately, compare with dot product -> O(1) per query (pre-compute all document embeddings) -> Lower quality (no joint query-document interaction) -> Use for RETRIEVAL (must handle millions of docs) Cross-encoder: process [query + document] together, output relevance score -> O(N) per query (must process each doc-query pair) -> Much higher quality (full attention between query and doc) -> Use for RERANKING (only top 20 candidates from retrieval)
Cosine similaritydot product of two normalised vectors. Range [-1, +1]. 1.0 = identical direction. 0 = unrelated. -1 = opposite. The standard similarity metric for embeddings
In-batch negativestreating all other samples in the same batch as negatives. Free: no extra data collection. Efficient: N-1 negatives per positive at no extra cost
Temperature (tau)controls how peaked the softmax distribution is. Lower tau = harder task (model must be more decisive). Typically 0.05-0.1 for contrastive learning
Hard negativessemantically similar but not truly equivalent examples. Much more informative than random negatives. Mining them: use current model to find near-misses, label them as hard negatives, retrain
Matryoshka embeddingsOpenAI text-embedding-3-large produces 3072-dim vectors that can be truncated to 256 dims with only ~5% quality loss. Nested representations at multiple scales, like Russian dolls
Key insight: The bi-encoder vs cross-encoder distinction is fundamental to production RAG systems. You cannot use a cross-encoder for retrieval (too slow against millions of documents). You cannot use a bi-encoder alone for final ranking (too imprecise). The solution: two-stage bi-encoder retrieves top-20, cross-encoder re-ranks to top-5. Best of both.
Remember it as: Bi-encoder = two independent descriptions, compared by distance. Cross-encoder = two people in a room talking to each other, producing a joint assessment. Cross-encoder is smarter but can only be used for small candidate sets.
NLP Task Reference
TaskModel ArchitectureTraining ObjectiveKey MetricProduction Stack
Text ClassificationBERT + [CLS] linear headCE loss on class labelsF1 (macro), PR-AUC for imbalancedDistilBERT for speed, SetFit for few-shot, LLM for zero-shot
Named Entity RecognitionBERT + token classification head (BIO tags)CE per tokenEntity-level F1 (exact span match)spaCy, GLiNER (arbitrary types), fine-tuned BERT
Extractive QABERT + start/end position headsCE on start+end token indicesExact Match, F1 (SQuAD style)haystack, fine-tuned BERT on domain data
Semantic Similarity / STSSBERT bi-encoderContrastive / MNR lossSpearman correlation with human scoressentence-transformers, cosine similarity
Machine TranslationEncoder-decoder transformerCross-entropy on target tokensBLEU, ChrF, COMETNLLB-200 (Meta, 200 languages), DeepL API
SummarizationEncoder-decoder (BART, T5)CE on summary tokensROUGE-L (recall-oriented)Hierarchical for long docs: summarize sections then combine
Production CV Patterns & NLP Evaluation
Image Classification Pipeline
Python ViT fine-tuning with HuggingFace
from transformers import ViTForImageClassification, ViTImageProcessor from torch.utils.data import DataLoader import torch processor = ViTImageProcessor.from_pretrained("google/vit-base-patch16-224") model = ViTForImageClassification.from_pretrained( "google/vit-base-patch16-224", num_labels=10, ignore_mismatched_sizes=True # replace classification head ) optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=2e-5) for epoch in range(5): for images, labels in dataloader: inputs = processor(images, return_tensors="pt", padding=True) outputs = model(**inputs, labels=labels) outputs.loss.backward() optimizer.step(); optimizer.zero_grad() # Zero-shot with CLIP: no fine-tuning needed from transformers import CLIPModel, CLIPProcessor clip = CLIPModel.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32") clip_proc = CLIPProcessor.from_pretrained("openai/clip-vit-base-patch32") def zero_shot_classify(image, class_names): texts = [f"a photo of a {c}" for c in class_names] inputs = clip_proc(text=texts, images=image, return_tensors="pt") logits = clip(**inputs).logits_per_image[0] return class_names[logits.argmax().item()]
NLP Evaluation Metrics Reference
BLEU (MT)N-gram precision between generated and reference text. BLEU-4: up to 4-grams. Brevity penalty for short outputs. Range 0-100. Standard for machine translation. Correlation with human judgement: moderate.
ROUGE (Summarization)Recall-oriented. ROUGE-L: longest common subsequence. ROUGE-1/2: unigram/bigram overlap. Standard for summarization. ROUGE-L more robust to paraphrase than ROUGE-1.
BERTScoreToken-level cosine similarity using BERT embeddings. Captures semantic similarity, not just lexical. Better correlation with human judgement than BLEU/ROUGE. Slower to compute.
COMET (MT)Neural MT evaluation using source, reference, and hypothesis. Best correlation with human quality judgements. Standard in research 2023+.
Entity F1 (NER)Exact span matching: predicted entity must have exact same start/end position AND correct type. Partial matches count as wrong. Standard seqeval library.
BPE Tokenizer: Complete Implementation from Scratch
Train & Encode with BPE
Python Full BPE: train + encode + decode
from collections import Counter, defaultdict import re class BPETokenizer: def __init__(self): self.vocab = {}; self.merges = {} def _get_vocab(self, corpus): "Build initial character-level vocab from corpus." freq = Counter(corpus.split()) return {" ".join(list(w)) + " </w>": f for w, f in freq.items()} def _get_pairs(self, vocab): "Count frequency of all adjacent symbol pairs." pairs = Counter() for word, freq in vocab.items(): syms = word.split() for i in range(len(syms)-1): pairs[(syms[i], syms[i+1])] += freq return pairs def _merge_pair(self, vocab, pair): "Merge the most frequent pair in all vocab words." merged = " ".join(pair) new_token = "".join(pair) pattern = re.compile(r"(?<!\S)" + re.escape(merged) + r"(?!\S)") return {pattern.sub(new_token, w): f for w, f in vocab.items()} def train(self, corpus, num_merges=50): "Train BPE on corpus, learning num_merges merge rules." vocab = self._get_vocab(corpus) # Build initial char vocabulary self.vocab = set(s for w in vocab for s in w.split()) for i in range(num_merges): pairs = self._get_pairs(vocab) if not pairs: break best = max(pairs, key=pairs.get) # most frequent pair vocab = self._merge_pair(vocab, best) self.merges[best] = i # record merge order self.vocab.add("".join(best)) # add merged token to vocab self._vocab_dict = vocab return self def encode(self, text): "Encode text using learned merge rules." tokens = [] for word in text.split(): word_syms = list(word) + ["</w>"] # Apply merges in training order while len(word_syms) > 1: pairs = {(word_syms[i],word_syms[i+1]) for i in range(len(word_syms)-1)} best = min((p for p in pairs if p in self.merges), key=lambda p: self.merges[p], default=None) if best is None: break i = word_syms.index(best[0]) while i < len(word_syms)-1: if (word_syms[i], word_syms[i+1]) == best: word_syms[i:i+2] = ["".join(best)] else: i += 1 tokens.extend(word_syms) return tokens # Demo bpe = BPETokenizer().train("low lower lowest lower low lowest", num_merges=10) print(bpe.encode("low lower")) # e.g. ['low</w>', 'low', 'er</w>'] print(f"Vocab size: {len(bpe.vocab)}") print(f"Merge rules: {list(bpe.merges.keys())[:5]}")
Why BPE Matters for LLM Quality
  • Tokenization fertility affects reasoning: "1024" in GPT-2 tokenizer ["10","24"]. Model never sees the full number as one unit poor arithmetic. LLaMA 3's 128K vocab: most integers 0-999 are one token better number handling as direct consequence.
  • Language efficiency gap: English text: ~1.3 tokens/word. Chinese: 1-2 characters/token in small-vocab models (over-segmented). Large vocab (Qwen2.5 uses 150K): Chinese approaches English efficiency. Impacts: context window use, inference cost, and effective context length for non-English users.
  • Fill-in-the-middle (FIM) tokenization: Code models use special tokens: <|fim_prefix|>, <|fim_suffix|>, <|fim_middle|>. Enables completing code in the middle of a function, not just appending. DeepSeek-Coder, Codestral trained this way. Critical for IDE integration (Copilot, Cursor).
  • Vocabulary size tradeoff: Larger vocab fewer tokens per document less compute per document. But: larger embedding table (vocab d_model params). LLaMA 1: 32K vocab, 500M embedding params for 7B model. LLaMA 3: 128K vocab, 2B embedding params. Worth it: 4 fewer tokens per document.
Prerequisites: Deep Learning
CV vs NLP Task Map
TaskCV EquivalentNLP EquivalentCommon Model
ClassificationImage classification (cat vs dog)Sentiment analysis (positive/negative)ResNet/ViT | BERT
Detection/ExtractionObject detection (bounding boxes)Named Entity Recognition (find names)YOLO/DETR | BERT-NER
Segmentation/ParsingSemantic segmentation (per-pixel labels)Dependency parsing (sentence structure)SAM | spaCy
GenerationImage generation (DALL-E, SD)Text generation (GPT, LLaMA)Diffusion | Transformer
Translation/TransformImage-to-image (style transfer)Machine translation (ENFR)Pix2Pix | T5/NLLB
SimilarityFace verification (same person?)Semantic similarity (same meaning?)FaceNet | SBERT
Transformer Transfer Learning CV vs NLP
NLP Transfer Learning
  • Pre-train: predict masked tokens (BERT) or next token (GPT) on internet text
  • Fine-tune: add classification head, train on labelled examples
  • Data needed to fine-tune: 10010K examples
  • Pre-trained weights: huggingface.co/models
  • Frozen layers: usually keep bottom layers frozen
CV Transfer Learning
  • Pre-train: ImageNet (1M images, 1000 classes) or CLIP on image-text pairs
  • Fine-tune: replace final classification layer, train on domain images
  • Data needed: 505K images per class
  • Pre-trained weights: torchvision.models or HuggingFace ViT
  • Frozen layers: usually freeze all except last 2-3 blocks
CV vs NLP Task Mapping
Task TypeCV ExampleNLP ExampleTypical Model
ClassificationImage classification (cat/dog)Sentiment analysis (pos/neg)ViT / BERT
Detection/ExtractionObject detection (bounding boxes)Named Entity RecognitionYOLO / BERT-NER
SegmentationSemantic segmentation (pixel labels)Dependency parsingSAM / spaCy
GenerationImage generation (Stable Diffusion)Text generation (GPT/LLaMA)Diffusion / Transformer
SimilarityFace verification (same person?)Semantic similarity (same meaning?)FaceNet / SBERT
TranslationImage-to-image (style transfer)Machine translation (ENFR)Pix2Pix / NLLB
Transfer Learning: CV vs NLP
NLP Transfer Learning
  • Pre-train: predict masked tokens (BERT) or next token (GPT)
  • Fine-tune: add classification head, train on labelled examples
  • Data needed: 10010K labelled examples
  • Typically keep lower layers frozen, fine-tune top layers
CV Transfer Learning
  • Pre-train: ImageNet (1M images) or CLIP (image-text pairs)
  • Fine-tune: replace classification head, retrain on domain images
  • Data needed: 505K images per class
  • Freeze all except last 23 blocks for small datasets
🌍 Real-world use case: An automated document-processing pipeline. A vision transformer classifies each incoming scan (invoice vs. receipt vs. contract), an OCR + NLP stage extracts the key fields, and a cross-encoder ranks which clauses a human should review — clearing the routine majority automatically and routing only the ambiguous documents to people.
🌏 Go Deeper — CV & NLP
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner Stanford CS231n — Lecture Slides (free) — The standard CV curriculum; Lectures 5–9 (CNNs, training, object detection) are the most interview-relevant and can be read in one day.
  • Intermediate Hugging Face NLP Course (free, 8 chapters) — The best hands-on path from tokenization through fine-tuning BERT/GPT-style models; Chapters 1–4 cover the core architecture intuitions this tab builds on.
  • Advanced Dosovitskiy et al. — An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: ViT (2020) — The Vision Transformer paper; read Section 3 to understand patch embeddings — the unifying design that bridges this tab’s CV and NLP sections.
The Agentic Knowledge Platform (AKP) is the enterprise architecture pattern for deploying AI agents at scale with governance. It defines six planes: Surfaces, Agent Runtime, Tool Plane, Governance/Trust, Knowledge & Retrieval, Data Backbone. This tab maps every component to deep technical explanations. Expected knowledge for AI Architects and Staff AI Engineers.
What is this tab? This tab is the enterprise reference architecture for agentic systems, showing which platform plane owns each capability and where responsibility boundaries should live.
Why it matters: Without a clear plane model, teams duplicate governance, tools, memory, and runtime logic in incompatible ways that become expensive to secure and operate later.
How to use it: Read the 8-plane reference first, then use the six-plane teaching view and per-plane sections to map architecture interview answers to concrete responsibilities.
Example: If you are asked who owns approvals, audit, and tool access in an agent platform, this tab gives the architecture-level answer in one place.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

The Agentic Knowledge Platform (AKP) is a blueprint for how a big company should organise all its AI agents, tools, knowledge, and governance so they work together instead of becoming 20 disconnected experiments. Think of it as the “reference floor plan” for an enterprise AI building — the teams moving in still pick their own furniture, but the walls, plumbing, and fire exits are decided once for the whole building.

Why architects care so much: if every team builds its own agent stack, you end up with 20 versions of authentication, 20 ways to log a call, 20 different tool registries, no way to share retrieval, and no consistent answer when legal asks “who has access to what?”. AKP defines the layers (called planes) so every team builds on top of the platform rather than alongside it. This is what gets discussed in Staff and Principal AI interviews.

What this tab covers: the 6 (or 8) planes broken down one by one — Surfaces (where users hit the system), Agent Runtime (where the agent loop runs), Tool Plane (what the agent can do), Knowledge & Retrieval (what it knows), Data Backbone (where the source data lives), and Governance / Trust / Identity (rules enforced on every request). For each plane: what it owns, what it must guarantee, and how it connects to the others.

AKP — 8-Plane Reference Architecture
Canonical 8-plane “Singletons & Planes” view — the diagram used in AKP architecture reviews. This is the strict 1:1 reference; the deeper “Six Planes” teaching section below intentionally bundles Surfaces and Platform Foundation with adjacent planes to streamline narrative, but every component lives in one of the eight planes here.
PLANE 1 · GOVERNANCE / TRUST / IDENTITY
Enforced on every request
SSO/IAM · RBAC/ABAC · Tool Auth · Tenant Isolation · Data Classification · Policy (OPA) · Guardrails · PRR · SecOps · Evals · Entity & Artifact Registry · DPP · Audit & Provenance
PLANE 2 · Surfaces (AI Studio)
Rollout · Knowledge · Ops · Impact Analysis · Dev Assistant · Test Gen · Sys Explorer · Dashboards · Admin · Observability · APIs
PLANE 3 · Agent Runtime Plans › Reasons › Decides
Workflow / Activity Orchestrator Reasoning / Planning Model Gateway (Bedrock / Claude) Reasoning & Decision Provenance Session & Memory Task Queue & Scheduler HITL / Approvals State & Context
PLANE 4 · Tool Plane Acts on the World
MCP Gateway · Tool Registry & Discovery · Schema-Pinned & Semantic Adapters · Connector Lifecycle · Quota & Rate Limit
PLANE 5 · Knowledge & Retrieval Grounds Answers › Verified Truth
Hybrid Retrieval (Vector + BM25 + Graph) · Query Planner · Context Enrichment · Cross-encoder Rerank · Structural & Semantic Graph (Confidence, Provenance, Temporal) · Anchored Chunks (Entity ↔ Docs) · Context Library (ADRs, Runbooks)
PLANE 6 · Data Backbone Ingestion / CDC › Storage
Stores: Graph · Vector · Search · RDBMS · Object · Streaming
Ingest: Parsers · LLM Extractors · Event Backbone (CDC · CI/CD · Git)
PLANE 7 · INTELLIGENCE & LEARNING
Observes Every Layer › Continuously Improves
Observe: Logs & Traces (OTel) · Perf & Cost Metrics · Dependency Topology · Anomaly Signals · Agent Telemetry · Model Eval  |  Learn: User Feedback / RLHF · Answer Corrections · Retrieval Quality · Workflow Outcomes · Prompt Tuning
PLANE 8 · PLATFORM FOUNDATION / ENVIRONMENTS
Runs Anywhere › At Scale
Cloud (AWS) · Kubernetes · KONG · CI/CD · IaC (Terraform) · Monitoring · DR · FinOps · DEV → QA → STAGE → PROD · Multi-Region · Data Residency · Environment-aware Configs
# Plane Job-to-be-Done Key Components
1Governance / Trust / IdentityEnforce on every requestSSO/IAM, RBAC/ABAC, Tool Auth, Tenant Isolation, Data Classification, OPA, Guardrails, PRR, SecOps, Evals, Entity & Artifact Registry, DPP, Audit & Provenance
2Surfaces (AI Studio)Where humans interactRollout, Knowledge, Ops, Impact Analysis, Dev Assistant, Test Gen, Sys Explorer, Dashboards, Admin, Observability, APIs
3Agent RuntimePlans › Reasons › DecidesWorkflow/Activity Orchestrator, Reasoning/Planning, Model Gateway (Bedrock/Claude), Reasoning & Decision Provenance, Session & Memory, Task Queue & Scheduler, HITL/Approvals, State & Context
4Tool PlaneActs on the worldMCP Gateway, Tool Registry & Discovery, Schema-Pinned & Semantic Adapters, Connector Lifecycle, Quota & Rate Limit
5Knowledge & RetrievalGrounds answers in verified truthHybrid Retrieval (Vector + BM25 + Graph), Query Planner, Context Enrichment, Cross-encoder Rerank, Structural & Semantic Graph (Confidence, Provenance, Temporal), Anchored Chunks (Entity ↔ Docs), Context Library (ADRs, Runbooks)
6Data BackboneIngestion / CDC › StorageStores: Graph, Vector, Search, RDBMS, Object, Streaming. Ingest: Parsers, LLM Extractors, Event Backbone (CDC, CI/CD, Git)
7Intelligence & LearningObserves › Continuously improvesObserve: Logs & Traces (OTel), Perf & Cost Metrics, Dependency Topology, Anomaly Signals, Agent Telemetry, Model Eval. Learn: User Feedback/RLHF, Answer Corrections, Retrieval Quality, Workflow Outcomes, Prompt Tuning
8Platform Foundation / EnvironmentsRuns anywhere › At scaleCloud (AWS), Kubernetes, KONG, CI/CD, IaC (Terraform), Monitoring, DR, FinOps, DEV → QA → STAGE → PROD, Multi-Region, Data Residency, Environment-aware Configs
Bridging note: The teaching sections below collapse Planes 2 + 8 into a “surfaces & foundation” frame and weave Plane 7 (Intelligence & Learning) into each plane it observes — this is intentional for narrative flow. When reviewing the architecture formally, use the 8-plane table above as the source of truth.
AKP — Six Planes Architecture
GOVERNANCE · TRUST · IDENTITY
SSO/IAM · RBAC/ABAC · Tool Auth · Tenant Isolation · Data Classification · OPA Policies · Guardrails · PRR · SecOps · Evals · Entity & Artifact Registry · Audit & Provenance · DPP
💥 Surfaces
AI Studio · Rollout · Knowledge · Ops · Impact Analysis · Dev Assistant · Test Gen · Sys Explorer · Dashboards · APIs
🧠 Agent Runtime (Plans Reasons Decides)
Workflow Orchestrator Model Gateway Reasoning Engine Decision Provenance Session & Memory Task Queue HITL / Approvals State & Context
🔧 Tool Plane (Acts on World)
MCP Gateway · Tool Registry & Discovery · Schema-Pinned & Semantic Adapters · Connector Lifecycle · Quota & Rate Limit
🔍 Knowledge & Retrieval (Grounds Answers)
Hybrid Retrieval (Vector + BM25 + Graph) · Query Planner · Context Enrichment · Cross-encoder Rerank · Knowledge Graph (Structural + Semantic) · Anchored Chunks · Context Library (ADRs, Runbooks)
💾 Data Backbone (Storage)
Graph · Vector · Search · RDBMS · Object · Streaming · Parsers · LLM Extractors · Event Backbone (CDC · CI/CD · Git)
📈 Intelligence & Learning (Observes All Layers, Continuously Improves)
OTel Traces · Perf & Cost Metrics · Dependency Topology · Anomaly Signals · Agent Telemetry · Model Eval · User Feedback/RLHF · Answer Corrections · Retrieval Quality · Workflow Outcomes · Prompt Tuning
Platform Foundation: Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP) · Kubernetes · KONG · CI/CD · IaC (Terraform) · Monitoring · DR · FinOps · DEV → QA → STAGE → PROD · Multi-Region · Data Residency · Environment-aware Configs
Agent Runtime Request Flow
User Request
via Surfaces API
→
API Gateway
Auth + Rate Limit
→
Model Gateway
Route + Cache + Cost
→
LLM Reasoning
Plan + Decide
→
Tool Plane
MCP + OPA check
→
Response
+ Provenance
Governance enforced on every arrow: Every hop through the flow checks: Is this request authorised? Does data classification allow this? Has the budget been exceeded? Is the action reversible? The governance plane is not a wrapper it intercepts every single request.
AKP Six-Plane Architecture
Plane 1: Agent Runtime
  • Workflow/Activity Orchestrator: Multi-step workflow state machine. Temporal/Durable Execution for crash-safe, resumable workflows. Checkpoint state periodically. LangGraph for graph-based control flow.
  • Model Gateway: Single entry point for ALL LLM calls. Route by: data classification (CONFIDENTIAL on-prem only), complexity score, cost budget. Fallback chain: GPT-4o GPT-4o-mini local LLaMA. Semantic cache check first. LiteLLM as implementation.
  • HITL Gates: Pause workflow for human approval on: high-risk actions, low confidence (<0.85), irreversible operations. Async approval: checkpoint state, create inbox task, resume on approval. Timeout escalation after X hours.
  • Session & Memory: Session = one interaction lifecycle (Redis, ephemeral). Memory = persistent cross-session (vector DB + structured DB). State = current workflow variables. Context = what LLM sees right now. FOUR distinct concepts, must be managed separately.
Plane 2: Tool Plane (MCP)
  • MCP (Model Context Protocol): Anthropic's open standard. MCP Server: exposes tools (functions), resources (data), prompts (templates). MCP Client: agent runtime. Transport: stdio (local) or HTTP+SSE (remote). JSON-RPC 2.0. Becoming the "USB standard" for AI tool integration.
  • Tool Registry: Catalog of all tools: name, description, JSON schema, version, owner, SLA. Agent queries registry at runtime. Schema validation before every tool execution (prevents hallucinated tool calls).
  • Schema-pinned adapters: All tool calls validated against declared JSON schema BEFORE execution. Failed validation structured error agent corrects without side effects. Never execute partially-formed tool calls.
  • Quota & Rate Limit: Per-tool quota (max N calls per workflow). Per-tenant budget (max $X/hour). Rate limiting to respect upstream API limits. Budget alerts before exhaustion.
Plane 3: Governance / Trust
  • OPA (Open Policy Agent): Rego policies evaluated before every tool call or write operation. Fail-closed: if OPA unavailable deny all writes. Policy as code: version controlled, PR-reviewed. Example: deny write if caller_maturity_level < L3.
  • RBAC + ABAC: RBAC for broad role-based permissions. ABAC (attribute-based) for context-sensitive: user.department=Finance AND resource.classification=FINANCIAL AND time.hour<18. Use ABAC when RBAC generates thousands of roles.
  • Tenant Isolation: Vector DB: namespace per tenant. SQL: row-level security. LLM API: separate keys for billing. Cache: key = tenant_id + query_hash. Always test for cross-tenant data leakage before each release.
  • Audit & Provenance: Immutable append-only audit log: timestamp, session_id, agent_id, tenant_id, action_type, input_hash, output_hash, decision_rationale. Stored in write-once S3. Every output traceable to source data and reasoning. Required for EU AI Act compliance.
Model Gateway Implementation
Python Enterprise Model Gateway
import litellm from litellm import acompletion from enum import Enum class DataClass(Enum): PUBLIC = "public" INTERNAL = "internal" CONFIDENTIAL = "confidential" class ModelGateway: MODEL_ROUTING = { DataClass.CONFIDENTIAL: "self-hosted/llama-3.1-70b", # never leaves perimeter DataClass.INTERNAL: "gpt-4o", DataClass.PUBLIC: "gpt-4o-mini", # default: cheapest } FALLBACK = {"gpt-4o": "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022", "gpt-4o-mini": "gpt-4o"} async def call(self, messages, data_class=DataClass.INTERNAL, complexity=0.5, tenant_id="default"): # 1. Check semantic cache cached = await self.cache.get(messages) if cached: return cached # 2. Route by data classification model = self.MODEL_ROUTING[data_class] # 3. Override for complex queries if complexity > 0.7 and data_class != DataClass.CONFIDENTIAL: model = "gpt-4o" # 4. Budget check if await self.budget.exceeded(tenant_id): model = self.MODEL_ROUTING[DataClass.PUBLIC] # degrade gracefully try: resp = await acompletion(model=model, messages=messages) except Exception: resp = await acompletion(model=self.FALLBACK[model], messages=messages) await self.budget.record(tenant_id, resp.usage) await self.cache.set(messages, resp) await self.provenance.log(model=model, tenant=tenant_id, usage=resp.usage) return resp
Data Backbone & Intelligence Loop
  • Polyglot persistence: Vector DB (Qdrant): embedding similarity. Graph DB (Neo4j): entity relationships, multi-hop. RDBMS (PostgreSQL): structured metadata, audit. Object storage (S3): raw docs, artifacts. Streaming (Kafka): real-time events. No single DB handles all these well.
  • Event Backbone (CDC): Change Data Capture: detect changes in source systems (Confluence, Salesforce, SAP). Publish to Kafka. Knowledge base always reflects current state. Debezium for RDBMS CDC.
  • OTel for AI: gen_ai.* semantic conventions. gen_ai.usage.input_tokens, gen_ai.response.finish_reason. Each agent step = one span. Tool call = child span. Full distributed trace across all 6 planes. Backends: Jaeger, Grafana Tempo, Datadog.
  • Feedback loop: User feedback (thumbs down) DPO preference pairs. Answer corrections RAG eval dataset. Retrieval quality embedding fine-tuning data. Workflow outcomes prompt optimization. This is the most valuable long-term asset of an agentic platform.
Model Gateway: Full Implementation & OTel Instrumentation
Complete Model Gateway with Tracing
Python Enterprise Model Gateway with OTel
from opentelemetry import trace from opentelemetry.semconv.ai import SpanAttributes # gen_ai.* import litellm, time tracer = trace.get_tracer("model-gateway") class ModelGateway: ROUTING = {"CONFIDENTIAL": "self-hosted/llama-3.1-70b", "INTERNAL": "gpt-4o", "PUBLIC": "gpt-4o-mini"} FALLBACK = {"gpt-4o": "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022", "gpt-4o-mini": "gpt-4o"} async def call(self, messages, data_class="INTERNAL", tenant_id="default", complexity=0.5): model = self.ROUTING[data_class] if complexity < 0.3 and data_class != "CONFIDENTIAL": model = "gpt-4o-mini" # downgrade simple queries with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm.call") as span: # Set gen_ai.* semantic convention attributes span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.LLM_SYSTEM, "openai") span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.LLM_REQUEST_MODEL, model) span.set_attribute("tenant_id", tenant_id) span.set_attribute("data_class", data_class) span.set_attribute("complexity", complexity) t0 = time.time() try: resp = await litellm.acompletion( model=model, messages=messages) except Exception: resp = await litellm.acompletion( model=self.FALLBACK.get(model, "gpt-4o-mini"), messages=messages) span.set_attribute("fallback_used", True) latency = time.time() - t0 span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.LLM_USAGE_PROMPT_TOKENS, resp.usage.prompt_tokens) span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.LLM_USAGE_COMPLETION_TOKENS, resp.usage.completion_tokens) span.set_attribute("latency_ms", round(latency*1000)) return resp
AKP Knowledge & Retrieval Plane
  • Query Planner: Before retrieval, decompose complex queries. "What are Company A's delivery SLAs and how do they compare across regions?" sub-query 1: "Company A delivery SLAs region 1", sub-query 2: "Company A delivery SLAs region 2". Execute in parallel. Merge results with deduplication. Powered by a lightweight LLM call with structured output.
  • Context Enrichment: After retrieval, enrich each chunk: add source metadata (document_id, section, date), authority score (official doc vs forum post), recency score, entity links (this "Tenant A" org_id:0001 in graph DB). Enrichment makes context more useful to LLM explicit attribution instead of implicit assumptions.
  • Anchored Chunks: Every chunk linked to the entities it mentions in the knowledge graph. Entity-centric retrieval: "find all chunks mentioning Tenant A" graph traversal returns entity get all anchored chunks. Multi-hop: Tenant A {systems: [System X, System Y, System Z]} retrieve configuration chunks for each system. Standard vector RAG can't do this.
  • Context Library (ADRs + Runbooks): Stable reference documents injected at AGENT START, not per-query. Architecture Decision Records explain WHY decisions were made. Runbooks explain HOW to handle operational situations. These are the agent's "reference manual" always available, never need to be retrieved because they're always relevant for the domain.
Prerequisites: Agents & Prompting → System Design
AKP 6-Plane Architecture Visual Breakdown
Plane 1: Model Gateway
Single entry point for all LLM calls. Routes to: GPT-4o (general), Claude (reasoning), Gemini (multimodal), local models (private data). Implements: rate limiting per tenant, cost attribution, circuit breakers for model outages. All calls pass through here no direct model API calls from apps.
Plane 2: Orchestration (Agentic)
LangGraph-based agent execution engine. Manages: conversation state, tool dispatch, HITL approval gates, agent memory. Each agent run is a graph: nodes=steps, edges=transitions, state=shared dict. Persists state in Redis for multi-turn workflows.
Plane 3: Knowledge (RAG)
Vector DB (pgvector or Qdrant) + sparse search (Elasticsearch BM25) + cross-encoder re-ranker. Serves: document Q&A, product knowledge, policy lookup. Hybrid retrieval: BM25 for keywords, dense for semantics, RRF to merge scores.
Plane 4: Data Privacy
PII detection at ingestion (presidio). Data classification: public/internal/confidential/restricted. Prevents restricted data entering LLM context. Masks PII in logs. GDPR: right-to-erasure hook deletes embeddings when user data is deleted.
Plane 5: Observability
OpenTelemetry gen_ai.* semantic conventions. Traces: every LLM call with tokens_in, tokens_out, model, latency, cost. Dashboards: cost/team, latency p99, faithfulness score, safety flag rate. Alerts: cost spike, faithfulness drop, error rate spike.
Plane 6: Governance
OPA policy enforcement: what can each team/role call. Artifact Registry: versioned prompts with eval scores. PRR (Production Readiness Review) gate: 10-point checklist before any agent goes live. Audit log: every LLM call logged for compliance.
MCP Gateway Tool Registry Pattern
Without MCP Gateway
  • Each agent hardcodes its own tool implementations
  • Duplicate auth logic across 20+ agents
  • No visibility into tool usage or costs
  • Security nightmare: each agent manages its own secrets
  • Tool changes require updating every agent
With MCP Gateway
  • Agents call tools via named registry: "search_products", "get_order"
  • Gateway handles auth, rate limiting, caching
  • Full audit trail: which agent called which tool when
  • Tool implementations updated in one place
  • New tools immediately available to all agents
AKP 6-Plane Architecture
Plane 1: Model Gateway
Single entry point for all LLM calls. Routes by model type, cost, latency SLA. Rate limiting per tenant. Cost attribution. Circuit breakers. All apps call gateway never raw model APIs.
Plane 2: Orchestration (Agentic)
LangGraph-based agent execution. Manages: conversation state, tool dispatch, HITL gates, agent memory. State persisted in Redis for multi-turn workflows.
Plane 3: Knowledge (RAG)
Vector DB + sparse BM25 + re-ranker. Serves document Q&A, product knowledge, policy lookup. Hybrid retrieval merges dense and sparse scores via RRF.
Plane 4: Data Privacy
PII detection at ingestion (presidio). Data classification (public/internal/confidential/restricted). Prevents restricted data entering LLM context. GDPR erasure hook.
Plane 5: Observability
OpenTelemetry gen_ai.* conventions. Traces every LLM call with tokens_in/out, model, latency, cost. Dashboards: cost/team, p99 latency, faithfulness, safety flag rate.
Plane 6: Governance
OPA policy enforcement. Artifact Registry with versioned prompts + eval scores. PRR gate (10-point checklist) before any agent goes live. Audit log for compliance.
MCP Gateway vs Direct Tool Call
Without MCP Gateway
  • Each agent hardcodes its own tool logic
  • Duplicate auth, rate limiting, caching across agents
  • No central audit of tool usage
  • Tool changes require updating every agent
With MCP Gateway
  • Agents call named tools via registry
  • Gateway handles auth, rate limiting, caching centrally
  • Full audit: which agent called which tool when
  • One update propagates to all agents
AKP vs Traditional API Architecture
Traditional API (Before AKP)
  • Direct LLM calls from each microservice
  • No unified observability or cost tracking
  • Security policies enforced inconsistently
  • Prompt versions ungoverned, drift undetected
AKP (After)
  • All LLM calls via Model Gateway single control plane
  • Every call traced: tokens, cost, latency, safety flags
  • OPA policy enforced uniformly at gateway
  • Prompt Registry with eval scores and approval gates
AKP Component Responsibilities
ComponentResponsibilityTechnologyKey Metric
Model GatewayRoute, rate-limit, cost-attribute all LLM callsLiteLLM + custom routerp99 routing latency <20ms
Orchestration EngineStateful agent execution, tool dispatch, HITLLangGraph + RedisTask completion rate >95%
Knowledge PlaneHybrid retrieval (dense+sparse), re-rankingpgvector / Qdrant + ESFaithfulness >0.85
Data Privacy PlanePII detection, data classification, GDPR erasurePresidio + OPAPII leak rate = 0
ObservabilityTrace every LLM call, cost/latency dashboardsOpenTelemetry + Grafana100% call coverage
GovernancePrompt registry, PRR gate, audit logOPA + PostgreSQL0 unapproved agents in prod
What is Agent Core?
Definition: Agent Core is the tightly-coupled nucleus of the Agent Runtime plane — the combination of the Reasoning Engine, Workflow Orchestrator, and State & Context Manager that executes the plan → act → observe loop for a single agent instance.

Why it matters: Without a clearly bounded Agent Core, teams add capabilities (memory, tools, guardrails) by bolting them onto the reasoning loop rather than wiring them into their correct planes. The result is an agent that works in isolation but becomes untraceable, non-resumable, and impossible to govern at scale. Naming the Core explicitly is what lets the architecture conversation graduate from “how does our agent work?” to “which plane owns this failure?”

Analogy: Think of a hospital ICU. The ICU has a cardiac monitor (Observability), a drugs cabinet (Tool Plane), a patient record system (Knowledge Plane), and an administrator verifying insurance (Governance). The Agent Core is the attending physician: the only component that synthesises all these inputs into a decision and takes the next action. Every other plane supports the physician — but the physician is the loop. You can swap the drug cabinet supplier without retraining the physician. Same principle here.

Where it lives in the AKP diagram: Agent Core sits inside Plane 3 — Agent Runtime (the purple centre column in the 6-plane view). It is not a separate plane; it is the active heart of that plane. The five surrounding planes are dependencies: Agent Core calls the Tool Plane; Governance intercepts that call. Agent Core reads from the Knowledge Plane; Knowledge Plane satisfies that read. Nothing calls Agent Core except the Surfaces plane — it is always the initiator, never the receiver.
Agent Core — Minimal LangGraph Config
Python — Agent Core loop (LangGraph)
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END from typing import TypedDict class AgentState(TypedDict): messages: list # conversation history (State & Context) tool_calls: list # pending Tool Plane requests step_count: int # loop guard against runaway agents def reason(state: AgentState) -> AgentState: # Agent Core proper: call Model Gateway, decide next action response = model_gateway.call(state["messages"]) state["tool_calls"] = response.tool_calls state["step_count"] += 1 return state def act(state: AgentState) -> AgentState: # Calls out to Tool Plane (MCP); Governance intercepts here results = tool_plane.execute(state["tool_calls"]) state["messages"].append({"role": "tool", "content": results}) state["tool_calls"] = [] return state def should_continue(state: AgentState) -> str: if state["tool_calls"]: return "act" if state["step_count"] >= 10: return END # runaway guard return END graph = StateGraph(AgentState) graph.add_node("reason", reason) graph.add_node("act", act) graph.add_edge("act", "reason") # act always returns to reason graph.add_conditional_edges("reason", should_continue) graph.set_entry_point("reason") agent_core = graph.compile() # wrap with memory + guardrails outside
Agent Core vs Agent Runtime — the distinction
  • Agent Runtime (Plane 3): the full plane, including Workflow Orchestrator, Model Gateway, HITL/Approvals, Task Queue, Session & Memory, State & Context, Decision Provenance. It is what the platform team builds and operates.
  • Agent Core: the subset that executes the reasoning loop itself — Reasoning Engine + Workflow Orchestrator + State & Context. It is what the application team configures and extends. The boundary matters because memory and HITL live in the Runtime plane but are called by the Core, not part of its loop logic.
  • In interviews: when asked “what is the most critical component in an agentic platform?” the correct answer is not “the LLM” — it is the Agent Core, because it is the only component that sees every input and every decision. If it is untraceable, the whole system is untraceable.
  • Key insight: The Agent Core has exactly one responsibility — decide the next action given current state. Tool execution, memory reads, and governance checks all happen outside the Core. An Agent Core that does its own tool calls, its own memory writes, and its own auth is an anti-pattern with no clear failure surface.
🌍 Real-world use case: An enterprise rolling out internal agents across teams. The governance plane enforces data classification (confidential queries route only to the self-hosted model), the tool plane exposes 50+ internal APIs through one MCP layer, and the observability plane streams traces to a dashboard — so every team builds on shared rails instead of re-implementing auth, tools, and audit each time.
🌏 Go Deeper — Agentic Platform (AKP)
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner Model Context Protocol — Introduction (official) — MCP is the tool integration standard AKP’s Tool Plane is built on; read this 10-minute intro before reviewing the MCP Gateway section in this tab.
  • Intermediate LangGraph — Concepts (official docs) — Read the State, Nodes, Edges, and Persistence pages; these map directly to the Agent Runtime plane components (Workflow Orchestrator, Session & Memory, State & Context) described in this tab.
  • Intermediate Open Policy Agent — Documentation — OPA is the Governance plane policy engine; read the Introduction and How Does OPA Work? sections to understand how Rego policies intercept tool calls at runtime.
  • Advanced OpenTelemetry GenAI Semantic Conventions (official spec) — The exact span attributes used by the AKP Observability plane; read to understand what gen_ai.* spans must capture for every LLM call and tool invocation.
Recommendation Systems, Time Series, and Tabular ML are the workhorses of most production ML deployments. Many ML Engineer roles at Netflix, Spotify, TikTok, Amazon focus primarily on these. This tab covers them with full depth including math and working code.
What is this tab? This tab is the production-ML field guide for recommenders, forecasting, and tabular systems, which are still the highest-volume workloads in many real ML teams.
Why it matters: Even teams building LLM features are often judged on recommender quality, ranking design, and time-series intuition because those systems drive direct revenue and retention.
How to use it: Start with two-tower retrieval and matrix factorization, then move to forecasting and tabular sections if your interview or role leans toward applied ML systems.
Example: If you need to explain how Netflix retrieves candidates before ranking them, this tab gives you the retrieval, ranking, and cold-start story end to end.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

Recommender systems are the engines behind every “for you” feed — Netflix's next show, Spotify's next song, Amazon's next product, TikTok's next video. They are the workhorse of most ML teams at consumer companies. Even though they sound simple, getting them right is a billion-dollar problem — small accuracy gains move huge revenue.

The basic idea in plain words: turn each user into a list of numbers (an embedding) that captures their taste, and turn each item (movie, song, product) into a list of numbers too. Recommend the items whose numbers are closest to the user's. The clever part is what goes into those numbers and how you update them every time a user clicks, skips, watches, or buys.

What this tab covers: two-tower models (one neural network for users, one for items, trained so matching pairs end up with close numbers), matrix factorisation (the classic algorithm that still works surprisingly well), the “cold start” problem (what to recommend to a brand-new user with no history), and the related fields of time-series forecasting and tabular ML — because the same teams usually own all three at most companies.

Recommendation Systems: Two-Tower + Matrix Factorization
Two-Tower Model: Math & Training
Two-tower models learn separate embeddings for users and items so you can pre-compute item embeddings and find relevant items instantly at query time.
Two-Tower Architecture: user_emb = UserTower(user_features) -> d-dimensional vector item_emb = ItemTower(item_features) -> d-dimensional vector score(user, item) = dot(user_emb, item_emb) / temperature Training with InfoNCE (in-batch negatives): Batch: N (user, interacted_item) positive pairs For pair (ui, ii): all other items ij in batch = negatives L = -sum_i log( exp(score(ui,ii)/tau) / sum_j exp(score(ui,ij)/tau) ) Key engineering decisions: Hard negatives: items user did NOT interact with but model scores highly -> Much more informative than random negatives -> Mix 80% in-batch + 20% hard negatives for best results Serving at scale: Offline: compute item embeddings for entire catalog -> build HNSW index Online: compute user embedding from real-time features -> ANN search: user_emb -> top-500 candidate items -> Total latency: 10-30ms for billions of items Full cascade (production standard at Netflix/YouTube/TikTok): 1. Two-tower retrieval: all items -> 500 candidates 2. Neural ranker (DLRM): 500 -> 20 3. Re-ranking: diversity, freshness, business rules -> final list
UserTowerneural network that transforms user features (history, demographics, context) to a d-dimensional embedding vector
ItemTowerneural network that transforms item features (title, category, metadata) to the same d-dimensional embedding space
Dot product scorecosine or inner product similarity between user and item embeddings. High score = this item is relevant for this user
Temperature (tau)controls how sharply the model discriminates. Low tau = harder task = better learned representations but harder training
Hard negativesitems similar to the positive but not interacted with. Random negatives are too easy model learns little from them. Hard negatives force learning finer distinctions
Key insight: The key insight of two-tower models: because user and item towers are independent, you can pre-compute ALL item embeddings offline. At serving time, you only need to compute the user embedding (fast, just the user tower) and do ANN search. This makes real-time recommendation over billions of items feasible.
Remember it as: Two-tower = speed dating. Each person (user) and each potential match (item) independently describes themselves. The matchmaker (dot product) finds who is most compatible. Since items already described themselves beforehand, matching is instant.
PyTorch Two-Tower model implementation
import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F class TwoTower(nn.Module): def __init__(self, user_feat_dim, item_feat_dim, emb_dim=128): super().__init__() self.user_tower = nn.Sequential( nn.Linear(user_feat_dim, 256), nn.ReLU(), nn.LayerNorm(256), nn.Linear(256, emb_dim)) self.item_tower = nn.Sequential( nn.Linear(item_feat_dim, 256), nn.ReLU(), nn.LayerNorm(256), nn.Linear(256, emb_dim)) self.temperature = nn.Parameter(torch.tensor(0.07)) def forward(self, user_feats, item_feats): u = F.normalize(self.user_tower(user_feats), dim=-1) v = F.normalize(self.item_tower(item_feats), dim=-1) return u, v def infonce_loss(self, u, v): # u: (B, emb), v: (B, emb) each row is a positive pair logits = (u @ v.T) / self.temperature.exp() # (B, B) labels = torch.arange(len(u), device=u.device) # CE: diagonal = positive, off-diagonal = in-batch negatives loss_u = F.cross_entropy(logits, labels) loss_v = F.cross_entropy(logits.T, labels) return (loss_u + loss_v) / 2
Matrix Factorization & ALS
Collaborative filtering recommends items by finding users with similar taste, without needing to know anything about what the items are.
Matrix factorisation approach: Rating matrix R (n_users x n_items) ~= U x V^T U: user latent factors (n_users x k) V: item latent factors (n_items x k) Predicted rating: r_hat(u, i) = U[u] . V[i] (dot product) ALS (Alternating Least Squares) for implicit feedback: Confidence: c_ui = 1 + alpha * r_ui (r_ui = interaction count) Optimise: min ||C * (P - U*V^T)||^2_F + lambda*(||U||^2 + ||V||^2) Alternating step (why 'alternating'): Fix V, solve for each u_i independently -> closed-form solution u_i = (V^T * C^u * V + lambda*I)^-1 * V^T * C^u * p^u Fix U, solve for each v_j independently -> closed-form solution Repeat until convergence (~10-20 iterations) Cold start (the main limitation): New user: no history -> cannot factorise -> no personalised recommendations New item: no ratings -> no embedding -> not recommended Fix: content-based features for new users/items (two-tower helps here)
k (latent factors)number of hidden dimensions capturing user/item characteristics. Typical: 50-200. More = richer representation but more compute
r_ui (interaction)implicit feedback: number of clicks, purchases, play counts. Not explicit ratings. 0 means no interaction observed (not necessarily dislike)
Confidence c_uialpha scales how much to trust each observed interaction. Higher interaction count = higher confidence it is a true preference. Typical alpha = 40
lambda (regularisation)prevents overfitting keeps user/item embeddings from growing too large. Penalises large L2 norm of U and V matrices
ALS closed formeach user vector (or item vector) can be solved exactly in one matrix equation when the other is fixed. No gradient descent needed much faster than SGD for this problem
Key insight: ALS is the workhorse of recommendation at scale. Spark MLlib includes it, making it easy to run on distributed data. It handles implicit feedback (clicks, purchases) better than methods designed for explicit ratings (1-5 stars) which is why it's used at Netflix, Spotify, and Amazon rather than SVD which assumes explicit ratings.
Remember it as: Collaborative filtering = 'tell me who your friends are and I'll tell you what you like'. Users who liked the same items in the past will likely like the same items in the future. Matrix factorisation extracts the hidden 'taste dimensions' that explain those patterns.
  • Cold start solutions: New user: content-based from profile (age, location, explicit preferences). New item: embed from metadata immediately (text, image) no ratings needed. Two-tower handles both natively.
  • SASRec (sequential): User's interaction history as token sequence. Causal self-attention predicts next item. Captures temporal patterns. Best for session-based recommendation.
Time Series Forecasting
Classical + Deep Learning Methods
ARIMA models time series as a combination of past values (AR), past errors (MA), and differencing to remove trends (I).
ARIMA(p, d, q) components: AR(p) AutoRegressive: y_t = phi_1*y_{t-1} + phi_2*y_{t-2} + ... + phi_p*y_{t-p} + epsilon_t 'Today's value depends on the last p values' phi: how strongly each lagged value influences the current one I(d) Integrated (differencing): delta_y_t = y_t - y_{t-1} (1st difference) Apply d times to make series stationary (constant mean and variance) Test stationarity: ADF test (p < 0.05 means stationary, proceed) MA(q) Moving Average: y_t = epsilon_t + theta_1*epsilon_{t-1} + ... + theta_q*epsilon_{t-q} 'Today's value depends on the last q forecast errors' Captures shock effects that decay over q periods Choosing p, q: ACF plot (autocorrelation): shows MA(q) order -> cuts off after lag q PACF plot (partial autocorrelation): shows AR(p) order -> cuts off after lag p SARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q,s): adds seasonal components s = seasonal period (12 for monthly, 7 for weekly)
p (AR order)how many past values predict the current value. ACF/PACF analysis determines this
d (differencing order)how many times to difference the series to achieve stationarity. Usually 0, 1, or 2
q (MA order)how many past errors are included. Captures residual serial correlation after AR terms are included
Stationarityseries has constant mean, variance, and autocorrelation over time. Required for ARIMA. ADF test checks for unit root (non-stationarity)
phi (AR coefficients)weight of each lagged value. phi_1=0.9 means today is 90% last period's value plus noise
theta (MA coefficients)weight of each lagged error. theta_1=0.5 means today includes 50% of last period's forecast error
Key insight: ARIMA is the standard benchmark for univariate time series. Prophet (Meta) wraps ARIMA concepts in an easier interface with automatic seasonality detection. For complex multi-variate forecasting or non-linear patterns: Temporal Fusion Transformer or N-BEATS typically outperform ARIMA.
Remember it as: ARIMA = describe tomorrow's value as a weighted sum of yesterday's values (AR) plus a weighted sum of recent forecast mistakes (MA). The differencing (I) removes the trend so the series behaves consistently over time.
Python TFT-inspired temporal model skeleton
from pytorch_forecasting import TemporalFusionTransformer from pytorch_forecasting.data import TimeSeriesDataSet dataset = TimeSeriesDataSet( data, time_idx="time_idx", target="sales", group_ids=["store_id", "item_id"], max_encoder_length=52, # 52 weeks history max_prediction_length=13, # 13 weeks forecast static_categoricals=["store_type"], time_varying_known_reals=["price", "promotion"], time_varying_unknown_reals=["sales"], ) model = TemporalFusionTransformer.from_dataset( dataset, hidden_size=64, attention_head_size=4, dropout=0.1, hidden_continuous_size=16, loss=QuantileLoss(quantiles=[0.1, 0.5, 0.9]), # prediction intervals learning_rate=3e-3, )
Tabular ML Best Practices
XGBoost improves gradient boosting by adding regularisation to the tree structure itself penalising both the number of leaves and the size of leaf values.
XGBoost objective: Obj = sum_i L(y_i, y_hat_i) + Omega(tree) Omega(tree) = gamma*T + (lambda/2)*sum_j w_j^2 T = number of leaves, w_j = weight (prediction) at leaf j Second-order Taylor expansion (why XGBoost is fast and precise): L(y_i, y_hat + f(x)) ~= L(y_i, y_hat) + g_i*f(x) + (1/2)*h_i*f(x)^2 g_i = dL/dy_hat (first derivative = gradient) h_i = d^2L/dy_hat^2 (second derivative = Hessian) Optimal leaf weight (closed form): w_j* = -G_j / (H_j + lambda) G_j = sum of gradients in leaf j H_j = sum of Hessians in leaf j Optimal split gain: Gain = (1/2) * [ G_L^2/(H_L+lambda) + G_R^2/(H_R+lambda) - G^2/(H+lambda) ] - gamma Split only if Gain > 0 (otherwise: pruning) LightGBM vs XGBoost: XGBoost: level-wise (BFS) tree growth -> more balanced trees LightGBM: leaf-wise (DFS) growth -> faster, can overfit small data GOSS: LightGBM samples only high-gradient instances -> up to 10x faster
Omega(tree)regularisation term on the tree structure itself. Penalises having too many leaves (T) and having very large leaf weights. Prevents overfitting
gammaminimum gain required to split a node. Acts as tree pruning. Higher gamma = fewer, shallower trees
lambdaL2 regularisation on leaf weights. Shrinks leaf predictions toward zero. Prevents extreme values at leaves
g_i (gradient)how wrong the current prediction is for sample i direction to improve. Same as in standard gradient boosting
h_i (Hessian)second derivative of loss curvature of the loss function at this point. Used by XGBoost to weight samples; allows more precise optimal leaf weights
Gain formulaimprovement in objective from making this split. If negative: the split makes things worse (overfitting) -> prune. gamma controls the threshold
Key insight: The second-order Taylor approximation is XGBoost's key advantage over vanilla gradient boosting. Using the Hessian (second derivative) alongside the gradient provides curvature information about the loss landscape, allowing more accurate optimal leaf weights and better regularisation without significantly more compute.
Remember it as: XGBoost = gradient boosting but smarter about splits. Instead of just asking 'which direction reduces error?' (gradient), it also asks 'how fast is the error changing?' (Hessian). Like using both the slope and the curvature of a hill to take better steps.
  • Feature engineering checklist: Log-transform right-skewed distributions. Target encoding for high-cardinality categorical (CatBoost handles this). Lag features for time series (t-1, t-7, t-30). Rolling statistics (mean, std of last N). Date decomposition: hour, day-of-week, is_holiday, days_since_event.
  • AutoML as baseline: AutoGluon (2024-2025 Kaggle dominant). Always run AutoGluon first if your manual model doesn't beat it, reconsider the approach. AutoGluon builds ensembles of XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, and neural nets automatically.
Deep Learning for Recommendations & Production Architecture
DLRM & Neural Ranking
DLRM is Facebook's production recommendation model it combines dense features (numbers) and sparse features (IDs) through embedding tables, then uses pairwise feature interactions.
DLRM Architecture: 1. Dense features (age, price, click rate): -> Bottom MLP: dense_features -> dense_embedding (d-dim vector) 2. Sparse features (user_id, item_id, ad_category): -> Embedding tables: each category has its own embedding table -> user_id -> lookup in user_table -> user_emb (d-dim) -> item_id -> lookup in item_table -> item_emb (d-dim) -> Each embedding: one row per category, d columns 3. Interaction layer (the key innovation): -> Concatenate dense_emb + all sparse_embs: [e1, e2, e3, ..., en] -> Compute ALL pairwise dot products: dot(ei, ej) for all i,j pairs -> interaction_features = concatenate all dot products -> This captures: 'how much does user's taste align with item's category?' 4. Top MLP: -> input: concatenate(dense_out, interaction_features) -> output: single logit -> sigmoid -> P(click) Scale at production: Embedding tables can be huge: 10M users * 128 dims * 4 bytes = 5 GB per table Multiple tables -> terabytes. Require distributed embedding across many machines.
Embedding tablea lookup table where each row is the embedding of one category. Sparse features are IDs that index into these tables. Tables can have millions of rows
Bottom MLPprocesses continuous (dense) features into an embedding of the same dimension as sparse embeddings, so they can be compared directly
Pairwise dot productsall dot products between pairs of embeddings capture how features relate to each other. dot(user_emb, item_emb) = user-item affinity. dot(user_emb, category_emb) = user-category interest
Top MLPtakes the interaction features and predicts the final probability. Two-stage: interaction extraction -> prediction
P(click)probability that the user will click/interact. Trained on binary labels (clicked=1, not clicked=0) with binary cross-entropy loss
Key insight: DLRM's interaction layer is what distinguishes it from a simple multi-layer perceptron. By explicitly computing all pairwise dot products between feature embeddings, it captures cross-feature interactions (user item, user category, item context) that a simple concatenation would miss.
Remember it as: DLRM = bring all your features to the same language (embeddings), then have every feature 'talk to' every other feature (pairwise dot products), then make a decision (MLP) based on all those conversations.
Python DLRM simplified
import torch, torch.nn as nn class DLRM(nn.Module): def __init__(self, dense_dim, emb_sizes, mlp_dims=[256,128,64]): super().__init__() self.emb_tables = nn.ModuleList([ nn.Embedding(vocab_size, emb_dim) for vocab_size, emb_dim in emb_sizes]) # Bottom MLP for dense features layers = [] in_d = dense_dim for out_d in mlp_dims: layers.extend([nn.Linear(in_d, out_d), nn.ReLU()]) in_d = out_d self.bottom_mlp = nn.Sequential(*layers) # Top MLP: dense_out + all pairwise interactions n_emb = len(emb_sizes) n_interactions = n_emb*(n_emb-1)//2 + n_emb * mlp_dims[-1] self.top_mlp = nn.Linear(n_interactions, 1) def forward(self, dense, sparse_ids): dense_out = self.bottom_mlp(dense) # (B, d) embs = [t(ids) for t, ids in zip(self.emb_tables, sparse_ids)] all_embs = embs + [dense_out] # Pairwise dot products (interaction features) interactions = [] for i in range(len(all_embs)): for j in range(i+1, len(all_embs)): interactions.append((all_embs[i]*all_embs[j]).sum(-1, keepdim=True)) x = torch.cat(interactions + [dense_out], dim=-1) return torch.sigmoid(self.top_mlp(x))
Production RecSys Architecture
Recall (billions 500)
Two-tower ANN. Matrix factorization. Rule-based (trending, new). Pre-computed item embeddings. <10ms SLA. Deployed on GPU with HNSW index.
Ranking (500 20)
DLRM or GBM ranker. Feature-rich. User item cross features. Session context. Target: watch completion, click, purchase. 50-100ms SLA.
Re-ranking (20 final)
Diversity (max intra-list similarity). Freshness boost. Safety filtering. Business rules (promoted slots). A/B test layer.
Feedback Loop
Explicit (rating, like, dislike). Implicit (watch %, skip, replay). Delayed labels (purchase after browse). Online learning for rapid adaptation.
Prerequisites: ML Fundamentals
Recommendation System Production Cascade Pipeline
📁 Stage 1: Candidate Retrieval (Billions → ~500 candidates, <20ms)
Two-tower model: Pre-compute item embeddings offline (HNSW index). At query time: compute user embedding only, ANN search. Also: Matrix factorisation (ALS) for collaborative patterns. Rule-based: trending, continue-watching, new releases. Merge all candidate sources, deduplicate to ~500.
📊 Stage 2: Scoring / Ranking (500 → 20, ~50ms)
Feature-rich neural ranker (DLRM): User features (history, demographics, device), item features (metadata, popularity, freshness), cross features (has user bought from this category? this creator?). Target: watch completion + purchase, NOT just click (CTR optimisation = clickbait).
🎯 Stage 3: Re-ranking (20 → Final list, <5ms)
Diversity: Max intra-list similarity (don't show all items from same creator). Freshness: Boost recently uploaded items. Business rules: Promoted slots, safety filtering, regulatory requirements. Serendipity: Inject some items outside user's usual patterns.
↺ Feedback Loop (continuous)
Explicit: Ratings, likes, skips. Implicit: Watch completion %, click, purchase. Delayed labels: Chargeback, return, complaint (24-72h after interaction). All feed back into feature store and periodic model retraining.
Two-Tower vs DLRM Retrieval vs Ranking
Two-Tower (Retrieval)
  • Encode user and item SEPARATELY
  • Pre-compute ALL item embeddings offline
  • At query: only compute user embedding
  • ANN search over pre-computed index
  • Fast: milliseconds for billions of items
  • Lower quality (no cross-feature interaction)
DLRM (Ranking)
  • Process user + item TOGETHER in one model
  • Pairwise dot products between all feature embeddings
  • Cannot pre-compute (depends on specific user-item pair)
  • Run only on top-500 candidates (too slow for all items)
  • Higher quality (captures interaction features)
  • CTR prediction, ranking optimisation
Recommendation Metrics What Matters and Why
MetricFormulaUse ForLimitation
Precision@K|relevant top-K| / KQuality of shown recommendationsIgnores rank within K
Recall@K|relevant top-K| / |relevant|Coverage of user's interestsRequires knowing all relevant items
NDCG@KDCG / ideal DCGRanking quality (rewards top positions)Requires relevance scores (not just binary)
MRRmean(1/rank of first relevant)Search / "find the one right answer"Only rewards first relevant item
CTR (online)clicks / impressionsDirect user engagement signalOptimising CTR alone clickbait
Watch/Purchase ratecompletions or purchases / impressionsBusiness outcome (downstream of CTR)Delayed feedback (hours to days)
Diversity indexavg pairwise distance in recommendation listFilter bubble preventionTradeoff with relevance
🌍 Real-world use case: A homepage recommendation feed. A two-tower model retrieves a few hundred candidate items in milliseconds from millions, a gradient-boosted ranker re-scores them with business rules (freshness, margin, diversity), and a content-based fallback handles brand-new items the model has never seen.
🌏 Go Deeper — RecSys, Time Series & Tabular
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner Google ML Crash Course — Recommendation Systems — Concise overview of collaborative filtering, matrix factorisation, and two-tower retrieval with real-world framing; ideal first read before the code-heavy sections of this tab.
  • Intermediate Netflix Tech Blog — System Architectures for Personalization and Recommendation — Primary source from the team that defined modern RecSys architecture; the two-stage (retrieval + ranking) pattern described here is still the industry default.
  • Intermediate LightGBM — Documentation & Tutorials — The de facto tabular ML library; read the parameter-tuning guide to understand learning rate, num_leaves, and regularisation in the context of the tabular section of this tab.
  • Advanced Yi et al. — Sampling-Bias-Corrected Neural Modeling for Large Corpus Item Recommendations (YouTube Two-Tower, 2019) — The paper behind YouTube’s two-tower retrieval; Section 4 explains the batch softmax and sampling correction that makes two-tower training at scale tractable.
The Data Scientist role covers causal inference, A/B testing, product metrics, and SQL analytics. These are tested in ~60-70% of DS interviews but rarely covered in LLM-focused prep guides. This tab covers them with full depth including implementation.
What is this tab? This tab is the statistics and decision-science track for experiments, causal inference, product metrics, and SQL reasoning in real product teams.
Why it matters: Many data-science interviews care less about huge models and more about whether you can prove a product change worked, quantify uncertainty, and explain the result clearly to stakeholders.
How to use it: Read causal inference first, then move to A/B testing and SQL patterns, and use the formulas as a reference sheet when practicing experiment questions aloud.
Example: If you are asked whether a pricing change caused higher conversion or just coincided with seasonality, this tab gives you the frameworks to answer rigorously.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

Data Science is the part of analytics that uses statistics to find what's actually true in data — what really drives sales, whether a new feature helped, what users are likely to do next. It is less about training giant models and more about asking sharp questions and proving the answer is real (not just random noise that happens to look like a pattern).

Three core skills tested in every DS interview: A/B testing (we changed something for half the users — did it really matter, and is that difference statistically real or just luck?), causal inference (did X actually cause Y, or did they just happen together by coincidence?), and SQL plus Python data wrangling (getting messy data into a shape you can actually analyse). The last one sounds boring but eats most of the job.

What this tab covers: the potential-outcomes framework for thinking about cause and effect, A/B test design (how many users, how long, when can we trust the result?), sequential testing (when can we stop early without lying?), product metrics that actually matter (and the traps in obvious ones), and how to write a clean experiment readout stakeholders will trust.

Causal Inference
Potential Outcomes Framework
Causal inference answers 'what would have happened if we had done something different?' which is impossible to observe directly, so we need clever statistical techniques.
Potential Outcomes Framework: Y_i(1) = outcome for unit i IF treated Y_i(0) = outcome for unit i IF NOT treated ITE (Individual Treatment Effect) = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference: We can only observe ONE of Y_i(1) or Y_i(0) for each unit Can never directly compute ITE for an individual Solution: estimate average effects across groups ATE (Average Treatment Effect): ATE = E[Y(1) - Y(0)] = E[Y(1)] - E[Y(0)] Estimable from a Randomised Controlled Trial (RCT / A/B test) Random assignment -> groups comparable on all factors (observed + unobserved) Confounding (why correlation != causation): Confounder Z causes BOTH treatment T AND outcome Y Example: exercise -> health (true). But income -> exercise (confounder) Naive comparison: treated vs untreated -> biased by income Solution: randomise T (breaks confounder path) OR control for Z statistically
Y_i(1)potential outcome under treatment. Counterfactual: what WOULD happen if this person received the treatment
Y_i(0)potential outcome without treatment. Only one of Y(0) or Y(1) is actually observed per person
ITEIndividual Treatment Effect. The causal impact specifically for person i. Never directly observable fundamental problem
ATEAverage Treatment Effect. Average over the population. Estimable from RCT. E[Y(1)] - E[Y(0)]
Confoundervariable that affects both who receives treatment and what outcome they get. Creates spurious correlation. Must be controlled for or eliminated by randomisation
RCT/A/B testgold standard: randomly assign treatment. Random assignment breaks the confounder path. Comparison of treated vs control groups gives unbiased ATE estimate
Key insight: The core insight: correlation != causation because there are often confounders variables that simultaneously cause both the treatment and the outcome, creating a misleading association. Ice cream sales and drowning deaths are correlated (both caused by hot weather). A model that recommends 'sell less ice cream to prevent drownings' would be wrong. Randomisation breaks the confounder path.
Remember it as: Causal inference = what would happen if we intervened? Correlation = what do we observe when things happen naturally? The difference matters whenever you plan to take action based on data.
Python Propensity Score Matching
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression import pandas as pd import numpy as np def propensity_matching(df, treatment_col, outcome_col, covariates): "Simple nearest-neighbor propensity score matching." # Step 1: estimate propensity score P(T=1|X) X = df[covariates]; T = df[treatment_col] ps_model = LogisticRegression().fit(X, T) df['ps'] = ps_model.predict_proba(X)[:,1] # Step 2: match treated to control by nearest ps treated = df[df[treatment_col]==1] control = df[df[treatment_col]==0] matched_outcomes = [] for _, t_row in treated.iterrows(): distances = np.abs(control['ps'] - t_row['ps']) match_idx = distances.idxmin() matched_outcomes.append(control.loc[match_idx, outcome_col]) ate = treated[outcome_col].mean() - np.mean(matched_outcomes) return ate
Quasi-Experimental Methods
