Difference-in-differences estimates the causal effect of a treatment by comparing the CHANGE in treated vs control groups controlling for pre-existing differences.
DiD formula: DiD = (mean_treated_after - mean_treated_before) - (mean_control_after - mean_control_before) = (change in treated) - (change in control) Regression version (cleanest implementation): Y = alpha + beta1*Treated + beta2*Post + delta*(Treated*Post) + epsilon delta = DiD estimate (coefficient on the interaction term) What DiD controls for: Time-invariant differences: if treated group is always higher, that is removed Common time trends: if both groups increase over time, that is removed What DiD does NOT control for: time-varying differences between groups Parallel trends assumption (CRITICAL): Without treatment, both groups would have changed by the same amount This is the key assumption that makes DiD valid Test: show that pre-treatment trends are parallel Example: A state raises minimum wage. Another does not. DiD = (treated employment change) - (control employment change) Removes: seasonal patterns, national economic trends Isolates: effect of minimum wage increase specifically
Treated groupunits that received the intervention. In natural experiments: those affected by a policy change, law, or program
Control groupunits that did NOT receive the intervention. Should be similar to treated except for the treatment
Pre/Posttime periods before and after the treatment/intervention occurred
Parallel trends assumptiontreated and control groups were on the same trend before the treatment. If trends were diverging before: DiD estimate is biased
delta (interaction term)the DiD estimate. Coefficient on Treated*Post interaction. Measures the excess change in treated group beyond what happened in control group
Time-invariant differencesbaseline differences between groups that exist both before and after treatment. DiD differences these out, so they don't bias the estimate
Key insight: DiD is one of the most widely used quasi-experimental methods because it does not require randomisation. Policy changes, natural disasters, law changes all create natural DiD experiments. The key threat: if treated and control groups were already trending differently BEFORE the treatment, the parallel trends assumption fails and DiD is invalid.
Remember it as: DiD = measure the change in the treated group, subtract the change in the control group. The control group tells you what would have happened to the treated group 'in a world without treatment'. The difference is the causal effect.
A/B Testing & SQL
A/B Test Design
Before running an A/B test, you must calculate how many users you need to detect a meaningful effect with high probability underpowered tests waste time and mislead.
Sample size per arm: n = 2 * (z_{alpha/2} + z_beta)^2 * sigma^2 / delta^2 Common values: z_{alpha/2} = 1.96 (two-sided test, alpha = 0.05 false positive rate) z_beta = 0.84 (80% power, beta = 0.20 false negative rate) z_beta = 1.28 (90% power) For conversion rate (proportion) tests: sigma^2 = p * (1-p) where p = baseline conversion rate Example: p=0.05, want to detect delta=0.005 (10% relative lift): n = 2 * (1.96 + 0.84)^2 * 0.05*0.95 / 0.005^2 n = 2 * 7.84 * 0.0475 / 0.000025 ~= 29,800 per arm Always calculate BEFORE the test, not after! Peeking problem: if you check p-value repeatedly until p<0.05, then stop, your true false positive rate is ~30%, not 5% Fix: pre-specify sample size and end date. Do not peek. Minimum detectable effect (MDE): The smallest lift you care about detecting Business decision: 'a 0.1% lift is too small to matter' Smaller MDE -> more samples needed (quadratic relationship)
alpha (false positive rate)probability of declaring a winner when there is no real effect. Typically 0.05 (5%). Controlled by significance threshold
beta (false negative rate)probability of missing a real effect. Typically 0.20 (80% power) or 0.10 (90% power)
z_{alpha/2}z-score for the significance threshold. 1.96 for alpha=0.05 two-sided. 'If null is true, only 5% of experiments would show effect this large'
delta (MDE)minimum detectable effect. The smallest business-meaningful improvement. Smaller delta = more samples needed (because you need to detect a faint signal)
sigma^2variance of the metric. For binary outcomes (clicked/not clicked): p*(1-p). For continuous (revenue): estimated from historical data
Peeking problemrepeatedly checking the p-value and stopping when it crosses 0.05 inflates actual false positive rate from 5% to ~30%. Always commit to sample size before starting
Key insight: The most common A/B testing mistake: running the test for 'a few days', seeing p<0.05, declaring success. If you checked every day for 10 days, the chance of getting at least one p<0.05 by chance is ~30%, not 5%. Either pre-commit to a sample size, or use sequential testing methods (SPRT) designed for continuous monitoring.
Remember it as: Sample size calculation = decide in advance how large a difference you care about (MDE), how confident you want to be (power), and how wrong you can afford to be (alpha). Then calculate the minimum experiment size. Do not start the test and check every day.
  • Network effects: Social platform: treatment leaks to control (Sutva violation). Fix: cluster randomize by friend group or city.
  • Novelty effect: Users click more on anything new. Run 2+ weeks. Compare new vs returning users.
Product Metrics Framework
  • North Star Metric: Single metric capturing core value. Airbnb: nights booked. Spotify: time spent listening. All decisions evaluated against NSM.
  • AARRR (Pirate Metrics): Acquisition Activation Retention Revenue Referral. Framework for finding where funnel leaks.
  • Guardrail metrics: Must NOT degrade while improving primary. Latency, error rate, unsubscribe rate. A/B test: optimize CTR, guardrail against complaint rate.
  • Uplift modeling: Predict treatment effect per user. 4 segments: Persuadables (target), Sure Things (waste to treat), Lost Causes, Do-Not-Disturb (negative effect). T-Learner, X-Learner, Doubly Robust estimator. CausalML / econml libraries.
SQL Analytics Patterns
SQL Key DS interview patterns
-- 1. Cohort retention (D1/D7/D30) SELECT cohort_date, ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN day_offset=1 THEN 1 END) / SUM(CASE WHEN day_offset=0 THEN 1 END), 1) AS d1_retention FROM user_activity_cohorted GROUP BY cohort_date; -- 2. Rolling 7-day active users (WAU) SELECT date, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) OVER ( ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW ) AS wau FROM daily_active_users; -- 3. Funnel conversion SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN viewed THEN session_id END) AS viewed, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN added THEN session_id END) AS cart, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN ordered THEN session_id END) AS orders FROM events; -- 4. Median (PERCENTILE_CONT) SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY revenue) AS median_revenue FROM orders;
Advanced A/B Testing & Uplift Modeling
Sequential Testing & Bayesian A/B
Sequential testing lets you monitor an A/B test continuously and stop as soon as you have enough evidence without inflating your false positive rate.
Problem with naive peeking: Check p-value after every 100 users Stop if p < 0.05 at any check True false positive rate: ~30% (not 5%!) This is because: you are running 10+ hypothesis tests, not 1 SPRT (Sequential Probability Ratio Test): At each step, compute likelihood ratio: Lambda_n = P(data so far | H1) / P(data so far | H0) Stop and reject H0 (declare B wins) if Lambda >= B = 1/alpha Stop and accept H0 (declare no difference) if Lambda <= A = beta/(1-alpha) Otherwise: keep collecting data SPRT guarantees: False positive rate <= alpha (even with continuous monitoring) False negative rate <= beta Expected sample size SMALLER than fixed-sample test! Alpha spending (Lan-DeMets): Alternative: pre-specify how you will 'spend' your alpha budget over time Each interim look uses a fraction of alpha More flexible than SPRT, commonly used in clinical trials
Lambda (likelihood ratio)how much more likely is the data we observed if H1 (treatment works) vs H0 (no effect). Grows as evidence for H1 accumulates
B = 1/alphathreshold to reject H0. Lambda >= 1/alpha: strong enough evidence that treatment works. With alpha=0.05: stop if Lambda >= 20
A = beta/(1-alpha)threshold to accept H0. Lambda <= A: strong enough evidence that no meaningful effect exists. Stop and declare null result
Expected sample savingsSPRT often requires fewer samples than a fixed-sample test because it can stop early when effect is large. If there is a large effect, evidence accumulates quickly
Alpha spendinginstead of SPRT's fixed boundaries, pre-specify how much of your alpha budget to spend at each look. More flexible. O'Brien-Fleming boundary is most commonly used
Key insight: SPRT is the principled solution to the peeking problem used by top technology companies (Etsy, Microsoft, Twitter). It preserves the alpha guarantee while allowing stopping as soon as evidence is sufficient. This can reduce experiment duration by 30-50% when effects are large.
Remember it as: SPRT = a scale that you can read continuously. Normal A/B test: weigh once at the end. SPRT: keep reading until it clearly tips to one side or the other. Stops early if effect is obvious. Keeps going if it's close.
Python Bayesian A/B test
from scipy import stats import numpy as np def bayesian_ab(n_A, conv_A, n_B, conv_B, n_samples=100_000): "P(B beats A) via Monte Carlo simulation." # Posterior: Beta(1 + conversions, 1 + non-conversions) post_A = stats.beta(1 + conv_A, 1 + n_A - conv_A) post_B = stats.beta(1 + conv_B, 1 + n_B - conv_B) samples_A = post_A.rvs(n_samples) samples_B = post_B.rvs(n_samples) prob_B_wins = (samples_B > samples_A).mean() expected_lift = (samples_B - samples_A).mean() / samples_A.mean() return {"P(B>A)": prob_B_wins, "expected_lift": expected_lift}
Uplift Modeling: T-Learner, X-Learner
Python T-Learner uplift model
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor import numpy as np class TLearner: "Two separate models for treated and control groups." def fit(self, X, T, Y): self.m1 = GradientBoostingRegressor() self.m0 = GradientBoostingRegressor() self.m1.fit(X[T==1], Y[T==1]) # treated model self.m0.fit(X[T==0], Y[T==0]) # control model return self def predict_uplift(self, X): "Estimated ITE: E[Y|T=1,X] - E[Y|T=0,X]" return self.m1.predict(X) - self.m0.predict(X) # Usage: rank users by predicted uplift # Top 20% = "persuadables" target with campaign # Negative uplift = "do-not-disturb" never target # Evaluate uplift model: Qini curve / AUUC def auuc(y_true, treatment, uplift_scores): order = np.argsort(-uplift_scores) cumulative_uplift = np.cumsum( (y_true[order] * treatment[order])/treatment.sum() - (y_true[order] * (1-treatment[order]))/(1-treatment).sum() ) return cumulative_uplift.mean()
Prerequisites: Math & Stats → ML Fundamentals
Confounding Why Correlation Fails
Confounding Variable (DAG)
Z (Confounder)
e.g. Hot Weather
↘
↗
T (Treatment)
Ice cream sales
→
Y (Outcome)
Drowning deaths
Observed: ice cream drowning (spurious correlation)
Truth: both caused by hot weather (confounder Z)
Fix: randomise T (breaks ZT path) OR control for Z statistically
Quasi-Experimental Methods When You Can't Randomise
MethodWhen to UseKey Assumption
DiDPolicy change affects one group, not anotherParallel trends before treatment
RDDTreatment assigned at a threshold (score 85 = scholarship)No manipulation of running variable near cutoff
IV (2SLS)Treatment is endogenous; have an instrumentInstrument affects T but not Y directly
Propensity ScoreObservational study with many measured confoundersNo unmeasured confounders (strong ignorability)
RCT (A/B test)Can randomise assignmentRandomisation worked (check balance)
A/B Test Design Flow
1
Define primary metric + guardrails BEFORE starting
Primary: conversion rate. Guardrails: session length must not drop >5%, error rate must stay below 1%. Write these down. Do not add metrics after seeing results that is p-hacking.
2
Calculate required sample size
n = 2(z_{/2} + z_) ϲ / . For conversion rate p=0.05, MDE=0.005 (10% lift), =0.05, 80% power: ~30K users per arm. Calculate this FIRST not after.
3
Run for predetermined duration do not peek
Commit to end date. Peeking daily and stopping when p<0.05 inflates false positive rate from 5% to ~30%. Use sequential testing (SPRT) if you must monitor continuously.
4
Analyse: primary metric + all guardrails
Primary significant AND guardrails not violated = ship. Primary significant BUT guardrail breached = investigate. Multiple comparison correction if testing many metrics.
5
Segment analysis + novelty effect check
Did improvement hold across user segments (new/returning, device type)? Was there a novelty spike week 1 that faded? Simpson's paradox: check that aggregate result isn't hiding subgroup reversals.
Uplift Modelling Target the Right Users
🎯
Persuadables
Would buy IF treated, would not buy without. Target these.
✅
Sure Things
Buy regardless. Treating them wastes budget but is harmless.
🚫
Do-Not-Disturb
Negative uplift treatment makes them LESS likely to convert. Avoid.
😵
Lost Causes
Won't buy regardless. Not worth targeting.
Why uplift matters: A traditional propensity model maximises P(purchase) it targets Sure Things (they already buy) wasting budget. Uplift models maximise P(purchase|treated) P(purchase|untreated) targeting only Persuadables. Typical result: 2040% budget reduction for same revenue impact.
🌍 Real-world use case: Measuring whether a new checkout flow actually helps. The team runs an A/B test, but because users self-select into mobile vs. desktop they use causal adjustment to remove the confound, size the experiment for the minimum detectable effect up front, and report a confidence interval — not just a single lift number — before rolling the change out.
🌏 Go Deeper — Data Science
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner pandas User Guide (official docs) — Read the Indexing, GroupBy, and Merge sections; these cover 80% of the data manipulation tasks in DS interviews and are the gaps that most self-taught engineers have.
  • Intermediate Kaggle Learn — Free micro-courses — The Intermediate ML, Data Visualisation, and Feature Engineering courses are each <4 hours and sharpen the practical skills this tab discusses theoretically.
  • Advanced Wes McKinney — Python for Data Analysis (O’Reilly, 3rd ed.) — Written by the pandas creator; the NumPy and pandas chapters are the reference-level read when you need to understand why a pandas operation behaves the way it does.
Live coding interviews are the highest-stakes component for ML/AI engineering roles. FAANG and research labs expect complete implementations in 20-30 minutes no libraries, clean code, explained as you go. This tab contains 8 core implementations (Multi-Head Attention, Backprop 2-layer net, K-Means, BPE, VAE, Transformer block, LSTM, Beam search) with complete working code, exactly what interviewers probe, and common follow-up questions.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

This tab is your live coding interview cheat sheet for AI / ML roles. The pattern is the same at every serious AI company: someone shares a code editor, you have 20-30 minutes, and you have to implement a classic ML building block from scratch — no LangChain, no high-level PyTorch shortcuts, just NumPy or raw PyTorch primitives. They want to see clean code, correct math, and you explaining what you are doing as you go.

Why this matters even if you do not love coding interviews: there is no faking your way through implementing Multi-Head Attention or backprop. Either you understand it well enough to write it, or you do not. Interviewers use this round precisely because it cannot be cheated by reading model cards or repeating buzzwords. Once you have implemented the core 8 algorithms a few times, future interviews stop being scary — you just type out what is already in your fingers.

What this tab covers: the 8 implementations that come up again and again — Multi-Head Attention, backprop for a 2-layer network, K-Means clustering, BPE tokenisation, VAE, a full Transformer block, LSTM, and beam search. For each one: complete working code, what interviewers probe (“why this and not that?”), and the common follow-up questions.

1. Multi-Head Attention (FAANG Standard)
Complete Implementation
PyTorch Multi-Head Attention from scratch
import torch, math import torch.nn.functional as F import torch.nn as nn class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d_model=512, n_heads=8, dropout=0.1): super().__init__() assert d_model % n_heads == 0 self.d_k = d_model // n_heads self.n_heads = n_heads # No bias in modern LLMs (matches LLaMA 3 architecture) self.Wq = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False) self.Wk = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False) self.Wv = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False) self.Wo = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False) self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout) def forward(self, x, mask=None): B, T, C = x.shape # Project and reshape: (B, T, C) (B, h, T, d_k) Q = self.Wq(x).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2) K = self.Wk(x).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2) V = self.Wv(x).view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2) # Scaled dot-product attention scores = (Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.d_k) if mask is not None: scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float("-inf")) attn = self.drop(F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)) out = (attn @ V).transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, C) return self.Wo(out) def causal_mask(T, device): "Upper triangular mask for autoregressive decoding." return torch.tril(torch.ones(T, T, device=device)).unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0) # Test mha = MultiHeadAttention(d_model=256, n_heads=8) x = torch.randn(2, 10, 256) # (batch, seq, d_model) mask = causal_mask(10, x.device) out = mha(x, mask) print(out.shape) # torch.Size([2, 10, 256])
Key Interview Questions
  • Why sqrt(d_k) scaling? QK^T has variance d_k (if Q,K ~ N(0,1)). Large d_k large scores softmax saturates near-zero gradients. Dividing by d_k variance = 1 stable gradients.
  • Why multi-head? Different heads attend to different relationship types (syntax, semantics, coreference). Learned implicitly from data. Single head = bottleneck on what relationships can be captured simultaneously.
  • Implement KV cache: Keep dict of past K,V. New token: compute Q only (from new token), compute K,V for new token, append to cache, run attention over all past K,V. Amortizes the O(T) cost across generation steps.
  • Flash Attention vs standard: Standard materializes full TT matrix in HBM (slow). Flash: tiles into SRAM (fast), online softmax, never stores full matrix. Same output. 2-4 faster, O(T) vs O(T) memory.
2. Backpropagation from Scratch
2-Layer Network with Full Derivation
NumPy Backprop from scratch
import numpy as np class TwoLayerNet: def __init__(self, d_in, d_h, d_out, lr=0.001): # He init: std = sqrt(2/fan_in) for ReLU self.W1 = np.random.randn(d_in, d_h) * np.sqrt(2 / d_in) self.b1 = np.zeros(d_h) self.W2 = np.random.randn(d_h, d_out) * np.sqrt(2 / d_h) self.b2 = np.zeros(d_out) self.lr = lr def _softmax(self, z): e = np.exp(z - z.max(axis=1, keepdims=True)) # numerical stability return e / e.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True) def forward(self, X): self.X = X self.z1 = X @ self.W1 + self.b1 self.a1 = np.maximum(0, self.z1) # ReLU self.z2 = self.a1 @ self.W2 + self.b2 self.p = self._softmax(self.z2) return self.p def backward(self, y_onehot): N = y_onehot.shape[0] # dL/dz2 = p - y_true (softmax+CE combined gradient) dz2 = (self.p - y_onehot) / N dW2 = self.a1.T @ dz2 db2 = dz2.sum(axis=0) # Backprop through ReLU: gradient = 1 if z1>0 else 0 da1 = dz2 @ self.W2.T dz1 = da1 * (self.z1 > 0) dW1 = self.X.T @ dz1 db1 = dz1.sum(axis=0) # SGD update self.W2 -= self.lr * dW2; self.b2 -= self.lr * db2 self.W1 -= self.lr * dW1; self.b1 -= self.lr * db1 loss = -(y_onehot * np.log(self.p + 1e-12)).sum(axis=1).mean() return loss
Why the Softmax+CE Gradient is Clean
The softmax + cross-entropy gradient is the most important result in deep learning and it comes out remarkably clean despite looking complex at first.
Setup: z = logits (raw model outputs, one per class) p_k = softmax(z)_k = exp(z_k) / sum_j exp(z_j) (probabilities) y_k = true label (1 for correct class, 0 for all others) L = -sum_k y_k * log(p_k) (cross-entropy loss) Goal: compute dL/dz_k (gradient of loss w.r.t. each logit) Derivation: dL/dz_k = sum_j (-y_j / p_j) * dp_j/dz_k Softmax self-derivative: dp_k/dz_k = p_k * (1 - p_k) Softmax cross-derivative: dp_j/dz_k = -p_j * p_k (for j != k) Substituting: dL/dz_k = -(y_k/p_k) * p_k*(1-p_k) + sum_{j!=k} (y_j/p_j) * p_j*p_k = -y_k*(1-p_k) + sum_{j!=k} y_j * p_k = -y_k + p_k * sum_j y_j = p_k - y_k (since sum_j y_j = 1 for one-hot labels!) Final result: dL/dz_k = p_k - y_k
p_kpredicted probability for class k after softmax. Between 0 and 1
y_ktrue label for class k. 1 if this is the correct class, 0 for all others (one-hot encoding)
dL/dz_k = p_k - y_kthe remarkably clean result: gradient is simply 'prediction minus truth'. Positive = model over-predicted (push down). Negative = model under-predicted (push up)
dp_k/dz_k = p_k(1-p_k)softmax self-derivative: sigmoid-like, but comes from the normalisation
dp_j/dz_k = -p_j*p_kcross-derivative: softmax at position j also depends on z_k through the normalisation sum. Negative because increasing z_k reduces all other probabilities
sum_j y_j = 1for one-hot labels: exactly one class has y=1, all others have y=0. This is what makes the sum collapse so cleanly
Key insight: This clean gradient (p_k - y_k) is why cross-entropy + softmax is the universal choice for classification. There is no saturation, the gradient is always non-zero when the prediction is wrong, and it has a beautiful intuitive interpretation: 'push the correct class up, push all others down, proportionally to how wrong you were'.
Remember it as: dL/dz = p - y = 'predicted minus actual'. If you predicted 0.3 for the right class but truth is 1.0: gradient is -0.7 -> push that logit up. If you predicted 0.2 for a wrong class: gradient is +0.2 -> push that logit down. Exactly what you'd want.
Live Coding Interview What Each Implementation Tests
ImplementationWhat It TestsKey Concepts to Explain While CodingCommon Follow-up
Multi-Head AttentionCore transformer understanding, tensor operationsWhy sqrt(d_k)? What does the mask do? Why multi-head?How would you add KV cache? Implement Flash Attention?
Backpropagation 2-layer netChain rule, gradient flow, understanding of NN trainingWhy is dL/dz = py so clean? What is the ReLU gradient?How would you add L2 regularisation? What causes vanishing gradients?
K-Means with K-Means++Clustering intuition, algorithmic thinking, vectorisationWhy K-Means++ over random init? How to choose k?How would you scale to 100M points? When does K-Means fail?
BPE TokenizerNLP fundamentals, text processing, algorithm designWhy subword over character/word? What is the fertility problem?How does SentencePiece differ? What is byte-level BPE?
LSTM from scratchSequential models, gating mechanisms, memoryWhat does each gate do? How does it solve vanishing gradients?Why did transformers replace LSTMs? When would you still use an LSTM?
Beam SearchDecoding strategies, search algorithms, NLP generationWhy not greedy? What is the length normalisation for?When does beam search fail? What is nucleus sampling?
VAE (encoder + decoder)Generative models, reparameterization trick, ELBO lossWhy reparameterize? What does the KL term enforce? Why not just autoencoder?How does -VAE differ? When use VAE vs diffusion vs GAN?
Transformer blockEnd-to-end transformer wiring, pre-norm vs post-norm, residualsWhy pre-norm? Why residuals around each sub-layer? Why SwiGLU FFN?How does this become a full decoder LM? Where do RoPE / KV cache plug in?
Interview Coding Framework 5 Steps Every Time
1
Clarify before writing (30 seconds)
"Should I use NumPy or pure Python?" "Should this handle batches?" "What precision?" This signals architect thinking, not just implementation.
2
State the algorithm in one sentence
Before any code: "K-Means repeatedly assigns points to nearest centroid then recomputes centroids until convergence." This proves you understand the concept, not just the code.
3
Write clean code with comments on the WHY
Comment non-obvious choices: "# subtract max for numerical stability" on softmax. "# B=zeros so DeltaW=0 at init, preserving pretrained weights" on LoRA. Interviewers test understanding, not typing speed.
4
Test with a simple example, narrate the output
"With input [1,2,3], softmax gives [0.09, 0.24, 0.67]. Highest input highest probability. Sum = 1.0 as expected." Running through a concrete example live shows you can debug.
5
Discuss complexity and real-world considerations
"This is O(nkditerations). In production I'd use scikit-learn's MiniBatchKMeans for 100M points, or FAISS for vector clustering. The main limitation is the assumption of spherical clusters."
3. K-Means with K-Means++ Init
Full Implementation
NumPy — K-Means clustering from scratch
import numpy as np def kmeans(X, k, max_iter=300, tol=1e-4, seed=42): np.random.seed(seed) n, d = X.shape # K-Means++ initialization centroids = [X[np.random.randint(n)]] for _ in range(k - 1): # Distance to nearest centroid (squared) D2 = np.array([min(np.linalg.norm(x - c)**2 for c in centroids) for x in X]) # Sample proportional to D spreads centroids out probs = D2 / D2.sum() centroids.append(X[np.random.choice(n, p=probs)]) centroids = np.array(centroids) for _ in range(max_iter): # Vectorized: (n,1,d) - (1,k,d) (n,k) distances dists = np.linalg.norm(X[:, None] - centroids[None], axis=2) labels = dists.argmin(axis=1) new_c = np.array([X[labels == i].mean(axis=0) if (labels == i).any() else centroids[i] for i in range(k)]) if np.linalg.norm(new_c - centroids) < tol: break centroids = new_c inertia = sum(np.linalg.norm(X[labels==i] - centroids[i])**2 for i in range(k)) return labels, centroids, inertia
More Essential Implementations
NumPy Softmax, cosine similarity, precision@k
import numpy as np def softmax(z): "Numerically stable softmax." e = np.exp(z - z.max(axis=-1, keepdims=True)) return e / e.sum(axis=-1, keepdims=True) def cosine_similarity(a, b): "Cosine similarity, handles batches." a_n = a / (np.linalg.norm(a, axis=-1, keepdims=True) + 1e-8) b_n = b / (np.linalg.norm(b, axis=-1, keepdims=True) + 1e-8) return (a_n * b_n).sum(axis=-1) def precision_at_k(relevant, retrieved, k): "Precision@K for information retrieval." top_k = set(retrieved[:k]) return len(top_k & set(relevant)) / k def cross_entropy(logits, y_true): "Cross-entropy loss with integer class labels." N = len(y_true) p = softmax(logits) return -np.log(p[np.arange(N), y_true] + 1e-12).mean() def rmsNorm(x, gamma, eps=1e-8): "RMS normalization (used in LLaMA, Mistral)." rms = np.sqrt((x**2).mean(axis=-1, keepdims=True) + eps) return gamma * x / rms
More Critical Implementations
LSTM from Scratch
Python LSTM cell implementation
import numpy as np class LSTMCell: def __init__(self, input_size, hidden_size): self.h = hidden_size # Xavier init for all 4 gate weight matrices stacked scale = np.sqrt(2.0 / (input_size + hidden_size)) self.W = np.random.randn(4*hidden_size, input_size+hidden_size)*scale self.b = np.zeros(4*hidden_size) def sigmoid(self, x): return 1/(1+np.exp(-x)) def forward(self, x_t, h_prev, c_prev): "One LSTM step. Returns (h_t, c_t)." xh = np.concatenate([x_t, h_prev]) gates = self.W @ xh + self.b # (4h,) all gates i = self.sigmoid(gates[:self.h]) # input gate f = self.sigmoid(gates[self.h:2*self.h]) # forget gate g = np.tanh(gates[2*self.h:3*self.h]) # cell gate o = self.sigmoid(gates[3*self.h:]) # output gate c_t = f * c_prev + i * g # cell state h_t = o * np.tanh(c_t) # hidden state return h_t, c_t
Beam Search
Python Beam search for text generation
import heapq def beam_search(model, start_token, beam_width=5, max_len=50, eos_token=2): "Beam search for autoregressive generation." # Beam: (neg_log_prob, token_sequence) beams = [(0.0, [start_token])] completed = [] for _ in range(max_len): candidates = [] for score, seq in beams: if seq[-1] == eos_token: completed.append((score, seq)); continue # Get next token probabilities from model log_probs = model.next_token_log_probs(seq) # (vocab,) # Take top beam_width candidates top_k = log_probs.argsort()[-beam_width:][::-1] for token in top_k: candidates.append( (score - log_probs[token], seq + [token])) # Keep top beam_width beams (min-heap on neg log prob) beams = heapq.nsmallest(beam_width, candidates, key=lambda x: x[0]) if not beams: break completed.extend(beams) best = min(completed, key=lambda x: x[0]/len(x[1])) # length-norm return best[1]
4. BPE Tokenizer (GPT / Llama tokeniser core)
BPE Training Loop
Python — Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch
from collections import Counter, defaultdict def get_pair_counts(corpus): "corpus = dict of {tuple_of_symbols: freq}. Returns Counter of adjacent pairs." pairs = Counter() for symbols, freq in corpus.items(): for i in range(len(symbols) - 1): pairs[(symbols[i], symbols[i+1])] += freq return pairs def merge_pair(corpus, pair): "Replace every occurrence of `pair` with its concatenation in every word." a, b = pair merged = a + b new_corpus = {} for symbols, freq in corpus.items(): out, i = [], 0 while i < len(symbols): if i < len(symbols)-1 and symbols[i]==a and symbols[i+1]==b: out.append(merged); i += 2 else: out.append(symbols[i]); i += 1 new_corpus[tuple(out)] = freq return new_corpus def train_bpe(words, n_merges=10000): "words = list of strings. Returns merge rules in learned order." # Each word starts as a tuple of single chars + end-of-word marker corpus = Counter(tuple(list(w) + ['</w>']) for w in words) merges = [] for _ in range(n_merges): pairs = get_pair_counts(corpus) if not pairs: break best = max(pairs, key=pairs.get) # most frequent pair corpus = merge_pair(corpus, best) merges.append(best) return merges def encode(word, merges): "Apply learned merges greedily to a new word." symbols = list(word) + ['</w>'] for a, b in merges: i = 0 while i < len(symbols)-1: if symbols[i]==a and symbols[i+1]==b: symbols = symbols[:i] + [a+b] + symbols[i+2:] else: i += 1 return symbols
Why BPE wins
Vocab controlFixed budget (50K). Char-level too long, word-level explodes & OOVs. BPE is the sweet spot.
No OOVAny UTF-8 byte sequence decomposes into known sub-tokens. Byte-level BPE (GPT-2+) handles emoji, code, every language.
Compression-drivenMost frequent pair merged first → common English words become single tokens, rare ones split. Average ~4 chars/token for English.
FertilityTokens/word ratio. English ~1.3, Hindi ~2.5, code ~1.8. Lower = cheaper inference per language.
Interview hook: “Walk me through how GPT tokenises ‘tokenization’.” Answer: starts as 12 chars + </w>, greedily applies merges in learned order, ends as ~3 tokens (‘token’, ‘ization’, </w>). Show the merge trace.
5. VAE — Variational Autoencoder (encoder + decoder + ELBO)
PyTorch implementation
PyTorch — minimal VAE with reparameterisation trick
import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class VAE(nn.Module): def __init__(self, in_dim=784, hidden=400, z_dim=20): super().__init__() # Encoder: x -> (mu, logvar) parameterising q(z|x) self.enc_fc = nn.Linear(in_dim, hidden) self.enc_mu = nn.Linear(hidden, z_dim) self.enc_logvar = nn.Linear(hidden, z_dim) # Decoder: z -> x_hat parameterising p(x|z) self.dec_fc1 = nn.Linear(z_dim, hidden) self.dec_fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden, in_dim) def encode(self, x): h = F.relu(self.enc_fc(x)) return self.enc_mu(h), self.enc_logvar(h) def reparameterise(self, mu, logvar): # Trick: z = mu + sigma * eps. Gradient flows through mu & logvar; # randomness lives in eps which is detached from the graph. std = torch.exp(0.5 * logvar) eps = torch.randn_like(std) return mu + eps * std def decode(self, z): h = F.relu(self.dec_fc1(z)) return torch.sigmoid(self.dec_fc2(h)) def forward(self, x): mu, logvar = self.encode(x.view(x.size(0), -1)) z = self.reparameterise(mu, logvar) return self.decode(z), mu, logvar def vae_loss(x_hat, x, mu, logvar): # Reconstruction: how well decoder recovers x from z recon = F.binary_cross_entropy(x_hat, x.view(x.size(0), -1), reduction='sum') # KL(N(mu, sigma^2) || N(0, I)) = 0.5 * sum(mu^2 + sigma^2 - log sigma^2 - 1) kl = -0.5 * torch.sum(1 + logvar - mu.pow(2) - logvar.exp()) return recon + kl # -ELBO
Why this matters
Reparam trickWithout it, sampling z breaks backprop (gradient cannot flow through a stochastic node). With z = μ + σ·ε, randomness is in ε only — gradient flows through μ & σ.
ELBOlog p(x) ≥ E_q[log p(x|z)] - KL(q(z|x) || p(z)). Maximise lower bound = reconstruction - KL.
KL = regulariserForces q(z|x) toward N(0,I) prior → latent space is smooth & samplable. Without it: plain autoencoder, no generation.
vs diffusionVAE: one-shot sample, sharper-but-blurrier images, fast. Diffusion: iterative denoising, higher fidelity, 25-50 steps.
Follow-up trap: “Why not just maximise log p(x) directly?” — because log p(x) = log ∫ p(x|z) p(z) dz is intractable for any non-trivial decoder. ELBO is the tractable surrogate; KL gap closes as encoder gets better.
6. Transformer Block (pre-norm, residuals, MHA + SwiGLU FFN)
PyTorch implementation
PyTorch — modern pre-norm transformer block (Llama-style)
import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class RMSNorm(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d, eps=1e-6): super().__init__() self.g = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(d)) self.eps = eps def forward(self, x): return self.g * x * torch.rsqrt(x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True) + self.eps) class SwiGLU(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d, hidden): super().__init__() self.w1 = nn.Linear(d, hidden, bias=False) # gate self.w2 = nn.Linear(d, hidden, bias=False) # up self.w3 = nn.Linear(hidden, d, bias=False) # down def forward(self, x): return self.w3(F.silu(self.w1(x)) * self.w2(x)) class MHA(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d, n_heads, causal=True): super().__init__() assert d % n_heads == 0 self.h, self.dh, self.causal = n_heads, d // n_heads, causal self.qkv = nn.Linear(d, 3 * d, bias=False) self.proj = nn.Linear(d, d, bias=False) def forward(self, x): B, T, D = x.shape q, k, v = self.qkv(x).split(D, dim=-1) q = q.view(B, T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 2) # (B,h,T,dh) k = k.view(B, T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 2) v = v.view(B, T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 2) # Uses Flash Attention under the hood when on CUDA/H100 out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=self.causal) out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, D) return self.proj(out) class TransformerBlock(nn.Module): def __init__(self, d, n_heads, ffn_hidden): super().__init__() self.attn_norm = RMSNorm(d) # pre-norm before attention self.attn = MHA(d, n_heads) self.ffn_norm = RMSNorm(d) # pre-norm before FFN self.ffn = SwiGLU(d, ffn_hidden) def forward(self, x): x = x + self.attn(self.attn_norm(x)) # residual + attn x = x + self.ffn(self.ffn_norm(x)) # residual + FFN return x
What interviewers probe
Pre-norm vs post-normPre-norm (Llama, GPT-NeoX) trains stably at scale — gradient highway is the unnormalised residual stream. Post-norm (original 2017) needs warm-up + tuning.
ResidualsWithout them, gradient must traverse every sub-layer. With them, ∂loss/∂x = ∂loss/∂y · (1 + ∂F/∂x) — the +1 is the highway.
RMSNorm vs LayerNormRMSNorm drops the mean-subtraction; ~10% faster, same quality. Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek all use RMSNorm.
SwiGLU FFN3 matrices (gate, up, down). Gate · up creates a learned filter; outperforms plain GELU on every modern benchmark. Hidden dim usually 8/3 × d_model.
StackingStack N=32 (Llama 7B) to N=126 (DeepSeek 671B). Final RMSNorm + LM head → vocab logits. That’s the entire decoder LM.
Live coding gotcha: remember to transpose(1,2) before AND after attention so heads sit on dim 1 during the dot product but dim 2 when concatenating back. Off-by-one on this is the #1 failure mode in interviews.
Q&A Volume 1: Q1–Q60 (Foundations & Core) Click any question to expand. ML ENG AI ENG DATA SCI MLOps ARCHITECT ALL
How to Use This Volume
What is this volume? This is the foundations and core interview drill set, covering the first block of questions that show up across ML, LLM, systems, and architecture interviews.
Why it matters: These questions test whether your basics are fluent enough that the rest of the interview can go deeper instead of getting stuck on first principles.
How to use it: Expand one question at a time, answer aloud first, then compare your answer against the reference and jump back to the linked tab when you need to repair a gap.
Example: If you miss a question on LoRA or HNSW, revisit the Fine-tuning or RAG tab immediately, then return and answer the question again from memory.
Interview Loop
Pick 10 questions
→
Answer aloud first
→
Compare with reference
→
Revisit weak tab
→
Re-answer from memory
Pass Focus Action
1 Core recall Answer 10 questions without expanding them first.
2 Gap repair Use the linked source tab to repair weak concepts immediately.
3 Retention Re-answer the same set later from memory only.
Q1. Explain the transformer architecture from tokens to output probabilities.ALL
Token IDs embedding lookup (vocab_size d_model). Positional encoding: RoPE rotates Q,K vectors by angle ᵢpos where ᵢ=10000^(-2i/d). Encodes relative position in QK naturally. N transformer blocks: Pre-RMSNorm Multi-head causal self-attention (GQA in modern LLMs) residual add Pre-RMSNorm SwiGLU FFN (3 weight matrices: gateup then down projection) residual add. Output: Final RMSNorm linear to vocab_size logits temperature scaling sampling. KV cache: K,V from past tokens cached; only compute Q for new token at each generation step.
Q2. What is the vanishing gradient problem and how does modern deep learning solve it?ML ENG
Problem: In deep networks, backprop multiplies gradients through each layer: L/W = L/z z/z ... z/W. If each term < 1 (sigmoid/tanh saturate with max gradient 0.25): after 50 layers, gradient 0.25 0. First layers learn nothing.

Modern solutions:
  • Residual connections (ResNet/Transformers): y = F(x) + x. Gradient: L/x = L/y(F/x + 1). The +1 ensures gradient 1 regardless of F gradient highway even if sublayer contributes nothing.
  • ReLU activations: Gradient = 1 when positive no saturation. No multiplicative shrinkage.
  • Pre-norm (LayerNorm before sublayer): Controls activation scale, prevents both vanishing and exploding gradients.
  • AdamW: Adaptive per-parameter LR. Low gradients get amplified. Even small gradient signal is utilized.
Q3. Derive the gradient of softmax + cross-entropy loss.ML ENG
This is the most important gradient in deep learning. Given logits z, class labels y (one-hot), and softmax p_k = exp(z_k)/exp(z_j):

Loss: L = -_k y_k log(p_k)

Derivation: L/z_k = _j (-y_j/p_j) p_j/z_k

Using softmax derivatives: p_k/z_k = p_k(1-p_k) and p_j/z_k = -p_jp_k for jk:

L/z_k = -(y_k(1-p_k)) + _{jk} y_jp_k = -y_k + p_k_j y_j = p_k - y_k

The remarkable result: gradient is simply predicted probability minus true label. No saturation, always nonzero when prediction is wrong. This clean form is why softmax+CE is the universal classification loss.
Q4. What is LoRA and why does it work?ML ENG
LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation): Instead of updating W (dk parameters), learn W = BA where BR^(dr) and AR^(rk) with rmin(d,k). Forward pass: h = Wx + (BA/scaling)x.

Why it works: The change in weights needed for a new task lies in a low-dimensional subspace of the full weight space. Pre-trained model has rich representations task adaptation doesn't need all dk dimensions, just the r critical ones.

Parameter savings: r=16, d=k=4096: LoRA = 131K vs 16.7M (0.78%). Memory: only adapters need gradients base model in 4-bit NF4 (QLoRA).

Initialization: B=zeros, A=random Gaussian. So W=0 at start preserves pre-trained behavior exactly. Training gradually builds up the needed adaptation.

Merging: W_merged = W + (/r)BA. Zero inference overhead. Or keep separate for multi-task switching.
Q5. Explain RLHF step by step including the KL penalty.ALL
Step 1 SFT: Fine-tune base LLM on (prompt, ideal_response) pairs. Standard cross-entropy on completion tokens. Teaches format and basic instruction following.

Step 2 Reward Model: Collect human preference data: (prompt, response_A, response_B, preference). Train RM with loss: -log((r_winner - r_loser)). RM outputs scalar reward for any (prompt, response).

Step 3 PPO RL: Total reward = RM(x,y) - KL(_(y|x) _ref(y|x)). PPO optimizes this reward while clipping probability ratio to prevent large updates: min(r_tA_t, clip(r_t,1-,1+)A_t).

Why KL penalty: Without it, policy finds outputs that fool the reward model (reward hacking). Sycophancy, extreme verbosity, repetition of high-scoring phrases. KL term pulls policy toward SFT reference the known-good baseline. controls tradeoff: low = more RL gain but more hacking risk.
Q6. What is DPO and how does it differ from RLHF?ML ENG
DPO (Direct Preference Optimization): Eliminates reward model + RL training loop entirely. Uses the LLM itself as an implicit reward model.

Loss: L_DPO = -E[log ((log _(y_w|x)/_ref(y_w|x) - log _(y_l|x)/_ref(y_l|x)))]. y_w = preferred, y_l = rejected, _ref = frozen SFT.

Intuition: Push model to increase likelihood of preferred response (relative to reference) and decrease likelihood of rejected. The term prevents deviation from reference (like KL in RLHF).

Advantages over RLHF: No separate reward model (saves training + memory). No online sampling + PPO loop (simpler). More stable training. Equal or better alignment quality on most tasks.

When RLHF still preferred: Very precise multi-dimensional reward shaping. Large-scale with massive compute budget. Process reward model (verify reasoning steps, not just final answer, as in DeepSeek-R1/o1).
Q7. How does RAG differ from fine-tuning for knowledge injection?ALL
RAG: Retrieve relevant documents at query time, inject into context. Best for: large knowledge bases, frequently updated information, need for citations, proprietary enterprise data. Knowledge stays external always current. Can cite sources. Works immediately without training.

Fine-tuning: Bake knowledge into model weights via training. Best for: stable knowledge (writing style, format, domain behavior), reducing prompt complexity, task-specific capabilities. NOT for: specific facts (LLMs hallucinate fine-tuned facts, may extrapolate incorrectly).

The key mistake: Using fine-tuning to inject factual knowledge. 'Tell the model about our product catalog' via fine-tuning model hallucinates plausible product details with confidence. Always use RAG for facts, fine-tuning for behavior/style.

Hybrid (best for most production): RAG for knowledge + fine-tuning for response format, domain tone, and task structure. Neither alone handles all requirements.
Q8. Explain the HNSW algorithm for approximate nearest neighbor search.AI ENG
HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World): Graph-based ANN index. Brute-force kNN is O(Nd) per query too slow for 10M+ vectors. HNSW achieves O(log N) with 95%+ recall.

Structure: Multi-layer graph. Top layers: few nodes with long-range connections (highway graph). Bottom layer: all nodes with short-range connections (local neighborhood). Exponentially fewer nodes per higher layer.

Search: Enter at random node at top layer greedily navigate to nearest neighbor descend to next layer repeat find top-k at base layer. Each layer refines the search in a smaller neighborhood.

Key params: M=16 (connections per node, more = better recall, more RAM). ef_construction=200 (candidates explored during build). ef_search=50 (candidates at query time, tune recall vs latency).

Recall vs latency: ef_search=10: ~88% recall, fastest. ef_search=200: ~99.5%, 10 slower. For RAG: 95% is usually sufficient and avoids unnecessary latency.
Q9. What is the difference between precision and recall and when do you optimize for each?DATA SCI
Precision = TP/(TP+FP): Of all predicted positives, what fraction are truly positive? Measures trustworthiness of positive predictions. Optimize when false positives are costly.

Recall = TP/(TP+FN): Of all actual positives, what fraction did you find? Measures coverage. Optimize when false negatives are costly.

Examples:
  • Spam filter: Optimize precision. Missing spam = annoying. Flagging legitimate email as spam = unacceptable.
  • Cancer screening: Optimize recall. Missing cancer = potentially fatal. False positive = further tests but manageable.
  • Legal document discovery: Optimize recall. Missing relevant document = legal risk. Reviewing extra docs = just work.
PR-AUC vs ROC-AUC: For imbalanced data (rare positive class), PR-AUC is more informative. ROC-AUC can be misleadingly high when negatives vastly outnumber positives (model looks good even if precision is terrible).
Q10. How do you handle class imbalance in a machine learning problem?DATA SCI
First: understand the imbalance. 1% positive rate vs 0.01% positive rate require different approaches. Identify: is the minority class truly rare or just undersampled?

Data-level: SMOTE (synthetic oversampling interpolate between existing minority points). Random undersampling (fast, may lose information). SMOTE+Tomek: oversample then clean noisy boundary samples. Apply only to training set.

Algorithm-level: Class weights: weight_pos = n_total/(n_classes n_pos). Pass to loss or estimator. class_weight='balanced' in sklearn. Focal loss: (1-p_t)^ CE, =2 standard. Downweights easy examples, focuses on hard ones.

Threshold tuning: Never use default 0.5 threshold on imbalanced data. Use precision-recall curve to find threshold that maximizes F1 or meets your FP/FN cost ratio.

Evaluation: Never report accuracy. Use F1, PR-AUC, Matthews Correlation Coefficient. Test set: keep imbalanced (don't balance it that's not the real distribution).
Q11. What is gradient clipping and when do you use it?ML ENG
Gradient clipping: If g > threshold: g = g (threshold/g). Preserves gradient direction. Clip by norm (standard) vs clip by value (changes direction, less common).

When mandatory: RNNs/LSTMs: BPTT multiplies gradients across T timesteps explodes exponentially for long sequences. LLM pre-training: occasional loss spikes due to bad batches. Without clipping, one bad batch corrupts training. All modern LLM training: GPT-4, LLaMA, Claude all use clip=1.0.

Typical values: max_grad_norm=1.0 for LLMs. 0.5 for RNNs. max_grad_norm applied after backward(), before optimizer.step().

Diagnosis: If you see NaN loss or weight norms exploding: gradient clipping is the fix. Monitor gradient norms during training sudden spike precedes explosion.

PyTorch: torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0). Call this every training step.
Q12. Explain the transformer pre-training objective and why it works so well.ALL
Causal language modeling (CLM): Given tokens x...x, predict x. Loss = -1/T log P(x|x...x). Applied to ALL positions simultaneously during training (teacher forcing). Simple but extraordinarily powerful at scale.

Why it works: To predict the next word accurately, the model MUST learn: grammar (syntax), facts (semantics), reasoning (can't predict conclusion without understanding argument), style (predict next word given established tone). All these learned implicitly as a byproduct of next-token prediction.

Why scaling helps: More parameters = more capacity to store world knowledge and patterns. More data = more facts, more reasoning patterns. More compute = more gradient updates. Smooth power-law improvement: loss scales predictably with compute/params/data.

Perplexity: exp(-1/N log P(xᵢ)) the effective vocabulary size the model is choosing from at each step. Lower = better. Random token predictor: perplexity = vocab_size. Human: ~40-80. GPT-4: ~6-8 on standard benchmarks.
Q13. How does speculative decoding work?ML ENG
Problem: Large models (70B+) generate one token at a time: each step requires full forward pass. Sequential = slow.

Speculative decoding: Small draft model (7B) generates K candidate tokens. Large target model verifies ALL K simultaneously in ONE forward pass. Accept/reject at each position based on probability ratio.

Acceptance rule: Token x accepted if: random_sample < P_target(x)/P_draft(x). If rejected: sample from corrected distribution. Return all accepted tokens + first rejected position's sample.

Why it works: Verification is a forward pass over K tokens (parallel) same cost as generating 1 token from scratch with the large model. If draft accuracy > 70%: accept ~3-5 tokens per target-model call 2-3 throughput.

Production use: vLLM with --speculative-model option. Best for: models where same-family smaller version exists (LLaMA 70B + LLaMA 7B draft). N-gram speculation: use n-gram model as draft, free (no extra model). Self-speculative: early exit layers as draft.
Q14. What are the key differences between BERT and GPT architectures?ML ENG
BERT (Encoder-only, bidirectional): Every token attends to ALL other tokens. Pre-training: MLM (predict masked tokens) + NSP. Best for: text understanding classification, NER, extractive QA, semantic similarity. Cannot generate text. Training is more data-efficient (dense prediction signal: every masked token).

GPT (Decoder-only, causal): Each token only attends to PREVIOUS tokens. Pre-training: next-token prediction. Best for: text generation, instruction following, reasoning, code. Also works for classification (via prompting).

Why decoder-only dominates now: Scales better (smoother loss curves with more compute/data). Emergent in-context learning at scale (BERT doesn't generalize the same way). Generation capability opens broader use cases. Simpler training objective scales predictably with Chinchilla laws.

Practical use: Still use BERT-family models for: embedding models (sentence-transformers), NER, classification where speed matters, cross-encoder reranking. Use GPT-family for everything generation-related.
Q15. How does Flash Attention work and why is it critical for long contexts?ML ENG
Standard attention problem: For T tokens, must materialize TT attention matrix in HBM (high-bandwidth GPU memory). At T=32K: 32K2 bytes = 2GB. Must read/write to HBM repeatedly. Memory bandwidth is the bottleneck, not compute.

Flash Attention solution: Compute attention in tiles that fit in SRAM (L1/L2 cache, ~50MB, 10-50 TB/s bandwidth). Key insight: use online softmax (Milakov & Gimelshein 2018) to compute running softmax normalization while processing tiles, without seeing all scores first.

Algorithm: Split Q into blocks. For each Q block: iterate over K,V blocks, compute partial attention and running softmax stats. Combine statistics to get exact softmax normalization. Never store full TT matrix.

Results: Same mathematical output as standard attention. O(T) memory vs O(T). 2-4 wall-clock speedup (IO-bound compute-bound). Required for 32K+ context on any GPU. Flash Attention 3 (H100): FP8, 1.5-2 over FA2.
Q16. Explain how you would evaluate a RAG system.AI ENG
Multi-layer evaluation strategy:

Layer 1 Retrieval quality: Context precision@k (fraction of retrieved chunks that are actually relevant). Context recall (did we get all necessary chunks?). Run on test set of (query, relevant_docs) pairs. Tool: RAGAS with labeled ground truth.

Layer 2 Generation quality: Faithfulness (is every claim in the answer supported by retrieved context? LLM judge). Answer relevancy (does answer address the question?). Factual correctness vs ground truth answers. Tools: RAGAS, LangSmith, Langfuse.

Layer 3 End-to-end: Task completion rate (did user get what they needed?). Follow-up question rate (proxy for answer quality). User satisfaction (thumbs up/down). A/B test vs baseline (GPT-4 without RAG? Previous version?).

Layer 4 Failure analysis: No-context queries (does model correctly say 'I don't know'?). Multi-hop (requires combining multiple chunks). Contradictory documents. Stale content. Build test cases for each failure mode.

Production monitoring: Sample 5% of real traffic. Run faithfulness check. Alert if average faithfulness drops below threshold.
Q17. What is MoE (Mixture of Experts) and how is it trained?ML ENG
MoE replaces the dense FFN layer: Instead of one FFN (all params active every token), use N expert FFNs + a router. Router selects top-k experts per token (k=2 standard). Only k of N experts activated. Total params: N more. Active params: k (sparse computation).

Router: scores = Softmax(W_rx). Select top-k. Output = ᵢ score_i FFN_i(x). Weight output by router score.

Load balancing challenge: Without regularization, router collapses all tokens route to same 1-2 experts. Others never train. Fix: auxiliary loss L_aux = ᵢ f_i P_i where f_i = fraction of tokens to expert i, P_i = average router prob. Penalizes uneven routing.

Expert capacity: Fixed buffer per expert per batch. If more tokens than buffer capacity: tokens dropped. Capacity factor = 1.25 to minimize dropping.

DeepSeek-V3: 256 fine-grained experts, top-2 active. 1 shared expert always active (reduces load imbalance). MLA compression of KV cache. 671B total, 37B active. Most efficient large MoE deployed.
Q18. How do you detect and handle data drift in production ML?MLOps
Types: Covariate shift (P(X) changes, P(Y|X) unchanged). Concept drift (P(Y|X) changes relationship itself changes). Label shift (P(Y) changes). Most dangerous: concept drift, hardest to detect without labels.

Detection methods:
  • Statistical tests (continuous monitoring): PSI (Population Stability Index): PSI = (actual - expected) log(actual/expected). PSI > 0.2 = significant drift (alert). KS test for distribution comparison. Run per feature, daily.
  • Output distribution monitoring: Shift in prediction distribution = likely drift even without ground truth.
  • Model-based detection: Train binary classifier: training data = class 0, production data = class 1. If AUC > 0.7: significant drift.
Response playbook: PSI 0.1-0.2: monitor more closely, investigate. PSI >0.2: trigger retraining pipeline. Concept drift: emergency retrain on recent data. Full distribution shift: fall back to simpler model while retraining.

Tools: Evidently AI (excellent drift reports, open source). WhyLogs (lightweight profiling). Arize, Arthur (commercial, real-time).
Q19. What are the main safety risks specific to agentic AI systems?ARCHITECT
Agentic systems = agents take REAL ACTIONS with real consequences, unlike chat LLMs.

OWASP LLM08 Excessive Agency: Agent granted too many permissions. Deletes production data, sends unauthorized emails, makes financial transactions without confirmation. Fix: principle of least privilege read-only by default, write requires explicit grant + HITL.

Prompt injection via tool results: Agent reads external data (email, web page, doc) containing adversarial instructions. Agent follows injected instructions unaware. Fix: sanitize all tool outputs before re-feeding to agent. Structural separation: data context instruction context.

Cascading errors: Step 2 error propagates through steps 3-10. Final answer confidently wrong. No warning. Fix: per-step output validation. Explicit error propagation. Agent should STOP and report on uncertainty rather than continue with bad data.

Goal misgeneralization: Agent achieves literal goal not actual intent. 'Close all tickets' marks all resolved without actually resolving. Fix: outcome validation, not just task completion. HITL for bulk operations.

Infinite loops: Same tool, same input, repeated forever. Fix: loop detector (same tool+input 3 times = halt), max steps limit.
Q20. How does PagedAttention in vLLM improve GPU utilization?MLOps
Problem with naive KV cache: Each request allocates a contiguous block of GPU memory for its KV cache (size = max_seq_len n_layers d_head 2 2 bytes). But actual sequence length varies. Result: internal fragmentation (reserved but unused memory) + external fragmentation (can't fit new request even if total free memory is sufficient). Only 40% utilization.

PagedAttention solution: Inspired by OS virtual memory. KV cache divided into fixed-size pages (typically 16 tokens each). Pages allocated on demand as sequence grows. Non-contiguous: request's KV cache spans multiple non-adjacent physical pages. Page table maps logical to physical pages same as OS virtual-to-physical mapping.

Benefits: 90%+ GPU memory utilization (vs 40%). Copy-on-write page sharing: different requests with same prefix (e.g., system prompt) share physical pages until they diverge. Prefix caching: if same system prompt used by many requests, its KV cache pages are shared across all of them huge savings for chatbots.

Continuous batching: When request finishes, its pages are immediately freed and reassigned to new request. No waiting for full batch to complete. 23 throughput improvement over static batching.
Q&A Volume 1 Extended Q21 to Q60
Q21. Implement softmax numerically stable from scratch.ML ENG
Problem with naive softmax: exp(1000) overflows to inf. exp(-1000)/exp(-1000+1) = 0/0 = NaN.

Numerically stable version: subtract max before exponentiating. Proof that it's equivalent: softmax(z)_i = exp(z_i - max)/exp(z_j - max) = exp(z_i)/exp(z_j) (the max cancels).

import numpy as np def softmax_stable(z): z_shifted = z - z.max(axis=-1, keepdims=True) # subtract max e = np.exp(z_shifted) return e / e.sum(axis=-1, keepdims=True) # Handles large values safely: print(softmax_stable(np.array([1000., 1001., 1002.]))) # [0.09, 0.245, 0.665] correct!

In PyTorch: F.softmax() is already numerically stable. Understanding the trick is what's tested.
Q22. What is the difference between model.eval() and torch.no_grad()?ALL
model.eval(): Sets the model to evaluation mode. Changes behavior of: Dropout (disabled all neurons active), BatchNorm (uses running statistics instead of batch statistics), any custom module that checks training flag. Does NOT disable gradient computation.

torch.no_grad(): Context manager that disables gradient tape. No gradients computed or stored. Reduces memory usage and speeds up inference (~50% faster). Does NOT change Dropout or BatchNorm behavior.

Production inference: Use BOTH together:
model.eval() with torch.no_grad(): output = model(input)

Common bug: Using torch.no_grad() alone in inference Dropout is still active, giving different results each call. model.eval() alone gradients still computed, wasting memory. Always use both.
Q23. Explain the intuition behind attention being 'self' attention.ALL
In standard attention (cross-attention), queries come from one sequence and keys/values from another (encoder-decoder attention). In self-attention, Q, K, V all come from the SAME sequence the sequence attends to itself.

Intuition: Each token asks 'which other tokens in this sequence are most relevant to me?' Token 'it' in 'The cat sat on it' needs to figure out that 'it' refers to 'cat' it attends back to 'cat' (high attention weight). The model learns these referential relationships from data.

Why this is powerful: Direct connections between any two tokens regardless of distance. RNNs must propagate information through T sequential steps to connect token 1 to token T. Self-attention connects them in 1 step (O(1) path length). Long-range dependencies are equally easy as short-range.

Causal mask: In decoder self-attention, token T can only attend to tokens 1..T (upper triangular masked). Prevents 'cheating' by looking at future tokens during training.
Q24. What is gradient accumulation and when do you use it?ML ENG
Problem: You want effective batch size 256 but only fit batch size 8 in GPU memory. Naively: just use batch size 8 (but noisy gradients, poor training).

Gradient accumulation: Instead of updating weights after every batch, accumulate gradients over N steps, then update once. Effective batch size = per_step_batch accumulation_steps.

accumulation_steps = 32 # effective batch = 8 32 = 256 optimizer.zero_grad() for i, (x, y) in enumerate(dataloader): loss = model(x, y) / accumulation_steps # scale loss! loss.backward() # accumulate gradients if (i+1) % accumulation_steps == 0: optimizer.step() optimizer.zero_grad()

Why divide loss by accumulation_steps: Each backward() call adds to the gradient buffer. After N steps, gradients are N larger than a single step of effective batch size. Dividing normalizes to the correct scale.

HuggingFace Trainer: gradient_accumulation_steps=32 handles this automatically.
Q25. How does XGBoost handle missing values?DATA SCI
XGBoost's approach: During training, for each feature with missing values, XGBoost learns the optimal default direction whether missing values should go left or right at each split. This is called the sparsity-aware split finding algorithm.

Algorithm: For each split candidate: evaluate placing missing values in left child, evaluate placing in right child. Choose the direction that gives higher gain. The default direction is stored with the split.

At inference: Missing values are routed to the learned default direction automatically. No imputation needed before prediction.

Why this matters: Most imputation strategies (mean, median, zero) can introduce bias. XGBoost's learned default direction uses the actual statistical relationship between missingness and the target. If 'missing income' correlates with 'low creditworthiness', XGBoost learns this and routes missing income left subtree (high-risk bucket) automatically.

LightGBM and CatBoost: Both handle missing values natively in the same way. In practice: just pass NaN directly, let the model handle it.
Q26. What is the KL divergence and why is it used in VAEs?ML ENG
KL divergence: D_KL(P||Q) = P(x) log(P(x)/Q(x)) = E_P[log P(x) - log Q(x)]. Measures how much distribution P differs from reference distribution Q. NOT symmetric. Always 0. Zero only when P = Q exactly.

In VAEs: The encoder outputs a distribution q(z|x) = N((x), ϲ(x)). We want this posterior to stay close to the prior p(z) = N(0,I). The KL term in the ELBO penalizes divergence:

KL(N(,ϲ)||N(0,I)) = ( + ϲ - log ϲ - 1)

Why this specific prior: N(0,I) is the simplest structured prior. Forces latent space to be organized (not spread out arbitrarily). Without KL: encoder uses the latent space however convenient can't interpolate, can't sample meaningfully. With KL: encoder compresses information into a structured Gaussian space.

-VAE: Multiply KL term by > 1. Forces even more structured (disentangled) latent space. Each latent dimension captures one independent factor of variation.
Q27. Explain how RLHF reward hacking occurs and three ways to prevent it.ML ENG
Reward hacking: Policy learns to maximize the proxy reward model score rather than genuine human preference. The proxy (RM) is an imperfect approximation of what humans actually want.

Common manifestations: Length exploitation (longer responses score higher policy generates verbose padding). Sycophancy (agreeing with user false claims scores higher policy agrees more). Confident hallucination (confident wrong answers score higher than uncertain correct answers).

Prevention 1 KL penalty: L = RM(y|x) - KL(_||_ref). KL term prevents policy from drifting too far from SFT reference. If policy tries to exploit RM, it must also stay near the reference. trades exploitation vs alignment.

Prevention 2 RM ensemble: Train 3-5 independent reward models on different data splits. Use minimum or average score. Exploiting one RM is hard; exploiting all simultaneously is much harder. Consensus reduces individual RM failure modes.

Prevention 3 Adversarial RM evaluation: Actively test whether high-RM-scoring outputs are actually good. Generate reward-hacked examples from policy, evaluate with independent human raters, retrain RM on these examples. Iterative red-teaming of the RM itself.
Q28. How do you implement a RAG system with metadata filtering?AI ENG
Why metadata filtering matters: Without it, a query about 'Company A SLAs' might return chunks from other tenants or partners. Retrieval must be scoped to the right context.

Architecture: At indexing time, store metadata alongside each chunk: {tenant_id, document_type, date, classification, access_level, source_system}. At query time, apply mandatory filters before similarity search.

from qdrant_client import QdrantClient, models client = QdrantClient(url='http://localhost:6333') # Query with metadata filter results = client.search( collection_name='knowledge_base', query_vector=query_embedding, query_filter=models.Filter( must=[ models.FieldCondition( key='tenant_id', match=models.MatchValue(value='tenant_a') ), models.FieldCondition( key='classification', range=models.Range(lte=user_clearance_level) ), models.FieldCondition( key='date', range=models.DatetimeRange( gte='2024-01-01' ) ) ] ), limit=20 )

Critical: Metadata filters must be applied BEFORE vector similarity search (not after), otherwise you retrieve 20 wrong results and filter down to 0.
Q29. What is multi-task learning and when does it outperform single-task?ML ENG
Multi-task learning (MTL): Train a single model on multiple related tasks simultaneously. Shared lower layers, task-specific heads. Tasks share representations and regularize each other.

When MTL outperforms: Tasks share relevant features (NER + POS tagging share syntactic knowledge). Limited data per task (tasks pool their supervision signal). Some tasks provide useful inductive biases for others (sentiment + subjectivity share emotional language patterns).

When single-task is better: Tasks interfere (translation + classification may conflict). One task has vastly more data than others (drowns out small tasks). Tasks require very different architectures.

Loss weighting: L_total = w_i L_i. Uniform weighting often suboptimal. GradNorm (Chen 2018): dynamically adjust weights so all tasks train at similar rate. Uncertainty weighting (Kendall 2018): w_i = 1/(2ᵢ), ᵢ learned tasks with higher uncertainty get lower weight.

In LLMs: Pre-training IS multi-task learning. Next-token prediction on diverse data simultaneously teaches: language understanding, reasoning, coding, math, translation. The diversity of the pre-training corpus IS the multi-task training.
Q30. How do you debug a training run where loss doesn't decrease?ML ENG
Systematic diagnosis:

Step 1 Check data pipeline first: Are labels correct? Are inputs normalized properly? Is there data leakage? Quick check: overfit one batch. model.train() on one batch, run 100 steps. Loss should go to ~0. If not: the model or loss function has a bug, not the data.

Step 2 Check loss function: Compute loss manually on one example. Does it match expected value? For N-class classification at init: loss should be log(N). If wildly different: loss function bug. Check: are you passing logits or probabilities? (F.cross_entropy expects logits; F.nll_loss expects log-probs).

Step 3 Check gradients: After backward(): print [p.grad.norm() for p in model.parameters()]. If all None or 0: backward() not being called, or loss not connected to model params. If very small: vanishing gradient. If NaN: exploding gradient (add gradient clipping).

Step 4 Check learning rate: Try 10 higher. If loss now decreases: LR was too small. Try 10 lower. If loss now decreases: LR was too large (causing divergence). Use LR range test: run for 100 steps with LR increasing exponentially, plot loss vs LR, find the LR just before loss starts increasing.

Step 5 Check data shuffle: Shuffle training data. If loss now decreases: model was memorizing batch order, not learning patterns.
Q31. What is the difference between online and offline evaluation for LLMs?AI ENG
Offline evaluation: Run on a fixed dataset before deployment. Metrics: BLEU, ROUGE, accuracy on benchmarks, LLM-judge scores on golden set. Fast, cheap, reproducible. Weakness: may not correlate with production quality a model that scores well on benchmarks may fail for your specific use case.

Online evaluation: Measure real user behavior in production. Metrics: task completion rate, session abandonment after response, thumbs up/down, follow-up 'that's wrong' corrections, return usage rate. Captures actual user value. Weakness: slow feedback, can't use before deployment, noisy (users' needs vary).

The correlation problem: Offline metrics often don't predict online metrics. A 5% improvement in RAGAS faithfulness may have zero effect on user satisfaction if the main user complaint is about response format, not factual accuracy. Always A/B test before concluding offline improvements translate to production gains.

Shadow deployment: Best of both worlds. New model runs in parallel, its outputs logged but not shown to users. Compare outputs of new vs old model with LLM judge. Detect issues before any user sees them. Promote when judge shows consistent improvement.
Q32. Explain the Adam optimizer's bias correction and why it matters.ML ENG
Problem without bias correction: Adam's first and second moment estimates start at 0. After step 1: m_1 = (1-_1)g_1 (with _1=0.9). This is heavily biased toward 0 g_1 is divided by 0.1 effectively. Learning is very slow in early steps.

Bias correction: Divide moment estimates by (1-_t): m_t = m_t/(1-_1^t) and v_t = v_t/(1-_2^t). At step 1: m_1 = m_1/0.1 = g_1 (correct scale). At step 1000: 1-_1^1000 1 (correction negligible). Only matters in early training.

Practical impact: Without bias correction: first few hundred steps have unstably small effective learning rates. Model appears stuck. With correction: learning begins at the right scale immediately after warmup. This is particularly important for fine-tuning where you want quick adaptation from the first batch.

Lion optimizer (no bias correction needed): Lion uses sign(m_t) for updates magnitude doesn't depend on moment scale. So bias correction is irrelevant. One fewer hyperparameter and less memory (only first moment, no second moment).
Q33. How do you design evaluation for a code generation model?AI ENG
The fundamental difference from text evaluation: Code is executable. You don't need a human to judge quality you run the tests.

pass@k metric (standard): Generate k solutions for each problem. pass@k = P(at least one passes all tests) = 1 - P(all k fail). Estimate: pass@k 1 - C(n-c, k)/C(n, k) where n=total generated, c=passing. Use n=200, k=1,10,100 for HumanEval comparisons.

Test-driven evaluation pipeline:
import subprocess, tempfile, os def evaluate_solution(code: str, test_cases: list) -> bool: with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='w', suffix='.py', delete=False) as f: f.write(code) f.write('\n\n' + '\n'.join(test_cases)) fname = f.name try: result = subprocess.run( ['python', fname], timeout=10, # prevent infinite loops capture_output=True ) return result.returncode == 0 except subprocess.TimeoutExpired: return False finally: os.unlink(fname)

Beyond HumanEval: SWE-bench (real GitHub issues), MBPP (beginner Python), HumanEval+ (harder test cases). CodeBLEU: BLEU + code syntax + dataflow analysis (better than plain BLEU for code). Execution-based metrics always preferred over text-similarity metrics for code.
Q34. What is the concept of 'grounding' in AI and why does it matter?ALL
Grounding: Connecting language model outputs to verifiable, external facts or real-world references. An 'ungrounded' model generates fluent text with no connection to reality it can say anything. A 'grounded' model's outputs are anchored to provided evidence.

Why it matters for enterprise AI: Legal: ungrounded responses create liability. Finance: ungrounded analysis leads to wrong decisions. Healthcare: ungrounded diagnosis endangers patients. Any domain where factual accuracy matters requires grounding.

Grounding mechanisms: RAG (retrieve evidence, generate from evidence). Function calling (look up real data via tools). Citations (require model to cite sources, enabling verification). Constitutional AI (ground responses against a defined set of principles). RLHF from domain experts (ground preference data in domain knowledge).

Evaluating groundedness: RAGAS faithfulness: is every claim in the answer supported by the provided context? Entailment check: is the response logically entailed by the retrieved documents? Citation accuracy: do cited sources actually say what the model claims?

Grounding vs RAG distinction: RAG is one implementation of grounding. Grounding also includes: real-time tool calls, database lookups, code execution, web search, calculator access. Any mechanism that connects generation to external truth.
Q35. How does the Transformer handle sequences longer than its training context?ML ENG
Short answer: it doesn't, by default. A transformer trained on 4K context sees position encodings up to position 4095. At position 4096, it encounters a rotation angle (RoPE) or learned embedding it has never seen quality degrades significantly.

Approaches to handle longer sequences:

1. RoPE interpolation (YaRN/LongRoPE): Scale RoPE frequencies so positions within the extended range map to seen rotation angles. Fine-tune 400 steps on long-context data. LLaMA 3.1: 8K 128K this way.

2. Sliding window attention: Each token only attends to last W tokens. No position OOD. Works for local reasoning tasks. Fails for tasks requiring distant context (like 'what was the password mentioned at the beginning of this 100K document?').

3. Truncation (practical fallback): For many tasks: truncate to context window. For summarization: map-reduce (summarize chunks, then summarize summaries). For QA: retrieve relevant chunks with RAG instead of stuffing everything.

4. State Space Models (Mamba): Avoids position encoding entirely. State h_t summarizes all history. Works with any length. Quality trade-off: loses some long-range recall vs transformer attention.
Q36. What is the difference between type I and type II errors in hypothesis testing?DATA SCI
Type I error (, false positive): Reject H when H is actually true. Conclude there's an effect when there isn't. Controlled by significance threshold . Setting =0.05: 5% chance of falsely declaring a result significant if there's truly no effect.

Type II error (, false negative): Fail to reject H when H is actually true. Miss a real effect. Power = 1- = P(correctly detecting a true effect). Standard target: 80% power (=0.20).

The tradeoff: Reducing (stricter significance) increases (more misses). Reducing requires larger sample size. Both errors have costs balance depends on the application.

A/B testing context: Type I: ship a change that doesn't actually work (wasted engineering effort, possibly user harm). Type II: miss a beneficial change (lost opportunity). Most A/B tests use =0.05 (5% false positive rate) and 80% power. For safety-critical decisions: use =0.01 and higher power.

Common mistake: Confusing 'p-value < 0.05' with '95% probability the effect is real.' P-value is P(data | H is true), NOT P(H | data). The latter requires Bayesian reasoning.
Q37. How do you implement efficient batched inference for embeddings?AI ENG
The problem: 100K documents 50ms per embedding API call = 83 minutes sequential. Solution: parallel async batching.

import asyncio, aiohttp, numpy as np from openai import AsyncOpenAI async def embed_batch(texts: list, client, model='text-embedding-3-small'): resp = await client.embeddings.create(input=texts, model=model) return [r.embedding for r in resp.data] async def embed_all(texts: list, batch_size=2048, max_concurrent=50): client = AsyncOpenAI() semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent) # rate limit batches = [texts[i:i+batch_size] for i in range(0, len(texts), batch_size)] async def _embed(batch): async with semaphore: # max 50 concurrent requests return await embed_batch(batch, client) results = await asyncio.gather(*[_embed(b) for b in batches]) return np.array([emb for batch in results for emb in batch]) # 100K docs, 50 concurrent = ~2 min vs 83 min sequential embeddings = asyncio.run(embed_all(all_texts))

Local model (BGE-M3): Batch on GPU: model.encode(texts, batch_size=256). Sort by length before batching (minimize padding waste). With A100: ~10K embeddings/second at 1024 dims.
Q38. What is catastrophic interference and how does continual learning address it?ML ENG
Catastrophic interference (forgetting): When a neural network trained on task B forgets task A. The new task's gradient updates overwrite the weights that encoded the old task. Demonstrated by McCloskey & Cohen (1989) for neural networks, still a fundamental challenge today.

Why it happens: Neural networks store all knowledge distributed across the same weights. Training on new distribution shifts weights away from configurations needed for old distribution. No separation between 'old knowledge' and 'new knowledge' in the weight space.

Continual learning approaches:
Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC): Penalize changing weights important for old tasks: L = L_new + ᵢ F_i(ᵢ - *ᵢ). F_i = Fisher information (importance weight). Expensive to compute Fisher for large models.

Replay methods: Store small buffer of old task examples. Mix old examples with new during training. Experience replay. Effective but requires storing data.

Progressive neural networks: Add new columns for new tasks, freeze old ones. Lateral connections from old to new. No forgetting but grows with tasks.

LoRA for continual fine-tuning: Practical solution base model weights never modified. New tasks get new adapters. Switch adapters at inference time. Scales to many tasks.
Q39. Explain how you'd design a feedback loop for a production AI system.MLOps
Feedback loops are the most important and most neglected part of MLOps. Without them, models decay as the world changes. With them, models improve automatically from usage.

Explicit feedback channels: Thumbs up/down on responses. User corrections ('actually the answer is...'). Task completion signals (did user abandon after response). Rating prompts (1-5 stars after important decisions).

Implicit feedback signals: Session length after response (longer = engaged, abandoned = bad). Follow-up 'can you clarify' or 'that's wrong' messages. Click-through on cited sources (validates retrieval quality). Return usage (users who come back had good experiences).

Feedback training data pipeline: Thumbs-down responses candidates for DPO rejected examples. User corrections SFT training examples (original input + corrected output). High-rated responses SFT positive examples. Retrieval feedback (was cited chunk actually useful?) embedding fine-tuning dataset.

Automated signal processing: Filter by confidence: only use feedback on cases where model was uncertain (high-signal corrections). Deduplicate: similar feedback examples merged. LLM quality check: ensure corrected examples are actually better than originals (users can also be wrong).

Closed loop: Weekly: collect feedback process add to dataset retrain evaluate deploy. The flywheel effect: better model more trust more usage more feedback better model.
Q40. How does the attention mechanism enable in-context learning?ALL
In-context learning (ICL): LLMs can perform new tasks from just a few examples in the prompt, without any weight updates. Few-shot prompting. This is qualitatively different from traditional ML where the model must be retrained for new tasks.

Mechanism (theoretical understanding, still debated): Attention allows the model to 'look up' patterns in the examples provided in context.
