Prompt adherence: does it fulfill the prompt requirements? Prompt: {prompt} Output: {output} Respond with JSON: {originality, coherence, engagement, adherence, rationale}''' # ... call judge model and return scores

Human evaluation best practices: Blind A/B comparison (evaluator doesn't know which system produced which). Multiple evaluators (at least 3 per sample) for reliability. Inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's alpha). Clear rubric shared with all evaluators. Sample from diverse prompt types.
Q155. What is the most important thing to understand about deploying LLMs in production?ALL
Cost scales with output tokens, not just requests. A single GPT-4o request that generates 2000 tokens costs 15 more than a request that generates 100 tokens ($0.030 vs $0.002). Most teams that deploy LLMs are shocked by costs at scale because they benchmarked cost per request, not cost per token. Every 'generate a comprehensive report' prompt at $0.03 100K requests/day = $3,000/day = $1M+/year. Always model token cost, not request cost.

LLMs are non-deterministic and will surprise you at scale. In testing with 100 requests: everything looks fine. In production with 1M requests: edge cases appear that you never imagined. The model occasionally generates harmful content despite guardrails. It sometimes confidently hallucinates about your specific domain. It occasionally ignores formatting instructions. Design for graceful failure, not perfect behavior.

Evaluation infrastructure is more important than model choice. Teams that can evaluate quickly (golden dataset + LLM judge + automated pipeline) can iterate to better performance in days. Teams without evaluation infrastructure spend months guessing whether changes help. Invest in evaluation first, before optimizing the model. The best team with mediocre models beats the mediocre team with the best models.

Users will abuse it in ways you can't predict. Plan for: jailbreak attempts, competitive intelligence extraction, data exfiltration attempts, adversarial inputs designed to confuse the model. Security is not optional for any LLM deployed to external users.
Q156. How does model quantization affect inference quality and what are the tradeoffs?ML ENG
Quality degradation by quantization level: FP32 FP16/BF16: typically 0% degradation. Same quality, 2 memory savings. This is free. FP16 INT8: 1-3% degradation on most benchmarks. 2 more memory savings. Generally acceptable. INT8 INT4 (GPTQ/AWQ): 3-8% degradation. Depends heavily on model and task. May be acceptable for lower-stakes tasks. INT4 INT2: significant degradation. Not recommended for production.

Task sensitivity to quantization: Very sensitive: mathematical reasoning (small numerical errors compound), long-form code generation (errors propagate). Less sensitive: summarization, classification, simple QA. Evaluation: always measure on YOUR specific task after quantizing. Generic benchmarks may not reflect your use case.

AWQ vs GPTQ quality comparison: Both INT4. AWQ: better perplexity, faster quantization (minutes vs hours for GPTQ). GPTQ: slightly better on some code tasks. AWQ is the current recommendation for most use cases.

Quantized training (QLoRA) vs quantized inference: QLoRA: quantize BASE weights to 4-bit NF4 during fine-tuning. Adapters trained in 16-bit. Result: excellent fine-tuning quality despite 4-bit base. The adapters compensate for quantization error. Different mechanism from post-training quantization adapters are trained to work with the quantized weights.
Q157. What is the role of synthetic data in modern LLM development?ALL
Phi models (Microsoft) the case for synthetic data: Phi-1 (2023): 1.3B parameters, trained primarily on synthetic 'textbook quality' data generated by GPT-4. Outperformed 7B models on coding benchmarks. Key insight: data quality >> data quantity. 7B tokens of curated synthetic > 700B tokens of raw web text for specific capabilities.

Types of synthetic data in LLM development: Pre-training data: generate textbook explanations of concepts, worked examples, code with comments. SFT data: use GPT-4 to generate instruction-following examples in your target format. Preference data (DPO): generate response pairs, use GPT-4 as judge to label preferred/rejected. Reasoning traces: use capable model to generate CoT reasoning, train smaller model to imitate. Math/code verification: generate problems, verify answers programmatically creates infinite labeled data.

Risks and mitigations: Distribution shift: synthetic data reflects the generator's biases and errors. Mitigation: human review of 5-10% sample, domain expert validation. Model collapse: training on synthetic next model degrades. Mitigation: always mix with human data, track provenance. Error amplification: if GPT-4 makes systematic errors, model trained on its outputs will too. Mitigation: verify factual claims in synthetic data against ground truth.

2025 state: Synthetic data is now mainstream. Every major model family (LLaMA 3, Gemma, Phi, Qwen) uses significant synthetic data. The bottleneck has shifted from 'how do we get data?' to 'how do we generate high-quality, diverse, verified synthetic data?'
Q158. How do you handle token budget constraints in production LLM applications?AI ENG
Token budget management strategies:

1. Measure before you optimize:
import tiktoken enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model('gpt-4o') def count_tokens(messages: list) -> dict: total = 0 breakdown = {} for msg in messages: role_tokens = 4 # message overhead content_tokens = len(enc.encode(msg['content'])) breakdown[msg['role']] = content_tokens total += role_tokens + content_tokens return {'total': total, 'breakdown': breakdown} # Before sending: check budget token_count = count_tokens(messages) if token_count['total'] > 100000: # 100K budget messages = compress_context(messages)

2. Context compression strategies (ordered by quality): Sliding window: keep last N turns (lossy, fast). Summarize old turns: run cheap model (gpt-4o-mini) to summarize turns beyond N (preserves info, adds latency/cost). Selective eviction: remove tool results that weren't cited in subsequent reasoning (smart, requires tracing). Semantic deduplication: cluster similar messages, keep representative (good for repeated patterns).

3. Prompt compression: LLMLingua: remove low-information tokens from retrieved context. 3-5 compression. ~5-10ms latency overhead. Selective context: include only the 3 sentences most relevant to the query from each retrieved chunk (instead of full chunks).

4. Output length control: System prompt: 'Be concise. Answer in 3 sentences maximum for simple questions.' max_tokens parameter: hard cap. Streaming + early detection: if answer complete before max_tokens stop generation early.
Q159. What is the key insight behind why transformers generalize better than RNNs?ML ENG
Path length and gradient flow: RNN: information from token 1 must traverse T sequential steps to reach token T. Each step: gradient multiplied by Wh '(z). Long paths vanishing gradient first tokens ignored. Transformer: any two tokens connected directly via attention. Gradient path length = 1 regardless of sequence length. Information from token 1 can directly influence token T through a single attention edge.

Expressive power per parameter: RNN hidden state: fixed-size (h dimensions). Must compress entire past history into h numbers. Long sequences information loss. Transformer attention: O(T) connections available. Can maintain different 'routing' for every (query, key) pair. More expressive: can represent 'token T attends to token 3, 7, and 42 specifically, not all past tokens uniformly'.

Parallelism enables better training: RNN: must process sequentially during training. Gradient flows through every timestep. Training on 1000-token sequence: 1000 backward steps. Transformer: all positions processed in parallel. Training on 1000-token sequence: same as 1-token in terms of parallelism. Faster convergence per epoch. This means transformers can train on more data in the same time, leading to better generalization.

The price: Transformers need O(T) memory and compute for attention. Quadratic scaling limits practical context length. This is why RNNs and SSMs (Mamba) remain relevant for very long sequences.
Q160. What advice would you give someone preparing for a senior AI architect interview?ARCHITECT
The most important shift in mindset: Senior architect interviews test judgment, not knowledge. They want to know: can you make the right tradeoffs? Can you say 'this is where I don't know, here's how I'd find out'? Can you design something that will actually work in production, not just on a whiteboard?

Concrete preparation advice:

1. Have a real system to talk about: 'I built a RAG system for X that handled Y million documents with Z latency at cost $W.' Know every decision you made. Why hybrid retrieval over pure vector? Why that embedding model? What broke at scale? Interviewers probe depth relentlessly. If you didn't actually build it, you will be found out.

2. Know the cost math cold: Cost per token by provider. When self-hosting breaks even vs API. Token vs time vs quality tradeoffs. Architects who can't speak to cost are not ready for architect roles. Every architecture decision has a cost implication.

3. Lead with clarifying questions: When given a design problem: 'What's the expected query volume? What's the latency SLA? What's the budget? What does the current data infrastructure look like?' This is architect behavior. Junior engineers jump to solutions. Architects gather requirements first.

4. Know what you don't know: 'I've read about this but haven't implemented it myself here's how I'd approach it and what I'd validate first.' This is much better than bluffing through something you don't know.

5. Connect every technical decision to business impact: Not 'we use GQA instead of MHA.' But 'we use GQA because it reduces KV cache memory 4, which halves our inference cost, enabling us to scale to 100K daily users within budget.'

Final checklist before your interview:
✅ Implement attention from scratch (no notes)
✅ Derive backprop on paper
✅ Design a RAG system end-to-end (live)
✅ Explain LoRA math without hesitation
✅ Answer every Q&A in under 2 min (timed)
✅ Know your cost-per-token numbers cold
✅ Walk through AKP 6 planes from memory
✅ Lead every design Q with clarifying questions
Governance is not a wrapper around the platform it is foundational to it. The AKP rightly puts Governance/Trust/Identity as the top plane enforced on every request. This tab covers what is missing from most agentic platform guides: the PRR checklist specific to AI, Artifact Registry, the Data Privacy Plane, data classification at ingestion, red-teaming with real tools, irreversible action gates, Saga compensation for agentic workflows, and HA for MCP servers.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

AI governance is the set of rules and checks that decide what an AI system is allowed to do, with whose data, on whose authority, and with what audit trail. Think of it as the “am I allowed to ship this?” layer that sits above the technical platform. Without governance, even a brilliant AI feature gets blocked by legal, security, or compliance — usually one week before launch.

Why this is separate from safety: safety is “does the model behave?” (a technical question, answered with tests). Governance is “are we permitted to use it this way?” (a process question, answered with policies and approvals). You need both. A safe model used on data you should not have touched is still a fired-CTO incident.

What this tab covers: the Production Readiness Review (PRR) checklist specific to AI/agentic systems (which boxes must be ticked before a system can go live), the Artifact Registry (every model, prompt, and dataset is versioned and traceable), data classification at the point of ingestion (so the system knows what is public vs. internal vs. confidential), Saga compensation patterns (how to undo an action when an agent does the wrong thing), and high-availability setup for the MCP tool servers that agents depend on.

Production Readiness Review (PRR) for AI/Agentic Systems
PRR Checklist AI/Agent Specific
  • Model card present: Which model, version, training data summary, known limitations, benchmark results, capability boundaries. Required before any production promotion.
  • Eval results on domain golden set: Score on task-specific golden dataset (min 100 curated examples). Must meet or exceed previous version baseline. No regression allowed.
  • Hallucination rate benchmarked: RAGAS faithfulness score on domain test set. Threshold: ≥0.88 for production. Lower than 0.88 = not ready.
  • Risk tier classified: Informational (agent answers questions, no system writes) vs Decision-support (agent recommends, human decides) vs Autonomous-action (agent writes to production systems). Each tier has proportional controls.
  • HITL gates defined: For every irreversible action in the workflow: which gate fires, which role approves, what is the timeout escalation path. No autonomous production writes without a defined gate.
  • Fallback behavior documented: If primary model is unavailable: what happens? If retrieval returns zero chunks: does agent say "I don't know" or hallucinate? Tested explicitly.
  • Blast radius assessed: If this agent malfunctions: how many records can it affect? Which systems can it write to? What is the maximum financial/operational damage? Documented and accepted by stakeholder.
  • Rollback plan: How to revert to previous model/prompt/retrieval version within 30 minutes. Tested in staging. Runbook signed off by ops team.
  • Cost budget set: Max tokens per run, max runs per day, cost alert threshold. Without budget: one runaway agent can exhaust the organization's API quota.
  • Red team exercise completed: At least 2 hours of adversarial testing per agent capability. Automated (Garak) + manual. Results documented and issues addressed before promotion.
Model Risk Management Framework
Tier 0 InformationalAgent answers questions from knowledge base. Read-only. No system writes. Controls: faithfulness check, scope restriction, citation requirement. Low risk.
Tier 1 Decision SupportAgent recommends an action, human decides. E.g., "Draft a partner response for CS agent review." Controls: T0 controls + human review gate before any action. Medium risk.
Tier 2 Supervised AutonomousAgent executes actions with HITL gate for high-impact operations. E.g., "Onboard partner auto-approve for low-risk steps, gate for provisioning." Controls: T1 + OPA policy + action budget + audit trail. High risk.
Tier 3 Fully AutonomousAgent executes end-to-end without HITL for individual steps. Only after extensive L2 track record. E.g., routine carrier rate updates. Controls: all previous + weekly human audit + instant rollback + anomaly detection. Critical.
Rule: No agent starts at Tier 2 or 3. Every new agent begins at Tier 0 (informational/shadow mode), accumulates a quality track record, then formally advances. Demotion is mandatory if quality degrades.
Artifact Registry: Versioning Everything
What Goes in the Artifact Registry
  • Prompt artifacts: Every system prompt, few-shot example set, and prompt template version-controlled with semantic hash. If hash changes without registry update: CI fails. Enables: which prompt version produced which output for every production decision.
  • Model artifacts: Model name + version + provider. Pin to specific model version (e.g., claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022, NOT claude-3-5-sonnet-latest). Unpinned model = silent behavior change on provider's update schedule.
  • Embedding index artifacts: Which embedding model, which chunking strategy, which document set, build timestamp. Index version pinned per deployment. Retrieval behavior changes are version-controlled, not silent.
  • Tool schemas: Every MCP tool definition version-controlled. Schema change = new version = agents explicitly updated to use new version.
  • Evaluation datasets: Golden test sets versioned. When domain experts update examples: new dataset version, full regression against new version before promotion.
Python Artifact Registry pattern
import hashlib, json from datetime import datetime class ArtifactRegistry: def register(self, artifact_type, name, content, metadata=None): "Register an artifact; return content hash as version." content_str = json.dumps(content, sort_keys=True) content_hash = hashlib.sha256(content_str.encode()).hexdigest()[:16] record = { "type": artifact_type, # "prompt" | "model" | "index" | "tool" "name": name, "version": content_hash, "content": content, "registered_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(), "metadata": metadata or {} } self.store[f"{artifact_type}:{name}:{content_hash}"] = record return content_hash def get_for_decision(self, decision_id): "Return all artifact versions that participated in a decision." return self.decision_audit[decision_id] # full provenance # CI check: fail if any deployed artifact changed without registry update def ci_hash_check(deployed_prompt, registry): current_hash = hashlib.sha256(deployed_prompt.encode()).hexdigest()[:16] if not registry.exists("prompt", current_hash): raise ValueError(f"Prompt hash {current_hash} not in registry block deploy")
DPP (Data Privacy Plane) & PII at Ingestion
  • PII must be masked at INGESTION, not just at output: Vector embeddings themselves can leak PII. If you embed "John Smith ordered 50 units of Product X" and an attacker queries "who ordered Product X?" the embedding space may surface the PII. Mask before embedding: "User_1234 ordered 50 units of Product X."
  • Data classification at ingestion time: Every document classified on ingest: PUBLIC / INTERNAL / CONFIDENTIAL / RESTRICTED. Classification propagated to: vector DB metadata (query filter enforced), graph DB edge attributes, RDBMS column tags. Classification at API boundary alone is insufficient retrieval bypasses API-level checks.
  • DPP pipeline: (1) NER + regex PII detection on raw document. (2) Replace PII with pseudonymous tokens (consistent per entity across documents). (3) Store PII-to-token mapping in encrypted vault (for authorized re-identification). (4) Embed and index the pseudonymized version only. (5) At output: re-identify only for authorized requestors.
  • Data residency at storage layer: Vector DB namespace per region: EU documents in eu-west-1 Qdrant collection, US in us-east-1. A query from an EU user NEVER crosses to US collection, even if US collection has more relevant content. Enforced at storage topology, not just application logic network topology + IAM policies prevent cross-region access at the infrastructure level.
  • GDPR right-to-erasure implementation: Deletion request (1) delete from vector DB by document ID filter, (2) delete from graph DB by entity ID, (3) delete from RDBMS, (4) re-embed and replace any derived chunks. Audit log of deletion event preserved (metadata only, no PII).
Red-Teaming AI/Agentic Systems
Structured Red-Team Exercise
  • Automated red-teaming (Garak): Garak is an LLM vulnerability scanner. Probes: prompt injection, jailbreaks, harmful content, data extraction, encoding attacks. Runs as CLI: python -m garak --model openai --probes all --report garak_report.json. Integrate in CI: fail if critical vulnerabilities found.
  • Automated red-team with ARTKIT (BCG): Structured adversarial test generation. Define attack objectives, generate test cases, evaluate responses. Output: vulnerability report with severity classification.
  • Manual red-team scope: For each new agent capability, 2-hour manual red team. Attack categories: (1) Jailbreaks (override system prompt). (2) Prompt injection via tool results. (3) Data exfiltration (extract other users' data). (4) Resource exhaustion (force expensive computation loops). (5) Knowledge poisoning (inject false information via document upload). (6) Unauthorized actions (convince agent to take actions beyond scope).
  • Red team findings gates: Critical (agent takes unauthorized production actions): block release. High (agent leaks cross-tenant data): block release. Medium (agent produces harmful content): fix before release. Low (agent is suboptimally helpful): track and improve post-release.
Bash Garak automated probing
# Install and run Garak against your LLM endpoint pip install garak # Probe for prompt injection specifically python -m garak \ --model rest \ --model_type openai \ --probes promptinject \ --report_prefix ./reports/garak_promptinject # Full probe suite (takes ~30min for GPT-4o) python -m garak \ --model openai --model_name gpt-4o \ --probes all \ --report_prefix ./reports/garak_full # Parse results block CI if critical found python scripts/parse_garak.py \ --report ./reports/garak_full.report.jsonl \ --fail-on critical,high
Irreversible Action Gates & Saga Pattern
  • Irreversible action classification: Every tool that modifies external state must be classified: Reversible (can be undone: create draft, soft delete), Partially reversible (can be corrected: send email can send correction), Irreversible (cannot undo: process payment, permanently delete, send regulatory notification). Irreversible tools require explicit HITL gate, no exceptions.
  • Saga pattern for agentic multi-step workflows: If an agent has completed steps 1-4 of a 6-step order management workflow and step 5 fails: the saga coordinator automatically executes compensating actions in reverse order. Step 4's compensating action, then step 3's, etc. Each tool must declare its compensating action at registration time.
Python Saga coordinator for agentic workflows
class SagaCoordinator: def __init__(self): self.completed_steps = [] # stack of (step, result, compensating_fn) async def execute_step(self, step_name, action_fn, compensate_fn, *args): try: result = await action_fn(*args) self.completed_steps.append((step_name, result, compensate_fn)) return result except Exception as e: await self._compensate() # rollback all completed steps raise SagaFailure(step_name, str(e), self.completed_steps) async def _compensate(self): # Execute compensating actions in REVERSE order for step_name, result, compensate_fn in reversed(self.completed_steps): try: await compensate_fn(result) except Exception as e: # Log and continue best-effort compensation await self.audit.log_compensation_failure(step_name, str(e)) # Usage in agentic workflow: saga = SagaCoordinator() order_id = await saga.execute_step( "create_order", oms.create_order, oms.cancel_order, # compensating action partner_id, line_items ) release_id = await saga.execute_step( "release_order", wms.release, wms.cancel_release, # compensating action order_id )
HA for MCP Servers
  • MCP server HA pattern: Deploy each MCP server as a K8s Deployment with min 2 replicas. Health check: GET /health returns 200 only when all tool connections (DB, external API) are healthy. K8s readiness probe removes unhealthy pod from load balancer. Circuit breaker (Resilience4j / custom) at MCP gateway: if >50% of requests to an MCP server fail open circuit return cached result or structured error.
  • Bulkheads for tool isolation: Separate thread pools (or K8s resource quotas) per MCP server. A slow or failing external API (e.g., Salesforce CRM down) consumes its own bulkhead's threads does not starve unrelated tools. Pattern: each MCP server has a dedicated connection pool and timeout budget. Tool A failure cannot cascade to Tool B.
Prerequisites: Agentic Platform
PRR Checklist Gate Before Every Production Release
10 Non-Negotiable Checks
1
Model card present
Model version, training data summary, known limitations, capability boundaries documented.
2
Domain golden set eval passes
≥100 curated (input, expected) pairs. Score meets or exceeds previous version. No regression allowed.
3
Hallucination rate benchmarked
RAGAS faithfulness ≥0.88 on domain test set. Below 0.88 = not ready for production.
4
Risk tier classified (T0T3)
Informational / Decision-support / Supervised autonomous / Fully autonomous. Controls applied accordingly.
5
HITL gates defined
Every irreversible action has a defined gate, approver role, and timeout escalation path.
 
6
Fallback behaviour documented
If model unavailable: what happens? If retrieval returns zero chunks: does agent say "I don't know" or hallucinate?
7
Blast radius assessed + accepted
Maximum records affected, systems writable, financial exposure. Documented and signed off by product owner.
8
Rollback plan tested
Can revert to previous model/prompt/retrieval version within 30 minutes. Tested in staging. Runbook signed off.
9
Cost budget set
Max tokens per run, max runs per day, cost alert threshold. Without this: one runaway agent exhausts quota.
10
Red team exercise completed
≥2 hours adversarial testing: automated (Garak) + manual. Results documented. Issues fixed before promotion.
Risk Tier Model T0 to T3
T0 Informational (Read-only)
What: Agent answers questions from knowledge base. No system writes. Examples: FAQ chatbot, document Q&A, knowledge assistant. Controls: Faithfulness check, scope restriction, citation requirement. Start here for all new agents.
T1 Decision Support (Human decides)
What: Agent recommends an action; human decides and acts. Examples: draft partner response for CS agent review, suggest order routing. Controls: T0 controls + human review gate before any external action.
T2 Supervised Autonomous (HITL for high-risk)
What: Agent executes actions autonomously except high-impact operations which require HITL gate. Examples: auto-approve low-risk partner config changes, gate on provisioning. Controls: T1 + OPA policy + action budget + audit trail.
T3 Fully Autonomous (Only after proven T2 track record)
What: Agent executes end-to-end without HITL for individual steps. Only after extensive T2 track record. Examples: routine carrier rate updates, automated data refreshes. Controls: All previous + weekly human audit + instant rollback + anomaly detection.
Rule: No agent starts at T2 or T3. Every new agent begins at T0 (shadow/read-only), accumulates a quality track record over 4+ weeks, then formally advances with PRR sign-off. Demotion is mandatory if quality degrades.
Saga Pattern Compensating Transactions in Agentic Workflows
What happens when step 5 fails after steps 1-4 succeeded?
Step 1: Validate
→
Step 2: Create order
→
Step 3: Allocate stock
→
Step 4: Notify WMS
→
Step 5: FAILS
Without Saga: Steps 1-4 committed. Step 5 failed. Systems are inconsistent. Manual cleanup required.
Step 5 fails
→
Undo Step 4
→
Undo Step 3
→
Undo Step 2
→
System consistent
With Saga: Each step registers a compensating action. On failure: execute compensating actions in reverse order. System returns to consistent state automatically.
AI Governance Policy vs Technology Controls
RiskPolicy ControlTechnical ControlVerification
HallucinationRAG-only policy for factual domainsFaithfulness score gate (>0.85)RAGAS eval in CI pipeline
Prompt injectionMandatory input validation policyInput sanitiser regex + LLM judgeRed team injection tests in CI
Data leakage (PII)Data classification policyPresidio PII masker at ingestionAudit logs reviewed weekly
Bias / discriminationFairness review for HR/credit AIFairness metrics in eval harnessDemographic parity test before deploy
Model driftRetraining SLA (monthly max)PSI drift alert (>0.2)Weekly automated drift report
Unauthorised useTiered access approval policyOPA policy + API key scopingMonthly access review
EU AI Act Risk Tier Decision
Which EU AI Act tier does your AI system fall into?
Unacceptable risk (BANNED)
Social scoring, real-time biometric surveillance in public, subliminal manipulation. Cannot deploy in EU regardless of safeguards.
High risk (heavy compliance)
HR/recruitment AI, credit scoring, medical devices, critical infrastructure, law enforcement. Requires: conformity assessment, human oversight, logging, transparency to affected persons.
Limited risk (disclosure only)
Chatbots, deepfake generators. Must disclose to users that they're interacting with AI. Minimal compliance burden.
Minimal risk (no obligation)
Spam filters, AI in games, recommendation systems (for most contexts). No mandatory requirements under the Act.
Policy vs Technical Controls
RiskPolicy ControlTechnical ControlVerification
HallucinationRAG-only for factual domainsFaithfulness gate >0.85RAGAS eval in CI
Prompt injectionMandatory input validationSanitiser + LLM judgeRed-team tests in CI
Data leakageData classification policyPresidio masker at ingestionAudit logs weekly
Model driftRetraining SLA (monthly)PSI alert >0.2Weekly drift report
EU AI Act Which Tier?
EU AI Act risk tier for your system
Unacceptable (BANNED)
Social scoring, real-time biometric surveillance in public, subliminal manipulation. Cannot deploy in EU.
High risk (heavy compliance)
HR/recruitment, credit scoring, medical devices, critical infrastructure. Conformity assessment required.
Limited risk
Chatbots, deepfakes. Must disclose AI to users. Minimal compliance burden.
Minimal risk
Spam filters, AI in games, most recommendation systems. No mandatory requirements.
🌍 Real-world use case: Putting an agent platform through a production-readiness review. Each release must pass a PRR checklist, register its model and data lineage in an artifact registry, clear a red-team pass (Garak), and declare a rollback path — so when an auditor or incident asks “what changed and who approved it,” the answer is one query away.
Agent Runtime Operations covers the operational gaps in most agentic platform guides: the four-layer memory architecture, the context budget manager, prompt drift detection, retrieval quality signals per RAG call, agent task tree visualization for debugging, and enterprise legacy connector specifics for IIB/TIBCO in an agent context.
What is this tab? This tab is the operations playbook for running agents reliably after they leave the prototype stage and start interacting with real systems, real users, and real budgets.
Why it matters: Agent failures are rarely single-model failures; they usually come from memory growth, tool loops, retrieval misses, prompt drift, or opaque multi-step execution trees that classical app monitoring does not explain.
How to use it: Start with memory and context budget management, then move through drift detection, retrieval signals, and legacy-system integration patterns in the same order agents usually fail in production.
Example: If an agent suddenly gets more expensive and less reliable, this tab tells you which metrics to inspect before you blame the base model.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

Agent runtime ops covers everything you need to actually run agents in production — not just build them. Agents differ from regular software in two big ways: they have memory (some short, some long) and they take steps in a loop. Both create new failure modes that classical ops tools were not designed for, so you need a new toolbox.

Why this is more than a sub-tab of MLOps: a single agent run can call the LLM 20 times, hit 6 tools, retrieve from 3 knowledge sources, and run for several minutes. When it fails, “the model returned an error” is not a useful answer — you need to see the whole tree of steps the agent took, why it chose each one, what each tool returned, and where it went off course. Standard logs are not enough.

What this tab covers: the four-layer memory architecture (working, episodic, semantic, procedural — what each one is for in plain words), the context budget manager (how to fit a long conversation back into a fixed-size window), prompt drift detection (catching when a prompt that worked yesterday starts failing today), retrieval quality signals (was the right document actually found?), agent task tree visualisation for debugging, and how to wire agents into enterprise integration buses (IIB / TIBCO).

Four-Layer Memory Architecture
Memory Architecture Deep Dive
Working Memory (in-context)The current context window. All messages, tool results, reasoning traces for the active run. Ephemeral destroyed when session ends. Active management required as it fills: summarize old messages, evict low-importance tool results. Target: always keep <70% full to preserve generation quality.
Episodic Memory (session history)Persisted log of past agent interactions for a given user/entity. What happened in past conversations? Stored in vector DB with timestamps. Retrieved by semantic similarity to current context. Enables: "Last time you asked about Company A SLAs, here's what I told you." Makes agents genuinely smarter over time, not just within one session.
Semantic Memory (world knowledge)Long-term factual knowledge about the domain: partner configurations, system architectures, business rules, product catalog, engineering standards. This is your RAG knowledge base. Retrieved at each step by relevance. Updated via ingestion pipeline as domain evolves.
Procedural Memory (skill library)Library of reusable agent workflows, decision trees, and tool-use patterns. "How to onboard a new partner." "How to diagnose a stuck order." Retrieved at task start. Encoded as structured workflow templates that agents can follow rather than re-discovering the procedure each time. Dramatically reduces errors on well-understood tasks.
Python MemoryManager implementation
class AgentMemoryManager: def __init__(self, session_id, entity_id, vector_db, budget=80_000): self.session_id = session_id self.entity_id = entity_id # user or partner this agent is working with self.vector_db = vector_db self.token_budget = budget # max tokens to allow in working memory self.working = [] # current context window messages async def get_context(self, current_task): "Assemble context from all four memory layers." # 1. Semantic: retrieve relevant domain knowledge semantic = await self.vector_db.search(current_task, top_k=5) # 2. Episodic: retrieve relevant past interactions episodic = await self.vector_db.search( current_task, namespace=f"episodic:{self.entity_id}", top_k=3) # 3. Procedural: fetch workflow template if task matches procedure = await self.vector_db.search( current_task, namespace="procedures", top_k=1) # 4. Working memory managed by context budget working = self._manage_working_memory() return {"semantic": semantic, "episodic": episodic, "procedure": procedure, "working": working} def _manage_working_memory(self): "Evict oldest non-critical messages if approaching token budget." total = sum(m["tokens"] for m in self.working) while total > self.token_budget * 0.7 and len(self.working) > 4: # Summarize the oldest 5 messages oldest = self.working[:5] summary = self._summarize(oldest) # cheap LLM call self.working = [summary] + self.working[5:] total = sum(m["tokens"] for m in self.working) return self.working
Context Budget Manager
  • Why context budget management is non-negotiable: In a 10-hop agentic workflow: 8K tokens for system prompt + 2K per tool result 5 tools + 1K per retrieved chunk 10 chunks + 2K user message = 32K tokens by hop 3. LLaMA 3.1 8B: 128K context limit. But quality degrades in the "lost in the middle" zone (middle of very long context). Budget manager keeps context in the quality zone.
  • Token accounting: Every message, tool result, and retrieved chunk has an estimated token count (use tiktoken for exact count). Budget manager tracks running total. When approaching threshold (70%): trigger compression decision.
  • Compression strategies: (1) Summarize old messages (oldest 5 1 summary, 80% token savings). (2) Evict low-relevance tool results (keep result if it was cited in subsequent reasoning, evict if not). (3) Compress retrieved chunks (extract only the cited sentences, discard the rest). (4) Summarize episodic context (keep key facts, discard conversation flow).
  • What to NEVER evict: System prompt. The most recent user message. The most recent tool result. Any result that was cited in a reasoning step as basis for a decision.
Prompt Drift Detection & Retrieval Quality Signals
Prompt Drift Detection
  • What prompt drift is: A prompt that worked well three months ago may work poorly today because: the model was silently updated by the provider (even pinned versions can have subtle changes), the data distribution shifted (new types of queries the prompt wasn't designed for), or user expectations evolved. Unlike model drift, prompt drift is invisible unless you measure it.
  • Detection method: Maintain a rolling window of LLM-judge scores on production outputs (sample 5-10%). Statistical test: is the current week's average score significantly below last month's baseline? Use Welch's t-test. Alert if p < 0.05 and absolute drop > 5%.
  • Per-prompt evaluation: Each prompt in the artifact registry has a baseline score established at promotion time. Weekly scheduled job: sample 50 recent calls per prompt, score with LLM judge, compare to baseline. Flag prompts with >5% degradation for review.
Python Prompt drift monitor
from scipy import stats import numpy as np def detect_prompt_drift(baseline_scores, current_scores, threshold=0.05): "Welch t-test to detect prompt quality drift." t_stat, p_val = stats.ttest_ind(baseline_scores, current_scores, equal_var=False) # Welch's mean_drop = np.mean(baseline_scores) - np.mean(current_scores) drift_detected = p_val < 0.05 and mean_drop > threshold return { "drift_detected": drift_detected, "p_value": p_val, "mean_drop": mean_drop, "action": "review prompt" if drift_detected else "ok" }
Retrieval Quality Signals per RAG Call
Context Budget Manager Keeping Agents Within Window
Token Budget by Context Source
System prompt~2K tokens (fixed)
Retrieved context (RAG)~8K tokens
Tool results (accumulates)~3K per hop
Conversation historygrows per turn
After 5 hops in a complex workflow: easily 40K+ tokens. Without management: context overflow = agent failure.
Context approaching limit what to evict?
NEVER evict
System prompt, most recent user message, any result that was cited in a subsequent reasoning step.
Summarise first
Oldest 5 conversation turns run cheap LLM call 1 summary (~200 tokens). 80% token savings for old turns.
Evict if not cited
Tool results that were not referenced in any subsequent reasoning step can be dropped the agent already used (or ignored) them.
Compress retrieved chunks
Extract only the sentences actually cited from each retrieved chunk. Discard surrounding paragraphs. 60-80% reduction.
Prompt Drift Detection Continuous Quality Monitoring
1
Establish baseline at promotion time
When a prompt version is promoted to production: run LLM-judge on 100 representative production calls. Record mean score (e.g., 8.4/10). This is the baseline against which future scores are compared.
2
Weekly: sample and score 50 production calls per prompt
Sample 50 recent calls for each active prompt version. Run same LLM-judge. Compute mean score for this week.
3
Statistical test: has quality changed?
Welch's t-test (unequal variance): compare this week's 50 scores vs baseline 100 scores. If p < 0.05 AND mean dropped > 5%: drift detected.
4
Alert + root cause
4 causes: (1) model silently updated by provider, (2) query distribution shifted (new use cases), (3) knowledge base became stale, (4) upstream data changed. Each has a different fix.
5
Fix and re-baseline
Adjust prompt, refresh knowledge base, or pin model version. After fix: run new baseline evaluation. New baseline replaces old. Continuous improvement loop.
IIB/TIBCO Legacy Adapter Bridging Agents to Enterprise Systems
The fundamental mismatch: Agents expect synchronous responses. IIB/TIBCO is asynchronous.
Agent calls tool
→
MCP Gateway
→
IIB Adapter publishes to MQ queue
→
IIB processes & calls Sterling/SAP
→
Response on reply queue
→
Adapter returns to agent (30s timeout)
Correlation ID
Every message gets a UUID. Adapter matches reply to waiting agent call. Without it: responses go to wrong caller.
30s timeout
Agent call has a hard timeout. IIB slower than 30s: structured error returned. Agent handles error in its reasoning (retry, escalate, continue).
Schema pinning
IIB message schema changes upstream: adapter validates against pinned schema, raises alert. Agent never receives silently broken data.
  • Log per RAG call: For every retrieval operation in production, log: top-k chunk IDs and similarity scores, whether the final answer cited those chunks (faithfulness), user satisfaction signal after the interaction. These signals are the foundation for continuous retrieval improvement.
  • Retrieval drift detection: If the average similarity score of top-1 retrieved chunk is trending downward: knowledge base is diverging from query distribution. New types of queries the index can't answer well. Trigger: add new documents to knowledge base, or fine-tune embedding model on domain-specific pairs.
  • Citation rate: What fraction of retrieved chunks were actually cited in the final answer? Low citation rate = retrieval is returning irrelevant chunks. This metric directly drives chunking and retrieval improvements.
Agent Task Tree Visualization
  • What it shows: For a multi-step agent run, visualize the full execution tree: root task subtasks tool calls LLM reasoning steps. Each node shows: inputs, outputs, latency, token cost, success/failure status.
  • Implementation: Use OpenTelemetry spans with parent-child relationships. Root span = overall agent run. Each agent step = child span. Each tool call = grandchild span. Each LLM call = great-grandchild span with gen_ai.* attributes. Grafana Tempo / Jaeger renders the tree automatically from span relationships.
  • Debugging value: "Why did the agent take action X?" Trace the span tree: LLM reasoning step that preceded action X tool result that informed that reasoning retrieval that fed that tool result. Full causal chain visible in <30 seconds. Without this: debugging takes hours.
Enterprise Legacy Connectors (IIB/TIBCO in Agent Context)
IIB/TIBCO Adapter Design for Agents
  • The fundamental mismatch: Agents expect tool calls to be synchronous request-response (call tool get result continue reasoning). IIB and TIBCO flows are often asynchronous message-based (publish message wait for response on reply queue may take seconds or minutes). The adapter must bridge this gap.
  • Synchronous adapter pattern: Agent calls MCP tool tool publishes message to IIB tool polls reply queue with timeout (e.g., 30s) if reply arrives: return to agent. If timeout: return structured error. Agent handles error in its reasoning (retry, escalate, or continue with partial info).
  • Schema-pinned adapter: IIB message schema changes upstream should not silently break the agent. Solution: adapter validates incoming IIB message against a pinned JSON schema. Schema drift (IIB adds/removes fields) adapter logs warning and may reject alert for human review update adapter with new schema version before continuing.
  • Sterling OMS via IIB: Sterling APIs return XML. Adapter converts XML JSON for agent consumption. Maps Sterling error codes to structured agent-readable error objects. Handles Sterling's pagination (getOrderList returns multiple pages) transparently to the agent.
Python IIB async adapter for MCP
import asyncio, uuid class IIBToolAdapter: "Bridge between synchronous MCP tool call and async IIB message flow." def __init__(self, mq_client, request_queue, reply_queue, timeout_s=30): self.mq = mq_client self.request_q = request_queue self.reply_q = reply_queue self.timeout = timeout_s self.pending = {} # correlation_id Future async def call(self, operation, payload): correlation_id = str(uuid.uuid4()) future = asyncio.get_event_loop().create_future() self.pending[correlation_id] = future # Publish request to IIB self.mq.publish(self.request_q, { "correlationId": correlation_id, "operation": operation, "payload": payload }) try: response = await asyncio.wait_for(future, self.timeout) return self._validate_schema(operation, response) except asyncio.TimeoutError: del self.pending[correlation_id] raise ToolTimeoutError(f"{operation} timed out after {self.timeout}s") def on_reply(self, msg): # called by MQ consumer on reply queue corr_id = msg["correlationId"] if corr_id in self.pending: self.pending[corr_id].set_result(msg["payload"])
Short-Lived Scoped Tool Credentials
  • Why shared service accounts are wrong: A shared API key for "all OMS operations" means a bug in one agent can affect all OMS operations platform-wide. A compromised agent has full OMS access. Audit trail shows "service_account_x did it" not which agent or workflow.
  • Short-lived credential pattern: At workflow start, the MCP gateway requests a scoped token from the credential vault (HashiCorp Vault, AWS STS): "I am workflow W1, I need read access to partner P1's data only, valid for 15 minutes." Vault issues a time-limited token. Tool calls use this token. After 15 minutes: token expires, any attempt to continue is rejected.
  • Implementation: OAuth2 client credentials flow per workflow. Scope = "oms:read:partner:P1" not "oms:all". TTL = workflow estimated duration + 20% buffer. Token stored in agent's working memory (not persisted). On renewal: agent re-requests from vault at TTL/2.
  • Audit benefit: Every tool call now logged with workflow-specific credential. "Workflow W1 (partner onboarding for Tenant A, run at 14:32) called oms:read with token T_abc123." Full attribution in audit trail.
Prerequisites: Agents & Prompting → Agentic Platform
Agent Operations Monitoring Dimensions
What to MonitorMetricAlert ThresholdRoot Cause When Fires
Task success rate% tasks completed without HITL interventionDrop >5% in 24hNew tool error, model regression, schema change in external API
Token usage per taskavg tokens per completed taskIncrease >30%Agent getting stuck in loops, context not being summarised
Tool call latencyp99 tool execution time>5s for any toolExternal API degradation, missing index, resource starvation
Hallucination rateFaithfulness score (LLM judge)Drop below 0.80Model update, retrieval quality degradation, prompt drift
Safety flag rate% calls triggering guardrailsSpike >10 baselineAdversarial users, model jailbreak vector discovered
Cost per task type$ per completed workflow typeIncrease >20%LLM routing changed, agent switched to more expensive model
HITL Gate Design Risk-Tiered Approvals
Tier 0: Auto-approve (no HITL)
Read-only operations, reversible writes, low business impact. Examples: search queries, read-only API calls, draft generation (not sent). Threshold: <$100 impact, fully reversible within 24h.
Tier 1: Async approval (15min SLA)
Moderate impact, reversible with effort. Examples: draft email ready to send, order amendment, report generation. Agent pauses, sends approval request to Slack/email. Approver clicks approve/reject.
Tier 2: Sync approval (immediate)
High impact or irreversible actions. Examples: payment processing, bulk data deletion, external communication to customers. Agent blocks on approval timeout triggers auto-rejection.
Tier 3: Multi-approver
Critical operations, regulatory impact. Examples: financial transactions >$10K, PII data export, system configuration changes. Requires N-of-M approvals (e.g., 2-of-3 senior approvers). SLA: 4 hours.
Agent Monitoring What to Watch
MetricAlert ThresholdRoot Cause When Fires
Task success rateDrop >5% in 24hTool error, model regression, external API schema change
Tokens per task (avg)Increase >30%Agent looping, context not summarised, infinite retry
Tool p99 latency>5s for any toolExternal API degraded, missing DB index
Faithfulness scoreDrop below 0.80Model update, retrieval quality drop, prompt drift
Safety flag rateSpike >10 baselineAdversarial users, new jailbreak vector
Cost per task typeIncrease >20%LLM routing changed, switched to pricier model
HITL Gate Risk-Tiered Approvals
Tier 0: Auto-approve
Read-only, reversible, low-impact. Examples: search queries, draft generation. <$100 impact. No human in loop.
Tier 1: Async (15min SLA)
Moderate impact, reversible. Examples: draft email ready to send, order amendment. Sends Slack/email approval request. Agent pauses until approved/rejected.
Tier 2: Sync (immediate)
High impact, irreversible. Examples: payment, bulk deletion, customer communication. Agent blocks. Timeout auto-rejects.
Tier 3: Multi-approver
Critical/regulatory. Examples: >$10K transactions, PII export. Requires 2-of-3 senior approvers. 4-hour SLA.
Agent Monitoring Metrics
MetricAlert ThresholdRoot Cause
Task success rateDrop >5% in 24hTool error, model regression, API schema change
Tokens per taskIncrease >30%Agent looping, context not summarised
Tool p99 latency>5sExternal API degraded, missing index
Faithfulness score<0.80Model update, retrieval quality drop, prompt drift
Safety flag rateSpike >10 baselineAdversarial users, new jailbreak vector
🌍 Real-world use case: Keeping a long-running research agent reliable. A four-layer memory stops it forgetting earlier steps, a context-budget manager trims history before it overflows the window, and a prompt-drift detector alerts the team when a model update quietly changes the agent's behaviour in production — before users notice.
HITL Risk-Tiered Approvals
Tier 0: Auto-approve
Read-only, reversible, <$100 impact. Examples: search, draft generation. No human in loop.
Tier 1: Async (15min SLA)
Moderate impact. Examples: draft email ready to send, order amendment. Slack/email approval. Agent pauses.
Tier 2: Sync (immediate block)
High impact, irreversible. Examples: payment, bulk delete, customer communication. Timeout auto-rejects.
Tier 3: Multi-approver
Critical/regulatory. >$10K, PII export. Requires 2-of-3 senior approvers. 4h SLA.
🌏 Go Deeper — Agent Ops
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner OpenTelemetry — What is OpenTelemetry? (official) — The 5-minute introduction to traces, metrics, and logs in OTel; read before the observability sections in this tab to understand the plumbing before the agent-specific conventions.
  • Intermediate LangSmith — Documentation (LangChain) — The most-used LLM observability/tracing tool in production; reading the Tracing and Evaluation pages gives you the hands-on counterpart to the OTel theory in this tab.
  • Advanced OpenTelemetry GenAI Semantic Conventions — Full Spec — The normative reference for what gen_ai.* spans must contain; the difference between a span attribute and a log event matters when you need to debug a multi-step agent trace in production.
Developer experience drives actual adoption. The best technical platform fails without it. This tab covers the internal tooling that makes building AI applications productive: prompt library, playground, SDK with starter templates, tiered model access portal, and cost attribution per team all of which are absent from most agentic platform architectures.
What is this tab? This tab is the developer-experience layer of the platform: the prompt library, playground, SDK, model access portal, onboarding templates, and cost visibility that turn platform ideas into daily engineering workflow.
Why it matters: A technically correct platform still fails if developers cannot discover prompts, test safely, get approved model access, or ship a first use case quickly enough to trust the platform.
How to use it: Read the prompt and SDK sections first, then use the governance and cost sections to understand how self-service access stays safe at scale.
Example: If a product team wants to launch a new AI feature next sprint, this tab shows the fastest path from prompt draft to governed production rollout.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

Developer experience (DX) is the set of tools, templates, and shortcuts that decide how fast your engineers can ship AI features — and whether they ship them safely or invent their own workarounds. Even with a brilliant platform underneath, if it takes a developer two weeks to make their first API call, adoption stalls and people start emailing OpenAI API keys around in DMs.

Why this gets its own tab: most AI platform guides cover the technical layers (models, retrieval, guardrails) and stop there. They forget that a platform with no SDK, no playground, no starter templates, and no clear way to test a new prompt is a platform nobody uses. DX is the difference between a platform that lights up your engineering org and one that sits unused while teams build shadow AI on their corporate cards.

What this tab covers: the platform AI SDK (one import, handles auth, routing, logging, and cost tracking — so engineers never call raw model APIs), a shared Prompt Library with versioning and evals, the Prompt Playground (test a prompt against real data before you ship it), a tiered model access portal (governance built in — who can use which model, and how), starter templates for the common app patterns, and an evaluation dashboard so every team can see how their AI feature is actually performing.

Prompt Library & Playground
Shared Prompt Library
  • What it is: A versioned, tested, searchable catalog of reusable prompts for common patterns across the platform. Partner data extraction. Order exception summarization. Impact analysis narrative generation. Integration test case generation. Each prompt: name, description, input schema, output schema, model compatibility, version history, eval scores, example inputs/outputs.
  • Why it matters: Without a shared library, every team re-invents the same prompts independently. Quality varies. Best practices don't propagate. The same mistake is made ten times. With a library: a team building a new feature starts from a battle-tested prompt, not a blank page.
  • Governance: Prompts promoted to library only after: eval score >0.85 on domain golden set, peer review by another engineer, documentation complete. Promoted prompts are read-only (fork to modify). Fork triggers new eval requirement before re-promotion.
  • Discovery: Semantic search on prompt library. "I need a prompt for extracting order line items from natural language" library returns 3 relevant prompts ranked by eval score and usage count. Engineers start from the best available baseline.
Prompt Playground
  • What it is: A platform web UI (part of the AI Studio) where engineers can test prompt changes against the REAL production retrieval pipeline before committing them. Not just a chat box it connects to the actual RAG index, actual tool schemas, and actual model routing.
  • Key features: Load any prompt from the artifact registry. Edit it. Run it against a set of test cases from the golden dataset. See: retrieved chunks, similarity scores, final output, LLM judge score, estimated token cost. Compare side-by-side with the current production prompt on the same test cases. One-click promote to staging if score improves.
  • Diff view: Shows exactly which parts of the output changed between the current and proposed prompt. Highlights improvements and regressions. Engineers see the impact of their change before it touches any user.
  • Integration with CI: Playground exports a test run report. That report can be attached to the PR as evidence that the prompt change was tested. CI can require a playground report with score >= baseline before merging prompt changes.
Platform AI SDK & Model Access Portal
Platform AI SDK
  • Purpose: Lower the barrier to building new AI use cases. Every team that wants to build an AI feature shouldn't need to re-implement model routing, prompt versioning, RAG retrieval, cost tracking, and observability from scratch. The SDK wraps all of this.
  • What the SDK provides: One-line LLM call with automatic routing, fallback, caching, cost tracking, and OTel tracing. RAG query with automatic hybrid retrieval, reranking, and citation formatting. Agent loop with HITL gate support, memory management, and Saga coordinator. All wired to the platform's governance layer (classification enforcement, OPA checks).
  • Starter templates: FAQ chatbot (RAG + streaming + citation). Document processing pipeline (parse + chunk + embed + extract). Agentic workflow with HITL (multi-step + approval gates + audit trail). Batch analysis (document summarization at scale with cost controls). Each template: fully working, tested, documented, cost-estimated.
Python Platform AI SDK usage example
from platform_ai_sdk import Agent, RAGQuery, LLMCall # RAG query all complexity hidden result = await RAGQuery( query="What are the SLAs for Tenant A in System X?", namespace="partner-docs", data_class="INTERNAL", tenant_id="tenant-a" ) # Automatically: hybrid search + reranking + citation + cost tracking # LLM call model routing, caching, tracing all automatic response = await LLMCall( prompt="Summarize this order exception: {exception_text}", variables={"exception_text": raw_text}, prompt_version="order-exception-summary:v2.1.0", # from artifact registry complexity=0.3 # routes to gpt-4o-mini automatically ) # Agentic workflow saga, memory, HITL all wired agent = Agent( workflow="partner-onboarding", # loads from procedure library tenant_id="tenant-a", hitl_tier=2 # supervised autonomous ) result = await agent.run(partner_id="TENANT_A")
Model Access Portal & Cost Attribution
  • Tiered model access portal: Self-service portal where teams request access to model tiers. Sandbox tier: rate-limited, cheap models only, for experimentation. Standard tier: production-grade models, per-team token budget. Premium tier: most capable models for high-stakes use cases, requires justification review. No central team as bottleneck teams move at their own speed within their tier.
  • Cost attribution per team: Every LLM call tagged with: team_id, feature_id, workflow_id. Weekly cost report per team. "Team A spent $X on embedding, $Y on generation, by feature: checkout assistant $A, order exception handler $B." Teams that see their spend naturally optimize. Cost anomalies (3 baseline) trigger alert to team lead.
  • Budget guardrails: Per-team daily budget cap. When 80% of daily budget consumed: team lead notified. At 100%: all non-production LLM calls throttled until reset (production unaffected). Prevents one team's runaway experiment from exhausting org-wide API quota.
  • Chargeback model: AI infrastructure costs allocated to product teams based on actual consumption. Drives accountability. Teams with high AI costs are incentivized to optimize prompts, use caching, and right-size model selection.
Prompt Engineering Tooling & Model Access Governance
Prompt Management Lifecycle
1
Draft in Playground
Engineer writes prompt in the AI Studio Playground. Tests against real RAG index and golden test cases. Sees retrieved chunks, similarity scores, LLM output, judge score. Side-by-side comparison with current production prompt.
2
Register in Artifact Registry
Promote prompt to registry: name, description, input schema, output schema, model compatibility, baseline eval scores. Content hash computed. PR required with playground test report as evidence.
3
A/B Test in Staging
Shadow deployment: both current and new prompt run on 5% of staging traffic. Metrics collected: LLM judge scores, user satisfaction proxies, cost per call. Promote if new version wins on all metrics.
4
Canary in Production
Route 5% of production to new prompt. Monitor for 48h. Drift detection on output quality. Auto-rollback if faithfulness drops >5%. Full rollout when confidence is high.
5
Continuous Monitoring
Weekly drift check: sample 50 production calls per prompt. LLM judge scores. Alert if drop >5% from baseline. Prompt version owns a baseline score that must be maintained for it to remain in production.
Cost Attribution & FinOps for AI
  • Attribution model: Every LLM call tagged: team_id, feature_id, model_version, prompt_version, tenant_id, complexity_tier. Stored in cost attribution table. Weekly summary: cost per team per feature. Monthly chargeback to product teams.
  • Cost optimization incentives: Teams that see their actual spend per user optimize naturally. Teams over budget: alert at 80%, throttle non-production at 100% (production SLA preserved). Spot instance optimization for batch workloads: 60-70% savings on inference jobs.
  • Model access tiers: Sandbox (dev only): rate-limited cheap models, no cost tracking. Standard: production models, per-team budget. Premium: most capable models, requires justification and director approval. Automatic downgrade when budget exceeded.
  • Cost anomaly detection: 3 spike above 7-day rolling average P2 alert. Common causes: runaway agent (infinite loop), new feature without cost guard, billing API error, DDoS. Each investigated within 4 hours.
Python Cost attribution tracker
from opentelemetry import metrics meter = metrics.get_meter("model-gateway") token_cost = meter.create_histogram( name="llm.token_cost_usd", description="LLM call cost in USD", unit="USD" ) def record_cost(usage, model, tenant_id, feature_id, team_id): # Pricing lookup rates = {"gpt-4o": (5.0, 15.0), "gpt-4o-mini": (0.15, 0.6)} input_rate, output_rate = rates.get(model, (5.0, 15.0)) cost = (usage.prompt_tokens * input_rate + usage.completion_tokens * output_rate) / 1_000_000 token_cost.record(cost, { "model": model, "tenant_id": tenant_id, "feature_id": feature_id, "team_id": team_id })
Prerequisites: MLOps / LLMOps → Agentic Platform
Prompt Lifecycle From Draft to Production
1
Draft in Playground
Write prompt in the AI Studio Playground. Test against real RAG index and golden test cases. See: retrieved chunks, similarity scores, LLM output, judge score. Compare side-by-side with current production prompt.
2
Register in Artifact Registry
Name, description, input schema, output schema, model compatibility, baseline eval score. Content hash computed. PR required with playground test report as evidence. CI checks hash matches registry.
3
A/B Test in Staging (shadow)
Both current and new prompt run on 5% of staging traffic. Outputs compared by LLM judge. Promote only if new version wins on all metrics without guardrail regressions.
4
Canary in Production (5% 100%)
Route 5% of live traffic to new prompt. Monitor faithfulness, cost, user satisfaction for 48h. Auto-rollback if faithfulness drops >5%. Expand to 100% when confident.
5
Continuous Drift Monitoring
Weekly: sample 50 production calls, score with LLM judge, compare to baseline. Alert if score drops >5% (p<0.05). Prompt versions have a baseline score they must maintain to stay in production.
Model Access Tiers Self-Service Portal
🆕 Sandbox
  • Cheap models only (mini, haiku)
  • Rate-limited (100 req/hour)
  • No billing tracking needed
  • For: prototyping, learning
  • Self-service: instant access
✅ Standard
  • Production-grade models
  • Per-team token budget
  • Cost attribution tracked
  • For: production features
  • Team lead approval required
⭐ Premium
  • Most capable models (GPT-4o, Claude Opus)
  • High-stakes use cases only
  • Director-level approval
  • For: medical, legal, financial
  • Justification review required
Developer Experience Key Components
Platform AI SDK
Single import: from platform_ai_sdk import RAGQuery, Agent, LLMClient. Handles: auth, rate limiting, model routing, logging, cost attribution. Developer doesn't interact with raw LLM APIs. The SDK is the right abstraction layer.
Prompt Playground
UI for: draft prompt test against real RAG index compare current vs new see retrieved chunks + scores + LLM output + judge score. Before any prompt change goes to registry, it must have a playground test report. Replaces: trial-and-error in prod.
Evaluation Dashboard
Per-agent metrics: faithfulness, cost, latency, safety flags. Trend over time. A/B comparison between prompt versions. Drill down to individual calls. Used by: dev team daily, platform team weekly, product team monthly.
Onboarding Accelerator
Day 1 templates: RAG app starter, chatbot starter, document processor starter. Cookbooks: "How to add a new tool", "How to implement HITL". Sandbox environment: safe to experiment, auto-reset weekly. Goal: new engineer productive in 1 day.
SDK vs Raw API Why the Abstraction Matters
Calling raw OpenAI API directly
  • Each team manages their own API keys (security risk)
  • No cost attribution "who spent $20K this month?"
  • No automatic retry/fallback when model is down
  • Different teams logging differently no unified observability
  • Model upgrades require changes in every team's code
Through Platform SDK
  • One API key managed by platform team in Vault
  • Every call tagged with team_id, feature_name, cost tracked
  • Automatic fallback: GPT-4o down Claude Gemini
  • Every call logged to unified OTel pipeline
  • Model upgrade: change one line in SDK, all teams benefit
Developer Experience Components
Platform AI SDK
from platform_ai_sdk import RAGQuery, Agent, LLMClient handles auth, routing, logging, cost tracking. No raw LLM API calls from application code. The SDK is the right abstraction layer for the platform.
Prompt Playground
UI: draft test against real RAG index compare current vs new see chunks + scores + output + judge score. All prompt changes must have playground test report before registry submission.
Evaluation Dashboard
Per-agent: faithfulness, cost, latency, safety flags over time. A/B comparison between prompt versions. Drill to individual calls. Used daily by devs, weekly by platform team.
Onboarding Accelerator
Day-1 templates: RAG starter, chatbot starter, document processor. Cookbooks: "Add a new tool", "Implement HITL". Sandbox (resets weekly). Goal: new engineer productive in 1 day.
SDK vs Direct API Call
Direct API Call (avoid)
  • Each team manages own API keys (security risk)
  • No cost attribution per team
  • No automatic retry/fallback
  • Model upgrade requires change in every team's code
Platform SDK (correct)
  • One key in Vault, managed by platform team
  • Every call tagged: team_id, feature, cost tracked
  • Automatic fallback: GPT-4o Claude Gemini
  • Model upgrade: one SDK change, all teams benefit
🌏 Go Deeper — Governance
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner EU AI Act — Plain-Language Summary — The fastest way to get the four-tier risk model and GPAI obligations into your head before reading the detailed compliance sections in this tab.
  • Intermediate NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) — The US federal governance framework; the GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE functions map to the same activities this tab covers and are the vocabulary North American enterprise AI reviews use.
  • Advanced Open Policy Agent — Policy as Code Philosophy — Understanding why OPA uses Rego instead of YAML rules is critical for designing governance that scales; this page explains the design decisions that make policy-as-code auditable and testable.
🌍 Real-world use case: An internal AI platform team improving developer adoption. They ship a prompt playground with versioned prompts and side-by-side diffs, expose model access through one SDK instead of raw APIs, and attribute token cost back to each team — so product engineers ship features in hours and finance can see exactly where spend goes.
🌏 Go Deeper — Dev Experience
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner GitHub Copilot — Official Docs — The most widely deployed AI dev tool; read the “Getting started” and “Using Copilot” sections to understand the prompt-to-completion pipeline that underpins the DX concepts in this tab.
  • Intermediate VS Code Copilot — Overview & Features — How the editor surfaces inline completions, chat, and code actions; useful for understanding the UX patterns that DX teams are building around and that this tab discusses at the platform level.
  • Advanced Chen et al. — Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code (Codex paper, 2021) — The Codex paper that launched AI coding assistants; read the HumanEval benchmark section to understand how code LLM capability is measured and what the numbers mean.
People, change, and adoption are architecturally the most underestimated domain. The best technical platform fails without this. This tab covers the skills foundation, change management program, operating model (CoE + champions), and responsible AI training that must be built in parallel with the technical platform not after it.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

Most AI platforms fail not because the technology is wrong but because nobody adopts them. Engineers do not know what to build with them. Product managers do not know what to ask for. Execs do not know how to budget for them. The legal team blocks them. Solving these “people” problems is just as much architecture work as choosing a vector database — and most platform guides skip it entirely.

Why this is an architect's problem, not just HR's: if you ship a beautiful platform and only 5% of engineers can actually use it, you have built a science project, not a platform. The fix is not a one-day training event — it is a deliberate program of literacy, role-specific deep-dives, embedded champions in every team, and a Centre of Excellence that owns the curriculum. Treat this as part of the launch, not an afterthought.

What this tab covers: the skills foundation every engineer needs (what LLMs can and cannot do, failure modes, prompt engineering, evaluation basics), specialist training by role (MLOps vs. PM vs. architect vs. data scientist), the Centre-of-Excellence-plus-embedded-champions operating model (and why it beats either alone), and a responsible-AI training program that satisfies both regulators and your own users.

Skills Foundation for AI Adoption
AI Literacy Program (All Engineers)
  • What LLMs can and cannot do: Can: pattern recognition, text generation, code generation, summarization, classification at scale. Cannot: real-time information (without RAG), guaranteed factual accuracy, deterministic outputs, complex arithmetic, proprietary knowledge (without RAG). Every engineer must know this to avoid the most common category of mistakes.
  • LLM failure modes: Hallucination (how to detect and mitigate). Prompt injection (what it is, why it matters). Context limitations (lost-in-the-middle effect). Model bias (what it looks like in outputs). Sycophancy (model agreeing with false claims). Understanding these failure modes prevents blind trust in LLM outputs.
  • Prompt engineering for engineers: Not just creative it is engineering. System prompt anatomy (persona, scope, format, constraints, examples). Temperature and sampling. Few-shot examples. Chain-of-thought for reasoning tasks. A well-crafted prompt sent to a cheaper model routinely outperforms a poorly crafted prompt to an expensive model.
  • Evaluation basics: How to evaluate LLM outputs. RAGAS metrics. LLM-as-judge pattern. Golden dataset construction. Without evaluation skills, engineers cannot tell if their AI feature is actually working.
Specialist Training by Role
MLOps/LLMOps engineersFull LLMOps certification: prompt versioning, evaluation pipelines, drift detection, cost management, OTel instrumentation, model registry operations. Minimum: 40-hour structured course + hands-on lab exercises on real platform tooling.
Product managersAI product management: scoping AI features (what requires AI, what doesn't), writing AI acceptance criteria, evaluation literacy (understanding RAGAS metrics), managing AI-specific risks (hallucination liability, bias complaints).
Solution architectsEnd-to-end AI system design: RAG architecture, agentic patterns, governance frameworks, cost modeling, vendor selection, AKP architecture patterns. The full content of this AI Gita is the study material.
AI engineersHands-on: fine-tuning (LoRA/QLoRA), RAG pipeline optimization, agent development with LangGraph, evaluation harness construction, prompt optimization techniques. Practical output: build a production-quality RAG system as capstone project.
Domain experts (business)How to evaluate AI outputs in their domain. How to provide high-quality feedback. How to identify when AI is wrong in domain-specific ways. How to contribute to golden dataset construction.
Change Management Program
Addressing the Fear-of-Replacement Concern
  • Be direct and honest: Corporate messaging ("AI will enhance your work") is transparent and counterproductive. Address directly: "Some repetitive tasks will be automated. Here is what that means for your role specifically, what new skills are valued, and what the transition looks like." Uncertainty is more stressful than clarity, even when the clarity is difficult.
  • Involvement as the intervention: The most effective change management is bringing affected people into the design process early. Make them co-authors of the solution. CS agents involved in designing the CS AI assistant will advocate for it, identify edge cases, and provide high-quality feedback rather than resisting it.
  • Success story socialization: Concrete examples of time saved, errors prevented, quality improved. "The order exception summarization agent saved CS Team A 2 hours per day and reduced misclassifications by 40%." Numbers and named teams. Not abstract percentages. Shared at all-hands and team meetings, not just internal comms channels.
  • Feedback loops must be genuine: If users report that an agent is wrong or unhelpful, that signal must result in visible improvement within weeks, not months. Users who provide feedback and see no action stop providing feedback and the system stagnates on poor quality.
Operating Model: CoE + Embedded Champions
  • Central AI CoE responsibilities: Sets standards (evaluation criteria, prompt engineering best practices, governance requirements). Builds and maintains shared infrastructure (AKP platform, SDK, prompt library, evaluation harness). Provides expertise on demand (architecture reviews, AI ethics review, security assessment). Runs the PRR process. Manages vendor relationships (OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS Bedrock contracts).
  • Embedded AI champions in product teams: 1-2 engineers per product team with deeper AI expertise. Own AI feature development in their domain. Bring CoE standards into day-to-day team practice. Provide early feedback to CoE on what's working and what isn't. Represent their team in CoE's quarterly platform roadmap sessions.
  • Why both are needed: CoE alone = ivory tower standards that teams ignore because they're not involved. Champions alone = inconsistent practices, reinvented wheels, no shared infrastructure. CoE sets direction, champions execute it in domain context. The multiplier effect: 5 CoE engineers 20 champions across 20 teams = 20 more AI development velocity than CoE doing everything.
  • Community of practice: Weekly AI practitioners meeting (30 min). Champions share what they built, what failed, what they learned. Rotating deep dives on specific topics. Informal knowledge sharing that formal training can't replicate. Creates the social infrastructure for AI excellence to spread organically.
Responsible AI Training
Mandatory Responsible AI Curriculum
  • Bias and fairness: Types of bias (representation, measurement, algorithmic). How to measure fairness across demographic groups. The impossibility theorem (demographic parity, equalized odds, calibration cannot all be achieved simultaneously). Practical: run bias evaluation on your model before production deployment.
  • Transparency and explainability: When explainability is required (high-risk AI under EU AI Act, SR 11-7 for financial models). SHAP values for tabular models. Attention visualization (with caveat: attention attribution). Counterfactual explanations ("the model would have approved your loan if income were X"). Ability to explain AI decisions to affected individuals.
  • Accountability chain: For every AI system in production, there must be a named human accountable. Not "the model" a person. That person is responsible for: monitoring quality, responding to complaints, initiating recall if something goes wrong. RACI for AI systems: who is Responsible (builds it), Accountable (answers for it), Consulted (domain experts), Informed (affected stakeholders).
  • EU AI Act obligations: Which of our AI systems are high-risk? What documentation is required? Who is the designated EU representative? What does the conformity assessment process look like for our highest-risk systems? Legal and compliance teams should co-deliver this training with AI engineers.
Adoption Playbook: Pilot Scale
1
Select pilot use case (Week 1-4)
Choose a specific, measurable, bounded use case with an engaged domain team. Not "AI for everything" "AI for order exception summarization for CS Team A." Measurable: time saved per exception, classification accuracy. Engaged team: they want this to work and will actively participate.
2
Build with the team, not for them (Week 5-12)
CS agents help create golden test cases. They review agent outputs weekly. They identify failure modes that engineers would never find alone. They become advocates, not resisters, because they co-created the solution.
3
Measure and publish results (Week 12-16)
Quantify: time saved (hours/week per agent), error reduction (misclassification rate before vs after), user satisfaction (NPS from CS agents). Publish internally. Name the team and the numbers. Create social proof for the next team.
4
Scale to adjacent teams (Week 16-24)
The pilot team becomes the reference. Next 3-4 teams adopt with the pilot team's support. SDK and shared infrastructure accelerates each successive deployment. By team 5: deployment time is 30% of what it was for team 1.
5
Continuous improvement culture (Ongoing)
Every team contributing feedback to the prompt library. Weekly metrics review. Quarterly CoE roadmap updates based on what teams are actually building. The platform improves because of the people using it, not despite them.
Change Management Fear and Response
Fear #1: "AI will replace my job"
Response: AI replaces tasks, not roles. Show evidence: CS reps using AI handle 40% more tickets AND have higher satisfaction scores. New role = "AI supervisor" vs old role = "manual responder". Use data, not reassurances.
Fear #2: "I'll make mistakes using AI I don't understand"
Response: Give people control. AI suggests, human decides. Show the confidence score and source. Design HITL for every high-stakes decision. Psychological safety requires: it's ok to reject AI suggestions.
Fear #3: "This is just another tech initiative that won't last"
Response: Early wins with their team. Show concrete time saved in THEIR workflow. Get their manager to sponsor publicly. AI adoption follows trust, and trust follows demonstrated value in context they recognise.
The Adoption Driver: Co-creation
Teams that help BUILD the AI tool adopt it at 3× the rate of teams it is built FOR. Involve domain experts in: defining test cases, reviewing outputs, identifying failure modes. They become advocates because they co-own the outcome.
AI Literacy Skills Matrix by Role
RoleFoundation SkillsPractical SkillsAdvanced Skills
