Business / Non-technicalWhat LLMs can / can't do, AI ethics basics, risk tiersPrompt writing, evaluating AI output quality, identifying use cases in their domainROI estimation, build vs buy framing, vendor risk awareness
Product ManagerAI capabilities map, EU AI Act risk tiers, evaluation literacyWriting AI feature specs, defining acceptance criteria, golden-dataset stewardshipAI product roadmap, build / buy decisions, post-launch metric ownership
Software EngineerLLM API usage, RAG basics, prompt-engineering vocabularySDK usage, tool implementation, structured-output patterns, basic eval harnessFine-tuning (LoRA), agent building (LangGraph), eval-driven development
Data EngineerEmbedding basics, vector DB concepts, chunking strategiesData pipeline for AI (CDC → chunk → embed → index), data-quality SLOs for LLMsFeature store for ML, hybrid retrieval indexes, semantic-cache design
ML / AI EngineerAll SWE skills + ML fundamentals, evaluation rigour, drift conceptsFine-tuning (LoRA / QLoRA), RAG optimisation, RAGAS evaluation, agent harnessPre-training, alignment (DPO / GRPO), infra at scale (FSDP, vLLM, KV cache)
Pilot vs Enterprise Rollout
Enterprise-first Rollout (avoid)
  • Big bang deployment to all teams at once
  • No early wins to build credibility
  • Resistance from teams with no involvement in design
  • Hard to diagnose failures across 20+ teams simultaneously
  • One bad incident burns trust across the whole org
Pilot-first Rollout (recommended)
  • Start with 1 willing team, bounded use case
  • Co-create with domain experts — they become advocates
  • Publish quantified results: hours saved, accuracy gained
  • Pilot team supports next 3–4 teams; speed compounds
  • Failures stay contained and informative, not catastrophic
🌏 Go Deeper — People & Adoption
Curated external resources — use these after finishing this tab, not instead of it.
  • Beginner McKinsey — State of AI (annual report) — The enterprise adoption data cited most often in business conversations about AI; read the “Why most AI pilots don’t scale” sections to understand the change management context this tab addresses.
  • Intermediate Martin Fowler — Building AI-Enabled Systems (martinfowler.com) — Practical patterns for integrating AI into engineering teams; the team topology and testing sections are directly relevant to the adoption challenges this tab covers.
Interview Strategy knowing the content is necessary but not sufficient. This tab covers what most prep guides skip: how FAANG AI interviews are actually structured, what interviewers write on their scorecards, the STAR method for behavioral questions with AI-specific templates, how to present your portfolio, and how to negotiate your offer.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.
How FAANG/Senior AI Interviews Are Structured
Typical 5-Round Loop (ML Engineer / AI Architect)
Round 1: Recruiter Screen (30 min)
Background, motivation, compensation range, visa. No technical depth. Goal: confirm basic fit and set expectations. Tip: know your numbers (current comp, target comp) before this call.
Round 2: Technical Phone Screen (60 min)
1-2 ML coding questions (implement from scratch) OR system design mini-version. Tests: can you code? Do you understand ML basics? Tip: practice talking aloud while coding silence kills you here.
Round 3: ML Coding (60 min)
Implement a model, algorithm, or component. Common: backprop, attention, k-means, tokenizer, beam search. Tests: depth of understanding. Not just "call sklearn.KMeans" but implement it. Tip: explain every design choice as you write.
Round 4: ML System Design (60 min)
Design an end-to-end ML system: recommendation engine, RAG chatbot, fraud detection, LLM platform. Tests: breadth, trade-off reasoning, production thinking. Tip: always ask clarifying questions first never jump to architecture immediately.
Round 5: Behavioral / Leadership (45-60 min)
STAR format questions. Past projects, conflicts, failures, leadership. Tests: culture fit, communication, self-awareness. Tip: have 5 strong stories prepared, each usable for multiple questions.
System Design Interview 45-Minute Structure
0-5
Clarify requirements (DO NOT skip this)
"What is the expected query volume?" "What is the latency SLA?" "What data is available?" "What does success look like in 6 months?" Interviewers score you on asking the RIGHT questions. Jumping to architecture = red flag.
5-15
High-level architecture + component identification
Name the main components. Data pipeline feature store training serving monitoring. Draw on whiteboard/screen. Show you understand the full lifecycle before diving into any one piece.
15-35
Deep dive (interviewer will steer)
Go deep on 2-3 components. ML model choice and justification. Data pipeline decisions. Serving architecture. State tradeoffs explicitly: "I chose A over B because X, accepting the cost of Y." This is what separates senior from junior.
35-42
Scale, failure modes, edge cases
"How does this handle 100 traffic?" "What happens when the model degrades?" "What is the rollback plan?" Proactively bring these up don't wait to be asked.
42-45
Questions for the interviewer
Always have 2-3 genuine questions. "What is the biggest ML challenge your team is facing right now?" "What does the model deployment process look like here?" Shows genuine interest and domain curiosity.
What Interviewers Actually Score
DimensionWhat They Look ForRed FlagsGreen Flags
Technical DepthCan explain WHY, not just HOW. Derives gradients. Knows failure modes."I just use the library." Can't explain why sqrt(d_k) in attention.Immediately explains trade-offs. Knows what breaks at scale.
Problem StructuringBreaks ambiguous problems into tractable pieces. Asks right clarifying questions.Jumps to solution without clarifying. Assumes requirements."Before I answer, let me clarify: what is the expected volume and latency SLA?"
Trade-off ReasoningKnows there are always multiple valid approaches. Can argue for and against each."This is the best approach." No alternatives considered."Option A is better when X. Option B when Y. Given your constraints I'd choose A because..."
CommunicationCan explain complex ideas simply. Thinks aloud during coding. Summarises clearly.Silent during coding. Jargon without explanation. Can't explain to non-technical audience.Narrates thought process. "Let me think about the edge cases here..."
Production MindsetThinks about monitoring, failure modes, rollback, data quality, cost.Stops at "model is trained." Never mentions serving, monitoring, or degradation.Proactively mentions: "We also need to think about model drift and a rollback plan."
Business SenseConnects every technical decision to business impact.All technical, no "why does this matter to the business?""This approach saves $X/day in inference cost, which matters at our scale of Y requests."
Behavioral Questions STAR Templates with AI Context
STAR Format
S
Situation set the scene in 2 sentences
Context, team size, what the system was. Do NOT spend more than 20% of your answer here.
T
Task your specific responsibility
"I was responsible for..." Not "we." Own your contribution clearly.
A
Action what you specifically did (60% of answer)
Concrete decisions made, alternatives considered, WHY you chose this approach. Technical detail goes here.
R
Result quantified outcome
Numbers: latency improved X%, cost reduced $Y/day, recall improved from A to B%. If no numbers: "enabled the team to ship Z weeks earlier."
5 Stories You Must Have Ready
Prepare ONE story each for these 5 archetypes
Technical challenge
"Tell me about a technically difficult ML problem you solved." Your best war story model that wasn't working, found root cause, fixed it, quantified improvement.
Disagreement/conflict
"Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision." Show you can push back respectfully, use data to argue your case, and commit to the team decision when outvoted.
Failure/learning
"Tell me about a time your model/system failed in production." Shows maturity. Focus on: how you diagnosed, what you fixed, what process you put in place to prevent recurrence.
Leadership/influence
"Tell me about a time you led a technical initiative without formal authority." Drove adoption of new framework, established ML standards, mentored someone.
Ambiguity/ownership
"Tell me about a time you had to figure out the right problem to solve." You identified a problem nobody else had defined, proposed approach, got buy-in, executed.
Portfolio Presentation for AI Engineers
💻 GitHub Profile
  • Pinned repos: 4-6 best projects with clear READMEs
  • README must include: problem, approach, results (with numbers), how to run
  • At least 1 project with: training code + evaluation + serving
  • Activity graph: consistent commits show active learning
  • Contributions to known repos (HuggingFace, LangChain) = credibility multiplier
📋 Project Talking Points
  • Problem: Why this problem? What was the business/technical gap?
  • Approach: What did you try first? What didn't work and why?
  • Results: Concrete numbers. % improvement. Cost saved. Users served.
  • Trade-offs: What would you do differently? What are the limitations?
  • Scale: What breaks at 10 load? How would you fix it?
💰 Salary Negotiation
  • Never give a number first "What is the budgeted range for this role?"
  • Research: levels.fyi for comp data at specific companies/levels
  • Negotiate everything: base, equity (cliff, vesting), signing, PTO
  • Counter with data: "Based on levels.fyi, IC5 ML at [company] is $XY"
  • Get 2+ competing offers before negotiating increases leverage 4060%
  • Always negotiate in writing after verbal offer gives you time to think
Common Interview Mistakes to Avoid
MistakeWhy It HurtsWhat to Do Instead
Jumping to code without clarifyingSignals junior thinking solving the wrong problem elegantlyAlways spend 2-3 min clarifying scope, constraints, success criteria
Only knowing the happy pathProduction engineers must think about what breaksProactively mention: "What if the model degrades?" "What if the data has nulls?"
"We did X" instead of "I did X"Interviewers can't assess your individual contributionOwn your work: "I designed the feature store schema. My teammate handled the serving layer."
Memorising answers without understandingInterviewers probe one level deeper you get exposed immediatelyUnderstand the WHY. If you can't explain it to a 10-year-old, you don't understand it yet.
No questions for the interviewerSignals lack of curiosity or preparationAlways have 2-3 genuine questions ready about the team's challenges
Over-engineering the answerSignals you can't distinguish important from unimportant complexity"The simplest solution that works is X. I'd only add Y if we hit Z scale threshold."
Underselling impact with vague language"Improved performance" means nothing every project "improved" somethingAlways quantify: "Reduced p99 latency from 2.3s to 0.4s, enabling 3 new customer segments"
AI Career Path understanding the progression from junior to staff/principal engineer, what actually gets you promoted, how to build a personal brand in AI, and how to break into the field from other backgrounds. The skills in this document get you interviews. This tab helps you navigate the career.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.
IC Levels What Each Level Actually Means in AI
IC3 Junior / New Grad ML Engineer
Scope: Well-defined tasks, clear requirements, close mentorship. Output: Implements features, fixes bugs in existing systems, runs experiments with guidance. What they don't do: Define what to build, handle ambiguity, lead others. Typical comp (US FAANG): $200280K total.
IC4 Mid-level ML Engineer (most common level)
Scope: Owns features end-to-end. Works independently. Some ambiguity. Output: Designs and ships complete ML features. Onboards IC3s. Makes reasonable technical decisions independently. Growth unlock: Proactively identifies problems before being asked. Comp: $280400K.
IC5 Senior ML Engineer (the target for most experienced engineers)
Scope: Owns a domain (e.g., "the recommendation system"). Defines technical direction. Mentors IC3/4. Output: Technical design docs, cross-team influence, code reviews, drives standards. Growth unlock: Makes the team around you better, not just yourself. Comp: $380550K.
IC6 Staff ML Engineer (large scope, high ambiguity)
Scope: Owns a product area or platform. Sets technical direction across multiple teams. Deals with org-level ambiguity. Output: Architectural decisions with multi-year impact, hiring bar-raiser, technical strategy. Growth unlock: Can identify problems that don't exist yet. Comp: $500K900K+.
IC7+ Principal / Distinguished Engineer (rare, high impact)
Scope: Company-level technical problems. May work across the entire engineering org. External reputation (papers, talks, open source). Output: Defines the company's technical future. Comp: $800K$2M+ (mostly equity at this level).
What Actually Gets You Promoted
IC4 IC5 (most common promotion path)
What changes between IC4 and IC5?
IC4 thinks about
"How do I implement this feature correctly and on time?"
IC5 thinks about
"Is this the right feature to build? What are the downstream effects on the system? What technical debt does this create?"
Key promotable behaviours
Proactively identifies problems before asked. Writes design docs others use. Unblocks teammates. Gives feedback in code reviews that prevents future bugs, not just current ones.
Common mistake
Being the best IC4 (executes perfectly) but never showing IC5 behaviours (defines what to execute). Promotion requires demonstrating the NEXT level, not perfecting the current one.
Building Visibility (critical for promotion)
  • Write, don't just code: Design docs, postmortems, technical blog posts. Writing amplifies your impact beyond your immediate team.
  • Present at team meetings: "I tried X, it failed because Y, next time I'd do Z." Teaching colleagues is IC5 behaviour, not IC4.
  • Find the high-leverage work: Some projects have 10 the visibility. Choose projects that multiple teams depend on over projects only your team sees.
  • Have a sponsor (not just a mentor): A mentor gives advice. A sponsor advocates for you in rooms you're not in. Find someone at IC6+ who will speak for you at promotion committees.
  • Quantify everything: "I improved the model" vs "I reduced inference latency by 62%, enabling product to launch in 3 new markets." The second gets into your promo doc.
  • External credibility: One well-cited paper, one popular GitHub repo, one conference talk = you are mentioned in hiring conversations even when you're not looking.
Breaking Into AI from Other Backgrounds
BackgroundTransferable StrengthsGaps to FillBest Entry Path
Software Engineer (SWE)Strong coding, systems thinking, debugging, production experienceML theory (backprop, statistics), model evaluation, data pipelinesFast-track: build end-to-end ML project. Target MLOps roles first, then ML Engineer roles after 1 year.
Data AnalystStrong SQL, statistics, business metrics, data intuitionPython ML stack, model training and serving, software engineering practicesMove to Data Scientist first. Learn scikit-learn, PyTorch basics. Then move toward ML Engineer.
Academic ResearcherDeep theory, paper reading, experimental rigour, statisticsProduction engineering, distributed systems, cost/latency awareness, shipping fastStrong pipeline for research-heavy teams (Anthropic, DeepMind, MSR). Add MLOps project to show production ability.
Domain Expert (doctor, lawyer, finance)Deep domain knowledge, use case identification, evaluation judgmentTechnical ML skills, coding, data pipelinesAI Product Manager AI Product Lead. Or: domain-specific AI Engineer at vertical-focused startups (medical AI, legal AI).
DevOps / InfrastructureKubernetes, CI/CD, monitoring, cloud platforms, reliabilityML-specific operations (model drift, experiment tracking, feature stores)MLOps Engineer directly. Extremely in demand. Add MLflow/Feast knowledge to existing infra skills.
Building Your AI Brand Open Source & Community
💻 Open Source
  • Contribute to HuggingFace Transformers even documentation fixes count
  • Build a dataset on HuggingFace Hub 500+ downloads = credibility
  • Create a useful PEFT fine-tuning recipe people cite these
  • Publish benchmark results comparing models gets picked up by newsletters
  • Even 1 well-known repo beats 10 unknown ones
📝 Writing
  • One deep technical blog post per month beats daily shallow posts
  • Best topics: "I implemented X from scratch and learned Y"
  • Cross-post: personal blog + Medium + Substack
  • Share on X/LinkedIn with the code link algorithm rewards code posts
  • Guest post on Towards Data Science, The Gradient
🎬 Talks & Community
  • Start: local ML meetups. No slides needed for first talk.
  • Progress to: PyData, MLOPS World, applied ML conferences
  • ICLR/NeurIPS papers: 1 accepted paper = 5 years of credibility
  • Kaggle: top 100 in one competition = strong signal for ML Eng roles
  • Discord communities: Eleuther AI, LM Systems active participation noticed
2025 AI Job Market Reality
FAANG AI Headcount: A Mixed Picture
Meta, Google, Amazon ran significant ML team reductions 2023-2024 cutting roles with unclear product impact. Meanwhile applied AI teams (LLM products, RAG, agents) are actively hiring. The divide: product-impact AI roles expand; pure-research roles at non-frontier labs contract. Know which world you are interviewing for.
Where AI Roles Are Concentrated (2025)
Tier 1 highest demand: LLM/AI Engineer (RAG, agents, fine-tuning pipelines), AI Platform/MLOps. Tier 2: ML Engineer (classical + LLM), Data Scientist with causal inference expertise. Tier 3 most competitive: Pure ML Research Engineer outside OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta FAIR.
Compensation Has Bifurcated Sharply
Frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI): $400K-$2M+ driven by pre-IPO equity. Large tech (FAANG+): $280-$550K IC4-IC6. Enterprise AI: $180-$320K. Leverage is highest with 2+ competing offers, especially one from a frontier lab. Never negotiate without competing data.
What Employers Actually Want in 2025
Ship speed over research depth. Production LLM experience beats 10 papers. Evaluation competence (RAGAS, LLM-as-judge, golden datasets) is a differentiator most engineers cannot build eval pipelines. Cost consciousness: "what does this cost at 10M requests/day?" is now a standard interview question. OWASP LLM security awareness tested at architect level.
IC Track vs Management Track
IC Track (Individual Contributor to Staff+)
  • IC5 to IC6 (Staff): Define technical direction across multiple teams. Architectural decisions with multi-year impact. Write design docs that shape the platform for years.
  • IC7 (Principal): Company-level technical strategy. External credibility via papers and talks. Often spans the entire engineering organisation.
  • Best for: People energised by deep technical problems. Don't want 60% of time in 1:1s and performance reviews.
  • Comp ceiling: IC7+ at FAANG can exceed $2M equity-heavy. Staff engineers at AI startups pre-IPO can exceed this.
  • Hardest gate: IC5 to IC6 requires demonstrated cross-team impact and sponsorship from a VP-level engineer or above.
Management Track (EM to Director)
  • Eng Manager to Senior EM to Director: Your metric shifts from personal output to team throughput. Responsible for hiring, performance, roadmap delivery.
  • Best for: People energised by growing others. Prefer influence through people over influence through code.
  • The trap: Many strong ICs become weak EMs. Coding skills atrophy, technical credibility erodes, and the work does not energise them. Never switch for comp alone.
  • In AI specifically: Technical EM is highly valued an EM who can still read code, review architectures, and hold engineers accountable technically. Far rarer than non-technical management.
  • When to switch: If you are consistently doing unasked-for management work (mentoring, coordinating, unblocking) and finding it energising that is the signal.
How to Evaluate an AI Team Before Joining
Ask these in your interviews the answers reveal engineering culture
How do you evaluate your AI features?
Red flag: "We look at user feedback." Green flag: "Golden datasets per feature, RAGAS scores in CI, deploy blocked on regression." Evaluation maturity is the single strongest signal of AI engineering culture.
What is your model deployment process?
Red flag: "We push to prod and watch it." Green flag: "Shadow then canary then full rollout with automated rollback on faithfulness drop." Shows production risk discipline.
How do you handle prompt changes?
Red flag: "Update in code and redeploy." Green flag: "Versioned artifact registry, playground testing against golden set, A/B before production." Shows engineering discipline around non-deterministic systems.
What is your biggest AI production failure?
Red flag: Can't name one (too new, or dishonest). Green flag: Specific story with diagnosis, fix, and process change. Teams that share failures learn; teams that hide them repeat them.
How do you decide what to build with AI vs without?
Red flag: "We add AI wherever we can." Green flag: "We use a framework: RAG for knowledge, agents for multi-step, fine-tune for format/style, otherwise prompting or no AI." Shows they resist hype.
What does Day 1 look like for an AI engineer?
Red flag: "Here is the repo, good luck." Green flag: "Sandbox access, starter templates, cookbooks, assigned buddy for four weeks." Shows investment in developer experience and onboarding.
Emerging Trends 20252026 the fastest-moving areas of AI that are actively reshaping what AI engineers and architects need to know. This tab covers reasoning models, agentic framework comparison, persistent memory agents, new fine-tuning methods (ORPO/SimPO), and multimodal LLM architecture.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

This tab is the “what changed last quarter” section. AI moves so fast that the techniques considered cutting-edge 12 months ago are now baseline, and brand-new ideas are reshaping what an AI engineer actually needs to know. If you only read the older parts of this guide, you will sound out-of-date in any 2026 interview — especially at frontier-model companies.

The four big shifts to know in 2026: (1) reasoning models that think longer before answering instead of needing a bigger brain (o1/o3, DeepSeek-R1), (2) agentic frameworks consolidating around a few winners after the explosion of 2024-25, (3) persistent memory that survives across sessions so agents actually get smarter over time, and (4) new and simpler fine-tuning methods (ORPO, SimPO, GRPO) that replace the older multi-step RLHF pipeline.

What this tab covers: each of these shifts explained at a level you can defend in an interview — the core idea, why it matters, the math or recipe in plain words, the trade-offs, and which framework or model is leading right now. Treat this tab as live: come back every few months because the leaders will change.

Reasoning Models — o1, o3, DeepSeek-R1 Class
A New Scaling Dimension
Traditional Scaling: Train Bigger Models
More parameters + more data + more compute = better model. GPT-3 to GPT-4: ~10x compute. Hitting diminishing returns and data scarcity.
Test-Time Compute Scaling (o1/o3/R1)
Instead of a bigger model: generate MORE reasoning tokens at inference time. Let the model "think" longer. Trade tokens for accuracy. This is the new scaling dimension.
Process Reward Model (PRM)
Standard RM: score only the final answer. PRM: score EACH reasoning step. Enables: best-of-N sampling (pick highest-scoring chain), MCTS-style search over reasoning paths. Used in o1/o3.
GRPO (DeepSeek-R1's approach)
No PRM needed for verifiable tasks (math, code). Binary reward: correct/incorrect. Group relative policy optimisation no value function. Model spontaneously learns CoT reasoning. Simpler, cheaper, effective.
When to use reasoning models vs standard LLMs?
Use reasoning models (o1/o3/R1) for
Complex multi-step math, competition programming, scientific reasoning, strategic planning tasks with verifiable answers. They trade latency for accuracy sometimes 10 slower.
Use standard LLMs (GPT-4o/Claude) for
Conversational tasks, RAG, summarisation, classification, code completion, creative writing. Faster, cheaper, sufficient for most production use cases.
Cost consideration
o3 generates hidden CoT tokens before answering. These are billed but not shown. A "simple" 100-token answer may cost 2000 tokens in internal reasoning. Always benchmark before using in production.
DeepSeek-R1 significance: Proved that emergent chain-of-thought reasoning can arise purely from RL with verifiable rewards no SFT on human reasoning traces needed. Open weights. This means any company with high-quality verifiable training data (code tests, math proofs, scientific facts) can train their own reasoning model.
Agentic Framework Comparison
FrameworkCore AbstractionBest ForProduction MaturityKey Limitation
LangGraphStateful graph nodes + edges + state dict. Explicit control flow.Complex multi-step workflows with branching, loops, HITL. Most production-ready.High used in prod at many companiesLearning curve for graph mental model. Verbose for simple linear chains.
CrewAIRole-based agents with goals, backstories, and tools. Supervisor pattern built-in.Rapid prototyping of multi-agent teams. Easy to set up supervisor/worker patterns.Medium good for demos, needs care in prodLess control over agent internals. Can be opaque when debugging failures.
AutoGenConversational multi-agent agents have conversations to solve problems.Code generation and debugging loops. Human-in-the-loop conversational workflows.Medium strong for code-centric tasksConversation model can loop indefinitely. Hard to predict when conversation ends.
SmolagentsCode-as-actions agent writes and executes Python code as its action.Data analysis, complex computations, tasks where code is more expressive than JSON tool calls.Growing HuggingFace backedSecurity risk (executes arbitrary code). Requires sandboxing in production.
Semantic KernelPlugin architecture. Strong .NET/C# support alongside Python.Enterprise .NET shops. Microsoft Azure OpenAI integration.High Microsoft backed, enterprise focusMore complex than needed for pure Python shops. Overkill for simple use cases.
New Fine-tuning Methods: ORPO and SimPO
DPO (Standard)
Requires frozen reference model. Loss compares log-ratios between current policy and reference.
L = -log ((log /_ref(y_w) - log /_ref(y_l)))
Cost: Need to load both current model AND reference model (2 GPU memory)
ORPO (Odds Ratio)
No reference model at all. Combines SFT + preference learning in one pass. Memory-efficient.
L = L_SFT - log (log odds(y_w) - log odds(y_l))
Advantage: Single forward pass for both SFT and alignment. Used in Phi-3 training.
SimPO (Simple Preference)
No reference model. Uses average log-probability as implicit reward. Length-normalised to prevent verbose responses.
r(x,y) = (1/|y|) log (y|x,y)
Advantage: Simpler than DPO, no reference model, prevents length exploitation reward hacking.
When to use which (2025 recommendation): ORPO when you want to combine SFT and alignment in one pass (memory-efficient). SimPO when you want reference-free alignment with length normalisation. DPO when you have a good SFT model and want maximal control. Standard RLHF (PPO) only at scale with dedicated infrastructure.
Multimodal LLM Architecture (GPT-4V / LLaVA / Gemini)
Architecture Patterns
Pattern 1: Adapter-based (LLaVA, LLaVA-1.5)
Freeze CLIP vision encoder + freeze LLM. Add small MLP projection layer between them. Train only projection. Fast, cheap. Visual tokens enter the LLM context as "image words".
Pattern 2: Cross-attention injection (Flamingo)
Add cross-attention layers to LLM that attend to vision encoder features. More expressive than projection. LLM can selectively attend to image regions.
Pattern 3: Unified natively multimodal (GPT-4o, Gemini)
Single model trained on interleaved image+text from scratch. No separate encoders. Audio/image/text/video all in one architecture. Best quality, most expensive to train.
Multimodal RAG Pattern
1
Embed images and text separately
Use CLIP or SigLIP to embed images. Use text embeddings for text. Store both in the same vector index with modality metadata.
2
Query can be text or image
Text query: embed with text encoder, search both image and text collections. Image query: embed with vision encoder, retrieve similar images + related text.
3
Feed retrieved items to multimodal LLM
Pass retrieved images (as base64 or URL) + text chunks to GPT-4V or LLaVA. Model can reason across both modalities. "This chart shows X, and the text document says Y, therefore Z."
Use case: Product catalog search (find product by photo). Technical documentation (query returns both text explanations and architecture diagrams). Medical imaging + clinical notes combined retrieval.
Persistent Memory Agents (MemGPT / Letta Pattern)
The Problem: Agents Forget Between Sessions
Standard Agent
  • Session 1: "I prefer concise answers"
  • Session 2: Starts completely fresh
  • Gives verbose answers again
  • No learning across sessions
  • Every conversation restarts from zero
Persistent Memory Agent
  • Session 1: learns "user prefers concise"
  • Writes to memory store: {preference: concise}
  • Session 2: loads relevant memories
  • Gives concise answers immediately
  • Improves with every interaction
MemGPT Architecture
Main Context (limited, expensive)
Current conversation + most recent retrieved memories. Standard LLM context window. Agent "thinks" here.
Core Memory (always loaded)
Key persistent facts: user name, preferences, ongoing tasks. Small, always in context. Updated by agent with write_to_core_memory() function call.
Archival Memory (retrieved on demand)
Unlimited storage. Past conversations, documents, knowledge. Retrieved via semantic search when relevant. Agent calls search_archival_memory("topic") as a tool.
Implementation: Agent is given special tools: write_to_memory(), read_from_memory(), search_memory(). Agent decides when to write (e.g., "user just said they prefer Python 3.11, I should remember that") and what to retrieve. Letta (formerly MemGPT) is the open-source platform for this pattern.
Model Context Protocol (MCP) The de-facto AI tool-use standard (2026)
Before MCP (bespoke integrations)
  • Each AI app builds its own tool-calling format
  • LangChain tools incompatible with LlamaIndex tools
  • Every new LLM requires re-implementing the same adapters
  • No standard for auth, rate limiting, schema versioning
  • Tool ecosystem fragmented — no reuse across teams
With MCP (Anthropic, Nov 2024 → industry standard 2025–26)
  • Standardised JSON-RPC protocol: server exposes tools, resources, prompts; any MCP client (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Cursor, Cline, Zed, IDE plugins) calls them
  • Adopted by OpenAI (Mar 2025), Google DeepMind (Apr 2025), Microsoft (May 2025), plus GitHub, AWS, Replit, Sourcegraph
  • Built-in: tool discovery, schema versioning, structured output, tool annotations, OAuth 2.1 auth, elicitation (server asks user)
  • Transports: stdio (local), HTTP/SSE, Streamable HTTP (remote, prod-grade)
  • Ecosystem: 1000+ servers (Anthropic, Smithery, mcpservers.org registries); pairs naturally with A2A (agent⇔agent) and AG-UI (agent⇔frontend) — see Agents tab
Computer Use / GUI Agents (2026 now GA)
What It Is
LLMs that see a screenshot (or DOM / accessibility tree), decide what to click or type, execute the action, observe the result, and loop. By mid-2026 all three frontier vendors ship a GA product: Claude Computer Use 2 (Anthropic, in Claude Agent SDK), ChatGPT Agent / Operator successor (OpenAI), and Antigravity + Project Mariner (Google Gemini 3.x). OSWorld scores: Gemini 3.5 Flash 78.7%, Claude Computer Use 2 close behind.
Architecture
Vision model sees screenshot LLM decides next action (click at x,y / type text / scroll / keyboard shortcut) automation framework executes (Playwright for browser, PyAutoGUI for desktop) new screenshot captured loop continues.
Current Limitations (critical for architect interviews)
Still high latency (1–5 s per step × many steps) and high cost. Fragile on dynamic UIs. Critical security risk: indirect prompt-injection — the agent reads attacker-controlled content on the page and follows it. Mitigations: sandboxed browser profile, allowlist of domains and actions, human approval for high-risk verbs (send, pay, delete), per-action OTel audit trail, no shared cookies with user’s real session. Success rate on complex enterprise tasks: 65–80% in 2026 — usable for narrow, well-scoped automations; still not a universal RPA replacement.
When to Use It (and When Not To)
Use ONLY for: legacy systems with no API, RPA replacement, web scraping when no API exists. Do NOT use when: there is an API (always prefer the API). Computer use is the tool of last resort expensive, slow, and fragile compared to direct API integration.
2026 Frontier Model Comparison
ModelKey AdvanceContext WindowArchitectural Significance
GPT-5.5 / 5.4 / 5.4-mini / 5.3-instant (OpenAI, Nov 2025)Unified "thinking" router collapses GPT-4o + o-series into one model with a reasoning_effort knob (minimal/low/medium/high). 5.5 = top intelligence, 5.4 = coding workhorse, 5.4-mini = strongest mini ever.~400K input / 128K outputSets the API contract every other vendor copies. Native multimodal, tool use, Computer Use, Apps SDK (MCP-compatible). Pricing: 5.4 at $2.50/$15 per MTok; 5.4-mini at $0.75/$4.50.
Claude Opus 4.8 / Sonnet 4.6 / Haiku 4.5 (Anthropic, Oct 2025 mid-2026)Adaptive thinking auto-selects effort per prompt. Sonnet 4.5/4.6 leads agentic coding (SWE-bench Verified ~77%). Memory tool + context-editing make week-long agents feasible.1M ctx, 128K output (Opus); 1M/64K (Sonnet); 200K (Haiku)Computer Use 2, Code Execution, File API as first-class tools. Powers Claude Code, Claude Agent SDK. Dateless model IDs (claude-opus-4-8) are pinned snapshots, not evergreen.
Gemini 3.5 Flash / 3.1 Pro / 3.1 Deep Think / 3.1 Flash-Lite (Google, 2026)3.5 Flash = stable frontier with best intelligence-per-dollar. Deep Think uses parallel reasoning paths. ARC-AGI-2 84.6%, OSWorld 78.7%, HLE 41.4%.1M (2M experimental)Native video; Gemini Embedding 2 puts text+image+code+audio in one space. Antigravity agent-first IDE (May 2026); Deep Research / Max; Nano Banana Pro / Veo 3.1 / Imagen 4 share the platform.
DeepSeek V4 Flash / V4 Pro + R1 (SepOct 2025)V4 Flash: $0.14 / $0.28 per MTok the new price floor. Thinking + non-thinking in one model. DSA (DeepSeek Sparse Attention) for cheap long context. R1 proved pure-RL reasoning (GRPO+RLVR).1M ctx / 384K outputMIT-style permissive license; Anthropic-API-compatible endpoint (drop-in failover). MoE + MLA + MTP the open recipe everyone copies. R1-Zero showed reasoning emerges from RL alone (no SFT).
Llama 4 Maverick / Scout (Meta, Apr 2025)MoE (128 / 16 experts, 17B active), native multimodal (early fusion). Scout fits a single H100; Maverick MMLU-Pro 80.5. ~$0.190.49 per MTok blended on distributed inference.10M (claimed)The dominant open-weight frontier. Meta also launched MSL Muse Spark (April 2026, closed elite research line); Llama remains the open flagship.
Qwen3 family + Qwen3-Max (Alibaba, 202526)0.6B 480B + trillion-param Max. Dense + MoE, dual-mode (thinking / non-thinking). Coder & VL refreshes same year.up to 1M (Max: 256K)Apache 2.0; 119 languages. The serious enterprise pick for self-hosted multilingual + tool-calling. Qwen3-Coder is the open SWE-bench leader.
Grok 4 / 4 Heavy (xAI)4 Heavy runs 8 reasoning agents in parallel and votes. Top HLE (44%) and live web access.~256KStrong on math/coding leaderboards; tightly coupled to X data; first-mover on "agent-vote" inference pattern.
Kimi K2, GLM-4.5/4.6, Mistral Large 2.1, Phi-4-Reasoning, gpt-oss-120b/20bKimi K2: 1T MoE, strong agentic. GLM: Zhipu, code+agent. Mistral: EU-sovereign frontier. Phi-4-Reasoning: small + smart. gpt-oss: OpenAI's first open reasoning models (Apache 2.0, Aug 2025).128K256KThe "long tail" that matters: gpt-oss legitimised open weights at OpenAI; Kimi/GLM/Qwen3 are the China-stack frontier; Mistral keeps the EU stack alive.
Reasoning Models the 2026 paradigm
Test-time compute became a first-class scaling axis in 2025 (after o1 in late 2024). Every frontier vendor now ships a "thinking" mode: hidden chain-of-thought (sometimes billed as reasoning_tokens), explicit reasoning_effort/thinking_budget knobs, and RL-trained reasoning recipes. As an architect: budget for the hidden CoT in your cost model (it can be 550 the visible output), expose the knob to the caller (not all queries need effort=high), and log the reasoning-token usage as a top-line metric.
FamilyRecipeKnob
OpenAI GPT-5.xRL on verifiable rewards + tool-use rollouts; unified routerreasoning_effort: minimal / low / medium / high
Anthropic Claude 4.xExtended thinking + adaptive thinking (Opus 4.8 auto-selects)thinking: enabled + token budget (or adaptive)
Google Gemini 3.x Deep ThinkParallel reasoning paths, then aggregationthinking_config / Deep Think variant
DeepSeek R1 / V4Pure RL (GRPO + RLVR), no SFT needed (R1-Zero); V4 has thinking + non-thinking modes in one modelthinking flag
Grok 4 Heavy8 parallel reasoning agents vote"Heavy" variant
Open: Qwen3, gpt-oss, GLM-4.6, Phi-4-ReasoningSFT-on-CoT + GRPO/GSPO/RLOO variantsvendor-specific or always-on
Mixture of Experts (MoE) Why Every Frontier Model Uses It
The Core Insight
Replace one large dense FFN layer with N specialist networks (experts). A learned router activates only k experts per token. DeepSeek-V3: 671B total parameters, 37B active per token. Inference cost = 37B dense model. Knowledge capacity = 671B model. You get both.
Why It Enables Efficiency
Dense model: ALL parameters run for every token. MoE with k=2, N=64: only 3% of FFN parameters run per token. Training cost is still high (all experts must learn), but inference cost scales with k not N. The larger N, the better the efficiency ratio.
The Load Balancing Problem
Without a balancing loss, all tokens collapse to 2-3 popular experts. The other 60+ experts receive no gradient and never improve (wasted capacity). Fix: auxiliary loss penalises unequal expert utilisation. DeepSeek uses per-device load balancing avoiding communication overhead of standard approaches.
MoE Serving Constraint
Critical for architects: you must load ALL expert weights to GPU memory even though only k run per token. DeepSeek-V3 requires 8H100s to run at bf16. This is why MoE models are cheaper per token but not cheaper per GPU than similarly-performing dense models. Plan infrastructure accordingly.
Structured Output Production Standard for 2025
The Problem
LLMs return text. Your code needs JSON. Naive approach: ask for JSON, parse it. Failure rate: ~15% (missing fields, wrong types, truncation, extra text). Production systems cannot tolerate 15% parse failure.
Solution 1: response_format
OpenAI/Azure: add response_format={"type":"json_object"}. Guarantees valid JSON syntax. Does NOT guarantee your schema model may return {}, missing fields, wrong types. Necessary but not sufficient.
Solution 2: Instructor + Pydantic (recommended)
Define output as a Pydantic model. Instructor wraps the LLM call: if output fails Pydantic validation, automatically retry with the validation error in the prompt. Achieves 99%+ valid schema output in practice. Works with OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, local models.
Solution 3: Constrained Decoding (Outlines)
Grammar-constrained generation: token sampling is restricted to only tokens that could extend a valid JSON document matching your schema. Mathematically guarantees valid output with zero retries. Works with local models via vLLM or llama.cpp. Zero overhead at inference.
🌍 Real-world use case: A product team adopting a reasoning model. They turn on extended thinking only for the hard analytical queries — where the accuracy gain justifies the extra latency and tokens — keep a fast non-thinking model for everything else, and wrap both in Instructor + Pydantic so the structured JSON their app expects validates 99% of the time.
Cloud AI Platforms practical decision-making for AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, GCP Vertex, and HuggingFace. Includes ROI and business case templates, build vs buy vs fine-tune framework, AWS SageMaker Pipelines deep dive, and managing technical debt in AI systems. Architecture decisions only matter if you can justify them to the business.
🟢 Stop here if you just need the concept. Read the intro above, you're done with this topic at a conceptual level.
🟡 Keep going if you're implementing this or prepping for an interview. The math and code below are what you'll be tested on.

This tab is the practical buyer's guide for AI cloud services. The three big providers (AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, GCP Vertex) all let you call top-tier models through their own APIs — but they differ a lot in which models they offer, how easy fine-tuning is, how they handle private data, how they price things, and how they connect to the rest of your stack.

Why this matters more than the tech alone: in real companies, the “best” model rarely wins on its own. The platform that already holds your data wins — because moving data across clouds is slow, expensive, and risky. So the right question is usually not “which model is most accurate?” but “which platform fits the stack we already pay for, the data we cannot move, and the compliance rules we have to follow?” That is a business decision dressed up as a tech decision.

What this tab covers: a side-by-side comparison of Bedrock vs. Azure OpenAI vs. Vertex on the dimensions that actually matter, a build-vs-buy-vs-fine-tune framework for justifying your choice to a CFO, a deep dive on AWS SageMaker Pipelines for production ML, and ROI templates so you can pitch an AI project in business terms instead of just model names.

Cloud LLM Platform Decision: AWS Bedrock vs Azure OpenAI vs GCP Vertex
DimensionAWS BedrockAzure OpenAI ServiceGCP Vertex AI
Model AccessClaude (Anthropic), Llama, Mistral, Titan, Command. Multi-provider via one API. Managed model updates.GPT-4o, GPT-4, GPT-3.5, o1/o3, DALL-E 3. Direct OpenAI model access via Azure infra. Best GPT models, fastest access.Gemini Ultra/Pro/Flash, Llama (via Model Garden), PaLM2. Best Gemini model access.
Ecosystem Lock-inAWS services (S3, Lambda, Bedrock Knowledge Base). Good for AWS-native shops. Strong if already on AWS.Azure OpenAI + Cognitive Search + Azure ML. Tight Microsoft integration. Best for .NET/Microsoft shops.GCP (BigQuery, Vertex AI, GCS). Best for data-heavy orgs already in GCP/BigQuery.
RAG IntegrationBedrock Knowledge Bases: S3 OpenSearch Serverless. Managed chunking, embedding, retrieval. Easy to set up.Azure AI Search: deep RAG integration, hybrid search (BM25+vector), built-in re-ranking. Most mature for RAG.Vertex AI Search (fka Enterprise Search). Managed RAG. Good but less mature than Azure for RAG.
Fine-tuningBedrock fine-tuning (for Titan, Llama). Limited model selection. Provisioned throughput needed for deployment.Azure OpenAI fine-tuning (GPT-3.5, GPT-4). Easiest UI-driven fine-tuning. Limited to OpenAI models only.Vertex AI Supervised Tuning, RLHF, QLoRA via Vertex. Most flexible for Gemini fine-tuning. Best integration with GCP data.
Pricing modelPer-token. Provisioned throughput units (PTU) for guaranteed capacity. Often most cost-effective for high volume.Per-token + PTU (Provisioned Throughput Units). Similar to AWS. Azure enterprise agreements often include credits.Per-token. Committed Use Discounts available. Gemini Flash is often cheapest per token for high-volume tasks.
Best forDiverse LLM needs (want Claude AND Llama AND Mistral). Multi-cloud strategy. AWS-native infrastructure.Best GPT model access. Microsoft enterprise compliance requirements. Azure AD integration. Office 365 data.Heavy BigQuery/GCP data. Multimodal tasks (best Gemini model). Google Workspace integration.
Decision Framework: Build vs Buy vs Fine-tune
Buy / Use API Directly
When to use:
  • Task is general purpose (summarisation, classification, Q&A)
  • Volume is low-medium (<10M tokens/day)
  • Data is not highly sensitive
  • Speed to market matters more than cost optimisation
  • No unique proprietary domain knowledge needed
Cost: $0.01$0.06/1K tokens. No infra cost. Scales instantly.
Fine-tune Existing Model
When to use:
  • Unique tone/style/format requirements (brand voice)
  • Domain-specific terminology that prompting can't reliably handle
  • High volume + cost reduction (replace GPT-4 with fine-tuned GPT-3.5)
  • Latency requirement fine-tuned small model can't meet with large model
  • You have 10010K high-quality examples
Cost: Fine-tune: $500$5K one-time. Savings: 60-80% per token vs flagship model.
Build / Train from Scratch
When to use:
  • Highly proprietary architecture requirements (robotics, chip design)
  • Data is too sensitive for any cloud provider (defence, top-secret healthcare)
  • Existing models fundamentally can't do what you need (new modality)
  • Team has 10+ ML research engineers and 12+ months
  • Company has petabytes of proprietary training data
Cost: $1M$100M+ compute. 1224 months. Very rarely the right answer.
AWS SageMaker Pipelines Deep Dive
What SageMaker Pipelines Does
Step 1: Data Processing (ProcessingStep)
Runs code in managed container. Input: S3 data. Output: processed data to S3. Configure: instance type, instance count, environment variables. Uses SageMaker Processing jobs under the hood.
Step 2: Training (TrainingStep)
Runs training script in managed container. Input: processed data from S3. Output: model artifacts to S3. Configure: estimator (PyTorch, TF, custom), hyperparameters, spot instances for cost savings.
Step 3: Evaluation (ProcessingStep)
Runs evaluation script on test set. Produces JSON report with metrics (accuracy, F1, AUC). This output is used by ConditionStep to decide whether to register the model.
Step 4: Conditional Registration (ConditionStep + RegisterStep)
ConditionStep: "If accuracy > 0.85, register model. Else, skip." RegisterStep: registers model in SageMaker Model Registry with version, approval status, metadata, evaluation report attached.
Step 5: Deployment (Lambda step or manual approval)
After registry approval (manual or automated), Lambda step deploys to endpoint. Can deploy canary (10% new, 90% old), blue/green, or replace entirely. Endpoint config specifies instance type and auto-scaling policy.
SageMaker Pipeline Code Structure
from sagemaker.workflow.pipeline import Pipeline from sagemaker.workflow.steps import ProcessingStep, TrainingStep from sagemaker.workflow.conditions import ConditionGreaterThanOrEqualTo from sagemaker.workflow.condition_step import ConditionStep # Define steps process_step = ProcessingStep( name="PreprocessData", processor=SKLearnProcessor( framework_version="0.23-1", instance_type="ml.m5.xlarge", instance_count=1 ), inputs=[ProcessingInput(source=input_data)], outputs=[ProcessingOutput(output_name="train")], code="preprocess.py" ) train_step = TrainingStep( name="TrainModel", estimator=PyTorch( entry_point="train.py", instance_type="ml.p3.2xlarge", use_spot_instances=True # up to 90% cheaper ), inputs={"train": TrainingInput(s3_data=process_step.properties.Outputs["train"])} ) eval_step = ProcessingStep(name="EvaluateModel", ...) register_step = RegisterModel( name="RegisterModel", estimator=estimator, model_data=train_step.properties.ModelArtifacts.S3ModelArtifacts, content_types=["application/json"], approval_status="PendingManualApproval" ) cond_step = ConditionStep( name="CheckAccuracy", conditions=[ConditionGreaterThanOrEqualTo( left=JsonGet(step=eval_step, property_file="evaluation.json", json_path="metrics.accuracy"), right=0.85 )], if_steps=[register_step], else_steps=[] ) pipeline = Pipeline( name="MyMLPipeline", steps=[process_step, train_step, eval_step, cond_step] ) pipeline.upsert(role_arn=role) pipeline.start()
Cost optimisation tip: Use use_spot_instances=True on training steps. Spot instances save 6090% on training cost. SageMaker handles spot interruption: checkpoints to S3, resumes automatically. Only avoid for jobs under 30 min (setup overhead not worth it).
HuggingFace Ecosystem Production Depth
ToolWhat It DoesWhen to Use in ProductionKey API Pattern
HubModel/dataset/space hosting. 500K+ models. Git-based versioning.Model registry, artefact sharing, private model hosting, inference endpointsfrom_pretrained("org/model-name") downloads weights, tokenizer, config in one call
TransformersUnified API for 500+ architectures: BERT, GPT, T5, LLaMA, Mistral, etc.Fine-tuning, inference, feature extraction across all major architecturespipeline("text-generation", model="...") for inference; AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained() for fine-tuning
PEFT (LoRA/QLoRA)Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning: LoRA, QLoRA, prompt tuning, prefix tuningFine-tune LLMs on consumer/cloud GPUs. 7B model fine-tuning on 1A100 (40GB).get_peft_model(model, LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32))
TRL (SFTTrainer, DPOTrainer)Full RLHF pipeline: SFT, reward modelling, PPO, DPO, ORPO, SimPOAlignment fine-tuning. Chat model training. Preference learning from human feedback.SFTTrainer(model, train_dataset, peft_config) handles tokenisation, packing, PEFT integration
DatasetsStreaming large datasets from Hub. Arrow-backed. Works with Pandas, PyTorch, JAX.Training pipelines with 100GB+ datasets that don't fit in RAM. Multi-GPU data loading.load_dataset("HuggingFaceFW/fineweb", streaming=True)
Inference EndpointsDeploy any Hub model as a serverless or dedicated API endpointQuick production deployment without Kubernetes. Autoscaling. Pay-per-use option.UI-based or API. GPU endpoint: ~$0.60/hr for T4 GPU. Scale to zero supported.
Text Generation Inference (TGI)Optimised serving engine: continuous batching, tensor parallel, quantisationSelf-hosted LLM serving at production scale. Powers HuggingFace Inference Endpoints internally.Docker: docker run -p 8080:80 ghcr.io/huggingface/text-generation-inference --model-id mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1
ROI Template for AI Projects
Cost Model
Cost CategoryHow to EstimateTypical Range
LLM API tokensAvg tokens/request requests/day price/1K tokens 365$10K$500K/yr
InfrastructureVector DB + inference compute + monitoring + storage$20K$200K/yr
Engineering buildEngineers time blended rate ($200K/engineer/yr)$200K$2M one-time
Ongoing maintenance20-30% of build cost annually (model updates, prompt tuning)$40K$600K/yr
Total Year 1Build + infra + tokens + maintenance$300K$3M
Value Model
Value TypeHow to MeasureTypical Range
Labor savingsHours saved/week FTEs hourly rate 52$200K$5M/yr
Error reductionError rate reduction cost per error error volume$100K$2M/yr
Revenue upliftConversion improvement revenue per conversion volume$500K$10M/yr
Cost avoidanceHeadcount not hired fully-loaded cost per FTE$150K$3M/yr
Break-evenBuild cost / (Annual value - Annual running cost)1224 months typical
Presenting to executives: Lead with the business outcome ("$2M/yr labour savings"). Put the cost second. Use a 3-year NPV not a 1-year cost figure. NPV looks better AND is more accurate for infrastructure investments.
Technical Debt in AI Systems
5 Forms of AI Technical Debt (Sculley et al., "Hidden Technical Debt in ML Systems")
1. Entanglement
Changing any input feature changes the behaviour of every feature. CACE principle: "Change Anything, Change Everything." Solution: feature isolation, individual feature ablation tests in CI.
2. Hidden Feedback Loops
Model predictions influence the data the model will be trained on next (e.g., recommendation system). System slowly shifts to serve its own predictions rather than true user preferences. Solution: log counterfactuals, periodically retrain on unbiased data.
3. Undeclared Consumers
Model used by teams who were never told its limitations. When it fails (silently), they don't know to look at the model. Solution: model cards, versioned APIs, mandatory change notifications to consumers.
4. Data Dependencies
Unstable data sources (schema changes, pipeline failures) silently degrade model quality. Solution: data contracts, schema validation (Great Expectations), data freshness alerts.
5. Pipeline Jungles
Experimental code that became production code. Spaghetti feature engineering scripts nobody dares change. Solution: regular refactor sprints, feature store to standardise features across experiments.
Preventing vs Paying Down AI Debt
When should you pay down AI tech debt?
High urgency to pay down
Model degrading and you can't diagnose why. Adding new features takes weeks because the pipeline is fragile. Onboarding a new engineer takes months because there are no abstractions.
Medium urgency
CI passes but coverage is low. Prod model and dev model have different preprocessing. Evaluation metrics are inconsistent across experiments.
Preventative (do always)
Data contracts from day 1. Feature store instead of ad-hoc feature engineering. Evaluation harness before first production deployment. Model cards for every model. Version pin all dependencies.
Rule of thumb
Allocate 20% of engineering time to debt reduction in mature AI systems. Treat like security: if you don't schedule it, it never happens and the cost of an incident is 10 the cost of prevention.
The compound interest of AI debt: In software, debt grows linearly. In AI, it compounds a degrading model causes bad training data, which degrades the next model, which further corrupts data. The feedback loop accelerates the deterioration. Pay it down sooner than you think you need to.
🌍 Real-world use case: A scale-up deciding build vs. buy for its first GenAI feature. It starts on a managed platform (Bedrock / Azure OpenAI / Vertex) to ship in weeks, builds an ROI model before committing headcount, and sets data contracts and model cards from day one — so the system doesn't accumulate the hidden technical debt that compounds in ML.
Curated learning paths — Pick the path that matches what you actually need to ship in the next 4–8 weeks. Each path is an ordered sequence — start at #1, go down. Your progress (from the "Mark as read" button on each topic) is shown live. The next topic to read is highlighted in purple.
What is this tab? This tab is the shortcut board for role- or project-based reading sequences across the document, so you can follow a single path instead of designing your own from scratch.
Why it matters: The main plan is broad, but many users need a tighter path for one outcome such as shipping RAG quickly, learning MLOps foundations, or preparing for a frontier-model interview.
How to use it: Choose the path closest to your immediate goal, work from step 1 downward, and use the progress markers to keep your next topic visible across sessions.
Example: If you need to become productive on RAG in a weekend, this tab gives you the exact subset and order without reading unrelated areas first.

How to Use the Glossary
What is this glossary? This is the fast-reference vocabulary layer for the document, collecting acronyms, platform terms, model concepts, and interview shorthand in one searchable place.
Why it matters: AI interviews often move fast, and missing the meaning of one acronym can break your understanding of an entire answer or architecture discussion.
How to use it: Search for a term, read the short definition, then jump straight to the linked deep-dive tab if you need the full implementation, math, or architecture context.
Example: If you forget what GRPO, HNSW, or OPA means in the middle of practice, use the glossary to recover the term quickly and return to the main content.
